topics.title: Types of Bolts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Bolts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Bolts in Steel Structures?

Bolts are the most common and versatile mechanical fasteners used to connect **structural steel members** such as beams, columns, gusset plates, and bracing members. According to **IS 800:2007** (the governing Indian Standard for General Construction in Steel), a bolt is a threaded fastener with a head and a shank, screwed into a nut to clamp two or more components together. The connection transfers loads — tension, shear, or a combination — through the bolt shank, the plates in contact, and the bearing surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Under the **2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** for *PECET864 - Advanced Design of Steel Structures*, Module 1 demands a rigorous classification of bolts based on the **mechanism of load transfer** and the **type of installation**. Students must distinguish clearly between *bearing-type* and *friction-type (slip-critical)* bolts.

## 1.2 Two Principal Mechanisms of Load Transfer

At the heart of bolt classification lies one simple question: *How does the load get from one plate to the other?*

| Mechanism | How Load Travels | KTU Term |
|---|---|---|
| Plate pressed against bolt shank | Shear in bolt + Bearing on hole wall | **Bearing-Type Connection** |
| Plate clamped tightly; friction resists slip | Friction between clamped surfaces | **Friction-Type / Slip-Critical Connection** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Two Wrenches Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine you are tightening two plates together using (A) a **regular hardware-store bolt** and (B) a **calibrated torque wrench on a high-grade bolt**.
>
> * In **(A)**, the bolt is tightened "as snug as possible by a spanner." The plates touch, but they could slip the moment you push sideways. The load is carried only by the bolt *pushing* against the hole's edge — this is **bearing-type**.
>
> * In **(B)**, the bolt is stretched (pre-tensioned) using a *controlled, very high* torque. The plates are now *squeezed* together with such force that they grip each other through **friction** — like two sheets glued by squeezing. The load is transferred by *friction*, before the bolt even touches the hole wall. This is the **HSFG (High Strength Friction Grip)** bolt.

## 1.4 Formal Classification as per IS 800:2007

The Indian Standard classifies bolts into the following categories:

### A. Bearing-Type Bolts (Ordinary / Common Bolts)
1. **Bolts in Clearance Holes** — also called *Black Bolts* or *Unfinished Bolts*.
2. **Bolts in Close-Tolerance / Reamed Holes** — also called *Turned / Fitted Bolts*.
3. **Ribbed Bolts** (Annex G of IS 800).
4. **Waisted Bolts** (Annex G of IS 800).

### B. Friction-Type Bolts (High Strength Friction Grip — HSFG)
1. **HSFG Bolts** conforming to **IS 3757** & **IS 4000**, installed per **IS 4000**.

### C. Special Category (for completeness)
- **Foundation Bolts / Anchor Bolts** — used to anchor columns to concrete pedestals.

## 1.5 Quick Snapshot Table

| Property | Black Bolts | Turned/Fitted Bolts | HSFG Bolts |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IS Code reference** | IS 800 Cl. 10.3 | IS 800 Cl. 10.4 | IS 800 Cl. 10.5 / IS 4000 |
| **Hole type** | Standard clearance | Close / reamed | Standard clearance (slightly larger) |
| **Tightening method** | Manual spanner | Hand snug | Calibrated torque/wrench control |
| **Load transfer** | Bearing + Shear | Bearing + Shear | **Friction only** |
| **Pre-tension** | None | None | **Very high (proof load)** |
| **Typical grade** | 4.6, 5.6 | 5.6, 8.8 | **8.8, 9.8, 10.9** |
| **Slip permitted?** | Yes | Negligible | **No slip at service load** |
| **Use case** | Light structures, towers | Machinery, dynamic loads | Bridges, heavy industrial structures |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bearing-Type Bolts (Ordinary Bolts)

These are the **most common bolts** used in Indian steel construction. They rely on the bolt shank **shearing** and the plate **bearing against the bolt shank** to transfer load.

### 2.1.1 Black Bolts (Unfinished Bolts)
- Manufactured from low-carbon steel, usually grade **4.6** (UTS = 400 MPa, YS = 0.6 × 400 = **240 MPa**).
- Installed in **standard clearance holes** — the hole diameter is typically **1.5 mm to 2.0 mm larger** than the bolt nominal diameter.
- Tightened to a "snug-tight" condition (just enough contact, no controlled preload).
- **Cheap, fast, but inherently slip-prone** — a small slip occurs the moment service load is applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why black bolts slip:** Because there is *no controlled pre-tension*, the bolt only goes into action **after** the plates have already slipped until the bolt shank contacts the hole wall. This slip is small (≈ 2 mm) but is critical for fatigue and dynamic loading.

### 2.1.2 Turned / Fitted Bolts (Close-Tolerance Bolts)
- Manufactured to **tight dimensional tolerances** — the shank is turned on a lathe to a precise diameter.
- Installed in **reamed holes** with a clearance of only **0.15 mm to 0.2 mm**.
- Slip is virtually eliminated — they behave closer to a rivet.
- Used where **no slip is tolerable**, e.g., dynamically loaded machinery supports.
- Typically grade **5.6** or **8.8**.

### 2.1.3 Ribbed Bolts
- The shank has **longitudinal ribs** that grip the hole wall, providing extra shear resistance.
- Hole clearance is standard, but the ribs cut into the plate during installation.
- Less common than HSFG but listed in **Annex G of IS 800:2007**.

### 2.1.4 Waisted Bolts
- The shank is **reduced in diameter (waisted)** in the middle portion where shear occurs.
- The reduced area (the *waist*) is the **shear plane**, but the threaded portion (larger) is excluded from the shear plane.
- This ensures **shear failure occurs on the unthreaded shank** rather than on threads — yielding a predictable shear strength.
- Also covered in **Annex G** of IS 800.

## 2.2 Friction-Type Bolts — HSFG Bolts

HSFG stands for **High Strength Friction Grip Bolts**. These are *pre-tensioned* high-grade bolts that transfer load entirely by **clamping friction** between the connected plates. The bolt shank does **not** touch the hole wall under service loads.

### 2.2.1 Key Properties
- Bolt grades: **8.8, 9.8, 10.9** (UTS up to 1040 MPa).
- Tightening is done by **calibrated wrench method, part-turn method, or direct-tension-indicator (DTI) washers**, all governed by **IS 4000**.
- The bolt is tightened to its **proof load** ($F_p$), which equals approximately **70 % of the ultimate tensile strength** times the *stress area*.

### 2.2.2 Why HSFG Bolts are Special
- **No slip at service load** → ideal for bridges, crane girders, structures under fatigue.
- Allowable stress design uses a **factor of safety of 1.25** (versus 1.25–1.5 for bearing bolts, depending on load type).
- Surfaces must be prepared to a defined **slip coefficient** — clean mill scale gives $\mu \approx 0.30$ to $0.35$; sand-blasted surfaces give $\mu \approx 0.45$ to $0.50$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use HSFG?** If a bridge truss is built with black bolts, every passing vehicle would cause the joints to *tick* (slip). Under millions of cycles, this slip wears the holes and causes fatigue cracking. HSFG bolts eliminate slip, hence eliminate the *tick*.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are from IS 800:2007 and are board-exam-ready.**

### Table 2.1 — Design Strength of a Bolt

| Failure Mode | Nominal Strength ($V_{dsb}$ or $V_{dpb}$) | Key Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear in shank** (bearing bolt) | $V_{dsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot (n_n \cdot A_{nb} + n_s \cdot A_{sb}) \cdot \gamma_{mb}$ | $f_{ub}$ = ultimate tensile strength of bolt; $A_{nb}$, $A_{sb}$ = nominal/shank area; $n_n$, $n_s$ = number of shear planes through threads/shank |
| **Bearing on plate** (bearing bolt) | $V_{dpb} = 2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u / \gamma_{mb}$ | $d$ = nominal bolt dia; $t$ = plate thickness; $f_u$ = ultimate tensile strength of plate; $k_b$ = 0.5 (for edge), 1.0 (general), 0.5 (oversize hole) |
| **Tension in bolt** | $T_{db} = 0.90 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_n / \gamma_{mb}$ | $A_n$ = net tensile stress area (threaded) |
| **HSFG bolt slip strength** | $V_{dsf} = V_{nsf} = \mu_f \cdot n_e \cdot K_h \cdot F_o / \gamma_{mf}$ | $\mu_f$ = slip coefficient; $n_e$ = number of effective interfaces; $K_h$ = hole factor (1.0 for std); $F_o$ = proof load; $\gamma_{mf}$ = 1.25 |
| **HSFG bolt bearing** | $V_{dpb}$ same as bearing-type | Identical to ordinary bolt |
| **HSFG bolt tension** | $T_{db}$ same as above but $F_o$ replaces $f_{ub}A_n$ in some checks | Clamping force limits applied tension |

### Table 2.2 — Partial Safety Factors ($\gamma_m$)

| Bolt Type | $\gamma_{mb}$ (bolt material) | $\gamma_{mf}$ (friction / slip) |
|---|---|---|
| Bearing-type bolt (shop) | **1.25** | — |
| Bearing-type bolt (field) | **1.25** | — |
| HSFG bolt | — | **1.25** |

### Table 2.3 — Proof Load ($F_o$) for HSFG Bolts

| Bolt Grade | $F_o$ (Proof Load) = $0.70 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb}$ |
|---|---|
| **8.8** | $0.70 \times 800 \times A_{nb} = 560 \cdot A_{nb}$ |
| **9.8** | $0.70 \times 900 \times A_{nb} = 630 \cdot A_{nb}$ |
| **10.9** | $0.70 \times 1040 \cdot A_{nb} = 728 \cdot A_{nb}$ |

*(Note: $A_{nb}$ = net tensile stress area of the bolt, from IS 1367 / IS 800 Table 1.)*

### Table 2.4 — Hole Diameter per IS 800:2007

| Bolt Diameter $d$ (mm) | Std. Hole (mm) | Oversize (mm) | Slotted (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 12 – 14 | $d + 1.0$ | $d + 3.0$ | $d + 4.0$ |
| 16 – 24 | $d + 2.0$ | $d + 4.0$ | $d + 6.0$ |
| > 24 | $d + 2.0$ | $d + 6.0$ | $d + 8.0$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Black bolts** dominate **transmission towers, light industrial sheds, prefabricated structures** — anywhere cost matters more than fatigue life.
- **Turned bolts** survive in **machinery beds and heavy machinery supports** where vibration and zero-slip are mandatory.
- **HSFG bolts** are mandatory for **railway and highway bridges, crane girders, nuclear structures, and high-rise frames under seismic loads** — wherever fatigue, slip-control, or ductility matters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Nominal Shear Strength of a Bearing-Type Bolt

### Step 1 — Start from the basic physics
When a bolt transmits load through a shear plane, the nominal shear stress is the **ultimate tensile stress divided by $\sqrt{3}$** (von Mises yield criterion for pure shear).

$$
\tau_{vb,\text{nom}} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Here, $f_{ub}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the bolt material. We use $\sqrt{3}$ because, for ductile steel, the shear yield is $\approx 0.577$ of the tensile yield (Tresca would use 0.5, but IS 800 adopts von Mises).

### Step 2 — Multiply by the area resisting shear
For a bolt in **single shear**, the resisting area is the bolt's **nominal shank area** $A_{sb} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$. For **double shear**, there are two planes, so the area doubles. In general, for $n_s$ shear planes passing through the unthreaded shank and $n_n$ planes passing through the threaded portion:

$$
V_{nsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \left( n_s \cdot A_{sb} + n_n \cdot A_{nb} \right)
$$

where $A_{nb}$ is the net tensile stress area of the bolt at the thread root.

### Step 3 — Apply the partial safety factor
To convert nominal strength to **design strength**, divide by $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ (for both shop and field, per IS 800 Cl. 10.3.3):

$$
V_{dsb} = \dfrac{V_{nsb}}{\gamma_{mb}}
$$

### Step 4 — Final compact form

$$
V_{dsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mb}} \cdot \left( n_s \cdot A_{sb} + n_n \cdot A_{nb} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The bolt shear strength depends on (a) the *material grade* ($f_{ub}$), (b) the *number of shear planes* ($n_s, n_n$), and (c) the *area* of the bolt at the shear plane. Double shear is twice as strong as single shear — this is why double-angle / double-shear connections are preferred.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Nominal Bearing Strength of the Plate

### Step 1 — Bearing stress concept
When the bolt pushes against the hole wall, the contact area is approximately the **bolt diameter × plate thickness** projected onto the perpendicular plane. IS 800 uses an empirical bearing stress limit of $2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot f_u$.

### Step 2 — Define the constants
- $d$ = nominal bolt diameter
- $t$ = thickness of the thinner plate (for a pack, use $t_{min}$)
- $f_u$ = ultimate tensile strength of the plate material
- $k_b$ = bearing factor:
  * $k_b = 0.5$ for bolts in **edge distance ≤ 1.5 d** or in **oversize / slotted holes**
  * $k_b = 1.0$ otherwise

### Step 3 — Bearing force
The nominal bearing force per bolt is the bearing stress × projected area:

$$
V_{npb} = 2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u
$$

### Step 4 — Apply partial safety factor
With $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$:

$$
V_{dpb} = \dfrac{2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{mb}}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify using the factor of safety approach
The factor of safety on ultimate bearing = $1.25 / 1.0 = 1.25$. Combined with the empirical $2.5 \cdot k_b$, this gives an allowable bearing stress of:

$$
\sigma_{b,\text{allow}} = \dfrac{2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot f_u}{1.25} = 2.0 \cdot k_b \cdot f_u
$$

For $k_b = 1.0$ and $f_u = 410$ MPa (E250 steel): $\sigma_{b,\text{allow}} = 820$ MPa — this is the allowable bearing stress used in WSD checks.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Bearing Bolt Shear Capacity (Class 4.6, M20)

**Given:**
- Bolt: **M20, Grade 4.6**, single shear, threads intercepted by shear plane.
- Plate thickness: $t = 12$ mm, plate grade **E250** ($f_u = 410$ MPa).
- Find: Design shear strength of the bolt.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Identify bolt properties (from IS 800 Table):**
- $d = 20$ mm
- $A_{sb} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.16$ mm²
- $A_{nb} = 245$ mm² (net tensile stress area for M20, from IS 1367)
- $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa (Grade 4.6)

**Step B — Compute nominal shear strength**

$$
V_{nsb} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot (0 \cdot 314.16 + 1 \cdot 245)
$$

$$
V_{nsb} = 230.94 \cdot 245 = 56{,}580 \text{ N} \approx 56.58 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step C — Apply partial safety factor**

$$
V_{dsb} = \dfrac{56.58}{1.25} = 45.26 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice that when the shear plane passes through the *threads*, we use $A_{nb}$ (smaller), not $A_{sb}$. If the designer specified a bolt with the *shank* in the shear plane (e.g., by using a longer bolt with a grip length), the capacity would jump to:
> $V_{dsb} = \dfrac{400/\sqrt{3} \cdot 314.16}{1.25} = 58.04$ kN — a **~28% increase** for free.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — HSFG Bolt Slip Strength

**Given:**
- Bolt: **M20, Grade 8.8**, in a double-cover-plate butt joint (2 interfaces).
- Surface class: **Class A** (clean mill scale), slip coefficient $\mu_f = 0.30$.
- Find: Design slip strength per bolt.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Bolt proof load**
For Grade 8.8, $f_{ub} = 800$ MPa and $A_{nb} = 245$ mm².

$$
F_o = 0.70 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb} = 0.70 \cdot 800 \cdot 245 = 137{,}200 \text{ N} = 137.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step B — Number of effective interfaces**
For a double-cover-plate butt joint, there are **2 slip planes** (one between main plate and each cover plate). So $n_e = 2$.

**Step C — Hole factor**
Standard clearance hole for M20: $K_h = 1.0$.

**Step D — Nominal slip strength**

$$
V_{nsf} = \mu_f \cdot n_e \cdot K_h \cdot F_o = 0.30 \cdot 2 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 137.2 = 82.32 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step E — Apply partial safety factor** $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$

$$
V_{dsf} = \dfrac{82.32}{1.25} = 65.86 \text{ kN}
$$

This is the **service-load slip capacity** of one bolt — i.e., no slip will occur up to ~65.86 kN. Beyond this, the joint slips, the bolt comes into bearing, and the bolt now acts like a bearing-type bolt.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Failure Modes for One Bolt (M20, 4.6, t=12 mm)

| Mode | Nominal (kN) | Design (kN) | % of governing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear through threads (single shear) | 56.58 | **45.26** | 48% |
| Shear through shank (if applicable) | 72.55 | 58.04 | 62% |
| Bearing on plate ($k_b=1.0$) | 246.00 | **196.80** | 209% |
| Tension in bolt | 88.20 | 79.38 | 84% |

**Governing capacity** = $\min$ of all modes = **45.26 kN** (shear through threads).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** In a typical bearing-type lap joint with thin plates, **bolt shear always governs** because the bearing area (d·t) is much larger than the bolt cross-sectional area. Plate tearing / block shear failure then becomes a *separate* check at the connection level.

---

## 3.6 Classification Flow — Selecting the Right Bolt

The decision tree below is the official KTU approach for choosing a bolt for a given situation:

1. **Is fatigue / dynamic / reversal of stress present?**
   - **YES** → HSFG bolt (mandatory for bridges, cranes).
   - **NO** → go to step 2.

2. **Is slip at service load unacceptable?**
   - **YES** → HSFG or turned bolts.
   - **NO** → black bolt is acceptable.

3. **Is the connection subject to impact / vibration (machinery support)?**
   - **YES** → turned / fitted bolts or HSFG.
   - **NO** → black bolt is acceptable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Bolts (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bolts in Steel Connections]:::root --> B[Bearing Type Bolts]:::branch
    A --> C[Friction Type Bolts HSFG]:::branch
    A --> D[Anchor Foundation Bolts]:::branch

    B --> B1[Black Bolts Unfinished]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Turned Fitted Bolts]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Ribbed Bolts]:::leaf
    B --> B4[Waisted Bolts]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Grade 8.8 HSFG]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Grade 9.8 HSFG]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Grade 10.9 HSFG]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Cast-in-place anchor]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Post-installed chemical anchor]:::leaf

    B1 --> B1a[Grade 4.6 / 5.6]:::sub
    B1 --> B1b[Standard clearance hole]:::sub
    B1 --> B1c[Snug tight manual]:::sub

    C1 --> C1a[Proof load 560 x Anb]:::sub
    C1 --> C1b[Calibrated wrench tightening]:::sub
    C1 --> C1c[Slip coefficient 0.30 to 0.50]:::sub

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#111827,stroke-width:1px
    classDef sub fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#111827,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Load Transfer Mechanism — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Applied Load P]:::load --> Q[Connected Plate 1]:::plate
    Q --> R{Mechanism?}:::decision

    R -- Bearing Type --> S1[Bolt Shank in Shear]:::bshear
    S1 --> S2[Bearing on Hole Wall]:::bbear
    S2 --> T[Plate 2 receives load]:::endnode

    R -- Friction Type --> F1[Pre-tensioned Bolt Clamps Plates]:::clamp
    F1 --> F2[Friction between contact surfaces]:::fric
    F2 --> T

    classDef load fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#111827
    classDef plate fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#111827
    classDef decision fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207,color:#111827
    classDef bshear fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#111827
    classDef bbear fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#111827
    classDef endnode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#111827
    classDef clamp fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#111827
    classDef fric fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#111827
```

## 4.3 HSFG Bolt Assembly — Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hexagonal Head]:::comp --> S[Shank]:::comp
    S --> T[Threaded Portion]:::comp
    T --> N[Nut]:::comp
    S -. gripped between .-> P1[Plate 1 Main Member]:::plate
    S -. gripped between .-> P2[Plate 2 Cover Plate]:::plate
    N --> W1[Washer under Nut]:::comp
    H --> W2[Washer under Head]:::comp

    W1 --> FT[Calibrated Pre-tension Proof Load Fo]:::force
    FT --> F1[Clamping Force on Plate 1]:::force
    FT --> F2[Clamping Force on Plate 2]:::force

    classDef comp fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#111827
    classDef plate fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#111827
    classDef force fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#111827
```

## 4.4 Failure Modes of a Single Bolt (Schematic Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Single Bolt in Shear and Tension]:::root --> F1[Mode 1 Shear Failure of Bolt]:::fail
    M --> F2[Mode 2 Bearing Failure of Plate]:::fail
    M --> F3[Mode 3 Tension Failure of Bolt]:::fail
    M --> F4[Mode 4 Shear Tear Out of Plate Edge]:::fail
    M --> F5[Mode 5 Net Section Rupture of Plate]:::fail

    F1 --> F1a[Single shear or double shear plane]:::detail
    F2 --> F2a[Crushing of hole wall]:::detail
    F3 --> F3a[Thread strip or shank necking]:::detail
    F4 --> F4a[Requires adequate edge distance]:::detail
    F5 --> F5a[Reduced by hole diameter]:::detail

    classDef root fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef fail fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207,color:#111827
    classDef detail fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#4b5563,color:#111827
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each Mermaid chart is a **block-level functional architecture** that maps the conceptual relationships between bolt types, load-transfer paths, and failure modes. Use these as revision maps before exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Distinguish between bearing-type and friction-type bolts with respect to their mechanism of load transfer."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bearing-Type Bolt | Friction-Type (HSFG) Bolt |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Load transferred by **shear in bolt** + **bearing on hole wall** | Load transferred by **friction between clamped surfaces** due to pre-tensioning |
| Pre-tension | Negligible / snug-tight | **Very high** — bolt tightened to proof load |
| Slip | Slip occurs at service load | **No slip** at service load |
| Bolt grade | 4.6, 5.6, 8.8 | **8.8, 9.8, 10.9** |
| IS code | IS 800 Cl. 10.3 | IS 800 Cl. 10.5 / IS 4000 |

*(Each row = 1 mark × 3 rows = full marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"What are the different types of bearing-type bolts as per IS 800:2007?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
As per **IS 800:2007**, bearing-type bolts are classified into the following four categories:
1. **Bolts in clearance holes** — also called *black bolts* or *unfinished bolts* (1 mark).
2. **Bolts in close-tolerance / reamed holes** — also called *turned* or *fitted bolts* (1 mark).
3. **Ribbed bolts** — with longitudinal ribs that grip the hole wall (½ mark).
4. **Waisted bolts** — with a reduced shank diameter ensuring shear failure occurs on the unthreaded portion (½ mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the different types of bolts used in steel structures as per IS 800:2007. State the IS code governing HSFG bolt installation and the proof load concept.
>
> **(b) [7 marks)** An M20 bolt of grade 4.6 is used in single shear in a lap joint connecting 12 mm thick plates of grade E250 steel. The shear plane passes through the threads. Determine:
> (i) the nominal shear strength of the bolt,
> (ii) the nominal bearing strength of the plate, and
> (iii) the design strength of the connection.
> Given: $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa, $A_{nb} = 245$ mm², $A_{sb} = 314.16$ mm², $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — [7 marks]

**(i) Bearing-Type Bolts (4 marks):**

1. **Black / Unfinished Bolts (1 mark):** Manufactured from low-carbon steel (grade 4.6), installed in clearance holes of 1.5–2.0 mm larger than bolt diameter. Tightened "snug-tight" by manual spanner. Load is transferred by *bearing on hole wall* and *shear in bolt shank*. Slip occurs at service load. Used in light structures like transmission towers and trusses.

2. **Turned / Fitted Bolts (1 mark):** The bolt shank is machined (turned) to precise diameter and fitted into reamed holes with only 0.15–0.2 mm clearance. Slip is virtually eliminated. Used in dynamically loaded structures and machinery supports.

3. **Ribbed Bolts (1 mark):** Have longitudinal ribs on the shank that grip the hole wall, providing additional shear resistance through rib-plate interlock. Specified in Annex G of IS 800.

4. **Waisted Bolts (1 mark):** The shank diameter is reduced (waisted) in the shear plane region, ensuring that shear failure occurs on the smooth shank and not on the threaded portion. This provides a predictable shear capacity.

**(ii) Friction-Type (HSFG) Bolts (2 marks):**

- HSFG bolts conform to **IS 3757** (manufacture) and **IS 4000** (installation procedure).
- Made from high-grade steel — **grade 8.8, 9.8, or 10.9**.
- Installed in clearance holes but tightened to **proof load** using calibrated wrench.
- **Proof load** $F_o = 0.70 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb}$ — the bolt is pre-tensioned to this value, generating clamping force on the plates.
- Load transfer: by **friction** between clamped surfaces (slip coefficient $\mu_f$). No slip under service load.
- Used in bridges, crane girders, and structures under fatigue / dynamic loads.

**(iii) Other Bolts (1 mark):**
- **Anchor / foundation bolts** are used to anchor columns to concrete pedestals; not part of the load-transfer classification but are part of overall bolt usage.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — [7 marks]

**Given data:**
- Bolt: M20, grade 4.6, $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa
- Plate: E250, $t = 12$ mm, $f_u = 410$ MPa
- $A_{nb} = 245$ mm², $A_{sb} = 314.16$ mm²
- $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$
- Single shear, threads in shear plane → $n_n = 1$, $n_s = 0$

**(i) Nominal shear strength of the bolt:**

$$
V_{nsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \left( n_s \cdot A_{sb} + n_n \cdot A_{nb} \right)
$$

$$
V_{nsb} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot (0 \cdot 314.16 + 1 \cdot 245)
$$

$$
V_{nsb} = 230.94 \cdot 245 = 56{,}581 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{56.58 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(2 marks for substituting and computing correctly)**

**(ii) Nominal bearing strength of the plate:**
For a standard hole with adequate edge distance, $k_b = 1.0$.

$$
V_{npb} = 2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u = 2.5 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 20 \cdot 12 \cdot 410
$$

$$
V_{npb} = 246{,}000 \text{ N} = \mathbf{246.0 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(2 marks for substitution and computing correctly)**

**(iii) Design strength of the connection:**

Design shear strength of bolt:

$$
V_{dsb} = \dfrac{56.58}{1.25} = \mathbf{45.26 \text{ kN}}
$$

Design bearing strength of plate:

$$
V_{dpb} = \dfrac{246.0}{1.25} = \mathbf{196.80 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Design strength of the connection** = minimum of the two failure modes:

$$
V_{d,\text{conn}} = \min(V_{dsb}, \, V_{dpb}) = \min(45.26, \, 196.80) = \mathbf{45.26 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(3 marks: 1 for design formulas, 1 for computing both, 1 for identifying the governing capacity)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **High Strength Friction Grip (HSFG) bolts**. Discuss their advantages over ordinary bolts, the installation procedure as per IS 4000, and the slip-strength formula.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A double-cover-plate butt joint is connected using **M20 HSFG bolts of grade 8.8** in standard clearance holes. The surface class is *Class A* (clean mill scale). Determine:
> (i) the proof load per bolt,
> (ii) the nominal slip strength per bolt, and
> (iii) the design slip strength per bolt.
> Given: $f_{ub} = 800$ MPa, $A_{nb} = 245$ mm², $\mu_f = 0.30$, $n_e = 2$, $K_h = 1.0$, $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**
HSFG bolts are high-tensile bolts (typically grades 8.8, 9.8, 10.9) installed with a controlled **pre-tension** equal to the *proof load* of the bolt. The pre-tension clamps the connected plates together so firmly that the **friction** developed at the contact interface resists the applied shear force, *before* the bolt shank comes into bearing against the hole wall. Hence they are also called *slip-critical* or *friction-type* bolts.

**Advantages over ordinary bolts (3 marks):**
1. **No slip at service load** — the joint behaves as a rigid unit; this is essential for fatigue-prone structures like bridges and crane girders.
2. **Higher fatigue life** — because there is no micro-slip, fretting corrosion and fatigue cracking are avoided.
3. **Smaller bolt count** may be possible — because the full pre-tension is utilised for friction.
4. **Better performance under reversal of stress** — the pre-tension keeps the joint in compression, reducing the risk of joint opening.
5. **Better performance under impact / dynamic loads.**

**Installation procedure as per IS 4000 (1 mark):**
1. Calibrated wrench method — torque wrench set to deliver proof-load tension.
2. Part-turn method — snug-tight, then rotate nut by a specified angle (typically ½ to ⅔ turn).
3. Direct Tension Indicator (DTI) washers — compress to a calibrated gap when proof load is reached.

**Slip-strength formula (1 mark):**

$$
V_{dsf} = \dfrac{\mu_f \cdot n_e \cdot K_h \cdot F_o}{\gamma_{mf}}
$$

where $F_o = 0.70 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb}$, and $\mu_f$, $n_e$, $K_h$ are as described earlier.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Bolt: M20, Grade 8.8 HSFG, $f_{ub} = 800$ MPa, $A_{nb} = 245$ mm²
- $\mu_f = 0.30$, $n_e = 2$, $K_h = 1.0$, $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$

**(i) Proof load per bolt:**

$$
F_o = 0.70 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb} = 0.70 \cdot 800 \cdot 245
$$

$$
F_o = 137{,}200 \text{ N} = \mathbf{137.2 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(2 marks for formula substitution and result)**

**(ii) Nominal slip strength per bolt:**

$$
V_{nsf} = \mu_f \cdot n_e \cdot K_h \cdot F_o
$$

$$
V_{nsf} = 0.30 \cdot 2 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 137.2
$$

$$
V_{nsf} = 82.32 \text{ kN}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Design slip strength per bolt:**

$$
V_{dsf} = \dfrac{V_{nsf}}{\gamma_{mf}} = \dfrac{82.32}{1.25}
$$

$$
V_{dsf} = \mathbf{65.86 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(2 marks for formula and final answer)**

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Each M20 HSFG bolt in this joint can resist up to **65.86 kN** of service load without slip occurring. If the applied load exceeds this, the joint slips and the bolt then acts as a bearing-type bolt with its shear and bearing capacities.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2–4 marks per question:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the partial safety factor** ($\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ or $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$). Board examiners specifically check this; missing it means *direct 1–2 mark deduction*.
> 2. **Using $A_{sb}$ when threads are in shear plane.** If the problem states "threads intercepted by shear plane," use $A_{nb}$; otherwise $A_{sb}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ in shear strength derivation.** This is the von Mises factor — missing it loses a mark for *incomplete derivation*.
> 4. **Confusing slip coefficient $\mu_f$ with friction coefficient in general mechanics.** In HSFG, $\mu_f$ depends on surface preparation (Class A = 0.30, Class B = 0.45, Class C = 0.55 per IS 4000). Using a wrong value is a frequent error.
> 5. **Wrong $n_e$ count.** For a lap joint, $n_e = 1$; for a double-cover butt joint, $n_e = 2$. This is a board-exam trap.
> 6. **Mixing up proof load with ultimate load.** $F_o = 0.70 f_{ub} A_{nb}$, not $f_{ub} A_{nb}$.
> 7. **Not stating the IS code reference.** Always cite *IS 800:2007*, *IS 4000*, or *IS 3757* wherever applicable — examiners reward code-citing answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Two fundamental bolt types:** Bearing-type vs Friction-type (HSFG). The difference is **load transfer mechanism**, not just material grade.
- **Bearing-type** = load via *shear in bolt + bearing on hole*. Slip allowed. Grades 4.6, 5.6, 8.8. IS 800 Cl. 10.3.
- **Friction-type (HSFG)** = load via *friction between clamped plates*. No slip at service. Grades 8.8, 9.8, 10.9. IS 800 Cl. 10.5; installation per IS 4000.
- **Four bearing-type sub-types:** Black, Turned/Fitted, Ribbed, Waisted.
- **Black bolt** = cheapest, fastest, but slip-prone; used in towers and sheds.
- **Turned/Fitted bolt** = tight clearance (0.15–0.2 mm), no slip, used in machinery supports.
- **HSFG bolt** = high pre-tension, calibrated tightening, mandatory for bridges and fatigue-loaded structures.
- **Shear strength formula:** $V_{dsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mb}} \cdot (n_s A_{sb} + n_n A_{nb})$ — always include the $\sqrt{3}$ and $\gamma_{mb}$.
- **Bearing strength formula:** $V_{dpb} = \dfrac{2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{mb}}$ — $k_b = 1.0$ generally, $0.5$ for edge / oversize hole.
- **HSFG slip strength:** $V_{dsf} = \dfrac{\mu_f \cdot n_e \cdot K_h \cdot F_o}{\gamma_{mf}}$ with $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$.
- **Proof load:** $F_o = 0.70 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb}$ — applies to HSFG bolts only.
- **Partial safety factors:** $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ (bearing bolt); $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$ (HSFG slip).
- **Hole clearance** for standard holes = $d + 1$ mm (≤14 mm bolts) or $d + 2$ mm (≥16 mm bolts).
- **Slip coefficient $\mu_f$:** Class A (clean mill) = 0.30, Class B (sand-blasted) = 0.45, Class C (galvanized) = 0.55.
- **Governing failure mode** for thin-plate bolted connection is almost always **bolt shear**, *not* plate bearing.
- **Always cite IS codes** in KTU answers — *IS 800:2007*, *IS 4000*, *IS 1367*, *IS 3757*.
- **Mnemonic to remember bolt grades:** *4.6 → 400 MPa UTS / 240 MPa YS*; *8.8 → 800 MPa UTS / 640 MPa YS*; *10.9 → 1040 MPa UTS / 940 MPa YS* (first × second-tenth for YS).
- **Bolt shear capacity** in single-shear, threads intercepted, M20 grade 4.6 ≈ **45 kN** (board-exam benchmark value — memorize this as a quick check).
- **HSFG M20 grade 8.8 slip capacity** in double-cover joint with $\mu_f = 0.30$ ≈ **66 kN** (board-exam benchmark — memorize).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearing and High Strength Bolts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bearing and High Strength Bolts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bearing Type Bolts — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bearing Type Bolts** are ordinary structural bolts (typically of grades **4.6, 5.6, or 8.8** as per **IS 1367**) used in connections where the load is transferred from one connected plate to another through **shear in the bolt shank** and **bearing (crushing) on the contact area between the bolt shank and the plate hole wall**. The bolt shank physically *bears* against the hole wall, hence the name. They are governed by **IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.3)**.

In a bearing type connection, the bolt is **not** initially tensioned to a specified proof load. The bolt only goes into tension if the connection is subject to external tensile forces. The clamping force (pre-tension) is incidental and not relied upon for design.

### 1.2 High Strength Friction Grip (HSFG) Bolts — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Strength Friction Grip (HSFG) Bolts** are pre-tensioned bolts of grades **8.8, 9.8, or 10.9** (commonly 10.9) that transfer shear loads between connected plates through **friction developed at the plate-to-plate interface** by the clamping action of the bolt. The shank is *not* designed to bear against the hole wall under service loads. They are governed by **IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.4)** and use **IS 3757** for supply, **IS 6620** for assembly, and **IS 6649** for tightening.

The bolt is tightened until the induced tensile force equals the **proof load** ($F_o = 0.7 \, f_{ub} \, A_{nb}$), creating a firm frictional grip that prevents slip. The shear capacity is governed entirely by the **friction coefficient** ($\mu_f$) at the interface.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Imagine a stack of books on a table held together by a tightly clamped C-clamp:**

- **Bearing type bolt** = Imagine two planks joined by a single nail hammered through them. When you push one plank sideways, the nail *bears* against the wood surrounding the hole. The nail takes the load by being squeezed against the wood. It can slip, and then works in shear. This is the **Bearing Type Bolt**.

- **HSFG bolt** = Now imagine the same two planks joined by a powerful screw clamp. The clamp is tightened so firmly that the two planks cannot slide against each other at all — the friction between the planks does the work of transferring the load. The bolt itself is in tension (stretched), and the load is transferred via *friction* at the contact face. This is the **HSFG Bolt**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick:** "Bearing bolts *bear* on the hole. HSFG bolts *grip* by friction." 

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (IS 800:2007)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\gamma_{mb}$ | Partial safety factor for material of bolt (bearing) | **1.25** |
| $\gamma_{mf}$ | Partial safety factor for HSFG bolt (service) | **1.10** |
| $\gamma_{mf}$ | Partial safety factor for HSFG bolt (ultimate) | **1.25** |
| $\mu_f$ | Slip coefficient (Coefficient of Friction) | **0.20 to 0.55** (surface dependent) |
| $f_{ub}$ | Ultimate tensile strength of bolt | **400 MPa (4.6) to 1040 MPa (10.9)** |
| $F_o$ | Proof load of bolt | $0.7 \, f_{ub} \, A_{nb}$ |
| $d$ | Nominal bolt diameter | **M16, M20, M24, M30, M36** |

### 1.5 Categories of HSFG Connections (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.4.4)

> [!NOTE]
> HSFG connections are classified into three categories based on the **slip resistance factor** ($\gamma_{mf}$):

- **Category A — Slip-Critical (Service):** Slip must not occur under **service loads**. Used where slippage would impair serviceability (e.g., bridges under repeated live loads). Uses $\gamma_{mf} = 1.10$.
- **Category B — Slip-Critical (Ultimate):** Slip must not occur under **1.2 × factored loads**. A middle-ground case. Uses $\gamma_{mf} = 1.20$.
- **Category C — Bearing-Type HSFG:** Slip is permitted under service loads, but ultimate strength is governed by bolt shear and bearing. Uses $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Failure Modes in a Bearing-Type Bolted Connection

When a bearing-type bolt is loaded in shear, **four distinct failure modes** are possible. The design strength of the connection is governed by the **minimum** of:

1. **Shear failure of the bolt shank** (single or double shear)
2. **Bearing failure of the plate** (crushing of plate material at hole wall)
3. **Tensile failure of the plate** (tearing across the net section)
4. **Block shear failure** (a combination of shear and tension on a corner block)

For this module, we focus on **(1) and (2)** — the bolt shear and plate bearing limits.

### 2.2 Bolt Shear Strength — Theoretical Foundation

When a bolt is subjected to pure shear, the **Tresca (maximum shear stress) theory** governs failure. The shear yield strength is $f_y / \sqrt{3}$ and the ultimate shear strength is $f_{ub} / \sqrt{3}$.

- If the **shear plane passes through the unthreaded shank**, the gross cross-sectional area $A_{sb} = \pi d^2 / 4$ resists the shear.
- If the **shear plane passes through the threaded portion**, the **net tensile stress area** $A_{nb}$ is used (since threads are the weaker cross-section in tension, and shear area is conservatively taken as the net area).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 800 Convention:** Unless specified otherwise, the shear plane is **assumed to pass through the threads** for design. The designer may optionally specify a connection where threads are excluded from the shear plane (Type 'X' connection), in which case $A_{sb}$ is used.

### 2.3 Bearing Strength — Theoretical Foundation

Bearing failure occurs when the contact pressure between the bolt shank and the hole wall exceeds the **crushing strength** of the plate. The bearing stress is the bolt load divided by the **projected bearing area**:

$$\text{Bearing Area} = d \times t$$

where $d$ is the bolt nominal diameter and $t$ is the plate thickness.

The nominal bearing strength per IS 800 is empirically calibrated as:

$$V_{npb} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u$$

The factor **2.5** is an empirical multiplier (not from a closed-form plasticity solution). The factor $k_b$ is a reduction factor to account for **edge distance** and **pitch** limitations.

### 2.4 Reduction Factor $k_b$ — Detailed Logic

The factor $k_b$ ensures that bolt spacing and edge distances are adequate to develop the full bearing capacity:

$$k_b = \min \left( \frac{e}{3 d_0}, \; \frac{p}{3 d_0} - 0.25, \; \frac{f_{ub}}{f_u}, \; 1.0 \right)$$

Where:
- $e$ = end distance (from bolt centre to connected edge of plate)
- $p$ = pitch (centre-to-centre spacing of bolts in the direction of load)
- $d_0$ = diameter of the bolt hole (typically $d + 1.5$ to $d + 2.0$ mm clearance)
- $f_{ub}$ = ultimate tensile strength of the **bolt**
- $f_u$ = ultimate tensile strength of the **plate**

> [!TIP]
> The term $f_{ub}/f_u$ ensures the bolt is not stronger than the plate. If $f_{ub} > f_u$, the bolt cannot yield the plate locally — it would fail in the plate first, hence a cap is required.

### 2.5 HSFG Bolt — Slip Resistance Theory

The slip resistance of an HSFG bolt is governed by the **frictional force** at the interface, which equals the product of the **slip coefficient** ($\mu_f$), the **number of effective friction interfaces** ($n_e$), the **bolt hole factor** ($K_h$), and the **proof load** ($F_o$):

$$V_{nsf} = \mu_f \, n_e \, K_h \, F_o$$

The proof load is computed as:

$$F_o = A_{nb} \times f_o = A_{nb} \times 0.7 \, f_{ub}$$

The factor $K_h$ accounts for the hole type (standard, oversized, slotted) and is typically **1.0** for standard holes.

### 2.6 Reduction Factors for Bearing Bolts (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.3.3)

> [!NOTE]
> Three reduction factors modify the design strength of bearing bolts when the connection geometry deviates from the standard:

- $\beta_{lj}$ — for **long joints** (length > 15d in the load direction)
- $\beta_{lg}$ — for **large grip lengths** (grip > 5d)
- $\beta_{pk}$ — for **packing plates** (packing thickness > 6 mm)

These factors are **multiplied** with the nominal strength:

$$V_{dsb} = \frac{\beta_{lj} \, \beta_{lg} \, \beta_{pk} \, V_{nsb}}{\gamma_{mb}}$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B questions frequently test these formulas directly.

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_o = 0.7 \, f_{ub} \, A_{nb}$ | Proof load of bolt |
| 2 | $V_{nsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \left( n_n \, A_{nb} + n_s \, A_{sb} \right)$ | Nominal shear capacity of bolt |
| 3 | $V_{dsb} = \dfrac{V_{nsb}}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | Design shear capacity ($\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$) |
| 4 | $V_{npb} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u$ | Nominal bearing capacity |
| 5 | $V_{dpb} = \dfrac{V_{npb}}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | Design bearing capacity |
| 6 | $k_b = \min\left( \dfrac{e}{3d_0}, \dfrac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25, \dfrac{f_{ub}}{f_u}, 1.0 \right)$ | Bearing reduction factor |
| 7 | $V_{dsf} = \dfrac{\mu_f \, n_e \, K_h \, F_o}{\gamma_{mf}}$ | Design slip resistance of HSFG bolt |
| 8 | $\beta_{lj} = 1.075 - 0.005 (l_j / d)$ | Long joint factor (valid for $15d < l_j \leq 60d$) |
| 9 | $\beta_{lg} = \dfrac{8d}{3d + l_g} \leq \beta_{lj}$ | Large grip length factor |
| 10 | $T_{nb} = 0.90 \, f_{ub} \, A_n < T_{db}$ | Nominal tensile capacity of bolt |
| 11 | $T_{db} = \dfrac{T_{nb}}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | Design tensile capacity |
| 12 | $V_{dsb,\,combined} = \sqrt{(V_{dsb})^2 + ...}$ | Interaction formula for shear + tension |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where are these bolts used?**
> - **Bearing type bolts** — Common in **building frames, trusses, and tower structures** where slip is acceptable. Cheaper and easier to install than HSFG.
> - **HSFG bolts** — Mandatory in **bridges, crane girders, and dynamically loaded structures** where slip would cause fatigue or serviceability issues. The Mumbai–Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail project and most Indian Railway bridges use HSFG bolts of grade 10.9.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Design Shear Strength of a Bolt (Threads Intercepting Shear Plane)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation follows the IS 800:2007 codal formulation from first principles of elasticity and plasticity.

**Step 1:** The bolt material exhibits **ultimate tensile strength** $f_{ub}$ in pure tension. According to the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca criterion)**, the ultimate shear strength of a ductile material is related to its ultimate tensile strength by:

$$f_{su} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 2:** For a bolt in **double shear** (e.g., a lap joint with cover plates on both sides), there are **two shear planes**. The total nominal shear capacity is therefore:

$$V_{nsb,\,double} = 2 \times \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \times A$$

**Step 3:** If the shear plane intersects the **threaded portion** of the bolt, the cross-sectional area resisting shear is the **net tensile stress area** $A_{nb}$, not the gross shank area $A_{sb}$. The IS 1367 value of $A_{nb}$ is computed approximately as:

$$A_{nb} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( d - 0.9742 / n \right)^2$$

where $n$ is the number of threads per mm. For M20, $A_{nb} \approx 245$ mm², while $A_{sb} = 314$ mm².

**Step 4:** Combining, the nominal shear capacity of a bolt in **double shear with threads in shear plane** is:

$$V_{nsb} = 2 \times \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \times A_{nb}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **partial safety factor** for bolt material to obtain the **design strength**:

$$V_{dsb} = \frac{V_{nsb}}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{2 \, f_{ub} \, A_{nb}}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mb}}$$

For a **Property Class 8.8 bolt** ($f_{ub} = 800$ MPa) of M20 size, $A_{nb} = 245$ mm², $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$:

$$V_{dsb} = \frac{2 \times 800 \times 245}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = \frac{392000}{2.165} \approx 181058 \, \text{N} \approx 181.06 \, \text{kN}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Design Bearing Strength of a Plate

**Step 1:** Bearing pressure is the force per unit **projected contact area** between the bolt shank and the plate hole wall:

$$p_b = \frac{V}{d \times t}$$

**Step 2:** The plate material yields in bearing when $p_b$ reaches a calibrated multiple of the ultimate tensile strength $f_u$. Experiments by Hawkins and Dowling on bolted connections showed that local bearing failure occurs at approximately $2.5 \, f_u$ under the projected area. Therefore, the **nominal bearing strength** is:

$$V_{npb} = 2.5 \, d \, t \, f_u$$

**Step 3:** Modify by the factor $k_b$ to account for **boundary conditions** (end distance $e$, pitch $p$):

$$V_{npb} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u$$

**Step 4:** For a **20 mm bolt** ($d = 20$ mm) in a **12 mm plate** ($t = 12$ mm) of **Fe 410** grade ($f_u = 410$ MPa) with $k_b = 1.0$ (no edge/pitch penalty):

$$V_{npb} = 2.5 \times 1.0 \times 20 \times 12 \times 410 = 246000 \, \text{N} = 246 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 5:** Design bearing strength:

$$V_{dpb} = \frac{V_{npb}}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{246}{1.25} = 196.8 \, \text{kN}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Design Slip Resistance of HSFG Bolt

**Step 1:** The proof load $F_o$ is the tensile force in the bolt at which the **bolt reaches its design proof stress** $f_o = 0.7 \, f_{ub}$:

$$F_o = f_o \times A_{nb} = 0.7 \, f_{ub} \, A_{nb}$$

**Step 2:** The frictional slip resistance at one interface is:

$$F_{friction} = \mu_f \times F_o$$

**Step 3:** For a connection with $n_e$ friction interfaces (e.g., a double cover plate butt joint has 2 interfaces):

$$V_{nsf} = \mu_f \, n_e \, F_o$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **hole factor** $K_h$ and the partial safety factor $\gamma_{mf}$:

$$V_{dsf} = \frac{\mu_f \, n_e \, K_h \, F_o}{\gamma_{mf}}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example — M20 HSFG bolt of grade 10.9 ($f_{ub} = 1040$ MPa, $A_{nb} = 245$ mm²), $\mu_f = 0.48$ (for sand-blasted surface), $n_e = 2$, $K_h = 1.0$, $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$ (Category C):

$$F_o = 0.7 \times 1040 \times 245 = 178360 \, \text{N} = 178.36 \, \text{kN}$$

$$V_{dsf} = \frac{0.48 \times 2 \times 1.0 \times 178.36}{1.25} = 137.0 \, \text{kN}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table for KTU Exam

| Property | Bearing Type Bolt | HSFG Bolt |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bolt Grade (typical) | 4.6, 5.6, 8.8 | 8.8, 9.8, 10.9 |
| Pre-tensioning | Not required | **Mandatory** (to proof load) |
| Load Transfer Mechanism | Bolt **shear + bearing** | **Friction** at plate interface |
| Slip Under Service | **Permitted** | Not permitted (Cat. A & B) |
| Failure Mode | Bolt shear / Plate bearing | Slip / Plate bearing |
| Partial Safety Factor $\gamma_m$ | 1.25 | 1.10 (Cat. A) / 1.25 (Cat. C) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (~2× to 3×) |
| Typical Application | Buildings, trusses | Bridges, dynamic structures |
| IS Code Reference | IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.3 | IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.4 |

### 3.5 Combined Shear and Tension in Bearing Bolts

When a bolt is subjected to **simultaneous shear and tension** (e.g., a bracket connection), the design must satisfy the **interaction equation** (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.3.6):

$$\left( \frac{V_{sb}}{V_{db}} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{T_b}{T_{db}} \right)^2 \leq 1.0$$

where:
- $V_{sb}$ = applied factored shear on the bolt
- $V_{db}$ = design shear capacity of the bolt
- $T_b$ = applied factored tension on the bolt
- $T_{db}$ = design tension capacity of the bolt

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The applied tension $T_b$ must be reduced** by the **prying action** factor (if applicable). The prying force can be 20% to 40% of the applied external tension and is conservatively often neglected or accounted by a reduction factor of **0.8** on the calculated design tension capacity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bolt Classification — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD

    A --> C[HSFG Bolts]

    C --> C1[Grade 8.8]

    C1 --> D2[Category B: Slip-Critical Ultimate]
    C1 --> D3[Category C: Bearing-Type HSFG]
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C2 --> D3
    D1 --> E1[gamma_mf = 1.10]
    D2 --> E2[gamma_mf = 1.20]
    D3 --> E3[gamma_mf = 1.25]

    B1A --> F2[Failure: Bearing of Plate]
    B1B --> F1
    B1B --> F2
    D1 --> G1[Failure: Slip at Interface]
    D3 --> G2[Failure: Shear of Bolt]
    D3 --> G3[Failure: Bearing of Plate]
```

### 4.2 Force Transfer Mechanism — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Applied Load V] --> S1[Force Path in Bearing Bolt]
    S1 --> S2[Bolt Shank in Shear]
    S1 --> S3[Plate Hole in Bearing]
    S2 --> R1[Resistance: fub / sqrt3 times Anb]
    S3 --> R2[Resistance: 2.5 kb d t fu]
    L2[Applied Load V] --> H1[Force Path in HSFG Bolt]
    H1 --> H2[Friction at Plate Interface]
    H2 --> R3[Resistance: mu_f ne Kh Fo]
    R3 --> R4[Proof Load Fo = 0.7 fub Anb]
    R1 --> OUT1[Governing Design Strength]
    R2 --> OUT1
    R3 --> OUT1
    R4 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> RES[Final Design Capacity = Min of All]
```

### 4.3 Design Procedure Flowchart for a Bearing Type Connection

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Given V, d, t, fu, fub] --> B[Compute Hole Diameter d0 = d + clearance]
    B --> C[Check e and p spacing]
    C --> D[Compute kb]
    D --> E[Compute Vnpb = 2.5 kb d t fu]
    E --> F[Compute Vnsb = fub/sqrt3 times Anb]
    F --> G[Check Reduction Factors]
    G --> H[Compute Vdsb = Vnsb / gamma_mb]
    H --> I[Compute Vdpb = Vnpb / gamma_mb]
    I --> J[Governing Capacity = Min Vdsb, Vdpb]
    J --> K{Number of Bolts Needed?}
    K --> L[n = V / Vdsb]
    L --> M[Verify Edge Distance and Pitch]
    M --> N[Design Complete]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of HSFG Bolt Pre-Tensioning

```mermaid

    A[HSFG Bolt Assembly] --> B[Nut]
    A --> C[Washer]
    A --> D[Threaded Shank]

    A --> F[Head]
    A --> G[Plate 1]
    A --> H[Plate 2]
    B --> I[Pre-tension Force Fo]
    I --> J[Acts along shank axis]
    J --> K[Compresses Plates 1 and 2]
    K --> L[Generates Clamping Force]
    L --> M[Friction mu_f times F_clamp]

```
