# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Distinguish between a 'framed connection' and a 'seated connection' in steel structures. List any two advantages of framed connections."**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Framed Connection | Seated Connection |
|---|---|---|
| Load transfer | Through web cleat / fin plate in **shear** | Through bearing **stiffened/un-stiffened seat** in **bearing** |
| Stiffener required at column? | No (only web cleat / fin plate) | Yes (for stiffened seat) |
| Field labour | **Simple site bolting / welding** | Site welding of cleat; more complex |
| Use | Light to moderate reactions | Heavy reactions, large beams |

**Two advantages of framed connection:**
1. **Economical** — no column stiffener required, fabrication is simpler.
2. **Ease of erection** — site work is straightforward, beams can be dropped into place from above.

*(½ mark for each distinction point + 1 mark for the two advantages)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"State the partial safety factors $\gamma_{m0}, \gamma_{m1}, \gamma_{mb}, \gamma_{mw}$ as per IS 800:2007 and indicate the limit state each applies to."**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
- $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ — yielding of **gross section** (ultimate limit state).
- $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ — ultimate stress (rupture of **net section**; ultimate limit state).
- $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ — bolt (shear, bearing, tension) ultimate limit state.
- $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ — weld ultimate limit state.

*(½ mark each — full list expected.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Note to students:** KTU ESE Part B has an *internal choice* — you must answer either **Q(A) or Q(B)** in full. Both questions below are framed for the same topic but test different sub-skills.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Design a framed connection between an ISMB 400 beam and an ISHB 400 column to transfer a working end reaction of 200 kN (LL) + 80 kN (DL). Use bolts of grade 4.6, 20 mm diameter. Sketch the connection showing all details.**

#### **(a) Determine bolt number and arrangement. [7 marks]**

**Solution:**

Factored load:
$$
V_u = 1.5 \times (200 + 80) = 1.5 \times 280 = 420 \text{ kN}
$$

**Bolt shear capacity (M20, 4.6, single shear, threads in plane):**
$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} \times 245 = 45.26 \text{ kN}
$$

**Bolt bearing capacity (assume $t_{\min} = 8$ mm cleat, $f_u = 410$ MPa):**
$$
V_{dpb} = \frac{2.5 \times 1.0 \times 20 \times 8 \times 410}{1.25} = 131.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Bolt value:**
$$
V_{db} = \min(45.26, 131.2) = 45.26 \text{ kN}
$$

**Number of bolts:**
$$
n = \frac{420}{45.26} = 9.28 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Provide 10 bolts (2 cols × 5 rows)}
$$

Provide **pitch = 60 mm**, **gauge = 70 mm**, **end distance = 35 mm**, **edge distance = 35 mm**.

Cleat length: $35 + 4 \times 60 + 35 = 310$ mm.

#### **(b) Design the cleat angle and check for all failure modes. [7 marks]**

**Solution:**

Adopt **ISA 100 × 100 × 10 mm** cleats, length 310 mm.

**i. Shear yield:**
$$
V_{dg} = \frac{250 \times 310 \times 10}{1.10} = 704{,}545 \text{ N} = 704.5 \text{ kN} > 420 \text{ kN} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**ii. Shear rupture (5 holes in section):**
$$
A_{nv} = (310 - 5 \times 22) \times 10 = 200 \times 10 = 2000 \text{ mm}^2
$$
$$
V_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 2000}{1.25} = 590{,}400 \text{ N} = 590.4 \text{ kN} > 420 \text{ kN} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**iii. Block shear** (one tension plane = 100 × 10 = 1000 mm²; shear plane = 200 × 10 = 2000 mm²):
$f_y A_{tg} = 250 \times 1000 = 250{,}000$ N. $0.9 f_u A_{vn} = 0.9 \times 410 \times 2000 = 738{,}000$ N.  
Since $f_y A_{tg} < 0.9 f_u A_{vn}$:
$$
V_{db1} = \frac{410 \times 2000}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} + \frac{250 \times 1000}{1.10} = 378{,}756 + 227{,}273 = 606{,}029 \text{ N} = 606 \text{ kN} > 420 \text{ kN} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**iv. Bending check on outstanding leg (per cleat = 210 kN, e = 100 mm):**
$$
M_u = 210{,}000 \times 100 = 21 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}, \quad Z_p = \frac{10 \times 100^2}{4} = 250{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$
$$
f_{bm} = \frac{21 \times 10^6}{250{,}000} = 84 \text{ MPa} < \frac{250}{1.10} = 227.3 \text{ MPa} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Connection is safe in all respects. Provide a **top angle of ISA 80 × 50 × 8 mm** for lateral restraint, and use **M20 4.6 bolts** with 2 cleats ISA 100 × 100 × 10, 310 mm long.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Design a fin plate connection between an ISMB 300 beam (web thickness 7.5 mm) and the flange of a column ISHB 300, to transfer a factored end shear of 250 kN. Use M16 bolts of grade 4.6 and a 6 mm fillet weld with E41xx electrodes. Provide a neat sketch.**

#### **(a) Design the bolt group and fin plate dimensions. [7 marks]**

**Solution:**

**Bolt shear capacity (M16, 4.6, single shear):**
$A_{nb} = 0.78 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 156.7$ mm².
$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} \times 156.7 = 28.95 \text{ kN}
$$

**Bolt bearing (assume $t_{\min} = $ fin plate 8 mm, $f_u = 410$ MPa):**
$$
V_{dpb} = \frac{2.5 \times 1.0 \times 16 \times 8 \times 410}{1.25} = 104.96 \text{ kN}
$$

**Bolt value:**
$$
V_{db} = 28.95 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(shear governs)}
$$

**Number of bolts:**
$$
n = \frac{250}{28.95} = 8.64 \;\Rightarrow\; 10 \text{ bolts (2 cols × 5 rows)}
$$

**Adopt fin plate: 280 × 100 × 8 mm** with pitch = 60 mm, gauge = 70 mm, end distance = 30 mm, edge distance = 30 mm.

**Shear yield of fin plate:**
$$
V_{dg} = \frac{250 \times 280 \times 8}{1.10} = 509{,}091 \text{ N} = 509.1 \text{ kN} > 250 \text{ kN} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Shear rupture (5 holes):**
$$
A_{nv} = (280 - 5 \times 18) \times 8 = 190 \times 8 = 1520 \text{ mm}^2 \quad (d_0 = 18 \text{ mm for M16})
$$
$$
V_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 1520}{1.25} = 448{,}704 \text{ N} = 448.7 \text{ kN} > 250 \text{ kN} \;\;\checkmark
$$

#### **(b) Design the weld and check block shear. [7 marks]**

**Solution:**

**Block shear of fin plate:** $A_{tg} = 70 \times 8 = 560$ mm²; $A_{vn} = 1520$ mm².  
$f_y A_{tg} = 250 \times 560 = 140{,}000$ N; $0.9 f_u A_{vn} = 0.9 \times 410 \times 1520 = 560{,}880$ N.  
Since $f_y A_{tg} < 0.9 f_u A_{vn}$:
$$
V_{db1} = \frac{410 \times 1520}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} + \frac{140{,}000}{1.10} = 287{,}795 + 127{,}273 = 415{,}068 \text{ N} = 415.1 \text{ kN} > 250 \text{ kN} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Weld design (6 mm fillet, E41xx):**
$$
f_{wd} = \frac{0.7 \times 6 \times 410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 795.4 \text{ N/mm}
$$

Weld on both sides of fin plate (each side 280 mm):
Capacity per side = $795.4 \times 280 = 222.7$ kN. Two sides = **445.4 kN** > 250 kN ✔.

**Min weld size check:** For 8 mm plate, $s_{\min} = 5$ mm ✔.  
**Max weld size:** $s_{\max} = 8 - 1.5 = 6.5$ mm ✔ — adopted 6 mm is within limits.

**Final specification:**
- 10 × M16 4.6 bolts (2 × 5 grid, 60 mm pitch, 70 mm gauge)
- Fin plate 280 × 100 × 8 mm, E250
- 6 mm fillet weld on both sides × 280 mm long
- End/edge distance 30 mm, 30 mm respectively

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting $\gamma$-factors** — the most common error. Use $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ for yielding, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ for rupture, $\gamma_{mb} = \gamma_{mw} = 1.25$. Marks lost: **1–2 per error**.
> 2. **Single vs double shear confusion** — web cleat connection is *double shear* on beam-side bolts, *single shear* on column-side bolts. Forgetting the $n_n A_{nb} + n_s A_{sb}$ factor: **-2 marks**.
> 3. **Net area calculation** — using $d_h = d$ (bolt diameter) instead of $d_0 = d + 1.5$ (clearance + 1.5 mm): the hole is **always larger than bolt**, typically $d + 1.5$ or $d + 2$ mm. Common mark-loss area: **-1**.
> 4. **Skipping block shear check** — IS 800 (Cl. 6.4) **mandates** it. Skipping: **-2 marks**.
> 5. **Pitch/edge distance not stated** — examiners expect explicit values. Stating "pitch = 60 mm" is worth 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing E250 with Fe 410 / E300 / E350** — $f_y$ values differ; using wrong grade changes capacity by 10–40%. **-2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU Quick-Recall Box — Must Memorise Before Exam**

- ✅ **Framed connection = pinned/semi-rigid joint, transfers SHEAR only.** No moment capacity assumed.
- ✅ **Two common types:** **Web cleat angle** (double shear at beam-side) and **Fin plate** (single shear, welded to column).
- ✅ **Load path:** Beam → Bolts (shear + bearing) → Cleat/Plate (yield + rupture + block shear) → Weld → Column.
- ✅ **Six mandatory checks** (do not skip any): **(1) Bolt shear, (2) Bolt bearing, (3) Plate shear yielding, (4) Plate shear rupture, (5) Block shear, (6) Weld strength.**
- ✅ **Partial safety factors (MUST be memorised):** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{m1} = \gamma_{mb} = \gamma_{mw} = 1.25$.
- ✅ **For 4.6 grade bolts, $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa; for 8.8 grade, $f_{ub} = 800$ MPa.** Threads assumed in shear plane unless stated otherwise.
- ✅ **Min pitch = $2.5d$, min end distance = $1.5d$, hole diameter $d_0 = d + 1.5$ (use $d + 2$ for conservative).**
- ✅ **Weld strength per mm = $\frac{0.7\,s\,f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}} = 0.795 s$ N/mm for E41xx + 6 mm weld.**
- ✅ **Min weld size (E250, $t \le 20$ mm) = 5 mm; max = $t - 1.5$ mm.**
- ✅ **Number of bolts is always rounded UP and arranged symmetrically.** Provide ≥ 2 bolts minimum.
- ✅ **Block shear uses two-case IS 800 Cl. 6.4.1 logic** based on comparison of $f_y A_{tg}$ vs $0.9 f_u A_{vn}$.
- ✅ **Top cleat/angle is a stability/lateral support element** — it does NOT take shear. Beam is free to rotate.
- ✅ **KTU Past Paper Pattern (2019–2024):** Q on framed connection appears once every 2–3 years as 14-mark question, with a 3-mark concept question (cleat vs seat distinction, $\gamma$ values) in Part A.

> **🎯 Examiner's Mantra:** *"Show every step with a $\gamma$-factor and you get full marks. Skip a check and you get half."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Beam to Beam Connection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuous Beam to Beam Connection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Continuous Beam to Beam Connection** is a structural joint that transfers loads between two intersecting steel beams such that the system behaves as a continuous (or semi-continuous) flexural member rather than a series of simply-supported spans. In the context of KTU's *PECET864 – Advanced Design of Steel Structures*, this refers to **moment-resisting (rigid/semi-rigid) connections** designed per **IS 800:2007** (and consistent with IS 800:2023 updates where applicable), where the joint is capable of transmitting **axial forces, shear forces, and bending moments** between the supported beam (secondary beam) and the supporting continuous beam (primary beam), thereby maintaining frame action across the connection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Module 1**
> Connections classified under *Limit State Design of Steel Structures* for continuous framing:
> 1. **Bolted End-Plate Moment Connections** (Flush, Extended, Haunched)
> 2. **Welded Flange-Plated Moment Connections** (Direct-welded, Flange-plated)
> 3. **Top-and-Seat Angle with Web Cleat** (semi-rigid category)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two wooden planks crossing each other on a table. If you simply nail them where they cross with a single nail, the planks can rotate freely relative to each other – the joint is **flexible (pinned)** and they behave like two separate, simply-supported beams. Now, imagine instead that you glue a metal plate on top and bottom, bolting them rigidly together. The planks can **no longer rotate independently** at the intersection – they must bend together. The plate forces them to act as one continuous beam. **This is the essence of a continuous beam-to-beam connection** – the hardware (end plate, flange plate, welds) supplies the rotational restraint that transforms a pinned assembly into a moment-resisting continuous member.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants/metrics used in this module:**
> - **Ultimate tensile strength of structural steel (fu):** 410 MPa (for E250 grade) or 450 MPa (for E350 grade)
> - **Yield strength (fy):** 250 MPa (E250) or 350 MPa (E350)
> - **Modulus of Elasticity (E):** 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa
> - **Partial safety factor for material (γm):** 1.10 (for shop welding) and 1.25 (for site welding/bolts)
> - **Partial safety factor for load (γf):** 1.50 (DL+LL combined)

### 1.3 Why Continuous Beam-to-Beam Connections?

- **Reduction in mid-span bending moment** → smaller beam depth
- **Increased lateral stiffness** of the framing system
- **Improved serviceability** (deflection control)
- **Frame action** for portal frames, multi-storey buildings, and bridge girders
- **Economy of material** when properly designed

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Connections (IS 800:2007 – Cl. 2.2.4.1)

IS 800 classifies connections by **stiffness**:

| **Class** | **Type** | **Behaviour** | **Design Assumption** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rigid (Moment)** | Welded flange + web, Extended End-Plate | Full rotational continuity | Carries ≥ 90% of fixed-end moment |
| **Semi-Rigid** | Top + Seat angle, Flush End-Plate | Partial rotational restraint | Carries 25%–90% of fixed-end moment |
| **Flexible (Simple)** | Web cleats, Fin plates | Pin-like behaviour | Designed for shear only (≤ 20% of moment) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU board problems, a **continuous beam-to-beam connection** is usually interpreted as a **Rigid (Moment-Resisting) connection** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

### 2.2 Types of Moment Connections Used in Continuous Beam-to-Beam Framing

1. **Bolted End-Plate Connection (BEEC)** – Most common; uses pre-installed end plate welded to the secondary beam and bolted to the primary beam flange/web.
2. **Welded Flange-Plated Connection** – The secondary beam flanges are directly welded (or bolted via flange plates) to the supporting beam.
3. **T-Stub Connection** – Heavily loaded; uses cut T-sections as end plates.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic for Design (Limit State Method)

A continuous beam-to-beam moment connection is designed for the following limit states:

**Step 1 – Force Estimation from Global Analysis**
- The maximum design moment **M_d** and shear **V_d** at the intersection are obtained from the analysis of the continuous beam (using moment distribution / stiffness method / matrix analysis).
- The reaction from the secondary beam is transferred as axial load on the supporting beam.

**Step 2 – Bolted End-Plate Design Logic**
- The **flange** of the secondary beam carries the bending moment → bolt pairs above and below the neutral axis are designed for **tension**.
- The **web** carries the entire shear force → bolts on either side of the web are designed for **shear**.

**Step 3 – Bolt Design Checks**
- Shear capacity: $$V_{dsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot A_{nb} \cdot n_b \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma_{mb}}$$
- Bearing capacity: $$V_{dpb} = 2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma_{mb}}$$
- Tension capacity: $$T_{db} = 0.90 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{s} \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma_{mb}} \le T_{nb}$$
- Combined shear and tension (IS 800 Cl. 10.3.6): $$\left(\frac{V_{sb}}{V_{db}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{T_b}{T_{db}}\right)^2 \le 1.0$$

**Step 4 – End-Plate Checks**
- **Flexural strength of plate (in tension region):** Based on T-stub analogy (IS 800 Cl. 10.4.7).
- **Plate thickness required (simplified):** $$t_p \ge \sqrt{\frac{4.0 \cdot M_d}{f_y \cdot b_{eff} \cdot \gamma_{m0}}}$$
  - where $b_{eff}$ is the effective width of the plate per IS 800 Cl. 10.4.4.

**Step 5 – Weld Design (Connecting Plate to Beam)**
- The end plate is fillet-welded to the secondary beam flanges and web.
- Fillet weld strength: $$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$
- Weld size $s$ is governed by the **thinner of the two plates** joined.

**Step 6 – Stiffener / Continuity Plate Design (on supporting beam)**
- The supporting (primary) beam web may fail by **web yielding** or **web crippling** under the concentrated tension/compression from the secondary beam flanges.
- **Web yielding check (IS 800 Cl. 8.7.1.1):** $$F_w = (b_f + 5t_f + s_p) \cdot t_w \cdot f_{yw} / \gamma_{m0}$$
- If inadequate → provide **tension stiffeners (continuity plates)** aligned with the secondary beam flanges.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Description** | **Reference (IS 800:2007)** |
|---|---|---|
| Design tensile strength of plate | $T_{dg} = A_g f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 6.1 |
| Design shear strength of bolt | $V_{dsb} = (f_{ub}/\sqrt{3}) \cdot A_{nb} \cdot n_b \cdot 1/\gamma_{mb}$ | Cl. 10.3.3 |
| Design bearing strength of bolt | $V_{dpb} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u \, (1/\gamma_{mb})$ | Cl. 10.3.4 |
| Design tension capacity of bolt | $T_{db} = 0.90 \, f_{ub} \, A_s \, (1/\gamma_{mb})$ | Cl. 10.3.5 |
| Combined shear + tension | $(V_{sb}/V_{db})^2 + (T_b/T_{db})^2 \le 1.0$ | Cl. 10.3.6 |
| Fillet weld strength | $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw})$ | Cl. 10.5.7.1.1 |
| Minimum weld size | $s_{min}$ from Table 21 (IS 800) | Cl. 10.5.3.1 |
| Effective width of end plate | $b_{eff}$ from Cl. 10.4.4 (T-stub method) | Cl. 10.4.4 |
| Web yielding capacity | $F_w = (b_f + 5t_f + s_p) \cdot t_w \cdot f_{yw} / \gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 8.7.1.1 |
| Web crippling capacity | $F_c = (s_p + 2t_f)(t_w) f_{yw}/\gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 8.7.1.2 |
| Moment capacity of compact section | $M_{d} = \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 8.2.1.2 |

> **NOTE:** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (for site) or 1.50 (for shop).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Continuous beam-to-beam connections are the backbone of:
- **Multi-storey commercial buildings** (composite action with slabs)
- **Industrial mezzanine floors** with primary-secondary beam grids
- **Bridge girders** with cross-girders over main girders
- **Pipe racks and conveyor galleries** in process plants
- **Earthquake-resistant moment frames** (special moment-resisting connections, SMF)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Width of End Plate (T-Stub Analogy)

The end plate is designed assuming **yield-line patterns** under bolt tension. For a **flush end-plate connection** (two bolts per row above tension flange), the effective width is taken as the minimum of:

- Width of plate projection beyond the beam flange
- A 45° spread from the bolt centre to the weld toe (approximately)
- The bolt pitch spacing

> The general formula per IS 800 Cl. 10.4.4 is:

$$
b_{eff} = \min \left\{ \begin{aligned}
& p \quad \text{(bolt pitch)} \\
& 2 \pi m_{x} \\
& \pi m_{x} + w \\
& \pi m_{x} + 2e
\end{aligned} \right.
$$

where:
- $m_x$ = horizontal distance from bolt centre to the **face of the beam web** + **root radius** of beam flange
- $w$ = length of the bolt (≈ 1.0 × bolt diameter)
- $e$ = distance from bolt centre to the **edge** of the plate
- $p$ = bolt pitch (centre-to-centre)

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Design Example (Typical KTU Board Problem Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A secondary beam **ISMB 300** ($f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa) is connected to the flange of a primary continuous beam **ISMB 450** using an **Extended End Plate Moment Connection** with 4 bolts of grade **8.8** (16 mm dia, M16). The factored design moment at the joint is **$M_d = 90$ kN·m** and the factored shear is **$V_d = 120$ kN**. The end plate is of grade E250 with width 180 mm. **Check the adequacy of the connection** and design the welds and continuity plates.

---

#### **Step 1: Section Properties of ISMB 300 (Standard values)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Depth of section, } h = 300 \text{ mm} \\
&\text{Flange width, } b_f = 140 \text{ mm} \\
&\text{Flange thickness, } t_f = 12.4 \text{ mm} \\
&\text{Web thickness, } t_w = 7.5 \text{ mm} \\
&\text{Root radius, } r_1 = 12 \text{ mm} \\
&\text{Depth of web, } h_w = h - 2t_f - 2r_1 = 300 - 24.8 - 24 = 251.2 \text{ mm} \\
&\text{Plastic section modulus, } Z_p \approx 385 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step 2: Bolt Force due to Moment (Lever Arm Approach)**

The lever arm between the tension bolt group and the compression flange is approximately:

$$
\text{Lever arm, } z = h - t_f = 300 - 12.4 = 287.6 \text{ mm}
$$

Total tensile force in the flange (and hence the bolt pair above tension flange):

$$
T_f = \frac{M_d}{z} = \frac{90 \times 10^6}{287.6} = 312,935 \text{ N} \approx 313.0 \text{ kN}
$$

For **2 bolts above tension flange**, force per bolt:

$$
T_b = \frac{T_f}{2} = \frac{313.0}{2} = 156.5 \text{ kN}
$$

---

#### **Step 3: Bolt Shear Force**

For 4 bolts in double shear carrying the entire shear:

$$
V_{sb} = \frac{V_d}{n_b} = \frac{120}{4} = 30.0 \text{ kN}
$$

---

#### **Step 4: Bolt Capacity Calculations**

**For M16 grade 8.8 bolt:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&f_{ub} = 800 \text{ MPa (ultimate), } f_{yb} = 640 \text{ MPa (yield)} \\
&d = 16 \text{ mm} \\
&d_0 = 18 \text{ mm (hole dia)} \\
&A_{nb} = 0.78 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 16^2 = 156.7 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (nominal thread root area)} \\
&A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 16^2 = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (shank area)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Design shear capacity (per bolt):**

$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{A_{nb}}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{800}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{156.7}{1.25}
$$

$$
V_{dsb} = 462.91 \cdot 125.36 = 57,909 \text{ N} \approx 57.9 \text{ kN}
$$

**Design tension capacity (per bolt):**

$$
T_{db} = \frac{0.90 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_s}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{0.90 \cdot 800 \cdot 201.06}{1.25}
$$

$$
T_{db} = 720 \cdot 160.85 = 115,812 \text{ N} \approx 115.8 \text{ kN}
$$

---

#### **Step 5: Combined Shear and Tension Check (IS 800 Cl. 10.3.6)**

$$
\left(\frac{V_{sb}}{V_{db}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{T_b}{T_{db}}\right)^2 \le 1.0
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{30.0}{57.9}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{156.5}{115.8}\right)^2 &= (0.518)^2 + (1.351)^2 \\
&= 0.268 + 1.826 \\
&= 2.094 \quad \textbf{> 1.0} \quad \Rightarrow \text{ NOT SAFE}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The interaction equation failing is the most common rejection reason. The remedy is to either (a) **upgrade bolt grade to 10.9** (f_ub = 1040 MPa) or (b) **increase the bolt diameter to M20** or (c) **add more bolt rows above the tension flange**.

**Revised trial with M20 grade 10.9 bolts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&d = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad d_0 = 22 \text{ mm} \\
&f_{ub} = 1040 \text{ MPa} \\
&A_{nb} = 0.78 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 20^2 = 245.0 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&A_s = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{1040}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{245.0}{1.25} = 600.5 \cdot 196.0 = 117,698 \text{ N} = 117.7 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
T_{db} = \frac{0.90 \cdot 1040 \cdot 314.16}{1.25} = 748.8 \cdot 251.33 = 188,189 \text{ N} = 188.2 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{30.0}{117.7}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{156.5}{188.2}\right)^2 = 0.065 + 0.691 = 0.756 \le 1.0 \quad \checkmark \textbf{ SAFE}
$$

---

#### **Step 6: End Plate Thickness Check**

The end plate must be thick enough to resist bending under bolt tension. Using simplified flexural capacity:

$$
M_{p,plate} = \frac{f_y \cdot t_p^2 \cdot b_{eff}}{4 \cdot \gamma_{m0}} \ge T_b \cdot m_x
$$

Assuming $m_x \approx 1.5d = 30$ mm and $b_{eff} = 100$ mm:

$$
\frac{250 \cdot t_p^2 \cdot 100}{4 \cdot 1.10} \ge 156,500 \cdot 30
$$

$$
5681.8 \cdot t_p^2 \ge 4,695,000
$$

$$
t_p^2 \ge 826.3 \implies t_p \ge 28.7 \text{ mm}
$$

**Adopt $t_p = 32$ mm** (next standard thickness).

---

#### **Step 7: Weld Design (End Plate to Beam Flange)**

The end plate is fillet-welded to the flange. Weld is loaded by the bolt tension reaction.

Required throat thickness:

$$
t_t = \frac{T_b}{0.7 \cdot l_w \cdot f_{wd}}
$$

where $f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} = 189.4$ MPa.

Weld length along flange ≈ 2 × (b_f - t_w) = 2 × (140 - 7.5) = 265 mm (both sides).

$$
t_t = \frac{156,500}{0.7 \cdot 265 \cdot 189.4} = \frac{156,500}{35,134} = 4.45 \text{ mm}
$$

Weld size $s = t_t / 0.7 = 6.36$ mm → adopt **$s = 8$ mm** fillet weld.

---

#### **Step 8: Stiffener / Continuity Plate on Supporting Beam**

The concentrated tension from the secondary beam flange is:

$$
F = 313.0 \text{ kN}
$$

Web yielding capacity of the primary beam (ISMB 450: $t_w = 9.4$ mm, $b_f = 150$ mm, $t_f = 17.4$ mm):

$$
F_w = \left( b_f + 5t_f + s_p \right) t_w f_{yw} / \gamma_{m0}
$$

Assuming $s_p$ (column/wall plate) = 0 (direct to web):

$$
F_w = (150 + 5 \times 17.4 + 0) \times 9.4 \times 250 / 1.10
$$

$$
F_w = 237 \times 9.4 \times 250 / 1.10 = 556,500 / 1.10 = 505,909 \text{ N} \approx 506 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_w = 506 \text{ kN} > F = 313 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \textbf{ SAFE}
$$

So **no continuity plate required** on the supporting beam.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Quick Summary Table

| **Check** | **Computed Demand** | **Computed Capacity** | **Status** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bolt shear (revised M20/10.9) | 30.0 kN | 117.7 kN | Safe ✓ |
| Bolt tension | 156.5 kN | 188.2 kN | Safe ✓ |
| Combined interaction | 0.756 | ≤ 1.0 | Safe ✓ |
| End plate thickness | 28.7 mm | Adopted 32 mm | Safe ✓ |
| Weld size | 6.36 mm | Adopted 8 mm | Safe ✓ |
| Supporting beam web yielding | 313 kN | 506 kN | Safe ✓ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram – Continuous Beam-to-Beam Connection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Continuous Primary Beam ISMB 450] --> B{Connection Zone}
    B --> C[Top Flange Compression Zone]
    B --> D[Neutral Axis Shear Zone]
    B --> E[Bottom Flange Tension Zone]
    E --> F[Extended End Plate 32 mm]
    F --> G[M20 Grade 10.9 Bolts in Tension]
    F --> H[8 mm Fillet Weld]
    H --> I[Secondary Beam ISMB 300]
    G --> J[T-stub yield line mechanism check]
    I --> K[Web Shear via 4 bolts in double shear]
    A --> L[Continuity Plate Check]
    L --> M{Web yielding safe}
    M -- Yes --> N[No stiffener needed]
    M -- No --> O[Provide tension stiffener]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart – Design Logic Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Global Analysis] --> B[Obtain Md and Vd at joint]
    B --> C[Estimate bolt force T = Md / lever arm]
    C --> D[Choose bolt grade and diameter]
    D --> E{Combined shear and tension check}
    E -- Pass --> F[Design end plate thickness]
    E -- Fail --> D
    F --> G[Design fillet weld size]
    G --> H{Web yielding check on primary beam}
    H -- Pass --> I[Design complete]
    H -- Fail --> J[Provide continuity stiffener]
    J --> I
```

### 4.3 Schematic – Force Transfer Mechanism in Extended End-Plate Connection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tension Flange Force Tf] --> B[Tension Bolt Group above flange]
    B --> C[End Plate in Flexure]
    C --> D[Bearing against Primary Beam Flange]
    E[Compression Flange Force Cf] --> F[Bearing on Primary Beam Flange]
    G[Shear Force Vd] --> H[Shear Bolt Group in Web]
    H --> I[End Plate in Shear]
    I --> J[Bearing on Primary Beam Web]
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The diagram shows how the **tension flange** of the secondary beam pulls the **end plate** which pulls on the **bolts**, transferring the moment as a **couple (Tension-Compression pair)**. The **shear** travels through the **web bolts** as a **vertical force pair**, ensuring the connection can resist combined action.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – 3 Mark Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
> *Distinguish between a flexible connection and a rigid connection in steel structures. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Flexible (Simple) Connection** | **Rigid (Moment) Connection** |
|---|---|---|
| Load transfer | Transfers shear only | Transfers shear, axial force and moment |
| Rotational restraint | None (assumed pinned) | Full (no relative rotation) |
| Design moment | Neglected in design | Full design moment is considered |
| Example | Web cleats, fin plate | Extended end-plate, welded flange plate |

A flexible connection is used when beam-to-beam framing is treated as simply supported, while a rigid connection is used for continuous beam framing where frame action is desired.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> *What is the significance of the interaction equation $(V_{sb}/V_{db})^2 + (T_b/T_{db})^2 \le 1.0$ in bolt design? When is it applied?*

**Model Answer:**

The interaction equation given in **IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.3.6** checks the adequacy of a bolt subjected to **combined shear and tension**. It is applied when a bolt carries both **shear force (V_sb)** and **tensile force (T_b)** simultaneously, which is typical in moment-resisting connections such as end-plate beam-to-beam joints. The equation ensures the **combined stress state** does not exceed the bolt's capacity, considering the parabolic (elliptical) yield interaction surface in the shear-tension plane.

---

### 5.2 Part B – 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** A continuous secondary beam **ISMB 350** is to be connected to a primary continuous beam **ISMB 500** using an **Extended End-Plate Moment Connection**. The factored design moment at the joint is **$M_d = 180$ kN·m** and factored shear is **$V_d = 220$ kN**. Use **4.6 grade M20 bolts** with an end plate width of 200 mm and E250 grade plate. **Design and check the connection** for bolt adequacy, end plate thickness, and weld size.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Bolt force due to moment:**
For ISMB 350, depth $h = 350$ mm, $t_f = 14.2$ mm.

$$
z = h - t_f = 350 - 14.2 = 335.8 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
T_f = \frac{M_d}{z} = \frac{180 \times 10^6}{335.8} = 536,033 \text{ N} \approx 536.0 \text{ kN}
$$

Force per bolt (2 bolts above tension flange):

$$
T_b = \frac{536.0}{2} = 268.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 – Shear per bolt (4 bolts):**

$$
V_{sb} = \frac{220}{4} = 55.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 – Bolt capacities for M20, 4.6 grade:**
$f_{ub} = 400$ MPa, $A_{nb} = 245$ mm², $A_s = 314.16$ mm².

$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{245}{1.25} = 45,267 \text{ N} \approx 45.3 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
T_{db} = \frac{0.90 \cdot 400 \cdot 314.16}{1.25} = 90,478 \text{ N} \approx 90.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 – Interaction check:**

$$
\left(\frac{55.0}{45.3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{268.0}{90.5}\right)^2 = 1.475 + 8.766 = 10.24 \gg 1.0
$$

**Conclusion:** 4.6 grade M20 bolts are **grossly inadequate**. **Upgrade to M24 grade 10.9 bolts** is required.

For **M24, 10.9** ($f_{ub} = 1040$ MPa, $A_{nb} = 353$ mm², $A_s = 452$ mm²):

$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{1040}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{353}{1.25} = 169,558 \text{ N} \approx 169.6 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
T_{db} = \frac{0.90 \cdot 1040 \cdot 452}{1.25} = 338,458 \text{ N} \approx 338.5 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{55.0}{169.6}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{268.0}{338.5}\right)^2 = 0.105 + 0.627 = 0.732 \le 1.0 \quad \checkmark \textbf{ SAFE}
$$

**Step 5 – End plate thickness check:**

With $m_x \approx 36$ mm and $b_{eff} = 120$ mm:

$$
t_p \ge \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 268,000 \cdot 36}{250 \cdot 120 \cdot 1.10}} = \sqrt{292.4} \approx 17.1 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **$t_p = 20$ mm**.

**Step 6 – Weld size:**

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

Weld length along flange ≈ $2(b_f - t_w) = 2(140 - 8.0) = 264$ mm.

$$
t_t = \frac{268,000}{0.7 \cdot 264 \cdot 189.4} = 7.66 \text{ mm} \implies s = 11 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **$s = 12$ mm** fillet weld (minimum practical for 20 mm plate).

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **step-by-step design procedure** for a **Bolted Extended End-Plate Moment Connection** between two continuous beams as per **IS 800:2007**. Mention the key limit state checks that must be satisfied.

**Model Answer:**

**Design Procedure:**

1. **Global analysis** to find factored moment $M_d$ and shear $V_d$ at the joint.
2. **Decide lever arm** $z = h - t_f$.
3. **Compute tension force** in flange: $T_f = M_d / z$.
4. **Bolt design**:
   - Choose bolt grade and diameter
   - Compute shear per bolt
   - Check combined shear + tension interaction
5. **End plate design**:
   - Compute effective width $b_{eff}$ (T-stub analogy)
   - Compute thickness using flexural yield criterion
6. **Weld design**:
   - Fillet weld size $s$ from force per unit length
   - Check $s_{min}$ and $s_{max}$ from IS 800 Table 21
7. **Supporting beam checks**:
   - Web yielding (Cl. 8.7.1.1)
   - Web crippling (Cl. 8.7.1.2)
   - Provide stiffeners if needed
8. **Deflection / rotation** check (serviceability)

**Key Limit States:**
- Bolt shear failure
- Bolt bearing failure
- Bolt tension failure
- Plate flexural failure (T-stub mechanism)
- Weld rupture
- Web yielding/crippling
- Column / beam buckling

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **forget the combined interaction check** and only verify tension and shear separately. This is the single most common cause of failed answers. **Always apply $(V_{sb}/V_{db})^2 + (T_b/T_{db})^2 \le 1.0$ when bolt carries both loads.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A continuous secondary beam **ISMB 400** is to be connected to the flange of a primary continuous beam using a **Welded Flange-Plated Moment Connection**. The factored design moment is **$M_d = 250$ kN·m** and shear is **$V_d = 300$ kN**. Use **E250 grade** for flange plates (250 mm wide × 16 mm thick) and **E41 electrodes** for shop welding. **Design the flange plate welds** and the **web connection (8 mm fin plate)**.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Force in flange (tension/compression):**

For ISMB 400: $h = 400$ mm, $t_f = 16.0$ mm.

$$
z = 400 - 16 = 384 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
T_f = \frac{250 \times 10^6}{384} = 651,042 \text{ N} \approx 651.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 – Fillet weld design for flange plate (two parallel welds):**

$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} = 189.4$ MPa

Weld length on each side of flange plate (both sides of secondary beam flange):

$$
l_w = 2 \times (b_{plate} - t_w - 2 \times 10 \text{ mm edge loss}) \approx 2 \times (250 - 8.9 - 20) = 2 \times 221.1 = 442.2 \text{ mm}
$$

Total throat required:

$$
t_t = \frac{T_f}{0.7 \cdot l_w \cdot f_{wd}} = \frac{651,000}{0.7 \cdot 442.2 \cdot 189.4}
$$

$$
t_t = \frac{651,000}{58,629} = 11.1 \text{ mm} \implies s = 15.86 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **$s = 16$ mm** fillet weld on both sides.

**Step 3 – Web connection check (8 mm fin plate, M16 4.6 bolts):**

Shear per bolt (assuming 4 bolts in single shear):

$$
V_{sb} = \frac{300}{4} = 75.0 \text{ kN}
$$

Bolt capacity (M16, 4.6):

$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{156.7}{1.25} = 28,955 \text{ N} = 29.0 \text{ kN} \ll 75 \text{ kN}
$$

Not safe → **increase to 8 bolts** (double row, 2 bolts each side, top and bottom):

$$
V_{sb} = \frac{300}{8} = 37.5 \text{ kN}
$$

Still > 29.0 kN → **Upgrade to 8.8 grade M16 bolts** (f_ub = 800 MPa):

$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{800}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{156.7}{1.25} = 57,909 \text{ N} = 57.9 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \textbf{ SAFE}
$$

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **classification of beam-to-beam connections** as per **IS 800:2007** with reference to their **moment-rotation characteristics**. Discuss how the choice of connection type affects the analysis and design of continuous beams.

**Model Answer:**

IS 800:2007 (Cl. 2.2.4.1) classifies connections based on the **initial rotational stiffness** and **moment capacity**:

1. **Rigid (Fully Restrained):** Maintains original angles between members. Carries ≥ 90% of the fixed-end moment. Analysis assumes full continuity.

2. **Semi-Rigid (Partially Restrained):** Permits some relative rotation but carries 25–90% of fixed-end moment. Analysis must use modified stiffness or the moment-rotation curve approach.

3. **Flexible (Simple):** Permits unrestrained rotation; carries ≤ 20% of moment. Treated as pinned in analysis.

**Effect on Continuous Beam Design:**

- For **rigid connections**: Continuous beam analysis with full fixity at interior supports → maximum negative moment at supports, reduced positive moment at mid-span.
- For **flexible connections**: Each beam analysed as simply supported → larger mid-span moments, smaller beam sizes but no continuity.
- For **semi-rigid connections**: A modified stiffness factor (often 0.5–0.7) is applied to the support moment; results fall between the two extremes.

The choice affects **deflection, vibration, and overall economy** of the structure. In KTU problems, the most common interpretation of a "continuous beam-to-beam connection" is a **rigid (moment) connection**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **continuous beam-to-beam connection** transfers **moment, shear and axial force**, unlike simple (web cleat) connections which transfer only shear.
- ✅ The **bolt group above the tension flange** resists the **tensile couple**; the **web bolts** resist **shear**; the **compression flange** bears directly on the supporting member.
- ✅ **Lever arm** for moment-to-force conversion is **$z = h - t_f$** (depth of beam minus flange thickness).
- ✅ **T-stub analogy** is used for **end-plate thickness** design (IS 800 Cl. 10.4.7).
- ✅ **Always apply combined interaction check:** $(V_{sb}/V_{db})^2 + (T_b/T_{db})^2 \le 1.0$ when bolts carry both shear and tension.
- ✅ **Partial safety factors:** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (steel yielding), $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ (bolts), $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (site weld) / 1.50 (shop weld).
- ✅ **Web yielding check** (Cl. 8.7.1.1) is mandatory on the **supporting (primary) beam**.
- ✅ **Continuity plates (stiffeners)** are required on the supporting beam web when concentrated flange force exceeds the web yielding capacity.
- ✅ **Weld size selection:** $s \ge s_{min}$ (Table 21) and $s \le t_{min} - 1.5$ mm (avoid fusion of parent metal).
- ✅ **Effective bolt spacing** $p \ge 2.5d$ and edge distance $e \ge 1.5d_0$ (IS 800 Cl. 10.2.4.2 and 10.2.4.3).
- ✅ **Bolt grade hierarchy:** 4.6 (lowest) → 8.8 → 10.9 (highest); higher grade allows smaller diameter.
- ✅ **IS 800 reference tables** to memorize: Table 21 (min weld size), Table 19 (bolt spacing), Cl. 10.4.4 (T-stub effective width).
- ✅ **Most common mistake:** Skipping the **interaction check**; always show it explicitly in the answer sheet.
- ✅ **End plate material:** usually E250 (Fe410) for cost; thickness typically 12–32 mm for standard beams.
- ✅ **In the KTU exam,** questions are typically valued at **3 marks (Part A)** and **14 marks (Part B)** with sub-parts (7 + 7), and you must present both design calculations and explanations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure and Properties of Weld Metal, Beam to Column Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structure and Properties of Weld Metal, Beam to Column Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Structure and Properties of Weld Metal

#### Formal Academic Definition

**Weld Metal** is the metallic filler material (electrode/core wire) that has been melted and solidified in the joint zone between two base metal pieces during the welding process. According to IS 814 and IS 2062, the weld metal in a steel connection is a distinct microstructural region whose chemical composition, mechanical properties, and metallurgical characteristics are governed by the parent metal, the filler electrode, the welding process parameters, and the heat input.

The **Structure of Weld Metal** refers to its as-deposited and post-solidification microstructure, which typically consists of:
- **Columnar grains** growing epitaxially from the fusion boundary into the weld pool
- **Equiaxed grains** forming at the weld centerline where solidification fronts meet
- **Dendritic sub-structures** within grains, dictated by the thermal gradient (G) and solidification growth rate (R)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The weld metal zone is *not* identical to the base metal. It generally exhibits **higher hardness and tensile strength** but **lower ductility and impact toughness** due to rapid cooling, coarse grain structures, and possible inclusions.

**Key Properties of Weld Metal (governed by IS 2062, IS 814, IS 816):**

| Property | Typical Range (SMAW) | Governing Code |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------| 
| Ultimate Tensile Strength (f_u) | **410–520 MPa** | IS 2062 / IS 814 |
| Yield Strength (f_y) | **330–410 MPa** | IS 2062 |
| Elongation (%) | 20–30% | IS 2062 |
| Charpy Impact Toughness | **27 J at 0°C** (min) | IS 2062 |
| Weld Strength Ratio (f_u weld / f_u base) | ≥ 1.0 (overmatching) | IS 816 |

#### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are **gluing two wooden blocks together**. The glue is your weld metal, the wood is your base metal, and the *interfacial layer* between the glue and wood is the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ). Now imagine the glue is rapidly cooled under tension — it sets quickly and becomes very strong, but it is also **brittle** (just like a superglue joint that snaps suddenly rather than bending). This is exactly what happens in a weld: the rapid thermal cycle produces a strong but **less ductile** region, which is why the *weld strength and ductility* are critical design parameters in connections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** In the 1994 Northridge earthquake (USA), many welded beam-to-column moment connections failed in the **weld metal and HAZ** because of low notch toughness. This led to a global revision of weld metal specification, demanding **Charpy impact-tested electrodes** (e.g., E7018 with 27J @ -29°C) — a lesson Indian codes (IS 2062) incorporated for seismic-grade construction.

---

### 1.2 Beam-to-Column Connections

#### Formal Academic Definition

A **Beam-to-Column Connection** is a structural joint designed to transfer forces (shear, axial, and moment) between a horizontal beam and a vertical column. Per **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel — Code of Practice)**, these connections are classified based on their rotational stiffness and moment resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** A connection is termed **"Rigid"** when its rotational stiffness is sufficient to maintain the original angle between beam and column under load (90% or more moment retained). It is termed **"Simple/Shear"** when it transfers only vertical shear with negligible moment transfer (< 10%).

**Types of Beam-to-Column Connections (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.1.1):**

1. **Rigid (Moment-Resisting) Connections**
   - Full-strength or Partial-strength
   - Capable of transferring calculated moments
2. **Semi-Rigid Connections**
   - Transfers a known fraction of the moment
   - Stiffness lies between rigid and pinned
3. **Simple (Shear) Connections**
   - Transfers only shear (vertical reaction)
   - Examples: Double web angles, Fin plates, Shear end plates

#### Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **bookshelf bracket** — a simple L-bracket (pinned connection) holds the shelf up but allows it to rotate slightly under heavy load (low moment resistance). Now think of the bracket being **welded solidly** to the wall and shelf — this is a **rigid connection**; the shelf cannot rotate, and a heavy load creates a large moment at the joint. The beam-to-column connection is the same idea, scaled up to a multi-storey steel building. The **type of connection chosen dictates whether the joint behaves like a hinge or like a continuous frame**, directly influencing the design moment and the overall structural behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern seismic design (post-Northridge) heavily favors **Reduced Beam Section (RBS)** or **dog-bone connections** in beam-to-column joints. The idea is to force the plastic hinge to form **inside the beam, away from the brittle weld metal and HAZ** — protecting the connection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Metallurgical Structure of Weld Metal (Deep Analysis)

The weld metal solidifies in a sequential manner, producing a **graded microstructure** from the fusion line to the weld centerline:

- **Fusion Zone (FZ):** A narrow region (0.05–0.15 mm wide) where the base metal has partially melted and mixed with the filler metal. The bond between weld and base metal is established here.
- **Weld Metal Zone (WMZ):** The bulk of the deposited filler metal.
  - **Columnar Grains:** Grow perpendicular to the pool boundary, in the direction opposite to heat flow.
  - **Equiaxed Grains:** Form at the centerline where the thermal gradient (G) is low and growth rate (R) is high. The **G/R ratio** dictates the solidification mode:
    - High G/R → Planar growth
    - Medium G/R → Cellular/Dendritic growth
    - Low G/R → Equiaxed growth
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ):** Base metal that did not melt but underwent microstructural changes (grain coarsening near fusion line, grain refinement in the sub-critical zone).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooling Rate vs. Microstructure Rule:** Faster cooling → finer grains → higher strength but higher hardness and risk of cold cracking. Slower cooling → coarser grains → lower strength but better ductility. This is governed by the **Heat Input (H)** formula:
> $$H = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$
> where $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/min), $k$ = thermal efficiency (0.6 for SMAW, 0.8 for GMAW, 1.0 for SAW).

### 2.2 Mechanical Properties of Weld Metal

The four key properties tested as per IS 2062 and AWS A5.1/A5.5 are:

1. **Yield Strength (f_y):** 0.2% proof stress. Critical for ductility.
2. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (f_u):** Maximum stress before failure.
3. **Elongation (%):** Measure of ductility (typically 22–30% for structural welds).
4. **Charpy V-Notch (CVN) Impact Energy:** Mandatory for seismic/welded connections, specified in Joules at a test temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Weld Metal Classification (AWS A5.1):** Electrodes are graded like **E7018** — "E" = Electrode, "70" = minimum UTS in ksi (70 ksi = 482 MPa), "1" = welding position (all positions), "8" = low-hydrogen, iron powder, DC+ polarity. In IS 814, the equivalent is **E4303** (42 kgf/mm² UTS, medium coating, AC/DC).

### 2.3 Beam-to-Column Connection Theory

The classification of connections is based on:
- **Strength Ratio:** $M_{u,conn} / M_{p,beam}$
- **Stiffness Ratio:** $S_{j,init} / (E \cdot I_b / L_b)$

Where $M_{u,conn}$ is the ultimate moment capacity of the connection, $M_{p,beam}$ is the plastic moment capacity of the connected beam, $S_{j,init}$ is the initial rotational stiffness of the connection, $E$ is Young's modulus, $I_b$ is beam moment of inertia, and $L_b$ is beam length.

**IS 800:2007 Classification Limits (Cl. 10.1.1):**

| Type of Connection | Strength Limit | Stiffness Limit |
|--------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Rigid (Full Strength) | $M_{u,conn} \geq M_{p,beam}$ | $S_{j,init} \geq 25 E I_b / L_b$ |
| Rigid (Partial Strength) | $0.25 M_{p,beam} \leq M_{u,conn} < M_{p,beam}$ | $S_{j,init} \geq 25 E I_b / L_b$ |
| Semi-Rigid | $0.25 M_{p,beam} \leq M_{u,conn} < M_{p,beam}$ | $0.5 E I_b / L_b \leq S_{j,init} < 25 E I_b / L_b$ |
| Simple (Flexible) | $M_{u,conn} < 0.25 M_{p,beam}$ | $S_{j,init} < 0.5 E I_b / L_b$ |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### 2.4.1 Weld Metal & Heat Input Formulas

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $H = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ | Heat input per unit length | kJ/mm |
| $t_{8/5} = f(H, T_{plate})$ | Cooling time between 800°C and 500°C (CCT diagram) | seconds |
| $CCT = \frac{k'}{H - C}$ | Empirical cooling time (k', C are constants) | seconds |
| $S = 0.707 \cdot t$ | Effective throat thickness for fillet weld | mm |
| $V_{weld} = 0.707 \cdot t \cdot L_w$ | Weld volume per unit length | mm³/mm |
| $f_{wd} = \frac{f_u / \sqrt{3}}{\gamma_{mw}}$ | Design strength of weld metal (von-Mises) | MPa |

#### 2.4.2 Fillet Weld Design Strength (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.5.3)

| Formula | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| $f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$ | Design strength of fillet weld |
| $R_{dw} = \frac{f_{wd} \cdot t_t \cdot L_w}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | Design strength of weld per unit length |
| $T_{db} = R_{dw} \cdot L_w$ | Total weld capacity in shear |
| $L_w \geq 4 t_t$ | Minimum effective length (whichever is less: $4t_t$ or actual length available) |
| $L_w \leq 100 t_t$ | Maximum effective length (else use effective length reduction factor $\beta_{lw}$) |
| $\beta_{lw} = 1.2 - \frac{0.2 L_w}{150 t_t}$ | But $1.0 \leq \beta_{lw} \leq 1.2$ for L_w > 100 t_t |
| $t_t \leq t - 1.5$ mm | For plates $\leq 10$ mm; $t_t \leq t - 2.0$ for $t > 10$ mm |

#### 2.4.3 Partial Safety Factors ($\gamma_{mb}$ and $\gamma_{mw}$ per IS 800:2007, Table 5)

| Loading & Bolt/Weld Type | $\gamma_{mb}$ or $\gamma_{mw}$ |
|--------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Bolts under shear (factory/shop) | 1.25 |
| Bolts under bearing (factory/shop) | 1.25 |
| Bolts under tension | 1.25 |
| Welds (shop/factory) | 1.25 |
| Welds (field/site) | 1.50 |

#### 2.4.4 Beam-to-Column Connection Forces (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.4)

For a bracket-type connection or fin plate connection, the **eccentric shear on a bolt group** is calculated as:

$$F_{bolt} = \sqrt{\left( \frac{V}{n} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{V \cdot e \cdot r_i}{\sum r_i^2} \right)^2}$$

where:
- $V$ = applied vertical shear (factored)
- $e$ = eccentricity from centroid of bolt group to load line
- $n$ = number of bolts
- $r_i$ = radial distance of i-th bolt from centroid

#### 2.4.5 Moment Capacity of Bolted End Plate Connection

For a T-stub (end plate) in tension:

$$M_{d} = \sum T_{d,bolt} \cdot d_{bolt}$$

where $T_{d,bolt}$ is the design tension capacity of the bolt row and $d_{bolt}$ is the distance from the bolt row to the compression center (typically the bottom flange of the beam).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The weld metal structure and beam-to-column connection design is the **backbone of steel frame construction**:

- **High-Rise Buildings:** Rigid beam-to-column moment connections in seismic zones transfer lateral wind and earthquake loads to the foundation.
- **Industrial Structures:** Simple shear connections are used in platforms, walkways, and pipe racks to economize on fabrication cost.
- **Bridges:** Welded moment connections are used in plate girder-to-cross girder joints.
- **Pre-Engineered Buildings (PEB):** Tapered sections connected via bolted end plates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice Rule:** Always design connections such that the **failure occurs in the member, not in the connection**. This is the **"Strong-Column Weak-Beam"** philosophy in seismic design — a beam-to-column connection should never be the weakest link in a frame.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Throat Thickness of a Fillet Weld

**Statement:** Show that for a 90° fillet weld with leg sizes $s_1$ and $s_2$, the effective throat thickness is $t_t = 0.707 \cdot s$ (when $s_1 = s_2 = s$).

**Derivation:**

The fillet weld is a right-angled triangle in cross-section. The effective throat is the minimum distance from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse (the face of the weld).

By geometry, if the two legs are equal ($s_1 = s_2 = s$), the hypotenuse makes a 45° angle with each plate.

The throat is perpendicular to the hypotenuse. Using trigonometry:

$$t_t = s \cdot \sin(45°)$$

$$t_t = s \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\boxed{t_t = 0.707 \cdot s}$$

If the legs are unequal ($s_1 \neq s_2$), then:

$$t_t = \frac{s_1 \cdot s_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2 + s_2^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 45°?** IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.5.3.2) specifies that the **fillet weld size $s$ should be the minimum leg size**, and the effective throat is taken as the perpendicular distance to the hypotenuse. For unequal legs, the *throat must be computed from the smaller leg's geometry* — the weld is only as strong as its minimum cross-section.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Design Strength of Fillet Weld (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.5.3.1)

**Statement:** Derive the design strength of a fillet weld per unit length.

**Derivation:**

The fillet weld is assumed to fail by **shear on the throat area**. The throat plane is oriented at 45° to the direction of the applied load. The state of stress on the throat is **pure shear** (assuming a balanced weld, no bending).

The **shear stress on the throat plane** at ultimate state is:

$$\tau = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}}$$

where $f_u$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the weld metal (or the parent metal, whichever is lower).

Applying the partial safety factor $\gamma_{mw}$:

$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u / \sqrt{3}}{\gamma_{mw}}$$

For factory welds: $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$

$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{1.732 \times 1.25} = \frac{f_u}{2.165}$$

The design strength per unit length is:

$$R_{dw} = f_{wd} \times t_t = \frac{f_u \cdot t_t}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

For a weld of total effective length $L_w$:

$$T_{db} = R_{dw} \cdot L_w = \frac{f_u \cdot t_t \cdot L_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The factor $1/\sqrt{3}$ comes from the **von-Mises yield criterion** (maximum shear stress theory for ductile materials). It converts uniaxial tensile strength to equivalent shear strength, which is the failure mode for fillet welds.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Problem: Design of Beam-to-Column Welded Connection

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> A beam ISMB 400 (@ $f_y = 250$ MPa) is welded to a column flange (10 mm thick) using **fillet welds on both sides of the web**. The factored shear force at the connection is $V = 350$ kN. The web thickness of ISMB 400 is $t_w = 8.9$ mm. Design the weld using E410 electrodes ($f_u = 410$ MPa). Use shop weld ($\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$).

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the effective throat limit.**

The throat thickness $t_t$ is limited by the thinner of the connected plates:
- Beam web: $t_w = 8.9$ mm
- Column flange: $t_f = 10$ mm
- Governing plate thickness: 8.9 mm

Using IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.5.3.2, for plate thickness 8.9 mm:
$$t_t \leq t - 1.5 = 8.9 - 1.5 = 7.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute the design strength of the weld per unit length.**

$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = \frac{410}{2.165} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}$$

For a double-sided weld (both sides of the web), each side has equal leg size. Let size of weld = $s$. Then:

$$t_t = 0.707 s$$

Try $s = 8$ mm:
$$t_t = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.66 \text{ mm}$$

Check limit: $5.66 \leq 7.4$ mm ✓

**Step 3: Calculate the capacity per side per unit length.**

$$R_{dw} = f_{wd} \times t_t = 189.4 \times 5.66 = 1072 \text{ N/mm} = 1.072 \text{ kN/mm}$$

For **two sides**:
$$R_{dw,total} = 2 \times 1.072 = 2.144 \text{ kN/mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute the required length of weld.**

$$L_w = \frac{V}{R_{dw,total}} = \frac{350}{2.144} = 163.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Check code limits.**

- Minimum length: $L_w \geq 4 t_t = 4 \times 5.66 = 22.6$ mm ✓
- Maximum length: $L_w \leq 100 t_t = 100 \times 5.66 = 566$ mm ✓
- Provide $L_w = 200$ mm (rounded up to practical length)

**Step 6: Verify minimum weld size as per IS 800:2007, Table 21.**

For a thicker plate of 8.9 mm, the minimum weld size = **5 mm**.
Our chosen weld size = 8 mm > 5 mm ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the factor of 2 for **double-sided welds**, and provide an undersized weld. Also, they sometimes use the *parent metal* $f_u$ instead of the *weld electrode* $f_u$ — always use the **lower** of the two values (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.5.3.1).

---

### 3.4 Numerical Problem: Bolted End Plate Connection Moment Capacity

**Problem Statement:**
> An ISMB 450 beam ($Z_p = 1535.7$ cm³, $f_y = 250$ MPa) is connected to a column flange using a **4-bolt extended end plate (EEP)** connection. The bolts are M20, Grade 8.8 ($f_{ub} = 800$ MPa, $A_{nb} = 245$ mm²). The lever arm between the top and bottom bolt rows is $d = 400$ mm. Compute the design moment capacity of the connection.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Design tension capacity of a single bolt.**

$$T_{nb} = 0.90 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb} = 0.90 \times 800 \times 245 = 176,400 \text{ N} = 176.4 \text{ kN}$$

$$T_{db} = \frac{T_{nb}}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{176.4}{1.25} = 141.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Number of bolts in tension.**

For EEP, 2 bolts at top and 2 at bottom (4 in tension zone).

$$T_{total} = 4 \times 141.1 = 564.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Design moment capacity.**

$$M_d = T_{total} \times d = 564.4 \times 0.400 = 225.76 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Compare with beam plastic moment.**

$$M_p = Z_p \cdot f_y = 1535.7 \times 10^{-6} \times 250 \times 10^3 = 383.9 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5: Strength ratio.**

$$\frac{M_d}{M_p} = \frac{225.76}{383.9} = 0.588 \approx 0.59$$

Since $0.25 \leq 0.59 < 1.0$, the connection is **Partial Strength Rigid** (per IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.1.1).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Interpretation:** A strength ratio of 0.59 means the connection can transfer 59% of the beam's full plastic moment. For seismic design, this is acceptable as long as the column remains stronger than the beam (Strong-Column Weak-Beam).

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Weld Metal Microstructural Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following flowchart maps the **microstructural regions** formed during arc welding of a steel plate joint.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Arc Welding Process] --> B[Heat Input H applied to Joint]
    B --> C[Molten Weld Pool forms]
    C --> D[Fusion Zone 0.05 to 0.15 mm]
    D --> E[Weld Metal Zone WMZ]
    E --> F[Columnar Grains near Fusion Line]
    E --> G[Equiaxed Grains at Weld Centerline]
    B --> H[Heat Affected Zone HAZ in Base Metal]
    H --> H1[Grain Coarsened Zone near Fusion Line]
    H --> H2[Grain Refined Zone Intermediate]
    H --> H3[Sub Critical Zone unaffected by Phase Change]
    H --> I[Unaffected Base Metal Parent Plate]
    F --> J[Cooling and Solidification]
    G --> J
    J --> K[Final Microstructure]
    K --> L[Mechanical Properties Tested per IS 2062]
    L --> L1[Yield Strength fy]
    L --> L2[Ultimate Tensile Strength fu]
    L --> L3[Elongation percent]
    L --> L4[Charpy Impact Energy CVN]
```

---

### 4.2 Beam-to-Column Connection Types (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This flowchart maps the **classification of beam-to-column connections** as per IS 800:2007.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Beam to Column Connection] --> B{Type of Force Transfer}
    B --> C[Moment Resisting Rigid]
    B --> D[Semi Rigid]
    B --> E[Simple Shear]
    C --> C1[Full Strength]
    C --> C2[Partial Strength]
    C1 --> F1[Bolted End Plate EEP]
    C1 --> F2[Welded Flange Plate]
    C1 --> F3[Reduced Beam Section RBS Dog Bone]
    C2 --> G1[Top and Seat Angle with Web Angle]
    D --> H1[Header Plate Connection]
    D --> H2[T Stub in Bending]
    E --> I1[Double Web Angle Cleat]
    E --> I2[Fin Plate Single Web Plate]
    E --> I3[Shear End Plate Flexible]
    I2 --> J[Design Checks per IS 800 2007 Cl 10.4]
    J --> J1[Block Shear Capacity]
    J --> J2[Bearing on Bolt Holes]
    J --> J3[Shear Capacity of Bolts]
    J --> J4[Net Section Rupture in Beam Web]
```

---

### 4.3 Fillet Weld Geometry (Mermaid Architecture)

The following is a **functional flow block** representing the geometry of a 90° fillet weld, with all key dimensions labeled.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Base Plate 1] --> B[Weld Root Point R]
    C[Base Plate 2] --> B
    B --> D[Leg Size s1 along Plate 1]
    B --> E[Leg Size s2 along Plate 2]
    D --> F[Hypotenuse Weld Face]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Effective Throat tt]
    G --> H[Perpendicular to Hypotenuse]
    H --> I[Strength Plane for Shear Failure]
    style I fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.4 Force Transfer Mechanism in Beam-to-Column Moment Connection (Schematic Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Vertical Shear V in Beam] --> B[Transferred to Column via Web Connection]
    A2[Bending Moment M in Beam] --> C[Resolved into Force Couple]
    C --> C1[Tension Force T in Top Flange]
    C --> C2[Compression Force C in Bottom Flange]
    C1 --> D[T via Top Flange Welds to Column Flange]
    C2 --> E[C via Bottom Flange Welds or Bearing]
    B --> F[V via Web Shear Connection Fin Plate or Web Cleats]
    D --> G[Connection Capacity check]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> G1[Compare with IS 800 2007 Design Strengths]
    G1 --> H{Safe or Unsafe}
    H --> I[Safe: Proceed with Detailing]
    H --> J[Unsafe: Revise Bolt Grade, Plate Thickness, or Weld Size]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Note:** These Mermaid diagrams are block-level functional representations. In physical practice, beam-to-column connections also include **stiffener plates, continuity plates, and doubler plates**, which are detailed in IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.7 and Cl. 10.4.7.
