## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model 1]**
Define **"Weld Metal"** and **"Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)"** with reference to a welded steel joint. State two distinct mechanical properties of the weld metal as compared to the base metal.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Weld Metal (1 Mark):** Weld metal is the filler electrode material (or parent metal in autogenous welds) that has been melted and re-solidified in the joint groove during the welding process. It is the region in the joint where the base metal and the filler metal have mixed and fused together, forming the load-carrying bond between the two pieces being joined.

**Heat Affected Zone - HAZ (1 Mark):** The HAZ is the region of the base metal immediately adjacent to the weld metal that has not melted, but has undergone microstructural changes due to the thermal cycle of welding. It typically consists of a grain-coarsened zone, a grain-refined zone, and a partially-transformed zone.

**Two Distinct Properties of Weld Metal vs Base Metal (1 Mark):**
1. **Higher tensile strength and hardness** — due to rapid cooling and refined dendritic structure.
2. **Lower ductility and impact toughness** — due to coarse columnar grains and possible residual stresses.
*(Accept any two valid contrasting properties; ½ mark each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model 2]**
List the **three classifications of beam-to-column connections** as per IS 800:2007. State the **stiffness criterion** that distinguishes a "rigid" connection from a "simple" connection.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Three Classifications (2 Marks):**
1. **Rigid (Moment-Resisting) Connections** — Transfer calculated shear and moment. The angle between beam and column remains practically unchanged.
2. **Semi-Rigid Connections** — Transfer a known fraction of the end moment. The angle change is intermediate between rigid and simple.
3. **Simple (Flexible/Shear) Connections** — Transfer only vertical shear. Negligible moment is transferred (less than 10% of the fixed-end moment).

**Stiffness Criterion (1 Mark):**
A connection is classified as **Rigid** if its initial rotational stiffness $S_{j,init} \geq 25 \cdot E \cdot I_b / L_b$ (where $E$ is Young's modulus, $I_b$ is the moment of inertia of the beam, and $L_b$ is the beam span).

A connection is classified as **Simple/Flexible** if $S_{j,init} < 0.5 \cdot E \cdot I_b / L_b$.

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** A beam ISMB 300 transfers a factored end reaction of **280 kN** to a column flange (12 mm thick). Design a **double web angle (cleat) connection** using 4 bolts of **M16 grade 4.6** in a single vertical column. Use E410 electrodes for the weld between the angle and beam web. Assume shop bolt and shop weld. Take $f_u$ of bolt = 400 MPa, $A_{nb} = 157$ mm², $f_y$ of angle = 250 MPa. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **microstructure of weld metal** in a multi-pass arc weld. Sketch and explain the role of **columnar and equiaxed grains** in determining the mechanical properties. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (14 Marks):**

##### Part (a) — Double Web Angle Design (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Bolt shear capacity (single bolt) (1 Mark)**

$$V_{dsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mb}} \cdot A_{nb} \cdot n_n$$

For a single shear lap joint, $n_n = 1$, $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ (shop):

$$V_{dsb} = \frac{400}{1.732 \times 1.25} \times 157 \times 1 = \frac{400 \times 157}{2.165} = \frac{62,800}{2.165} = 29,007 \text{ N} = 29.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Bolt bearing capacity (1 Mark)**

$$V_{dpb} = \frac{2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{mb}}$$

For M16 bolt, $d = 16$ mm, $k_b = \min(e/(3d_0), p/(3d_0) - 0.25, f_{ub}/f_u, 1.0)$.

Assume edge distance $e = 35$ mm, pitch $p = 50$ mm, $d_0 = 18$ mm:

$k_b = \min(35/54, 50/54 - 0.25, 400/410, 1.0) = \min(0.648, 0.676, 0.976, 1.0) = 0.648$

For 8 mm thick web of ISMB 300: $t = 8$ mm

$$V_{dpb} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.648 \times 16 \times 8 \times 400}{1.25} = \frac{82,944}{1.25} = 66,355 \text{ N} = 66.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Bolt value (1 Mark)**

$$V_{db} = \min(V_{dsb}, V_{dpb}) = \min(29.0, 66.4) = 29.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Number of bolts required (1 Mark)**

$$n = \frac{V}{V_{db}} = \frac{280}{29.0} = 9.65 \approx 10 \text{ bolts}$$

Provide **2 columns of 5 bolts** (10 bolts) per web angle × 2 angles = 20 bolts total.

**Step 5: Design of angle size (1 Mark)**

For 2 rows × 5 bolts per angle, the angle length is at least $5 \times 50 = 250$ mm. Use 100 mm × 100 mm × 10 mm ISA, length = 250 mm.

**Step 6: Weld design between angle and beam web (2 Marks)**

Use 6 mm fillet weld, double side.

$$t_t = 0.707 \times 6 = 4.24 \text{ mm}$$

Check: $t_t \leq t_{angle} - 1.5 = 10 - 1.5 = 8.5$ mm ✓

$$f_{wd} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}$$

$$R_{dw} = 189.4 \times 4.24 = 803 \text{ N/mm} = 0.803 \text{ kN/mm}$$

For two sides, total length required:
$$L_w = \frac{280}{2 \times 0.803} = 174.4 \text{ mm} \approx 200 \text{ mm}$$

Provide 6 mm weld for full 250 mm length (more than required). ✓

##### Part (b) — Weld Metal Microstructure (7 Marks)

**Multi-Pass Weld Microstructure (3 Marks):**
In a multi-pass arc weld, each pass creates its own **dendritic and columnar structure**. The reheating of the previous pass by the subsequent pass causes:
- **Tempering of the prior HAZ**
- **Partial re-austenitization and grain refinement**
- The final weld metal exhibits a **banded structure** of refined sub-grains with improved toughness.

**Columnar Grains (2 Marks):**
- Form near the **fusion boundary** in the direction opposite to heat flow (perpendicular to the isotherms).
- Long, parallel crystals give the weld **anisotropic** properties.
- Tend to be **brittle** along the grain boundaries due to solute segregation.

**Equiaxed Grains (2 Marks):**
- Form at the **weld centerline** where the thermal gradient is low.
- Random orientation provides **isotropic** properties.
- Finer equiaxed grains give **higher toughness** and are preferred for service conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse columnar and equiaxed zones** — remember columnar forms near the fusion line, equiaxed at the center.
> - For the bolted connection, **forget to use $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ for shop** — using 1.50 (field) makes the design conservative but under-marked for "correct code reference."
> - In Part (b), students **list properties without linking to microstructure** — always tie properties (e.g., hardness, toughness) to the underlying grain structure and cooling rate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alt Choice]**

**(a)** A column ISHB 300 (@ $f_y = 250$ MPa, flange thickness = 11.6 mm) is connected to a beam ISMB 450 (web thickness = 9.4 mm) by a **welded flange plate moment connection**. The factored moment at the joint is $M = 350$ kN·m and factored shear = 200 kN. Design the **flange plate size** and the **fillet weld** at the tension flange, assuming E410 electrodes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Northridge failure** of welded moment connections and outline the **modern detailing practice** to mitigate such failures. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (14 Marks):**

##### Part (a) — Welded Flange Plate Design (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Flange force from the moment (2 Marks)**

For a welded flange plate moment connection, the bending moment is resisted by a force couple between the top (tension) and bottom (compression) flange plates. The lever arm = beam depth = 450 mm.

$$T = C = \frac{M}{d} = \frac{350}{0.450} = 777.78 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Design the tension flange plate width and thickness (2 Marks)**

For a width similar to the beam flange (150 mm), try plate: 150 mm × 20 mm.

Plate area: $A_p = 150 \times 20 = 3000 \text{ mm}^2$

Plate tensile capacity:
$$T_{dp} = \frac{A_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{3000 \times 250}{1.10} = 681,818 \text{ N} = 681.8 \text{ kN}$$

Since $681.8 < 777.78$, **increase plate thickness to 25 mm**.

New area: $A_p = 150 \times 25 = 3750 \text{ mm}^2$

$$T_{dp} = \frac{3750 \times 250}{1.10} = 852,272 \text{ N} = 852.3 \text{ kN} > 777.78 \text{ kN} ✓$$

**Step 3: Fillet weld design at tension flange (3 Marks)**

The flange plate is welded to the column flange and the beam flange. Critical throat limit: $t_t \leq t - 1.5$ for thinner plate.

Beam flange thickness of ISMB 450 = 17.4 mm
Column flange thickness = 11.6 mm ← governs

$$t_t \leq 11.6 - 1.5 = 10.1 \text{ mm}$$

Try weld size $s = 12$ mm:
$$t_t = 0.707 \times 12 = 8.48 \text{ mm} \leq 10.1 \text{ mm} ✓$$

Weld strength per unit length (E410, $f_u = 410$ MPa):
$$f_{wd} = \frac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}$$

Capacity per mm (single weld line):
$$R_{dw} = 189.4 \times 8.48 = 1606 \text{ N/mm} = 1.606 \text{ kN/mm}$$

For a 150 mm wide plate with welds on two longitudinal edges:
- Two welds, each of length = 150 mm (but this is too short; usually plates run 100–200 mm along beam)

Total capacity:
$$T_{weld} = 2 \times 1.606 \times 150 = 481.8 \text{ kN}$$

For full 777.78 kN, **plate length** required:
$$L_{plate} = \frac{777.78}{2 \times 1.606} = 242.2 \text{ mm}$$

Provide plate length = 250 mm, with 3-sided weld (top + 2 sides).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Detailing Tip:** In seismic design, the weld is usually provided on **3 sides** of the flange plate (top and 2 longitudinal edges) to ensure the plate behaves monolithically. The bottom edge is left unwelded to avoid intersecting welds at the corner.

##### Part (b) — Northridge Failure Analysis (7 Marks)

**What happened at Northridge (1994) (3 Marks):**
- The 1994 Northridge (California) earthquake caused **brittle fractures** in welded beam-to-column moment connections of steel frame buildings.
- The fractures initiated in the **weld metal and the HAZ** of the bottom flange groove weld, not in the beam itself.
- Surprisingly, **the welds had not yielded** — they fractured in a brittle, low-cycle fatigue mode.
- Key reasons identified:
  - **Low notch toughness** of the weld metal (Charpy values were often below 27 J at 0°C).
  - **High triaxial stress state** at the weld root due to backing bar and groove geometry.
  - **Strain concentration** at the beam bottom flange — a complex 3D stress state.
  - **Drainage holes** in the column web, which created stress concentrations.

**Modern Detailing Practice to Mitigate (4 Marks):**
1. **Use of Notch-Tough Weld Metal:** Specify electrodes with **Charpy V-Notch ≥ 27 J at -29°C** (E7018 or equivalent). This is mandated in FEMA 350 and Indian IS 2062 (for seismic zones).
2. **Welded Unreinforced Flange (WUF-W) Connection:** Remove backing bar, use a **double-sided complete joint penetration (CJP) weld**, and add a **fillet weld at the bottom of the backing bar** to reduce the notch effect.
3. **Reduced Beam Section (RBS) — "Dog-Bone":** Trimming the beam flanges near the column forces the plastic hinge to form **away from the connection**, in the ductile beam material.
4. **Strong-Column Weak-Beam Design:** Ensure the column's flexural capacity exceeds the beam's by **at least 20%** (per IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.7.6), so the beam yields before the column.
5. **Continuity Plates / Doubler Plates:** Add column web stiffeners opposite the beam flanges to reduce the stress concentration in the column panel zone.
6. **Improved Welding Procedures:** Use **low-heat-input processes** (e.g., GMAW with robotic control) to refine the weld metal microstructure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In Part (a), students **forget to apply $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ for steel member capacity** — using $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ is incorrect for plate yielding.
> - In Part (b), students list only **"use better welds"** without naming specific electrodes (E7018) and test temperatures — examiners expect the **Charpy value AND test temperature** for full marks.
> - Avoid generic statements like *"buildings failed due to earthquake"* — focus on the **mechanism** (brittle fracture at weld, low toughness, stress concentration).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Weld Metal & Beam-to-Column Connections):**

- [x] **Weld Metal Definition:** Molten filler + base metal, re-solidified in the joint.
- [x] **HAZ Definition:** Adjacent base metal, microstructurally altered, not melted.
- [x] **Grain Structure:** Columnar (near fusion line, perpendicular to isotherms) + Equiaxed (at centerline).
- [x] **Heat Input Formula:** $H = kVI/(v \times 1000)$ kJ/mm; faster cooling = harder, more brittle weld.
- [x] **Effective Throat:** $t_t = 0.707 s$ (equal legs); $t_t = s_1 s_2 / \sqrt{s_1^2 + s_2^2}$ (unequal legs).
- [x] **Fillet Weld Design Strength:** $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw})$.
- [x] **Partial Safety Factors:** $\gamma_{mb} = \gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop), 1.50 (field).
- [x] **Connection Classification:** Rigid / Semi-Rigid / Simple — based on moment transfer and stiffness.
- [x] **Rigid Stiffness Criterion:** $S_{j,init} \geq 25 EI_b/L_b$.
- [x] **Simple Stiffness Criterion:** $S_{j,init} < 0.5 EI_b/L_b$.
- [x] **Types of Rigid Connections:** EEP, Welded Flange Plate, RBS, Cover Plate.
- [x] **Types of Simple Connections:** Double Web Angle, Fin Plate, Shear End Plate.
- [x] **Eccentric Shear Formula:** $F = \sqrt{(V/n)^2 + (V e r_i / \sum r_i^2)^2}$.
- [x] **Code References:** IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.1, 10.4, 10.5), IS 814, IS 816, IS 2062.
- [x] **Northridge Lesson:** Specify notch-tough electrodes (E7018, CVN ≥ 27 J @ -29°C).
- [x] **Design Philosophy:** Strong-Column Weak-Beam; ductile failure in beam, never in weld.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "design" a connection, always present the solution in **five standard steps** — (1) Compute factored load, (2) Check minimum/maximum code limits, (3) Trial size, (4) Capacity check, (5) Detailing. Examiners reward **structured presentation** with 1–2 extra marks beyond raw correctness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stiffened Beam Seat Connection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Stiffened Beam Seat Connection # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IS 800:2007 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stiffened Beam Seat Connection** is a type of **flexible (simple) shear connection** in steel structures, used to transfer the end reaction of a supported beam to a supporting member (column or girder), in which a **vertical stiffener plate** is provided between the web of the supporting member and the web/flange of the supported beam to prevent local bending, buckling, and crippling of the seat angle under concentrated reaction loads. The connection is classified under **Type A / B (simple framing)** per **IS 800:2007, Clause 10.2.4** — assuming the beam is "shear-connected" with rotation possible and end moment treated as zero for design purposes. ### 1.2 Components of a Stiffened Beam Seat | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Seat Angle / Plate (Horizontal Element)** | Provides bearing surface for the beam; resists vertical reaction | | **Stiffener Plate (Vertical Element)** | Prevents local buckling of the seat; transfers reaction as a cantilever to the column | | **Filler Beam / Notch (if any)** | Levels the seat to required elevation | | **Connecting Welds** | Fillet welds joining stiffener to column flange and seat plate to stiffener/column | | **Beam Web** | Rests on seat, transfers reaction through bearing | | **Top Cleat (Tension Cleat)** | Optional — provides lateral restraint and nominal end-moment capacity | > [!NOTE] > The **stiffener** is the key distinguishing feature. Without it, the connection is called an **Unstiffened Beam Seat** (suitable for small reactions only, typically up to 40–60 kN). ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > Think of a **wooden chair with a backrest**: > - The **horizontal seat** is like the chair's bottom plank where you sit. > - The **vertical backrest** is like the **stiffener** — it stops the seat from bending or breaking when a heavy weight (the beam reaction) is placed at its edge. > - The **chair legs (or wall mounting)** are like the **column flange** to which the seat is welded. > - The **person sitting** represents the **beam transferring its end reaction** to the seat. In a real steel frame, imagine a long horizontal beam (like a roof purlin or floor beam) resting on a small horizontal bracket welded to a vertical column. Without reinforcement, the bracket would bend like a weak shelf. Adding a vertical triangular gusset (stiffener) underneath transforms the bracket into a robust shelf that can carry heavy loads. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics (IS 800:2007) - **Partial Safety Factor for Steel:** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ - **Partial Safety Factor for Welds:** $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop) / 1.50 (field) - **Minimum seat length / bearing length:** $b_w \geq 50$ mm - **Fillet weld size minimum:** $s_{\min} = 3$ mm (≤ 10 mm plate), 5 mm (>10 mm plate) - **Maximum weld size:** $s_{\max} = t - 1.5$ mm (on rolled edge) --- ## 1.5 Why We Use Stiffened Seats 1. **Higher Load Capacity** — Stiffened seats can carry 2×–4× the reaction of unstiffened seats. 2. **Stiffness Control** — Prevents excessive rotation/deflection at the support. 3. **Localized Stress Mitigation** — Spreads the reaction over a larger column area, reducing web crippling/bearing failures. 4. **Erection Friendly** — Beam can be simply placed on the seat without temporary propping (self-supporting). 5. **Ductility** — Allows in-plane rotation, classifying it as a flexible connection per IS 800:2007. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Load Transfer Mechanism A stiffened beam seat transmits loads through the following **sequential load path**: 1. **End Reaction $V_u$** acts downward at the beam's bearing location on the seat. 2. Reaction passes from **beam web → horizontal seat plate/angle → vertical stiffener → column flange** via welds. 3. The stiffener behaves as a **short cantilever plate** fixed to the column flange, transferring the eccentric load. ``` Beam (loads on top) │ ▼ [ Beam Web (end face) ] │ ▼ (bearing on seat) [ ━━━ Horizontal Seat Plate/Angle ━━━ ] ← bent under load │ ▼ (welded to) [ ┃ Vertical Stiffener Plate ┃ ] ← cantilever from column │ ▼ (welded to) [ Column Flange ] ``` > [!NOTE] > The critical **eccentricity** in a stiffened seat is the distance from the line of action of the beam reaction to the face of the column. This eccentricity creates a moment that must be resisted by the welds (or the seat's flexural strength). ## 2.2 Design Philosophy: Limit State Method (IS 800:2007) The design must satisfy the following limit states per **Cl. 8, IS 800:2007**: - **Limit State of Strength** (yielding, rupture, buckling) - **Limit State of Serviceability** (deflection, vibration — less critical here) - **Limit State of Fatigue** (for cyclically loaded structures, e.g., crane girders) ## 2.3 Failure Modes Checked in Design | # | Failure Mode | Critical Element | Check | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Web Bearing / Crippling** | Beam web under seat | $V_u \leq V_{dbearing}$ | | 2 | **Bending of Seat Plate/Angle** | Horizontal plate | $M_u \leq M_{dp}$ | | 3 | **Shear in Stiffener** | Vertical stiffener | $V_u \leq V_{dstiffener}$ | | 4 | **Buckling of Stiffener** | Vertical stiffener | Treated as short column | | 5 | **Weld Rupture** | Fillet welds | $R \leq R_{dw}$ | | 6 | **Bearing on Stiffener** | Stiffener against column | $V_u \leq (f_{yw} \cdot A_{st})/\gamma_{m0}$ | | 7 | **Bearing on Column** | Column flange | Check flange local bearing | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations for KTU ESE — direct 14-mark design problems test them.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Reference (IS 800:2007) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Design end reaction** | $V_u = 1.5 \cdot R_D + 1.5 \cdot R_L$ (factored) | Cl. 9.3.1 | | 2 | **Bearing length of beam** | $b_b = \dfrac{V_u}{t_w \cdot f_{yw} / \gamma_{m0}}$ | Cl. 10.3.1 | | 3 | **Bearing length limitation** | $\dfrac{b_b}{t_w} \leq 1.5\,\sqrt{\dfrac{250}{f_y}\cdot\dfrac{E}{f_y}}$ (Class 2) | Cl. 8.7.1.2 | | 4 | **Bearing strength of beam web** | $F_{br,Rd} = \dfrac{f_{yw}\cdot t_w \cdot b_b}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | Cl. 10.3.1 | | 5 | **Weld design strength per mm** | $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}}$ | Cl. 10.5.7 | | 6 | **Strength of fillet weld (per mm length)** | $R_{wd} = 0.7 \cdot s \cdot f_{wd}$ | Cl. 10.5.7 | | 7 | **Bending moment on seat plate** | $M = V_u \cdot e$ (e = eccentricity) | — | | 8 | **Moment capacity of seat plate** | $M_{d} = \dfrac{\beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$, $\beta_b=1.0$ for plates | Cl. 8.2.1.2 | | 9 | **Buckling of stiffener (slenderness)** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cr}}} \leq \sqrt{2}$ | Cl. 8.7.1.1 | | 10 | **Stiffener effective width** | $b_{st,eff} = b_{st} - 2 \cdot t_{st} - 12$ mm (for two fillet welds) | — | | 11 | **Stiffener bearing capacity** | $F_{st,Rd} = \dfrac{f_{yw}\cdot b_{st}\cdot t_{st}}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | Cl. 8.7.4 | | 12 | **Eccentricity for vertical stiffener** | $e = b_{seat} - t_{f,col}/2$ (or column face) | — | | 13 | **Polar moment of weld group** | $I_{xx} + I_{yy}$ (for eccentric welds) | Cl. 10.5.9 | | 14 | **Combined stress in welds** | $f_r = \sqrt{f_{vx}^2 + f_{vy}^2}$ | Cl. 10.5.10 | | 15 | **Min. stiffener thickness** | $t_{st} \geq \dfrac{1}{12} b_{st}$ (for plate) | Engineering rule | > [!TIP] > For Part B (14-mark) questions, the question almost always **traverses all formulas in sequence**. Memorize the order: **(1) Reaction → (2) Bearing length → (3) Stiffener sizing → (4) Weld design**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Buildings** — Connecting roof purlins/rafters to columns. - **Floor Framing** — Secondary beams resting on primary girders. - **Mezzanines & Walkways** — Light-to-medium load framing. - **Pre-Engineered Buildings (PEB)** — Most PEB beam-to-column connections are stiffened seat connections. - **Crane Girders** — With additional check for fatigue (per IS 800:2007, Cl. 13). - **Bridges** — Cross-girders seated on main girders. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Design Procedure — Step-by-Step (IS 800:2007) > [!NOTE] > **Standard Problem Statement:** *Design a stiffened beam seat connection for a beam of section ISMB 400 transferring an end reaction of $V_u$ kN to a column flange of width $B_c$. Grade of steel: Fe 410. Weld grade: E41.* ### STEP 1: Estimate Design Reaction $V_u$ Sum factored DL, LL (and WL if applicable): $$ V_u = 1.5 \, D_L + 1.5 \, L_L $$ (Add $1.2 D_L + 1.2 L_L \pm 1.2 W_L$ combination for wind-loaded frames.) ### STEP 2: Compute Required Bearing Length $b_b$ The beam web must bear safely on the seat. The required bearing area is: $$ A_{b,req} = \frac{V_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_{yw}} $$ Since the bearing length is supported by the full web thickness $t_w$: $$ b_b = \frac{V_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{t_w \cdot f_{yw}} $$ But $b_b$ is also limited to prevent **web crippling** (Cl. 8.7.1.2): $$ b_b \le 1.5 \, t_w \cdot \sqrt{\frac{250 \cdot E}{f_y^{\,2}}} $$ Choose the **greater** of the two: $b_{b,provided} = \max(50 \text{ mm}, \, b_b)$. > [!NOTE] > **For ISMB 400:** $t_w = 8.9$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa. $b_b$ rarely exceeds 100 mm for routine reactions. ### STEP 3: Size the Stiffener Plate **a) Width of stiffener:** It must extend along the full depth of seat, typically: $$ b_{st} = b_{seat} \approx b_b + 25 \text{ mm (edge clearance)} \approx 100 \text{ mm} $$ **b) Thickness of stiffener:** The stiffener acts in **bearing and possibly buckling** under the reaction: $$ t_{st,min} = \frac{V_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{b_{st} \cdot f_{yw}} $$ Use **standard plate thickness ≥ 10 mm** for practical stiffeners, with: $$ t_{st} \ge \frac{b_{st}}{12\sqrt{250/f_y}} \quad \text{(for buckling check, Cl. 8.7.1.4)} $$ ### STEP 4: Check Stiffener as a Short Column (Buckling) The stiffener, when projecting from the column, is treated as a **short cantilever column**. The slenderness ratio (for buckling out of its plane): $$ \lambda = \frac{h_{st}}{\sqrt{I_{st}/A_{st}}} = \frac{0.7 \, h_{st}}{t_{st}} $$ where $h_{st}$ = projection length of stiffener. For stiffener projecting from column flange: $$ \frac{h_{st}}{t_{st}} \le 13.6 \,\sqrt{\frac{250}{f_y}} $$ (For Fe 410: $h_{st}/t_{st} \le 13.6$.) ### STEP 5: Design Fillet Weld (Stiffener-to-Column) The vertical reaction $V_u$ is carried by the two fillet welds along the two vertical edges of the stiffener. Weld length available (effective, accounting for end returns): $$ l_w = h_{st} - 2 \cdot s \quad \text{(on each edge)} $$ Total weld length (two edges): $$ L_{w,total} = 2 \cdot (h_{st} - 2 s) $$ Weld capacity (per mm): $$ r_{wd} = 0.7 \, s \cdot f_{wd} = 0.7 \, s \cdot \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}} $$ Total weld capacity: $$ R_{wd,total} = 2 \cdot l_w \cdot r_{wd} \ge V_u $$ **Solve for s:** $$ s \ge \frac{V_u}{2 \cdot l_w \cdot 0.7 \cdot f_{wd}} $$ Choose $s$ ≥ $s_{min}$ (3 mm for ≤10 mm plate) and ≤ $t_{st} - 1.5$ mm. ### STEP 6: Check Eccentric Load on Welds (If Applicable) If the stiffener is welded to the column with the weld group carrying a moment, compute combined stresses: $$ f_{vx} = \frac{V_u}{2 \, l_w} \qquad f_{vy} = \frac{M}{Z_w} \qquad f_r = \sqrt{f_{vx}^2 + f_{vy}^2} \le f_{wd} $$ where $Z_w$ is the section modulus of the weld group. ### STEP 7: Check Bearing of Stiffener Against Column Flange $$ \sigma_{bearing} = \frac{V_u}{b_{st} \cdot t_{st}} \le 0.6 f_y \quad \text{(allowable for bearing per Cl. 10.3.4)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For **direct bearing** on the column flange (the stiffener is in line contact), the permissible bearing stress is $0.6 f_y$ for close-fit conditions. If not in line contact, reduce to $0.5 f_y$. ### STEP 8: Check Seat Plate Bending (If Seat Plate is Separate from Stiffener) The seat plate/angle bends under $V_u$ if there's a free overhang. For a plate of length $L$ cantilevered from the stiffener: $$ M = V_u \cdot a \quad \text{where } a = \text{overhang from stiffener center} $$ $$ M_d = \frac{1.0 \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{f_y \cdot b_{seat} \cdot t_{seat}^2}{6 \cdot \gamma_{m0}} \ge M $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Given:** - Beam: **ISMB 400** ($D = 400$ mm, $b_f = 140$ mm, $t_f = 16$ mm, $t_w = 8.9$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa) - Column Flange: **ISMB 500** ($b_f = 180$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa) - Factored Reaction: $V_u = 350$ kN - Material: **Fe 410** ($f_u = 410$ MPa) **Solution:** ### (i) Bearing Length $$ b_b = \frac{V_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{t_w \cdot f_{yw}} = \frac{350 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{8.9 \times 250} = 172.9 \text{ mm} $$ > Check crippling: $b_b/t_w = 172.9/8.9 = 19.4$. Allowable: $1.5 \cdot \sqrt{250 \cdot 2 \times 10^5 / 250^2} = 1.5 \cdot \sqrt{2000} = 67.1$. OK. > **Provide $b_b = 175$ mm (round up).** ### (ii) Stiffener Size $$ b_{st} = b_b + 25 = 200 \text{ mm} $$ $$ t_{st,req} = \frac{350 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{200 \times 250} = 7.7 \text{ mm} $$ > **Use stiffener plate: $b_{st} \times t_{st} = 200 \times 10$ mm** (practical choice). ### (iii) Stiffener Buckling Check Projecting length $h_{st}$ from column face = full depth of beam web clearance + 50 mm = $\sim 250$ mm. $$ \frac{h_{st}}{t_{st}} = \frac{250}{10} = 25 > 13.6 \quad \text{(FAILS for direct projection)} $$ > **Solution:** Weld the seat plate to the column also, OR reduce $h_{st}$ to $13.6 \times 10 = 136$ mm (use a deeper seat plate or thicker stiffener). > [!TIP] > In practice, the seat plate is also welded to the column, which provides **lateral restraint** to the stiffener edge, eliminating buckling. If the seat plate provides restraint, no separate buckling check is needed. ### (iv) Weld Design (Stiffener to Column) Weld size: assume $s = 6$ mm. Effective weld length (per edge): $l_w = 200 - 2 \times 6 = 188$ mm. Weld strength per mm: $$ f_{wd} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ N/mm} $$ Capacity per mm length: $0.7 \times 6 \times 189.4 = 795.5$ N/mm. Total capacity (two welds): $2 \times 188 \times 795.5 = 299{,}108$ N $= 299$ kN. > **Required ≥ 350 kN. FAILS.** Increase $s$ to 8 mm: Capacity per mm: $0.7 \times 8 \times 189.4 = 1060.6$ N/mm. Total: $2 \times 184 \times 1060.6 = 390{,}300$ N = 390 kN > 350 kN. **OK.** > **Provide 8 mm fillet weld on both vertical edges of stiffener.** ### (v) Bearing of Stiffener on Column $$ \sigma_{br} = \frac{350 \times 10^3}{200 \times 10} = 175 \text{ MPa} < 0.6 \times 250 = 150 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(FAILS slightly)} $$ > **Solution:** Increase stiffener thickness to 12 mm. New bearing stress: $350 \times 10^3 / (200 \times 12) = 145.8$ MPa < 150 MPa. **OK.** > **Final Design: Stiffener 200 × 12 mm, 8 mm fillet welds on both vertical edges.** --- ## 3.3 Summary of Design Decisions | Check | Result | Action | |---|---|---| | Bearing length of beam web | 175 mm required | Provide 175 mm | | Stiffener size | 200 × 12 mm | Use PL 200×12 | | Stiffener buckling | Restricted by seat plate | OK if seat plate welded to column | | Weld (stiffener to column) | 8 mm fillet | Provide 8 mm fillet | | Bearing on column | 145.8 MPa < 150 MPa | OK | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 3D Functional Block Diagram — Stiffened Beam Seat Connection ```mermaid flowchart TB beam["ISMB 400 BEAM
End Reaction Vu = 350 kN
fw yd = 250 MPa"] seat["HORIZONTAL SEAT PLATE
Length 175 mm x Width 200 mm x Thickness 12 mm
Welded to Stiffener and Column Flange"] stiffener["VERTICAL STIFFENER PLATE
200 mm Wide x 12 mm Thick
Cantilevered from Column
Acts as Load Transfer Element"] welds1["FILLET WELD - VERTICAL EDGES
8 mm Size, 184 mm Effective Length
Two Welds Total"] welds2["FILLET WELD - SEAT TO COLUMN
6 mm Size, 175 mm Length
Transfers Direct Vertical Reaction"] column["COLUMN FLANGE
ISMB 500, Width 180 mm
Acts as Support"] bearing["BEARING CONTACT ZONE
Beam Web Bears on Seat
Stress 175 MPa less than fywd 250 MPa"] moment["BENDING IN SEAT PLATE
Due to Eccentricity e
Moment Mu = Vu times e"] beam --> bearing bearing --> seat seat --> welds2 welds2 --> column seat --> stiffener stiffener --> welds1 welds1 --> column seat --> moment stiffener --> column style beam fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style seat fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF style stiffener fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF style welds1 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style welds2 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style column fill:#808080,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF style bearing fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style moment fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 4.2 Load Path Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Beam End Reaction Vu"] --> B["Beam Web Bearing
on Seat Plate"] B --> C{"Seat Plate
Bends?"} C -->|Yes| D["Bending Stress Check
Mu less than Md"] C -->|No| E["Direct Transfer"] D --> F["Stiffener Plate
Cantilever Action"] E --> F F --> G["Weld Group
8 mm Fillet"] G --> H["Column Flange
Local Bearing"] H --> I["Foundation"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#808080,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Design Procedure Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD s1["STEP 1
Compute Factored Reaction Vu"] s2["STEP 2
Bearing Length bb"] s3{"STEP 3
bb/tw check
Crippling"} s4["STEP 4
Stiffener Width and Thickness"] s5{"STEP 5
Buckling Check
hst/tst"} s6["STEP 6
Weld Design s and lw"] s7{"STEP 7
Bearing on Column"} s8["STEP 8
Provide Final Sizes"] s9["DESIGN COMPLETE"] s1 --> s2 --> s3 s3 -->|OK| s4 s3 -->|Fails| s2 s4 --> s5 s5 -->|OK| s6 s5 -->|Fails| s4 s6 --> s7 s7 -->|OK| s8 s7 -->|Fails| s4 s8 --> s9 style s1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style s2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style s3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style s4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style s5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style s6 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style s7 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style s8 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style s9 fill:#00FF00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Connection Classification Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["BEAM SEAT CONNECTIONS"] U1["UNSTIFFENED SEAT
Capacity 40 to 60 kN
Single Angle or Plate
No Vertical Stiffener"] ST1["STIFFENED SEAT
Capacity up to 400 kN
Plate with Vertical Stiffener
Used in Industrial Frames"] S1 --> U1 S1 --> ST1 style S1 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF style U1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style ST1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Q1.** Differentiate between a **stiffened** and **unstiffened** beam seat connection. State the typical load ranges for each. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Unstiffened Seat | Stiffened Seat | |---|---|---| | Stiffener | Not provided | Vertical plate provided | | Capacity | 40–60 kN | 150–400 kN | | Seat type | Single angle or plate | Plate with stiffener | | Failure mode | Bending of seat angle | Bearing / buckling of stiffener | | Economy | Light frames | Industrial / heavy frames | | Rotation capacity | High | Moderate | > **In an unstiffened seat**, the reaction $V_u$ is transferred through the **bending of the seat angle** alone. The angle acts as a short cantilever, and failure occurs by yielding at the fillet. > **In a stiffened seat**, a **vertical stiffener plate** is welded between the seat plate and the column, transforming the connection into a **load-bearing cantilever**. The reaction transfers via direct bearing on the stiffener → welds → column flange, drastically increasing the capacity. --- ### **Q2.** Why is a **minimum bearing length of 50 mm** specified for beam seats in IS 800:2007? What does this control? **[KTU University Exam — December 2022]** **Model Answer:** The **minimum bearing length of 50 mm** is specified to: 1. **Prevent local web crippling** of the beam due to concentrated reactions. 2. **Ensure adequate contact area** for stress dispersion from the beam web to the seat plate. 3. **Provide tolerance** for fabrication and erection inaccuracies (so the beam doesn't slip off the seat). 4. **Maintain stability** during erection (the beam is self-supporting on the seat without temporary props). 5. **Control edge stresses** — below 50 mm, the bearing stress gradient becomes too steep, leading to web buckling. > **Code reference:** IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.2.4.5 — *"The bearing length shall not be less than 50 mm."* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 Scheme provides choice between two questions of the same module. Both Question A and Question B are provided below for your practice. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** A floor beam of section **ISMB 350 ($t_w = 8.1$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa)** transfers a factored end reaction of **$V_u = 280$ kN** to the flange of a column **ISMB 500 (flange width 180 mm)**. Design a **stiffened beam seat connection** using shop fillet welds. Material: **Fe 410 ($f_u = 410$ MPa)**. Adopt **Limit State Design** as per IS 800:2007. #### **Part (a) — Size the seat plate and stiffener plate. (7 Marks)** **Step 1: Bearing Length of Beam Web** $$ b_b = \frac{V_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{t_w \cdot f_{yw}} = \frac{280 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{8.1 \times 250} = 152.1 \text{ mm} $$ > **Provide $b_b = 155$ mm** (rounded up to 5 mm). **Check crippling limit:** $$ \frac{b_b}{t_w} = \frac{155}{8.1} = 19.13 \le 67.1 \quad \text{OK} $$ **Step 2: Stiffener Plate Dimensions** Width: $b_{st} = b_b + 25 = 180$ mm. Thickness (bearing): $$ t_{st,req} = \frac{280 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{180 \times 250} = 6.84 \text{ mm} $$ > **Provide $b_{st} \times t_{st} = 180 \times 10$ mm** (practical minimum for erection). **Step 3: Check Buckling** Assume $h_{st} = 200$ mm (projection). $$ \frac{h_{st}}{t_{st}} = \frac{200}{10} = 20 $$ > Limit: $13.6 \cdot \sqrt{250/f_y} = 13.6$ for Fe 410. > The seat plate (welded to column) provides lateral restraint to the top of the stiffener. With **both edges restrained**, this becomes a **stiffened plate** scenario, and buckling is controlled by the **web buckling coefficient** of stiffened elements. Practically, OK. > **Alternative:** Increase $t_{st}$ to 14 mm → $h_{st}/t_{st} = 200/14 = 14.3$, just over the limit. Increase to 16 mm: $h_{st}/t_{st} = 12.5$ < 13.6. **OK with 16 mm.** > **Decision:** Use **180 × 16 mm** to be safe (or 12 mm if seat plate fully restrains). > **Step 4: Bearing of Stiffener on Column** $$ \sigma_{br} = \frac{280 \times 10^3}{180 \times 16} = 97.2 \text{ MPa} < 0.6 \cdot 250 = 150 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{OK} $$ **Part (a) Answer:** Stiffener PL $180 \times 16$ mm, seat plate $155 \times 180 \times 12$ mm. #### **Part (b) — Design the fillet welds and sketch the connection. (7 Marks)** **Step 1: Weld Size Determination** Try $s = 6$ mm fillet welds on both vertical edges of stiffener. Effective weld length per edge (after deducting weld size from both ends): $$ l_w = h_{st} - 2s = 200 - 2 \times 6 = 188 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2: Weld Strength** $$ f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa} $$ Capacity per mm of weld: $0.7 \times 6 \times 189.4 = 795.5$ N/mm. **Step 3: Total Weld Capacity (Two Welds)** $$ R_{wd,total} = 2 \times 188 \times 795.5 = 299{,}108 \text{ N} = 299.1 \text{ kN} $$ **Comparison:** $299.1$ kN > $V_u = 280$ kN. **OK.** **Step 4: Seat-Plate-to-Column Weld (Optional — for seat stability)** Provide 6 mm fillet weld on top and bottom of seat plate → length ≈ 175 mm each. > **Final Design Summary:** - **Seat plate:** 155 × 180 × 12 mm PL - **Stiffener plate:** 180 × 16 mm PL - **Fillet weld (stiffener to column):** 6 mm on both vertical edges - **Fillet weld (seat to column):** 6 mm on bottom face --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — December 2023]** Design a **stiffened beam seat connection** for an **ISMB 450** beam ($t_w = 9.4$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa) carrying a factored end reaction of **$V_u = 320$ kN**. Column is **ISHB 300** with flange width 200 mm. Material Fe 410, shop welds. State all assumptions clearly. #### **Part (a) — Compute the design bearing length, stiffener dimensions, and check the buckling. (7 Marks)** **Step 1: Bearing Length** $$ b_b = \frac{320 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{9.4 \times 250} = 149.8 \text{ mm} $$ > **Provide $b_b = 150$ mm.** **Step 2: Stiffener Dimensions** Width: $b_{st} = 150 + 25 = 175$ mm → **provide 180 mm** (for standard sizes). Thickness (bearing): $t_{st,req} = \frac{320 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{180 \times 250} = 7.82$ mm → **provide 10 mm.** **Step 3: Buckling Check** $h_{st}/t_{st} = 200/10 = 20$. Limit = 13.6 for Fe 410. **FAILS.** > **Solution:** Use **12 mm thick stiffener** → $h_{st}/t_{st} = 200/12 = 16.7$. Still fails. **Use 16 mm** → $h_{st}/t_{st} = 12.5$. **OK.** > **Final Stiffener:** 180 × 16 mm. **Step 4: Bearing on Column** $$ \sigma_{br} = \frac{320 \times 10^3}{180 \times 16} = 111.1 \text{ MPa} < 150 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{OK} $$ #### **Part (b) — Design welds and check combined stresses. (7 Marks)** **Step 1: Weld Sizing** Try 6 mm fillet welds on both vertical edges of stiffener. $l_w = 200 - 2 \times 6 = 188$ mm per edge. Weld capacity per mm: $0.7 \times 6 \times 189.4 = 795.5$ N/mm. Total (two welds): $2 \times 188 \times 795.5 = 299$ kN < 320 kN. **FAILS.** > **Increase to $s = 7$ mm.** Capacity per mm: $0.7 \times 7 \times 189.4 = 928$ N/mm. Total: $2 \times 186 \times 928 = 345.2$ kN > 320 kN. **OK.** **Step 2: Combined Stress Check (Eccentricity)** The reaction $V_u$ may act at an eccentricity from the weld centroid. If the load is centered on the stiffener (no eccentricity), the direct shear check above suffices. If $e$ = distance from weld centroid to load = 90 mm (half the stiffener width): Moment on weld group: $M = 320 \times 0.090 = 28.8$ kN·m. Section modulus of weld group (two lines of length 188 mm separated by 180 - 2×7 = 166 mm): $$ I_w = 2 \times \left[ \frac{188^3}{12} + 188 \times (83)^2 \right] = 2 \times [554{,}000 + 1{,}294{,}000] = 3{,}696{,}000 \text{ mm}^4 $$ $$ Z_w = I_w / (93) = 39{,}742 \text{ mm}^3 $$ Bending stress in weld: $$ f_b = \frac{M}{Z_w} = \frac{28.8 \times 10^6}{39{,}742} = 724.7 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(Very high!)} $$ > [!WARNING] > **This is a CRITICAL KTU pitfall** — if the load is eccentric, the bending stress is massive. **Solution:** Place the load (i.e., the beam end) **directly over the stiffener centroid**, not at its edge. The bearing on the seat is a **concentrated contact**, and the load is transferred to the stiffener through bearing (not as a single point load on the weld). > **Corrected Analysis:** The welds carry **direct shear only** (no moment), because the seat plate is in line bearing against the stiffener. The 6 mm weld was inadequate; use **7 mm weld**. > **Final:** **7 mm fillet weld on both vertical edges.** Seat plate welded to column with 6 mm fillet. --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Points in Stiffened Beam Seat Design:** > > 1. **Forgetting $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$** in the bearing length calculation. *(2–3 marks lost!)* > 2. **Ignoring web crippling check** on the beam web. Examiners specifically test $b_b/t_w$ ≤ limit. > 3. **Not applying $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$** (shop) or **1.50** (field) in weld design — this drops capacity by 16%. > 4. **Confusing $f_y$ and $f_u$** in weld design: weld uses $f_u$ (ultimate), plate uses $f_y$ (yield). > 5. **Buckling check on stiffener** — forgetting the 13.6 limit for Fe 410 is a classic trap. > 6. **Using $V_u$ (unfactored) instead of factored reaction.** Always use factored $V_u$. > 7. **Not specifying minimum weld size** $s_{min} = 3$ mm or maximum $s_{max} = t - 1.5$ mm. > 8. **Eccentricity mistake** in weld group: assume simple shear transfer (load directly above stiffener) unless stated otherwise. > 9. **Missing bearing check on column** ($\sigma \leq 0.6 f_y$) — losing 2 marks. > 10. **Final sketch missing labels** (component sizes, weld sizes, member orientations) — 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - ☐ **Stiffened seat = seat plate + vertical stiffener**, used for reactions **> 60 kN**. - ☐ **Bearing length formula:** $b_b = \dfrac{V_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{t_w \cdot f_{yw}}$ - ☐ **Min. $b_b$ = 50 mm** (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.2.4.5). - ☐ **Crippling check:** $b_b/t_w \le 1.5 \sqrt{250 E / f_y^2}$ (Fe 410 limit: 67.1). - ☐ **Stiffener acts as a short cantilever** — buckling limit: $h_{st}/t_{st} \le 13.6 \sqrt{250/f_y}$ (Fe 410). - ☐ **Weld design strength:** $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw})$ — use $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop) or 1.50 (field). - ☐ **Weld capacity per mm:** $0.7 \cdot s \cdot f_{wd}$. - ☐ **Bearing on column:** $\sigma_{br} \le 0.6 f_y$ (close fit) or $0.5 f_y$ (loose fit). - ☐ **Total weld length on stiffener** = 2 × (height − 2 × weld size). - ☐ **Stiffener thickness** ≥ 1/12 × width (engineering rule of thumb). - ☐ **Always state partial safety factors** in the answer — examiners reward this. - ☐ **Sketch must show:** beam, column, seat plate, stiffener, welds (with sizes), and a clear dimensioned elevation/plan. - ☐ **Code:** All design must be tagged as **"Limit State Design as per IS 800:2007."** - ☐ **For 14-mark problems**, follow the **8-step design sequence** — examiners scan for the correct order. - ☐ **Top cleat / web side cleats** are NOT required for shear-only connections; mention only if end-moment is to be transferred. --- > **END OF NOTES — Stiffened Beam Seat Connection (Module 2)** > *PECET864 — Advanced Design of Steel Structures | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Web Angle and End Plate Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Web Angle and End Plate Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 800:2007 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Angle Connection (Double Angle Web Cleat Connection):** A flexible, semi-rigid type of bolted connection used to join a secondary beam (supported member) to a primary beam or column (supporting member) using a pair of unstiffened angles (web cleats) bolted to the web of the supported beam and the flange/web of the supporting member. These are classified as **Type 2 (bearing-type) connections** with **slip-resistant behavior under service loads** per IS 800:2007 Table 12 (Classification of Connections).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End Plate Connection (Bolted End Plate Connection):** A connection in which a plate is welded to the end of a beam and bolted to the flange of a column or the web of another beam. End plate connections are categorized into **(a) Flexible End Plate (Partial Depth)**, **(b) Rigid/Full-Depth End Plate (with stiffener or unstiffened)**, based on the rotational stiffness and moment resistance per IS 800:2007.

These connections primarily transfer **shear** (web angle) or **shear + moment** (end plate) between members in a steel framed structure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Web Angle Connection Analogy — "The Bookend on a Shelf":**
Imagine two L-shaped bookends supporting a row of books on a wooden shelf. The shelf (secondary beam) rests on a bracket (seat angle) and is held laterally by two small angle pieces (web cleats) bolted to the vertical wall (column). The web cleats don't carry much moment; they simply keep the shelf from sliding or tipping. The load is transferred vertically through the bottom seat angle, and the web angles provide lateral stability — exactly the role a double-angle web connection plays in steel construction.

**End Plate Connection Analogy — "The Door Hinge Plate":**
Think of a heavy metal door whose edge is welded to a flat plate, which is then bolted to a strong door-frame post. The plate distributes the load over a large area of bolts, allowing the door-to-post junction to carry bending (moment) as well as shear. A full-depth end plate connection behaves similarly — the plate welded to the beam end distributes moment-induced tension/compression forces through bolt rows across the beam depth.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Parameters (Standard Metrics in **bold**)

- **Bolt Hole Diameter $d_0$ = bolt diameter + clearance (1.5 mm to 2.0 mm)** for standard clearance holes
- **Pitch (spacing between bolts) = $2.5\,d$** (minimum) and **$32\,t$ or $300\,\text{mm}$** (maximum)
- **Edge Distance = $1.5\,d_0$** (minimum) and **$12\,t\,\varepsilon$** (maximum)
- **Grade of bolts used: 4.6, 8.8, 9.8, 10.9** (HSFG) — Web angles typically use **grade 4.6** (bearing type)
- **Prying Force $Q$** — additional tension per bolt in end plate connections due to flexural deformation of the plate flange

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Web angle and end plate connections form the foundation of **flexible and semi-rigid beam-to-column connections**. Mastery of these topics is essential for the design of building frames, industrial sheds, and composite structures as per **IS 800:2007** (General Construction in Steel — Code of Practice).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Web Angle (Double Angle Cleat) Connection — Theory of Behavior

A web angle connection consists of:
1. **Two web cleats (angles)** — usually equal-leg angles of size $65 \times 65 \times 6$ mm to $90 \times 90 \times 10$ mm
2. **Bolts connecting cleats to the supported beam web** (in single or double shear)
3. **Bolts connecting cleats to the supporting member** (flange of column/beam)

### Force Transfer Mechanism
- The vertical shear from the supported beam is transferred as:
  - **Reaction component** → through the bottom seat angle in **bearing**
  - **Shear component** → through the bolts connecting web angle to the supporting member

### Failure Modes (IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.3)

1. **Shearing of bolts** — bolt fails in single/double shear
2. **Bearing failure** — bolt hole elongates due to crushing
3. **Tearing of plate between bolt hole and edge** — block shear
4. **Tearing of plate between bolt holes** — net section rupture
5. **Crushing/yielding of angle leg in bearing** — angle leg thickness inadequate

### Design Assumptions (IS 800:2007)
- Bolts are **pre-tensioned only enough to ensure firm contact** (not slip-resistant unless HSFG)
- Threads in the shear plane are permissible
- Connection is assumed to rotate freely (pin behavior)

## 2.2 End Plate Connection — Theory of Behavior

End plate connections transfer **both shear and moment**. The plate is shop-welded to the beam end and field-bolted to the supporting column flange or beam web.

### Types of End Plate Connections
| Type | Description | Moment Capacity | Use |
|------|-------------|-----------------|-----|
| Flexible (Partial Depth) | Plate extends only top and bottom flanges | Low (~20% of full plastic moment) | Minor beams, low-moment joints |
| Rigid/Full Depth (Unstiffened) | Plate covers full beam depth | Medium-High | Standard moment connections |
| Rigid/Full Depth (Stiffened) | Plate has additional stiffener plates | High | Heavy moment connections, seismic |

### Prying Action — The Critical Phenomenon
When bolts are in tension (due to moment), the end plate **bends** between the bolt line and the beam flange/weld. This bending produces a **prying force $Q$** that **adds** to the bolt tension. The prying effect is most severe for **thin, flexible end plates**.

The prying force is bounded by two limits:
- **Lower limit:** $Q = 0$ (very thick, rigid plate)
- **Upper limit:** $Q = 0.5 \times \text{bolt tension (T)}$ (very thin, flexible plate)

IS 800:2007 uses a simplified prying model based on **Douty & McGuire / Kennedy** approach.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **IS 800:2007 compliant** and aligned with KTU Module 2 syllabus.

### Table 2.1: Bolted Connection Design Formulas (IS 800:2007)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Nominal shear capacity of bolt (single shear) | $V_{nsb} = \dfrac{A_{nb} \cdot f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mb}}$ | $A_{nb}$ = net shear area; $f_{ub}$ = ultimate tensile strength; $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ |
| 2 | Nominal shear capacity of bolt (double shear) | $V_{nsb} = \dfrac{2 A_{nb} \cdot f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mb}}$ | Two shear planes |
| 3 | Nominal bearing capacity of bolt | $V_{npb} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u / \gamma_{mb}$ | $k_b$ = min of $\dfrac{e}{3d_0}, \dfrac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25, \dfrac{f_{ub}}{f_u}, 1.0$ |
| 4 | Nominal bearing capacity of plate | $V_{npb,plate} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u / \gamma_{mb}$ | $f_u$ of plate |
| 5 | Bolt strength (HSFG / Pretensioned) | $V_{dsb} = \dfrac{\mu_f \, n_e \, K_h \, F_0}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | Slip resistance; $F_0$ = proof load |
| 6 | Design tension capacity of bolt | $T_{db} = 0.90 \, f_{ub} \, A_n / \gamma_{mb} < f_{yb} \, A_{sb} / \gamma_{mb}$ | For end plate bolts |
| 7 | Number of bolts required (shear) | $n = \dfrac{V}{V_{db}}$ | $V_{db}$ = design shear capacity of bolt |
| 8 | Minimum pitch | $p_{min} = 2.5\,d$ | $d$ = nominal bolt diameter |
| 9 | Maximum pitch (tension member) | $p_{max} = 16\,t$ (or $200\,\text{mm}$) | $t$ = thinner plate |
| 10 | Minimum edge distance | $e_{min} = 1.5\,d_0$ | For sheared/hand-flame cut edges: $1.7 d_0$ |
| 11 | Net effective area of plate | $A_{nc} = \left[ B - n_1 \, d_0 - \sum \dfrac{s^2}{4g} \right] \cdot t$ | Cl. 6.3.1, IS 800:2007 |

### Table 2.2: End Plate Prying Force Formulas (IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.4.7)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Lever arm for prying | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{\rho} \left[ \dfrac{F_b / F_o - \gamma}{1 + \gamma} \right]$ (non-dimensional) | $F_b$ = bolt tension; $F_o$ = proof load |
| 2 | Effective flange width (per bolt) | $b_e = $ min of flange width or column flange width minus $2 \times$ fillet weld | |
| 3 | Plate thickness for no prying | $t_{p,req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.2 \, M_{ep}}{f_{yp}}}$ | $M_{ep}$ = end plate moment capacity |
| 4 | Prying force limit (upper) | $Q \leq 0.5 \, T_b$ | T = bolt tension demand |
| 5 | End plate flexural capacity | $M_{ep} = 1.2 \, f_{yp} \, Z_e / \gamma_{m0}$ | $Z_e$ = elastic section modulus of plate per unit width |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web angle connections** are the most common beam-to-column connections in **multi-storey building frames** (e.g., Tata Consultancy Services' building, Mumbai). They allow field assembly with simple bearing-type bolts.
- **End plate connections** are preferred in **prefabricated steel structures** (PEB systems) due to ease of erection, full moment continuity, and minimal site welding.
- Both are extensively used in **industrial buildings, warehouses, and bridge girders**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Design of Double Angle Web Cleat Connection (Exhaustive Procedure)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

> **Given:**
> - Factored reaction on the secondary beam: $V = 250\,\text{kN}$ (factored)
> - Supported beam section: **ISMB 300** ($h = 300\,\text{mm}$, $t_w = 7.5\,\text{mm}$, $b_f = 140\,\text{mm}$, $t_f = 12.4\,\text{mm}$)
> - Supporting column section: **ISHB 300** ($h = 300\,\text{mm}$, $b_f = 250\,\text{mm}$, $t_f = 10.8\,\text{mm}$)
> - Bolt grade: **4.6**, diameter $d = 20\,\text{mm}$, hole diameter $d_0 = 22\,\text{mm}$
> - Angle cleat: **ISA 75 × 75 × 8 mm**, steel grade **Fe 410** ($f_y = 250\,\text{N/mm}^2$, $f_u = 410\,\text{N/mm}^2$)
> - Partial safety factor for material: $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$, $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Calculate Bolt Shear Strength

**Step 1a:** For grade 4.6 bolt, $f_{ub} = 400\,\text{N/mm}^2$.

**Step 1b:** Bolt net shear area (threads in shear plane, assume conservatively):
$$A_{nb} = 0.78 \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d^2}{4} = 0.78 \cdot \dfrac{\pi (20)^2}{4}$$
$$A_{nb} = 0.78 \cdot 314.159 = 245.04\,\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 1c:** Nominal single shear capacity:
$$V_{nsb} = \dfrac{A_{nb} \cdot f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mb}} = \dfrac{245.04 \times 400}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25}$$
$$V_{nsb} = \dfrac{98016}{2.165} = 45270\,\text{N} \approx 45.27\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 1d:** Since double angle cleat has bolts in **double shear** (cleat sandwiched between supporting member and supported beam web? — No, in a web cleat, the supported beam web is between the two cleats, giving single shear; supporting flange side has single shear; average single shear is used):

For a **web cleat**, bolts on **supported beam side** are in **single shear** (cleat-to-beam web, one shear plane).
For **conservative design**, use single shear capacity:
$$V_{dsb} = V_{nsb} = 45.27\,\text{kN}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Calculate Bolt Bearing Strength

**Step 2a:** Determine $k_b$ factors:
- Edge distance $e = 35\,\text{mm}$ (assume): $\dfrac{e}{3 d_0} = \dfrac{35}{3 \times 22} = 0.530$
- Pitch $p = 50\,\text{mm}$ (assume): $\dfrac{p}{3 d_0} - 0.25 = \dfrac{50}{3 \times 22} - 0.25 = 0.758 - 0.25 = 0.508$
- $\dfrac{f_{ub}}{f_u} = \dfrac{400}{410} = 0.976$
- $1.0$ (upper bound)

Therefore $k_b = \min(0.530, 0.508, 0.976, 1.0) = 0.508$

**Step 2b:** Plate thickness $t = 8\,\text{mm}$ (cleat), $t_w = 7.5\,\text{mm}$ (beam web). Minimum governs.

**Step 2c:** Bearing capacity of bolt on cleat:
$$V_{npb,cleat} = 2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u / \gamma_{mb}$$
$$V_{npb,cleat} = 2.5 \times 0.508 \times 20 \times 8 \times 410 / 1.25$$
$$V_{npb,cleat} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.508 \times 20 \times 8 \times 410}{1.25} = \dfrac{83296}{1.25} = 66637\,\text{N}$$
$$V_{npb,cleat} \approx 66.64\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 2d:** Bearing capacity on beam web (since $t_w < t_{cleat}$):
$$V_{npb,web} = 2.5 \times 0.508 \times 20 \times 7.5 \times 410 / 1.25 = 62498\,\text{N} \approx 62.50\,\text{kN}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Bolt Design Strength

$$V_{db} = \min(V_{dsb}, V_{npb}) = \min(45.27, 62.50) = 45.27\,\text{kN}$$

> **Shear governs the design.**

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Number of Bolts Required

$$n = \dfrac{V}{V_{db}} = \dfrac{250}{45.27} = 5.52$$

**Provide $n = 6$ bolts per cleat, arranged in 2 rows × 3 columns on each side.**

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Bolt Spacing Check (IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.2.2)

- Minimum pitch: $p_{min} = 2.5\,d = 2.5 \times 20 = 50\,\text{mm}$ ✓ (use $p = 50\,\text{mm}$)
- Maximum pitch: $p_{max} = 32\,t = 32 \times 7.5 = 240\,\text{mm}$ (or $300\,\text{mm}$)
- Minimum edge distance: $e_{min} = 1.5\,d_0 = 1.5 \times 22 = 33\,\text{mm}$ ✓ (use $e = 35\,\text{mm}$)

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Check Angle Cleat in Shear (Tear-Out / Block Shear)

The bolts produce tearing in the angle leg along the shear plane. The net shear path area must be checked:

$$A_{vn} = 2 \times (L_v - n_1 \cdot d_0) \times t = 2 \times (150 - 2.5 \times 22) \times 8$$
$$A_{vn} = 2 \times (150 - 55) \times 8 = 2 \times 95 \times 8 = 1520\,\text{mm}^2$$

Wait — using a typical cleat length: $L_v = 2 \times 50 + 35 + 35 = 170\,\text{mm}$. Let's adopt $L_v = 175\,\text{mm}$ (2 pitches + 2 edges):

$$A_{vn} = 2 \times (175 - 2.5 \times 22) \times 8 = 2 \times 120 \times 8 = 1920\,\text{mm}^2$$

Shear yielding strength:
$$V_{d,cleat} = \dfrac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{1920 \times 250}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10}$$
$$V_{d,cleat} = \dfrac{480000}{1.905} = 251900\,\text{N} \approx 251.9\,\text{kN}$$

Since $251.9 > 250$ kN, the angle cleat is safe in shear. ✓

## 3.2 Design of Flexible End Plate Connection (Exhaustive Procedure)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

> **Given:**
> - Beam: **ISMB 400** ($h = 400\,\text{mm}$, $b_f = 140\,\text{mm}$, $t_f = 16\,\text{mm}$, $t_w = 8.9\,\text{mm}$)
> - Factored moment: $M = 180\,\text{kNm}$, Factored shear: $V = 150\,\text{kN}$
> - End plate grade: **Fe 410**, $f_y = 250\,\text{N/mm}^2$, $f_u = 410\,\text{N/mm}^2$
> - Bolt grade: **8.8**, $d = 20\,\text{mm}$, $d_0 = 22\,\text{mm}$, $f_{ub} = 800\,\text{N/mm}^2$, $f_{yb} = 640\,\text{N/mm}^2$
> - Column: ISHB 300, $b_f = 250\,\text{mm}$

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Bolt Layout & Tension Demand

**Bolt arrangement:** 4 bolts in 2 rows × 2 columns, with the top row at $h_1 = 350\,\text{mm}$ from the bottom (under the top flange), bottom row at $h_2 = 50\,\text{mm}$ from the bottom.

Force in top bolt (tension flange side):
$$T_1 = \dfrac{M \cdot h_1}{h_1^2 + h_2^2} = \dfrac{180 \times 10^6 \times 350}{350^2 + 50^2} = \dfrac{63 \times 10^9}{125000}$$
$$T_1 = 504000\,\text{N} = 504\,\text{kN (per 2 bolts)}$$
$$\therefore T_{b,top} = 252\,\text{kN per bolt}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Nominal Bolt Tension Capacity

For 8.8 grade bolt:
$$A_{sb} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 314.16\,\text{mm}^2$$
$$A_{nb} = 0.78 \times 314.16 = 245.04\,\text{mm}^2$$

$$T_{nb} = 0.90 \times f_{ub} \times A_{nb} / \gamma_{mb} = 0.90 \times 800 \times 245.04 / 1.25$$
$$T_{nb} = \dfrac{176429}{1.25} = 141143\,\text{N} \approx 141.1\,\text{kN}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Account for Prying Force

Prying factor $\beta$ from IS 800:2007:

The prying force formula (simplified):
$$Q = \dfrac{w}{2} \left[ t_p \sqrt{\dfrac{f_{yp}}{4.2 M_{ep}}} - 1 \right]$$

For initial design trial, assume $t_p = 16\,\text{mm}$ (matching flange thickness).

Check the prying ratio:
$$Q / T_b = \dfrac{0.5 \times t_p \times f_{yp} \times b_e - T_b (a' + b')}{T_b (a' + b')} \leq 0.5$$

Using the IS 800 limit-state formulation, if the prying is excessive, increase plate thickness. **Assume adequate prying for trial: $Q = 0.5 \times T_b$**

Total bolt force including prying:
$$T_{b,total} = T_b + Q = 1.5 \times T_b = 1.5 \times 252 = 378\,\text{kN}$$

This exceeds $T_{nb} = 141.1$ kN. **Bolt fails!** Need to upgrade bolt size.

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Re-Design with M24 Bolts (8.8 Grade)

$$A_{sb} = \dfrac{\pi \times 24^2}{4} = 452.39\,\text{mm}^2$$
$$A_{nb} = 0.78 \times 452.39 = 352.86\,\text{mm}^2$$
$$T_{nb} = 0.90 \times 800 \times 352.86 / 1.25 = 203257\,\text{N} \approx 203.3\,\text{kN}$$

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Prying Force Verification

With $T_b = 252/1 = 252\,\text{kN}$ (still using 1 bolt per row):

Try $t_p = 20\,\text{mm}$ plate. The plate flexural capacity:
$$M_{ep} = 1.2 \times f_{yp} \times Z_e / \gamma_{m0}$$

For a unit width plate, $Z_e = t_p^2 / 6$ (elastic):
$$M_{ep} = 1.2 \times 250 \times (20^2/6) / 1.10 = 1.2 \times 250 \times 66.67 / 1.10$$
$$M_{ep} = 20000 / 1.10 = 18182\,\text{N·mm/mm}$$

Using prying lever $\beta = 1.0$ (assumed):
$$Q = 0.5 \times T_b = 0.5 \times 252 = 126\,\text{kN}$$
$$T_{b,total} = 252 + 126 = 378\,\text{kN} > 203.3\,\text{kN}$$

Still fails. **Use 2 bolts per row (4 bolts total in 2 rows × 2 columns).**

New bolt force per bolt:
$$T_b = 252 / 2 = 126\,\text{kN (per bolt)}$$

$$T_{b,total} = 1.5 \times 126 = 189\,\text{kN} < 203.3\,\text{kN} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.2.7 Step 6 — Check Plate Thickness (No-Prying Condition)

The minimum plate thickness to eliminate prying:
$$t_{p,min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.2 \, M_{ep,req}}{f_{yp}}}$$
where $M_{ep,req} = T_b \times b'$, with $b' = 35\,\text{mm}$ (assumed lever arm):
$$M_{ep,req} = 126 \times 10^3 \times 35 = 4.41 \times 10^6\,\text{N·mm}$$
$$t_{p,min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.2 \times 4.41 \times 10^6}{250}} = \sqrt{74088} = 272\,\text{mm}$$

This is impractical. **Hence prying is unavoidable in this case.** Acceptable with $t_p = 20\,\text{mm}$ and acknowledging $Q$.

### 3.2.8 Step 7 — Weld Design

Weld between beam flange and end plate:
$$F_{weld} = T_{b,total} \times n_{bolts\ in\ row} = 189 \times 2 = 378\,\text{kN}$$

Weld length (per flange): $L_w = 2 (b_f - 2s) = 2(140 - 20) = 240\,\text{mm}$ (2 fillet welds of length 140 - 10 mm).

Required throat thickness:
$$t_t = \dfrac{F_{weld}}{0.7 \times L_w \times f_{wd}}$$

For grade E41 electrode, $f_{wd} = 410/\sqrt{3}/1.25 = 189.4\,\text{N/mm}^2$:
$$t_t = \dfrac{378000}{0.7 \times 240 \times 189.4} = \dfrac{378000}{31820} = 11.88\,\text{mm}$$

Use **12 mm fillet weld** ✓ (leg size $s = 17$ mm nominal).

## 3.3 Summary of Key Numerical Results

| Design Parameter | Web Angle Connection | End Plate Connection |
|------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Bolt grade | 4.6 | 8.8 |
| Bolt size | M20 | M24 |
| Number of bolts | 6 per cleat | 4 (2×2) |
| Plate thickness | 8 mm | 20 mm |
| Weld size | N/A (bolted only) | 12 mm |
| Governing mode | Shear of bolt | Tension + Prying |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between web angle connection and end plate connection with respect to the type of forces transferred and field fabrication."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Web Angle Connection | End Plate Connection |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Primary force** | Shear only (flexible) | Shear + Moment (semi-rigid) |
| **Type per IS 800** | Type 2 flexible | Type 1 / Type 2 based on detailing |
| **Field work** | Bolting only | Bolting + (optional) welding |
| **Moment capacity** | Negligible (~10% Mp) | 20–100% Mp depending on stiffening |

Both use bearing-type bolts typically. Web angle uses two angles (cleats); end plate uses a single plate welded to the beam.

**Distribution of marks:** Definition (1M), Comparison Table (1.5M), Practical example (0.5M).

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"Define prying force in bolted end plate connections. List two factors that affect its magnitude."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Prying force** is the additional tensile force induced in bolts of an end plate (or T-stub) connection due to the flexural deformation of the flange/plate, which produces a contact force at the plate tip acting as a lever amplifying the bolt tension.

**Two factors affecting prying force:**
1. **End plate thickness** $t_p$ — thinner plate → higher prying (lower flexural rigidity)
2. **Lever arm geometry** — distance between bolt center and plate tip ($b'$) — longer $b'$ → higher $Q$

(Other valid factors: bolt stiffness, flange thickness of supporting member, edge distance, bolt pretension.)

**Distribution of marks:** Definition (1.5M), Two factors with brief explanation (1.5M).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Web Angle Design**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Using **double shear** capacity of bolts on the cleat-to-beam web side — this side has only **single shear**! (1–2 marks lost)
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to check **net section rupture** in the angle cleat leg after deducting bolt holes (2–3 marks lost)
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Using $f_u$ of **plate** instead of **bolt** for shear capacity formula — units must match! (1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not checking **bearing on beam web** (since $t_w$ governs in many cases) — the minimum governs.

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]**

**Q. A (a) [7 Marks]:** A secondary beam **ISMB 300** transfers a factored reaction of **300 kN** to the flange of a column **ISHB 300** using a **double angle web cleat connection**. Design the connection using **M20 bolts of grade 4.6** and Fe 410 angles. Provide bolt layout, cleat size, and spacing.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Bolt data**
- $d = 20$ mm, $d_0 = 22$ mm, $f_{ub} = 400$ N/mm²
- $A_{nb} = 0.78 \times \pi (20)^2/4 = 245.04$ mm²
- $A_{sb} = 314.16$ mm²

**Step 2: Single shear capacity of bolt**
$$V_{nsb} = \dfrac{A_{nb} \cdot f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mb}} = \dfrac{245.04 \times 400}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 45.27\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 3: Bearing capacity**
- Assume pitch $p = 50$ mm, edge distance $e = 35$ mm
- $k_b = \min(e/3d_0, p/3d_0 - 0.25, f_{ub}/f_u, 1.0) = \min(0.530, 0.508, 0.976, 1.0) = 0.508$
- Plate (cleat) thickness $t = 10$ mm
- $V_{npb} = 2.5 \times 0.508 \times 20 \times 10 \times 410/1.25 = 83.3$ kN
- Beam web: $t_w = 7.5$ mm (governs) → $V_{npb,web} = 2.5 \times 0.508 \times 20 \times 7.5 \times 410/1.25 = 62.5$ kN

**Step 4: Design strength of bolt**
$$V_{db} = \min(45.27, 62.5) = 45.27\,\text{kN (shear governs)}$$

**Step 5: Number of bolts per cleat**
$$n = \dfrac{300}{45.27} = 6.63 \rightarrow \text{Use } n = 8 \text{ bolts (2 rows × 4 columns)}$$

**Step 6: Cleat selection — ISA 90 × 90 × 10 mm**
- $L_{cleat} = 2 \times 50 + 2 \times 35 = 170$ mm, use 175 mm
- Bolt layout: 2 rows × 4 columns, pitch = 50 mm
- Depth of cleat leg = 90 mm (must accommodate 4 bolts on 50 mm pitch? Need 175 mm) → use **ISA 100 × 100 × 10 mm** cleats

**Step 7: Check cleat in shear**
- $A_{vg} = 2 \times (175 - 2.5 \times 22) \times 10 = 2 \times 120 \times 10 = 2400$ mm²
- $V_d = 2400 \times 250/(\sqrt{3} \times 1.10) = 314.9$ kN $> 300$ kN ✓

**Step 8: Check edge distance & pitch** — all satisfy IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.2. ✓

**Final Design:** 2 × ISA 100 × 100 × 10 mm cleats, 8 M20-4.6 bolts per cleat, pitch = 50 mm, edge = 35 mm.

**Distribution:** Bolt capacity (2M), Number (1M), Cleat selection (2M), Spacing & detailing (2M)

---

**Q. A (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the different failure modes of a **bolted web angle connection** with neat sketches. Which mode is the most common in single-angle connections and why?

**Model Answer:**

**Failure modes in bolted web angle connections:**

1. **Shear failure of bolts** — Bolt shank shears at the shear plane; critical in single shear or thin plates.
2. **Bearing failure of plate/bolt** — Hole elongates; plate thinned by bolt crushing.
3. **Tearing of plate between hole and edge** — End edge too close.
4. **Tearing of plate along the net section** — Pitch too large, insufficient net area.
5. **Block shear failure** — Combination of shear yield on one path and tension rupture on perpendicular path (IS 800:2007 Cl. 6.4.1).
6. **Shear yielding of angle cleat** — Angle too short in shear length.

**Most common in single-angle connections:** **Bearing failure on the angle leg** (since the angle is thin and the connection is eccentric, prying-like effects increase bearing stress).

**Reasoning:**
- Single angles have inherent **eccentric loading** (load not through shear plane center) → moment amplifies bearing stress.
- The angle leg is typically thinner than the gusset or beam web → bearing governs.
- Absence of redundancy (only one angle) means each bolt must take full design load.

**Distribution:** List 6 modes (3M), Most common identification (1.5M), Reasoning with sketch (2.5M)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]**

**Q. B (a) [7 Marks]:** A simply supported beam **ISMB 450** carries a uniformly distributed load. It is connected to a column **ISHB 300** using a **flexible end plate (partial depth) connection**. The factored end moment is **120 kNm** and factored shear is **90 kN**. Design the end plate and bolt layout using **M20 bolts of grade 8.8**. Steel grade: **Fe 410**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Bolt layout & force distribution**
- Beam depth $h = 450$ mm, flange width $b_f = 150$ mm, $t_f = 17.4$ mm
- Use end plate 200 mm deep (partial), 200 mm wide
- Bolt rows: 2 rows, top at $h_1 = 380$ mm, bottom at $h_2 = 70$ mm from center
- Bolts per row: 2 (1 on each side, 50 mm apart laterally)

**Step 2: Bolt tension demand**
$$T_{top} = \dfrac{M \cdot h_1}{h_1^2 + h_2^2} = \dfrac{120 \times 10^6 \times 380}{380^2 + 70^2}$$
$$T_{top} = \dfrac{45.6 \times 10^9}{144400 + 4900} = \dfrac{45.6 \times 10^9}{149300} = 305.4\,\text{kN}$$

Per bolt (2 bolts per row): $T_b = 305.4/2 = 152.7$ kN

**Step 3: Bolt tension capacity (M20-8.8)**
$$A_{nb} = 0.78 \times \pi (20)^2/4 = 245.04\,\text{mm}^2$$
$$T_{nb} = 0.90 \times 800 \times 245.04 / 1.25 = 141.1\,\text{kN}$$

Since $T_b = 152.7$ kN $> T_{nb} = 141.1$ kN, **bolts are inadequate even before prying!**

**Step 4: Upgrade to M22-8.8 bolts**
$$A_{nb} = 0.78 \times \pi (22)^2/4 = 296.5\,\text{mm}^2$$
$$T_{nb} = 0.90 \times 800 \times 296.5 / 1.25 = 170.8\,\text{kN} > 152.7\,\text{kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Prying force**
- Use end plate thickness $t_p = 16$ mm
- $Q = 0.5 \times T_b = 0.5 \times 152.7 = 76.4$ kN
- $T_{b,total} = 152.7 + 76.4 = 229.1$ kN $> T_{nb} = 170.8$ kN

**Step 6: Increase plate to $t_p = 20$ mm or add stiffener**
With $t_p = 20$ mm and stiffener, prying reduces to $Q \approx 0.2 T_b = 30.5$ kN.
$$T_{b,total} = 152.7 + 30.5 = 183.2\,\text{kN} > 170.8\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 7: Use 3 bolts per row**
$$T_b = 305.4/3 = 101.8\,\text{kN per bolt}$$
$$T_{b,total} = 1.5 \times 101.8 = 152.7\,\text{kN} < 170.8\,\text{kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Design:** End plate 200 × 200 × 20 mm, 6 × M22-8.8 bolts (2 rows × 3 columns), pitch = 60 mm, edge = 40 mm, Fillet weld = 10 mm.

**Distribution:** Force distribution (2M), Bolt selection (1.5M), Prying check (1.5M), Plate sizing (1M), Weld (1M)

---

**Q. B (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **classification of beam-to-column connections** as per IS 800:2007 (Type 1, Type 2, Type 3) with suitable examples. How does this classification affect the choice between web angle and end plate connections?

**Model Answer:**

**IS 800:2007 Connection Classification (Cl. 12):**

| Type | Name | Behavior | Examples |
|------|------|----------|----------|
| **Type 1** | Rigid (Framed) | Original angle between members remains unchanged | Welded moment connection, Full-depth end plate with stiffeners |
| **Type 2** | Semi-Rigid (Flexible) | Carries some moment, allows rotation | Web angle connection, Flexible (partial depth) end plate |
| **Type 3** | Simple (Flexible) | Assumed to rotate freely, transfers only shear | Double web cleat (un-stiffened seat) with no top angle |

**Effect on design choice:**

- **Web angle connections** are classified as **Type 2 or Type 3** depending on detailing:
  - With **both top AND bottom cleats** + web angles → **Type 2 (Semi-rigid)** — can transfer 5–20% of full moment.
  - With **only web angles (no top/bottom seat)** → **Type 3 (Simple)** — pure shear transfer; used when beam is laterally supported by slab.

- **End plate connections** can be **Type 1, 2, or 3**:
  - **Flexible (partial depth)** → Type 3 or low Type 2
  - **Full-depth unstiffened** → Mid Type 2
  - **Full-depth stiffened (4-bolt extended)** → Type 1 (Rigid)
  - Used when **moment continuity is required** (e.g., portal frames, multi-storey moment frames).

**Practical selection criteria:**
- Building frame with simple bracing → Web angle (Type 3) is economical
- Portal frame with no bracing → End plate (Type 1) is mandatory
- Composite construction → Web angle sufficient as slab provides lateral restraint

**Distribution:** Three types explained (3M), Examples for each (1.5M), Selection rationale (2.5M)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — End Plate Design**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Ignoring prying force entirely — student assumes bolts only carry moment-induced tension. Prying can **double the bolt demand** in extreme cases. (2–3 marks lost)
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using **shank area $A_{sb}$** instead of **net tensile area $A_{nb}$** for tension capacity — incorrect, IS 800:2007 explicitly uses $A_{nb}$ for 0.9 $f_{ub}$ check. (1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to check **weld capacity** between end plate and beam flange — many designs pass the bolt check but fail at the weld. (2 marks)
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not specifying **bolt edge distance** from beam flange (typically 1.5–2 × $d_0$ from flange tip).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Points to Remember for KTU Exam (Module 2)**

### A. Web Angle Connection Essentials
- [x] Two equal-leg angles bolted back-to-back on either side of supported beam web
- [x] **Bolts on supported beam side: single shear** (NOT double!)
- [x] Design strength = min of **bolt shear** and **bolt bearing** capacity
- [x] **Bearing on beam web often governs** (since $t_w < t_{cleat}$)
- [x] Always check **net section rupture** in angle leg
- [x] Classified as **Type 2 (semi-rigid)** per IS 800:2007
- [x] Edge distance ≥ 1.5 $d_0$; Pitch ≥ 2.5 $d$

### B. End Plate Connection Essentials
- [x] Plate welded to beam end, bolted to column
- [x] Types: **Flexible (partial)**, **Full-depth unstiffened**, **Full-depth stiffened**
- [x] **Prying force $Q$** is the critical additional bolt tension
- [x] Prying upper limit: $Q \leq 0.5 T_b$ (IS 800:2007)
- [x] **Plate thickness $t_p \geq 16$ mm** typically required for rigid behavior
- [x] Bolt tension check uses **net tensile area** $A_{nb}$
- [x] Stiffener plates reduce prying by shortening lever arm

### C. Key Design Constants
- [x] $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ (bolt material factor)
- [x] $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (plate material factor)
- [x] $f_{ub} = 400$ (4.6), 800 (8.8), 1040 (10.9) N/mm²
- [x] Bearing factor $k_b = \min(e/3d_0, p/3d_0 - 0.25, f_{ub}/f_u, 1.0)$

### D. Common Numerical Values (KTU Frequently Asked)
- [x] $V_{dsb,4.6,M20} \approx 45.27$ kN (single shear)
- [x] $T_{nb,8.8,M20} \approx 141.1$ kN
- [x] $T_{nb,8.8,M24} \approx 203.3$ kN
- [x] $V_{dsb,8.8,M20} \approx 90.5$ kN (single shear)

### E. Failure Modes (Memorize 6 for Web Angles)
- [x] Bolt shear
- [x] Bolt bearing (on plate OR on bolt — minimum governs)
- [x] Plate edge tearing
- [x] Net section rupture
- [x] Block shear
- [x] Plate crushing/bearing

### F. End Plate Special Considerations
- [x] Always design for **combined shear + tension** in bolts
- [x] Check **weld** between plate and beam flange (grade E41/E43)
- [x] For **partial depth flexible end plate** — moment capacity ≈ 20% of $M_p$
- [x] **No stiffener** → Type 2; **With stiffener** → Type 1 (Rigid)
- [x] Use IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.4.7 for prying force calculation

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
