<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tubular Connection** (also called a **Hollow Section Connection** or **CHS/RHS Joint**) is a structural joint formed between two or more hollow steel sections — typically Circular Hollow Sections (CHS), Rectangular Hollow Sections (RHS), or Square Hollow Sections (SHS) — where a smaller **branch member** is welded directly to a larger **chord member** without the use of gusset plates, stiffeners, or cleats.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, tubular connections are classified based on their **load-transfer mechanism** and **geometric configuration** into:

- **Planar connections** (lying in a single plane): T, Y, X, K, N, KT joints
- **Multi-planar connections** (spatially distributed): TT, XX, KK joints

The principal reference standard for design in the Indian context is **IS 800:2007 (Section 10 – Connections in Tubular Members)**, supplemented by **CIDECT Design Guide No. 1** and **Eurocode EN 1993-1-8**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PECET864 (Advanced Design of Steel Structures) places heavy emphasis on the **limit-state design philosophy** for tubular connections, where each joint is checked against multiple **failure modes** rather than a single allowable stress. Examiners expect students to identify the **governing failure mode** for a given joint geometry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bamboo-Cross Bamboo Joint"**
>
> Imagine you are building a **bamboo trellis** for a creeper plant. You take a thick horizontal bamboo (the **chord**) and lash a thinner diagonal bamboo (the **branch**) directly to it. The joint fails in one of a few distinct ways:
> 1. The horizontal bamboo **crushes and dents** where the load is applied (this is *chord plastification*).
> 2. The lash **cuts through** the wall of the horizontal bamboo like a punch (*punching shear*).
> 3. The thinner diagonal bamboo itself **splits or buckles** (*branch failure*).
> 4. The horizontal bamboo **shears vertically** in half (*chord shear*).
>
> A tubular steel connection behaves in **exactly the same way** — the design codes simply provide mathematical expressions predicting each of these failure modes, and the engineer selects the **lowest capacity** (the "weakest link") as the governing strength.

### 1.3 Why Tubular Connections?

| Property | Tubular Sections | Open Sections (I/H) |
|---|---|---|
| Strength-to-weight ratio | **Excellent** | Moderate |
| Torsional rigidity | Very high (closed section) | Low (open section) |
| Wind / fluid drag | **Low** (no flat surfaces) | High |
| Aesthetic appeal | Modern, sleek | Industrial |
| Corrosion surface area | Low (no internal ledges) | High (flange-web junction traps moisture) |
| Joint complexity | **High** (multi-mode checks) | Low (simple gusset plates) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Intuition:** Because the tube is a closed shell, loads from the branch must "flow" **through the chord wall** to reach the supports. This is fundamentally different from an I-section, where the load travels through the flanges and web. The chord wall acts like a **pressure vessel under localized load**, and this is the source of all the design complexities.

### 1.4 Geometric Parameters (Universal Notation)

These dimensionless ratios govern **every** strength equation in tubular connection design:

- $\beta = \dfrac{d_1}{d_0}$ → branch-to-chord diameter ratio
- $\gamma = \dfrac{d_0}{2 t_0}$ → chord radius-to-thickness ratio (slenderness of chord wall)
- $\tau = \dfrac{t_1}{t_0}$ → branch-to-chord thickness ratio
- $\theta$ → angle between branch axis and chord axis
- $\alpha = \dfrac{2 L}{d_0}$ → chord length parameter (for effective length checks)

Where subscript **0** refers to the chord and subscript **1** to the branch.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Tubular Joints

**Based on Geometry:**

| Joint Type | Configuration | Typical Loading |
|---|---|---|
| **T Joint** | Single branch at 90° in one plane | Axial branch load |
| **Y Joint** | Single branch at oblique angle | Axial branch load |
| **X Joint** (Cross) | Two branches in same plane, opposite sides | Equal & opposite axial |
| **K Joint** | Two branches on same side, balanced | Tension + compression branch pair |
| **N Joint** | Similar to K but with eccentricity | Branch pair with eccentricity |
| **KT Joint** | Three branches on same chord face | Multi-branch load transfer |

### 2.2 Failure Modes (The Seven Pillars)

IS 800:2007 §10.4 and CIDECT identify the following failure modes. The designer **must** check each one and design for the minimum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Factoid:** A 14-mark KTU question often asks *"Identify and explain the failure modes of a tubular T-joint under axial branch loading."* Memorize the seven modes below.

1. **Chord Plastification (Mode A)** — The chord wall yields and forms a plastic mechanism under the branch load. Dominant for low $\beta$ (≤ 0.6) and high $\gamma$.
2. **Punching Shear of Chord Wall (Mode B)** — The branch "punches a hole" through the chord wall. Dominant for small $\beta$ and high $\tau$.
3. **Branch Failure (Mode C)** — The branch member itself fails by yielding, crippling, or local buckling near the intersection.
4. **Chord Shear (Mode D)** — The chord cross-section shears across two parallel planes through the branch footprint. Important for thin chords.
5. **Local Buckling of Chord Side Wall (Mode E)** — The chord side walls buckle outward under compression from the branch.
6. **Bracing Failure (Mode F)** — Local buckling of the branch compression face.
7. **Unzipping / Lamellar Tearing (Mode G)** — Weld pulls away from chord along a tear line; rare in modern practice.

### 2.3 Validity Limits (IS 800:2007 Table 11)

For the design equations to be valid, the joint geometry must satisfy:

$$
0.2 \le \beta \le 1.0
$$
$$
\gamma \le 50 \quad \text{(for CHS),} \quad \gamma \le 40 \quad \text{(for RHS)}
$$
$$
0.2 \le \tau \le 1.0
$$
$$
30° \le \theta \le 90°
$$

If any parameter falls outside these limits, the joint is **outside the scope of the standard formulae** and must be designed by FEA or specialized references.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are per IS 800:2007 §10.4.5 for CHS T, Y, X joints under axial branch loading.** Memorize the form; numerical factors are board-exam favourites.

| # | Failure Mode | Design Strength $N_{Rd}$ (or applicable function) | Governing Geometric Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chord Plastification | $N_1^* = \dfrac{5.0 \, f_{y0} \, t_0^2}{(1 - 0.81\beta) \sin\theta} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{\sin\theta} \cdot k_p$ | $0.2 \le \beta \le 1.0$ |
| 2 | Punching Shear | $N_1^* = \dfrac{f_{y0}}{\sqrt{3}} \, t_0 \, \pi \, d_1 \cdot \dfrac{1+\sin\theta}{2\sin^2\theta}$ | $\beta \le 0.6$ (approx.) |
| 3 | Branch Yielding | $N_1 = f_{y1} \cdot t_1 \cdot (2 h_1 - 4 t_1 + 2 b_e)$ (for RHS) | All |
| 4 | Chord Shear | $V_{chord} = 0.6 \, f_{y0} \cdot A_0 / \gamma_{m0}$ (effective $A_0$ reduction) | All |
| 5 | Side-wall Buckling | $N_1^* = f_{y0} \cdot t_0 \cdot (2 h_0 + 2 b_0 - 8 t_0)$ | $\beta = 1.0$, RHS only |
| 6 | Effective Width | $b_e = \dfrac{10}{b_0/t_0} \cdot \dfrac{f_{y0} t_0}{f_{y1} t_1} \cdot b_1 \le b_1$ | RHS joints |

**Where:**
- $f_{y0}, f_{y1}$ = yield strength of chord and branch
- $t_0, t_1$ = wall thickness of chord and branch
- $d_0, d_1$ = diameter of chord and branch (CHS)
- $h_0, b_0$ = depth and width of chord (RHS)
- $h_1, b_1$ = depth and width of branch (RHS)
- $k_p$ = plasticity function $\approx 1.0$ for typical joints
- $\gamma_{m0}$ = partial safety factor for material = **1.10**

### 2.5 The Function $f(\beta, \gamma)$ — The Heart of the Design

For **chord plastification** in CHS T, Y, X joints, the characteristic strength is:

$$
N_{1,Rd} = \dfrac{C_k \, f_{y0} \, t_0^2}{\sin\theta} \cdot \left( \dfrac{2 \beta}{\sin\theta} \right)^{0.5} \cdot f(\beta, \gamma)
$$

where $C_k$ is a constant (typically 5.0 for X joints, 5.5–5.7 for T/Y joints) and the function $f(\beta, \gamma)$ captures the plastic mechanism geometry.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore Platforms** (oil & gas) — every brace-to-leg joint is a tubular connection
- **Transmission Towers** — leg-to-bracing connections
- **Airport Terminals / Stadiums** — long-span roof trusses using CHS chords
- **Pedestrian Bridges** — arch and truss systems
- **Crane Jibs** — lattice boom connections
- **Architectural Exposed Structures** — glass-roof support grids

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Chord Plastification Resistance for CHS T-Joint

**Starting Point (Ring Model Assumption):**

The chord wall is treated as a ring of radius $r_0 = d_0/2$ and thickness $t_0$, loaded by a line load $P$ from the branch acting over a contact length $b \approx d_1 \sin\theta$ (the projected width of the branch footprint).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Setup:** A branch pushes the chord wall outward (or pulls it inward). The chord wall resists by bending like a curved beam. Yield-line analysis gives the plastic collapse load.

**Step 1 — Plastic Hinge Length:**

The chord wall develops plastic hinges on either side of the branch footprint. The characteristic length over which the hinge line forms is:

$$
L_p = 2 r_0 \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{d_1}{2 r_0}\right) \approx d_1 \quad \text{(for small } d_1/d_0\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Plastic Moment Capacity of Chord Wall per Unit Length:**

The chord wall, treated as a strip of width 1 mm and thickness $t_0$, has plastic moment:

$$
m_p = \dfrac{f_{y0} \, t_0^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Equating External Work to Internal Work (Upper Bound Theorem):**

The branch load $N_1 \sin\theta$ (component perpendicular to chord) does work as the chord wall deflects by $\delta$. The internal work is dissipated along the four plastic hinge lines of length $L_p$ rotating by angle $\theta_p$:

$$
N_1 \sin\theta \cdot \delta = 4 \cdot m_p \cdot L_p \cdot \theta_p
$$

Since $\theta_p = \delta / r_0$ (rotation of the chord wall):

$$
N_1 \sin\theta \cdot \delta = 4 \cdot \dfrac{f_{y0} t_0^2}{4} \cdot L_p \cdot \dfrac{\delta}{r_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N_1$:**

$$
N_1 = \dfrac{f_{y0} \, t_0^2 \, L_p}{r_0 \sin\theta}
$$

Substituting $L_p \approx d_1 = \beta d_0 = 2\beta r_0$:

$$
N_1 = \dfrac{f_{y0} \, t_0^2 \cdot 2\beta r_0}{r_0 \sin\theta} = \dfrac{2\beta \, f_{y0} \, t_0^2}{\sin\theta}
$$

**Step 5 — Empirical Calibration (IS 800 / CIDECT):**

Experimental tests on real joints show that the ring model **underestimates** capacity (it ignores membrane action and strain hardening). The empirical correction introduces a constant $C_k$ and an exponent on $\beta$:

$$
N_1^* = \dfrac{C_k \, f_{y0} \, t_0^2}{\sin\theta} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\beta}{\sin\theta}\right)^{\alpha}
$$

With $C_k = 5.0$ for X joints, $C_k = 5.5$ for T/Y joints, and $\alpha \approx 0.5$ for T/Y joints, $\alpha = 0$ for X joints.

> [!TIP]
> **The Factor $(1 - 0.81\beta)$:** As $\beta \to 1.0$ (branch diameter equals chord diameter), the geometric correction factor $(1 - 0.81\beta)$ in the denominator goes to $(1 - 0.81) = 0.19$, dramatically **increasing** the capacity because the load spreads over a larger area. As $\beta \to 0.2$, the factor becomes 0.838, **reducing** capacity.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style 14-Mark Numerical Problem Framework:** A complete design problem is solved by the following 8-step sequence. This is the **exact pattern** KTU examiners reward.

**Problem Statement (Representative):**
*Design a CHS T-joint: Chord $d_0 = 219.1$ mm, $t_0 = 8$ mm; Branch $d_1 = 114.3$ mm, $t_1 = 5$ mm; $f_{y0} = 250$ MPa, $f_{y1} = 350$ MPa; $\theta = 90°$; $f_{u0} = 410$ MPa. Find axial branch capacity.*

**Step 1 — Compute Geometric Parameters:**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{d_1}{d_0} = \dfrac{114.3}{219.1} = 0.522
$$

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{d_0}{2 t_0} = \dfrac{219.1}{2 \times 8} = 13.69
$$

$$
\tau = \dfrac{t_1}{t_0} = \dfrac{5}{8} = 0.625
$$

**Step 2 — Verify Validity Limits:**

$0.2 \le 0.522 \le 1.0$ ✓ ; $13.69 \le 50$ ✓ ; $0.2 \le 0.625 \le 1.0$ ✓ ; $\theta = 90°$ ✓

**Step 3 — Chord Plastification Capacity:**

$$
N_1^* = \dfrac{5.0 \cdot f_{y0} \cdot t_0^2}{(1 - 0.81\beta) \cdot \sin\theta} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{\sin\theta} \quad \text{[T/Y joint form]}
$$

Substituting:

$$
N_1^* = \dfrac{5.0 \times 250 \times 8^2}{(1 - 0.81 \times 0.522) \times 1} \times \dfrac{0.522}{1}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{5.0 \times 250 \times 64}{0.577} \times 0.522
$$

$$
= \dfrac{80000}{0.577} \times 0.522 = 138648 \times 0.522 = 72374 \text{ N} \approx 72.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Punching Shear Capacity:**

For $\beta \le 0.6$, applicable:

$$
N_1^* = \dfrac{f_{y0}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot t_0 \cdot \pi \cdot d_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 + \sin\theta}{2 \sin^2\theta}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{250}{\sqrt{3}} \times 8 \times \pi \times 114.3 \times \dfrac{1 + 1}{2 \times 1}
$$

$$
= 144.34 \times 8 \times 3.1416 \times 114.3 \times 1
$$

$$
= 144.34 \times 2879.5 = 415{,}604 \text{ N} \approx 415.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Branch Yielding:**

$$
N_1 = f_{y1} \cdot A_1 = 350 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} (114.3^2 - 104.3^2)
$$

$$
= 350 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} (13064.5 - 10876.5) = 350 \times 0.7854 \times 2188
$$

$$
= 350 \times 1718.4 = 601{,}440 \text{ N} \approx 601.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Chord Shear Capacity (effective shear area):**

Effective chord shear length $L_v = 2 d_1 = 2 \times 114.3 = 228.6$ mm; $A_v = 2 L_v \cdot t_0 = 2 \times 228.6 \times 8 = 3657.6$ mm²:

$$
V_{chord} = 0.6 \cdot f_{y0} \cdot A_v / \gamma_{m0} = 0.6 \times 250 \times 3657.6 / 1.10 = 498{,}727 \text{ N} \approx 498.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify Governing Mode:**

| Failure Mode | Capacity (kN) |
|---|---|
| Chord Plastification | **72.4** ← GOVERNING |
| Punching Shear | 415.6 |
| Branch Yielding | 601.4 |
| Chord Shear | 498.7 |

**Step 8 — Apply Partial Safety Factor and Report:**

$$
N_{Rd} = \dfrac{72.4}{1.10} = 65.8 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student error is to **omit the $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$** division at the end. KTU examiners typically deduct **1 mark** for this oversight. Also, students often report the largest capacity (e.g., 601 kN for branch yielding) instead of the smallest — this is a fundamental conceptual error reflecting misunderstanding of limit-state design.

### 3.3 K-Joint Special Provisions

For K-joints, the **branch forces must balance** within a tolerance of 10% (i.e., $N_1 \sin\theta_1 \approx N_2 \sin\theta_2$). If unbalanced, the joint is treated as a T or Y joint with an **equivalent axial load** of:

$$
N_1 = N_{1,compression} + 0.5 N_{2,tension} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sin\theta_2}{\sin\theta_1}\right)
$$

Additionally, an **eccentricity term** $e$ modifies the capacity. Eccentricity $e$ is measured positive when it lies **outside the chord** (towards the branch toes). IS 800 provides a reduction factor when $e > 0$ for certain configurations.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define a tubular connection. With the help of a neat sketch, list any **four** types of planar tubular connections.

**Model Answer:**

A **tubular connection** is a structural joint formed between hollow steel sections (CHS, RHS, or SHS) where a branch member is welded directly to a chord member without gusset plates.

Four types of planar tubular connections:

1. **T Joint** – Single branch perpendicular to chord.
2. **Y Joint** – Single branch at an oblique angle to chord.
3. **X Joint (Cross Joint)** – Two collinear branches on opposite sides of chord.
4. **K Joint** – Two branches on the same side of chord, balancing each other.

(Additional: N Joint, KT Joint may be mentioned.)

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for sketch label + 1 mark for the four types (0.25 each). 2 × 3-mark questions are mandatory in every KTU ESE paper.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What are the various **failure modes** of a CHS T-joint under axial branch loading? Mention the geometric parameters that govern them.

**Model Answer:**

The principal failure modes (per IS 800:2007 §10.4) are:

1. **Chord plastification** – chord wall yields and forms plastic mechanism.
2. **Punching shear of chord wall** – branch punches through chord.
3. **Branch failure** – branch itself yields or buckles.
4. **Chord shear** – chord cross-section shears at branch plane.
5. **Local buckling of chord side wall** – chord walls buckle outward.
6. **Bracing failure** – local buckling of branch compression face.
7. **Lamellar tearing / weld unzipping** – rare in modern practice.

**Governing geometric parameters:**
- $\beta = d_1/d_0$ (branch-to-chord diameter ratio)
- $\gamma = d_0/(2t_0)$ (chord slenderness)
- $\tau = t_1/t_0$ (thickness ratio)
- $\theta$ (angle between branch and chord axis)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Module Convention:** Module 2 contributes a 14-mark question in Section II (Module Choice). The student is offered two alternatives (Question A OR Question B). Both must be presented with complete solutions as the examiner does not have a "key" — only the student answers one. The notes below contain full worked solutions for both.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

A tubular T-joint is formed by welding a CHS branch to a CHS chord. The following data is given:

- Chord: $d_0 = 219.1$ mm, $t_0 = 8$ mm, $f_{y0} = 250$ MPa
- Branch: $d_1 = 168.3$ mm, $t_1 = 6.3$ mm, $f_{y1} = 350$ MPa
- Angle: $\theta = 90°$
- Partial safety factor: $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$

**(a)** Compute the geometric parameters and verify the joint falls within the **validity limits** of IS 800:2007. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **axial branch capacity** of the joint considering chord plastification, punching shear, branch yielding, and chord shear failure modes. Identify the governing mode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Geometric Parameters and Validity Check (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute $\beta$:**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{d_1}{d_0} = \dfrac{168.3}{219.1} = 0.768
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\gamma$:**

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{d_0}{2 t_0} = \dfrac{219.1}{2 \times 8} = 13.69
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\tau$:**

$$
\tau = \dfrac{t_1}{t_0} = \dfrac{6.3}{8} = 0.7875
$$

**Step 4 — Validity Check (IS 800:2007 Table 11):**

| Parameter | Computed | Required Range | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\beta$ | 0.768 | $0.2 \le \beta \le 1.0$ | ✓ OK |
| $\gamma$ | 13.69 | $\le 50$ | ✓ OK |
| $\tau$ | 0.7875 | $0.2 \le \tau \le 1.0$ | ✓ OK |
| $\theta$ | 90° | $30° \le \theta \le 90°$ | ✓ OK |

**Conclusion:** All parameters are within validity limits; the joint qualifies for the standard design equations.

---

**(b) Capacity Computations (7 marks)**

**Mode 1: Chord Plastification (IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.4.5)**

$$
N_1^* = \dfrac{5.0 \cdot f_{y0} \cdot t_0^2 \cdot \beta}{(1 - 0.81 \beta) \cdot \sin^2\theta}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{5.0 \times 250 \times 8^2 \times 0.768}{(1 - 0.81 \times 0.768) \times 1^2}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{5.0 \times 250 \times 64 \times 0.768}{(1 - 0.622) \times 1}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{61{,}440}{0.378} = 162{,}539 \text{ N} \approx 162.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Mode 2: Punching Shear (applicable since $\beta = 0.768 > 0.6$, not critical here, but check anyway):**

> Note: For CHS T-joints, punching shear is typically only critical when $\beta < 0.4$. Since $\beta = 0.768$, this mode is unlikely to govern.

$$
N_1^* = \dfrac{f_{y0}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot t_0 \cdot \pi \cdot d_1 \cdot \dfrac{1+\sin\theta}{2\sin^2\theta}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{250}{\sqrt{3}} \times 8 \times \pi \times 168.3 \times \dfrac{1+1}{2 \times 1}
$$

$$
= 144.34 \times 8 \times 3.1416 \times 168.3 \times 1
$$

$$
= 144.34 \times 4231.0 = 610{,}581 \text{ N} \approx 610.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Mode 3: Branch Yielding:**

Branch cross-sectional area:

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(d_1^2 - (d_1 - 2t_1)^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(168.3^2 - 155.7^2)
$$

$$
= 0.7854 \times (28{,}325 - 24{,}243) = 0.7854 \times 4082 = 3205.6 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
N_1 = f_{y1} \cdot A_1 = 350 \times 3205.6 = 1{,}121{,}960 \text{ N} \approx 1122.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Mode 4: Chord Shear:**

Effective chord shear area:

$$
A_v = 2 L_v \cdot t_0 = 2 \times 2 d_1 \times t_0 = 4 \times 168.3 \times 8 = 5385.6 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
V_{chord} = 0.6 \cdot f_{y0} \cdot A_v = 0.6 \times 250 \times 5385.6 = 807{,}840 \text{ N} \approx 807.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Governing Capacity (before $\gamma_m$):**

| Mode | Capacity (kN) |
|---|---|
| Chord Plastification | **162.5** ← GOVERNING |
| Punching Shear | 610.6 |
| Branch Yielding | 1122.0 |
| Chord Shear | 807.8 |

**Design Capacity:**

$$
N_{Rd} = \dfrac{162.5}{1.10} = 147.7 \text{ kN}
$$

> **The governing failure mode is chord plastification with a design capacity of 147.7 kN.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

An RHS K-joint is part of a roof truss. The chord and branch are square hollow sections:

- Chord: $b_0 = h_0 = 150$ mm, $t_0 = 8$ mm, $f_{y0} = 250$ MPa
- Branch (both branches identical): $b_1 = h_1 = 100$ mm, $t_1 = 5$ mm, $f_{y1} = 350$ MPa
- Branch angle: $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 45°$ (balanced K-joint)
- No eccentricity

**(a)** Verify the joint geometry falls within the validity limits of IS 800:2007 for RHS K-joints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the chord plastification capacity of the RHS K-joint using the effective width method. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Validity Check (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Geometric Parameters:**

For RHS, the relevant ratios are based on width:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{b_1}{b_0} = \dfrac{100}{150} = 0.667
$$

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{b_0}{2 t_0} = \dfrac{150}{2 \times 8} = 9.375
$$

$$
\tau = \dfrac{t_1}{t_0} = \dfrac{5}{8} = 0.625
$$

**Step 2 — Validity Check:**

| Parameter | Computed | Range | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\beta$ | 0.667 | $0.35 \le \beta \le 1.0$ (RHS K-joint) | ✓ |
| $\gamma$ | 9.375 | $\le 40$ (RHS) | ✓ |
| $\tau$ | 0.625 | $0.4 \le \tau \le 1.0$ (RHS) | ✓ |
| $\theta$ | 45° | $30° \le \theta \le 90°$ | ✓ |

**Conclusion:** Joint is within validity limits.

**Step 3 — K-Joint Force Balance Check:**

For a K-joint, the perpendicular components of branch forces must balance. With $N_1 = N_2$ and $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 45°$:

$$
N_1 \sin 45° = N_2 \sin 45° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Balanced ✓}
$$

---

**(b) Chord Plastification Capacity – Effective Width Method (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Effective Width $b_e$:**

$$
b_e = \dfrac{10}{b_0/t_0} \cdot \dfrac{f_{y0} t_0}{f_{y1} t_1} \cdot b_1
$$

$$
= \dfrac{10}{150/8} \cdot \dfrac{250 \times 8}{350 \times 5} \cdot 100
$$

$$
= \dfrac{10}{18.75} \cdot \dfrac{2000}{1750} \cdot 100
$$

$$
= 0.5333 \times 1.143 \times 100 = 60.95 \text{ mm}
$$

Check: $b_e \le b_1$ → $60.95 \le 100$ ✓

**Step 2 — Effective Area for Each Branch:**

$$
A_e = 2 h_1 \cdot t_1 + (b_e - 2 t_1) \cdot t_1
$$

Wait — for RHS K-joint, the IS 800 formulation uses the **branch force distributed over effective width**:

$$
A_{eff} = 2 h_1 t_1 + (b_e - 2 t_1) t_1 \quad \text{(per branch)}
$$

Actually, the standard form is:

$$
N_1^* = f_{y0} \cdot t_0 \cdot \left( 2 h_1 / \sin\theta_1 + b_e + b_{e,\text{opp}} \right) / \gamma_{m0}
$$

For balanced K-joint (both branches identical and symmetric):

$$
b_{e,1} = b_{e,2} = 60.95 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacity:**

$$
N_1^* = \dfrac{f_{y0} \cdot t_0}{\sin\theta_1} \left( 2 h_1 + b_{e,1} + b_{e,2} \right)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{250 \times 8}{\sin 45°} \left( 2 \times 100 + 60.95 + 60.95 \right)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{2000}{0.7071} \times (200 + 121.9) = 2828.4 \times 321.9
$$

$$
= 910{,}663 \text{ N} \approx 910.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Design Capacity:**

$$
N_{Rd} = \dfrac{910.7}{1.10} = 827.9 \text{ kN}
$$

> **The RHS K-joint has a chord plastification design capacity of approximately 828 kN per branch.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$** in the final step — deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing CHS and RHS formulas** — CHS uses $\beta = d_1/d_0$ and circle geometry; RHS uses $\beta = b_1/b_0$ and rectangular geometry. Mixing them up leads to gross numerical errors.
> 3. **Not verifying validity limits first** — KTU examiners expect this as a 1–2 mark sub-step. Skipping it loses easy marks.
> 4. **Reporting the largest capacity as the answer** — fundamentally wrong; the *minimum* governs.
> 5. **Using $\sin\theta$ without proper units** — angle must be in degrees converted to radians (or use $\sin 45° = 0.7071$ directly).
> 6. **Ignoring the $1 - 0.81\beta$ term** in the denominator — common transcription error.
> 7. **Punching shear formula requires the $(1 + \sin\theta)/(2\sin^2\theta)$ factor** — students often write only the leading term.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

**🔹 Fundamental Concepts**
- [ ] Tubular connection = branch welded directly to chord, no gusset plates.
- [ ] Design follows **limit-state philosophy** per IS 800:2007 §10.
- [ ] **Multi-mode design** — joint is checked against 4–7 failure modes; minimum governs.
- [ ] Partial safety factor $\gamma_{m0} = \mathbf{1.10}$ for steel.

**🔹 Geometric Parameters (Memorize)**
- [ ] $\beta = d_1/d_0$ (CHS) or $b_1/b_0$ (RHS)
- [ ] $\gamma = d_0/(2t_0)$ — chord slenderness; high $\gamma$ = thin chord
- [ ] $\tau = t_1/t_0$ — thickness ratio
- [ ] $\theta$ — branch-chord angle (30°–90°)

**🔹 Validity Limits (IS 800 Table 11)**
- [ ] CHS: $\beta \in [0.2, 1.0]$, $\gamma \le 50$, $\tau \in [0.2, 1.0]$
- [ ] RHS: $\beta \in [0.35, 1.0]$ (K-joint), $\gamma \le 40$, $\tau \in [0.4, 1.0]$

**🔹 Failure Modes to Remember**
- [ ] **Chord plastification** — dominant for low $\beta$, high $\gamma$
- [ ] **Punching shear** — dominant for very low $\beta$
- [ ] **Branch yielding** — usually not governing unless very thick branch
- [ ] **Chord shear** — important for short, thick chords
- [ ] **Side-wall buckling** — only RHS, when $\beta = 1.0$

**🔹 Key Formulas to Memorize**
- [ ] Chord plastification: $N_1^* = \dfrac{5 f_{y0} t_0^2 \beta}{(1 - 0.81\beta) \sin^2\theta}$ (T/Y CHS)
- [ ] Punching shear: $N_1^* = \dfrac{f_{y0}}{\sqrt 3} t_0 \pi d_1 \cdot \dfrac{1+\sin\theta}{2\sin^2\theta}$
- [ ] Branch yielding: $N_1 = f_{y1} A_1$
- [ ] Chord shear: $V = 0.6 f_{y0} A_v$ where $A_v = 4 d_1 t_0$
- [ ] Effective width RHS: $b_e = \dfrac{10}{b_0/t_0} \cdot \dfrac{f_{y0} t_0}{f_{y1} t_1} \cdot b_1 \le b_1$

**🔹 Numerical Traps**
- [ ] Always convert kN ↔ N: $1 \text{ kN} = 1000 \text{ N}$
- [ ] $\sin 90° = 1$, $\sin 45° = 0.7071$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$
- [ ] $1/\sqrt 3 = 0.5774$
- [ ] $\pi/4 = 0.7854$

**🔹 K-Joint Special Rules**
- [ ] Force balance: $N_1 \sin\theta_1 \approx N_2 \sin\theta_2$ (±10% tolerance)
- [ ] Eccentricity $e$ measured from branch intersection to chord axis
- [ ] Load eccentricity **increases** capacity when $e < 0$ (favourable) and **decreases** when $e > 0$ (unfavourable)

**🔹 Weld Design Hint (Quick Recall)**
- [ ] Fillet weld at tubular connection: throat thickness $\ne 0.7 \times$ weld leg
- [ ] Effective weld length = perimeter of branch intersection curve (elliptical for oblique)
- [ ] IS 800 §10.5 specifies minimum weld size based on thinner member thickness

**🔹 Last-Minute Exam Wisdom**
- [ ] Always **start** with geometric parameter computation and validity check.
- [ ] **List all four (or more) failure modes** before computing — examiners reward systematic approach.
- [ ] **Conclude with a summary table** comparing capacities — this is the easiest way to communicate the governing mode.
- [ ] **Divide by $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$** at the end — do not forget!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameters of an In-Plane Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parameters of an In-Plane Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In-Plane Joint (Major-Axis Beam-to-Column Moment Connection):** An **in-plane joint** in steel framed structures is a structural joint in which **all connecting members (beam and column) lie in a single common vertical plane**, and the joint is required to transfer an **axial force, a shear force, and a bending moment** from the beam to the column flange (major axis). The **parameters of an in-plane joint** are the complete set of **geometric, mechanical, and rotational descriptors** that uniquely define its load–deformation behaviour. As per the **Eurocode 3 (EN 1993-1-8) component method** — which is also the reference philosophy adopted by IS 800:2007 and IS 800:2023 — the three primary behavioural parameters that govern an in-plane joint are:
> 1. **Design Moment Resistance, $M_{j,Rd}$** (kN·m)
> 2. **Rotational Stiffness, $S_j$** (kN·m/rad)
> 3. **Design Rotation Capacity, $\phi_{Cd}$** (mrad)

A fourth *derivative* parameter is the **classification of the joint** — by **strength** (Full / Partial / Pinned) and by **stiffness** (Rigid / Semi-rigid / Pinned) — which is obtained directly from the three primary parameters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Joint as a 3-Spec Spring Hinge**
> Imagine the beam-to-column connection as the **hinge of a door** — but instead of being a perfectly rigid hinge or a perfectly loose pin, it is a **calibrated rotational spring** with three independent physical properties:
> - 🏋️ **Strength** — the heaviest door (moment) the hinge can hold before it breaks.
> - 🌀 **Stiffness** — how tightly the hinge resists being pushed open; a *stiff* hinge barely rotates under load, a *loose* hinge swings freely.
> - 🔄 **Ductility** — how far the hinge can rotate *plastically* before it fails.
> The **engineer** must therefore characterise the connection by measuring exactly these three "hinge properties." Together they form the **Moment–Rotation ($M$–$\phi$) signature curve** of the joint. In **PECET864**, designing an in-plane joint is the engineering act of *sizing the bolts, plate, welds, and column stiffeners* such that this signature curve satisfies the **strength, stiffness, and ductility demands** of the surrounding frame.

### 1.3 Governing Standard Metrics

The standard reference values used throughout this module are:

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus of Steel | $E$ | **200 000 MPa (2 × 10⁵ N/mm²)** |
| Yield Strength (typical S355) | $f_y$ | **355 MPa** |
| Ultimate Strength (typical S355) | $f_u$ | **490 – 630 MPa** |
| Partial Safety Factor (steel) | $\gamma_{M0}$ | **1.10** |
| Partial Safety Factor (bolts) | $\gamma_{M2}$ | **1.25** |
| Limiting Slenderness (Class 1 web) | $c/t \leq$ | **$72 \varepsilon$** |
| Reference Strain | $\varepsilon$ | $\sqrt{235/f_y}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Primary Parameters — Theory

#### (A) Design Moment Resistance, $M_{j,Rd}$
The moment resistance is governed by the **weakest component** in the load-transfer chain. Because each component has its own design resistance $F_{i,Rd}$, the maximum tension force that can be transmitted through the equivalent T-stub at the beam tension flange is:

$$F_{T,Rd} = \min \left\{ F_{T,1,Rd} \; ; \; F_{T,2,Rd} \; ; \; F_{T,3,Rd} \right\}$$

and the corresponding joint moment resistance becomes:

$$M_{j,Rd} = F_{T,Rd} \cdot z$$

where $z$ is the **lever arm**, taken as the distance between the mid-thickness of the beam compression flange and the mid-thickness of the beam tension flange.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "weakest component" governs the design:** The joint is a **series system** of components in the load path (bolt → plate → flange → web → column). Like links in a chain, the failure of any single link collapses the joint. The component method therefore requires every individual component to be checked, and the *minimum* capacity dictates $M_{j,Rd}$.

#### (B) Rotational Stiffness, $S_j$
For an in-plane joint, the **initial rotational stiffness** is obtained by assembling the stiffness coefficients $k_i$ of all the *tension, compression, and shear* components acting in series:

$$\boxed{\, S_{j,ini} \;=\; \dfrac{E \, z^{2}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i} \dfrac{1}{k_{i}}} \,}$$

where each $k_i$ represents the stiffness of one component per unit elongation (or shear).

#### (C) Design Rotation Capacity, $\phi_{Cd}$
The rotation capacity is the **maximum inelastic rotation** the joint can sustain before its moment resistance drops below the design value. For ductility-based design (seismic / plastic design):

$$\phi_{Cd} \;=\; \phi_{Cd}^{bolt} \;+\; \phi_{Cd}^{plate} \;+\; \phi_{Cd}^{flange}$$

For most practical extended end-plate joints, $\phi_{Cd}$ lies in the range **25 – 60 mrad** for ductile failure modes.

### 2.2 Joint Classification Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification by Strength** (ratio $\mu = M_{j,Rd}/M_{b,pl,Rd}$):
> - **Full Strength:** $\mu \geq 1.0$
> - **Partial Strength:** $0.25 < \mu < 1.0$
> - **Pinned:** $\mu \leq 0.25$

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification by Stiffness** (ratio $\lambda = S_{j,ini} L_b / E I_b$):
> - **Rigid (Braced frame):** $\lambda \geq 25$
> - **Rigid (Unbraced frame):** $\lambda \geq 8$
> - **Pinned:** $\lambda \leq 0.5$
> - **Semi-rigid:** $0.5 < \lambda < 25$ (braced)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Key Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tension in T-stub | $F_t = M/z$ | $M$ = applied moment, $z$ = lever arm | N |
| 2 | Plastic Moment of T-stub flange | $M_{pl,1,Rd} = \dfrac{0.25 \, \ell_{eff} \, t_f^{2} \, f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}$ | $\ell_{eff}$ = effective length, $t_f$ = flange thickness | N·mm |
| 3 | T-stub Mode 1 resistance (full flange yielding) | $F_{T,1,Rd} = \dfrac{4 \, M_{pl,1,Rd}}{m}$ | $m$ = bolt-to-flange toe distance | N |
| 4 | T-stub Mode 2 resistance (mixed) | $F_{T,2,Rd} = \dfrac{2 \, M_{pl,1,Rd} + n \, \Sigma F_{t,Rd}}{m + n}$ | $n$ = bolt-to-rib distance, $\Sigma F_{t,Rd}$ = bolt tension capacity | N |
| 5 | T-stub Mode 3 resistance (bolt failure) | $F_{T,3,Rd} = \Sigma F_{t,Rd}$ | Bolt resistance sum | N |
| 6 | Design Moment Resistance | $M_{j,Rd} = \min(F_{T,Rd}) \cdot z$ | Weakest T-stub force | N·mm |
| 7 | Initial Rotational Stiffness | $S_{j,ini} = \dfrac{E \, z^{2}}{\Sigma(1/k_i)}$ | Sum over active components | kN·m/rad |
| 8 | Bolt Tension Capacity (grade 8.8) | $F_{t,Rd} = \dfrac{k_2 \, f_{ub} \, A_s}{\gamma_{M2}}$ | $A_s$ = tensile stress area, $k_2 = 0.9$ for 8.8 | N |
| 9 | Column Web Shear | $V_{wp,Rd} = \dfrac{0.9 \, f_{y,wc} \, A_{vc}}{\gamma_{M0}}$ | $A_{vc}$ = shear area of web | N |
| 10 | Effective Length (non-circular pattern) | $\ell_{eff,1} = \alpha \, m$ | $\alpha$ from EC3 Table 6.4 | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This parameterisation framework is used in:
> - **Seismic-resistant design** (capacity-based design of moment frames).
> - **Sway vs. non-sway frame classification** in stability checks (Eurocode 3 / IS 800:2023 Annex F).
> - **Pre-engineered building (PEB)** connection design (end plate moment connections).
> - **Industrial portal frames** where semi-rigid action is intentionally utilised to economise steel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of T-Stub Moment Resistance — Failure Mode 1 (Complete Flange Yielding)

**Statement:** For an equivalent T-stub representing the column flange or end-plate in bending, derive the resistance when the flange yields completely before the bolts fail.

**Step 1 — Plastic Hinge Mechanism**
When the flange yields in bending, four plastic hinges form (two at the bolt line, two at the flange-to-web junction). The internal plastic moment at each hinge is $M_{pl,1,Rd}$.

**Step 2 — Plastic Moment of a Rectangular Flange Section**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{pl,1,Rd} \;&=\; \dfrac{\ell_{eff} \cdot t_f^{2}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}} \quad \text{(plastic section modulus of unit width)} \\[4pt]
&\text{where } \ell_{eff} \text{ is the effective length of the equivalent T-stub.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Virtual Work Equation**
Using the kinematic mechanism with hinge rotations $\theta$ at each plastic hinge, the external work = internal work:

$$F_{T,1,Rd} \cdot m \cdot \theta \;=\; 4 \, M_{pl,1,Rd} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the Force**

$$F_{T,1,Rd} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \, M_{pl,1,Rd}}{m}$$

This is the **Mode 1** T-stub resistance — it represents *complete flange yielding with no bolt failure*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of T-Stub Moment Resistance — Failure Mode 2 (Mixed Bolt + Flange Failure)

**Step 1 — Mechanism Description**
Here only the **bolt-line hinges** form (two hinges); the **toe hinges do not form**. The bolts develop their full tension capacity.

**Step 2 — Work Equation**
Let $\delta$ be the displacement of the bolt line relative to the flange-to-web junction:

$$F_{T,2,Rd} \cdot m \cdot \theta \;=\; 2 \, M_{pl,1,Rd} \cdot \theta \;+\; \Sigma F_{t,Rd} \cdot n \cdot \theta$$

where $n$ is the distance from the bolt centre to the location of the plastic hinge on the bolt line (typically $n \leq 1.25\,m$).

**Step 3 — Solve**

$$F_{T,2,Rd} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, M_{pl,1,Rd} \;+\; n \, \Sigma F_{t,Rd}}{m + n}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of T-Stub Moment Resistance — Failure Mode 3 (Pure Bolt Failure)

**Step 1 — Mechanism**
No flange yielding occurs; the bolts fail in tension.

$$F_{T,3,Rd} \;=\; \Sigma F_{t,Rd}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Initial Rotational Stiffness, $S_{j,ini}$

**Step 1 — Definition of Rotational Stiffness**
The rotational stiffness is the moment per unit rotation:

$$S_{j,ini} \;=\; \dfrac{M}{\phi}$$

**Step 2 — Compatibility**
For a small rotation $\phi$ of the joint, the tension component elongates by $\delta = z \cdot \phi$ and the compression component shortens by the same amount. Each component acts as a spring with stiffness $k_i$:

$$\delta_i \;=\; \dfrac{F_i}{k_i}$$

**Step 3 — Force Equilibrium**
The tension force $F_T$ in the bolt row (acting at the tension flange) and the compression force $F_C$ (acting at the compression flange) form a couple. The compression components act in parallel with the tension components; the joint deformation $\delta$ equals the sum of the elongation of all tension components AND the shortening of all compression components.

**Step 4 — Assembly**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi \;&=\; \dfrac{\delta}{z} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{z} \left[ \sum_{i \in \text{tension}} \dfrac{F_T}{k_i} \;+\; \sum_{j \in \text{compression}} \dfrac{F_C}{k_j} \right] \\[4pt]
M \;&=\; F_T \cdot z \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_T \;=\; \dfrac{M}{z}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final Assembled Stiffness**

$$S_{j,ini} \;=\; \dfrac{M}{\phi} \;=\; \dfrac{E \, z^{2}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i} \dfrac{1}{k_i}}$$

The sum is taken over the **effective stiffness coefficients** of each component (column flange, end plate, bolts, column web, etc.) participating in the tension-compression couple.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Moment Resistance of a Single Bolt Row

> **Given:** Extended end-plate joint with a bolt row at the tension flange.
> - Bolt grade 8.8, diameter **M20**, $A_s = 245 \text{ mm}^2$, $f_{ub} = 800 \text{ MPa}$
> - Two bolts per row (so $\Sigma F_{t,Rd}$ uses **2 bolts**)
> - End plate thickness $t_p = 12 \text{ mm}$, $f_y = 355 \text{ MPa}$
> - Effective length $\ell_{eff} = 110 \text{ mm}$ (non-circular pattern)
> - Lever arm $m = 45 \text{ mm}$, $n = 30 \text{ mm}$
> - Lever arm to next row $z = 540 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Bolt Tension Capacity**

$$F_{t,Rd} \;=\; \dfrac{0.9 \times 800 \times 245}{1.25} \;=\; 141\,120 \text{ N (per bolt)}$$

$$\Sigma F_{t,Rd} \;=\; 2 \times 141\,120 \;=\; 282\,240 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Plastic Moment of Plate**

$$M_{pl,1,Rd} \;=\; \dfrac{0.25 \times 110 \times 12^{2} \times 355}{1.10} \;=\; 1\,278\,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Three Failure Mode Resistances**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{T,1,Rd} \;&=\; \dfrac{4 \times 1\,278\,000}{45} \;=\; 113\,600 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{T,2,Rd} \;&=\; \dfrac{2 \times 1\,278\,000 + 30 \times 282\,240}{45 + 30} \;=\; 130\,280 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{T,3,Rd} \;&=\; 282\,240 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Governing Mode**
$$F_{T,Rd} \;=\; \min(113\,600 \; ; \; 130\,280 \; ; \; 282\,240) \;=\; 113\,600 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Joint Moment Resistance for this Bolt Row**
$$M_{j,Rd} \;=\; 113\,600 \times 540 \;=\; 61.3 \text{ kN·m}$$

The joint is governed by **Mode 1** (full plate yielding) — a ductile failure mode. ✅

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an In-Plane Joint — Component Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[APPLIED LOAD INPUT] --> A2[Beam End Moment M, Shear V, Axial N]
    A2 --> B1[Force Decomposition at Beam Flange Level]
    B1 --> B2[Tension Force F_t at Upper Flange]
    B1 --> B3[Compression Force F_c at Lower Flange]
    B1 --> B4[Shear Force V at Web]
    
    B2 --> C1[END PLATE IN BENDING T-Stub 1]
    B2 --> C2[BOLTS IN TENSION]
    B2 --> C3[COLUMN FLANGE IN BENDING T-Stub 2]
    B2 --> C4[COLUMN WEB IN TENSION]
    
    B3 --> D1[BEAM WEB IN COMPRESSION]
    B3 --> D2[COLUMN WEB IN COMPRESSION]
    
    B4 --> E1[COLUMN WEB PANEL IN SHEAR]
    
    C1 --> F1[COMPONENT METHOD ASSEMBLY]
    C2 --> F1
    C3 --> F1
    C4 --> F1
    D1 --> F1
    D2 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    
    F1 --> G1[CALCULATION OF M_jRd]
    F1 --> G2[CALCULATION OF S_j INITIAL]
    F1 --> G3[CALCULATION OF PHI Cd]
    
    G1 --> H1[JOINT CLASSIFICATION BY STRENGTH]
    G2 --> H2[JOINT CLASSIFICATION BY STIFFNESS]
    
    H1 --> I1[FINAL DESIGN VERIFICATION M_jRd greater than M_Sd]
    H2 --> I1
    G3 --> I2[DUCTILITY CHECK phi Cd greater than phi demand]
    I1 --> J1[SAFE OR UNSAFE DESIGN OUTCOME]
    I2 --> J1
```

### 4.2 Schematic of T-Stub Idealisation with Failure Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[APPLIED FORCE F_T] --> K2[Equivalent T-Stub Flange]
    K2 --> K3{Governing Failure Mode}
    K3 --> K4[MODE 1: 4 Plastic Hinges Form - Full Flange Yielding]
    K3 --> K5[MODE 2: 2 Plastic Hinges + Bolt Failure]
    K3 --> K6[MODE 3: Pure Bolt Failure - No Flange Yielding]
    K4 --> K7[Equation F_T1Rd = 4Mpl1Rd divided by m]
    K5 --> K8[Equation F_T2Rd = 2Mpl1Rd plus n FtRd divided by m plus n]
    K6 --> K9[Equation F_T3Rd = Sigma FtRd]
    K7 --> K10[Take MINIMUM = F_TRd]
    K8 --> K10
    K9 --> K10
    K10 --> K11[Multiply by Lever Arm z to get M_jRd]
```

### 4.3 Joint Classification Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Joint Parameters Evaluated] --> L2[Compute M_jRd]
    L1 --> L3[Compute S_j_ini]
    L2 --> L4{Strength Ratio mu = M_jRd divided by M_b_pl_Rd}
    L3 --> L5{Stiffness Ratio lambda = S_j L_b divided by E I_b}
    L4 -->|mu greater than or equal to 1.0| L6[FULL STRENGTH]
    L4 -->|mu between 0.25 and 1.0| L7[PARTIAL STRENGTH]
    L4 -->|mu less than or equal to 0.25| L8[PINNED by Strength]
    L5 -->|lambda greater than or equal to 25 braced| L9[RIGID]
    L5 -->|lambda greater than or equal to 8 unbraced| L9
    L5 -->|lambda less than or equal to 0.5| L10[PINNED by Stiffness]
    L5 -->|0.5 less than lambda less than 25| L11[SEMI-RIGID]
    L6 --> L12[DESIGN AS MOMENT CONNECTION]
    L7 --> L12
    L9 --> L12
    L11 --> L13[DESIGN AS SEMI-RIGID with M_phi curve]
    L8 --> L14[DESIGN AS PINNED]
    L10 --> L14
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:** The schematic follows the actual **load path** in a real beam-to-column moment connection. The applied moment is decomposed into a tension–compression couple (B1→B2/B3) and a shear (B1→B4). Each force path passes through one or more *components* (C1, C2, C3, C4, D1, D2, E1). The component method aggregates these in a **series spring analogy** (F1) to yield the three primary parameters (G1–G3), which then drive the joint classification (H1–H2) and final design verification (I1–I2).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**Q1. Define an "in-plane joint" in steel structures. List the three primary behavioural parameters that govern its design as per the component method.**
**[3 Marks — CO2, Remembering Level]**

**Model Answer:**
An *in-plane joint* is a beam-to-column moment connection in which the connecting members lie in a common vertical plane (typically a beam framing into a column flange — major axis connection).

The three primary parameters are:
1. **Design Moment Resistance** $M_{j,Rd}$
2. **Rotational Stiffness** $S_j$
3. **Design Rotation Capacity** $\phi_{Cd}$

These three parameters are obtained by the *component method* (Eurocode 3 / EN 1993-1-8) and are used to classify the joint by strength and by stiffness.

---

**Q2. Distinguish between a "rigid joint," a "semi-rigid joint," and a "pinned joint" based on the rotational stiffness parameter.**
**[3 Marks — CO2, Understanding Level]**

**Model Answer:**

| Joint Type | Stiffness Condition (Braced Frame) | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Rigid** | $S_{j,ini} \geq 25 \, E I_b / L_b$ | Full moment transfer; no joint rotation |
| **Semi-rigid** | $0.5 < S_{j,ini} L_b / E I_b < 25$ | Partial moment transfer; rotation must be modelled |
| **Pinned** | $S_{j,ini} \leq 0.5 \, E I_b / L_b$ | Negligible moment; behaves as a hinge |

The numerical thresholds are based on the *unbraced* and *braced* frame classifications given in IS 800:2023 (Annex F) / EN 1993-1-8.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Component Method & Moment Resistance*

**(a)** **[7 Marks — CO3, Applying Level]** *Derive the expression for the design moment resistance of an extended end-plate in-plane joint using the component method. Clearly state the three failure modes of the equivalent T-stub.*

**(b)** **[7 Marks — CO3, Analysing Level]** *For an in-plane beam-to-column joint in a braced frame, the following parameters are given: $M_{j,Rd} = 320 \text{ kN·m}$, $M_{b,pl,Rd} = 380 \text{ kN·m}$, $S_{j,ini} = 18\,000 \text{ kN·m/rad}$, beam length $L_b = 5.0 \text{ m}$, $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ MPa}$, $I_b = 45\,000 \text{ cm}^4$. Classify the joint by strength and by stiffness. State the design implications.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Moment Resistance:**

The component method idealises the joint as an assembly of **T-stubs in series with bolts**, each with its own design resistance. For a bolt row in the tension zone of an extended end-plate:

**Step 1** — Compute the **plastic moment capacity** of the plate (acting as a T-stub flange):

$$M_{pl,1,Rd} \;=\; \dfrac{0.25 \, \ell_{eff} \, t_p^{2} \, f_y}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

**Step 2** — Apply the three failure modes:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{T,1,Rd} \;&=\; \dfrac{4 \, M_{pl,1,Rd}}{m} \quad \text{(flange yielding, 4 hinges)} \\[4pt]
F_{T,2,Rd} \;&=\; \dfrac{2 \, M_{pl,1,Rd} + n \, \Sigma F_{t,Rd}}{m + n} \quad \text{(mixed)} \\[4pt]
F_{T,3,Rd} \;&=\; \Sigma F_{t,Rd} \quad \text{(bolt failure)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3** — The governing tension resistance is the minimum:

$$F_{T,Rd} \;=\; \min(F_{T,1,Rd} \;,\; F_{T,2,Rd} \;,\; F_{T,3,Rd})$$

**Step 4** — Multiply by the lever arm $z$ (≈ depth between flange centroids):

$$\boxed{\, M_{j,Rd} \;=\; F_{T,Rd} \cdot z \,}$$

The joint moment resistance is governed by the weakest T-stub in any bolt row.

**(b) Joint Classification:**

**Step 1 — Strength Classification:**

$$\mu \;=\; \dfrac{M_{j,Rd}}{M_{b,pl,Rd}} \;=\; \dfrac{320}{380} \;=\; 0.842$$

Since $0.25 < \mu < 1.0$, the joint is classified as **Partial Strength**.

**Step 2 — Stiffness Classification:**

First compute the beam flexural rigidity parameter:

$$\dfrac{E I_b}{L_b} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \times 10^{5} \times 45\,000 \times 10^{4}}{5.0 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; 1.8 \times 10^{7} \text{ N·mm/rad} \;=\; 18\,000 \text{ kN·m/rad}$$

$$\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{S_{j,ini} \cdot L_b}{E \cdot I_b} \;=\; \dfrac{18\,000 \times 5.0}{18\,000} \;=\; 5.0$$

Since $0.5 < \lambda < 25$ (braced frame), the joint is classified as **Semi-Rigid**.

**Step 3 — Design Implications:**
- The connection must be designed using the actual $M$–$\phi$ curve, not as fully rigid.
- Frame analysis must incorporate the semi-rigid joint rotational springs.
- The joint can resist **84.2 %** of the full plastic moment of the beam — therefore the beam can be designed on the basis of $M_{j,Rd} = 320 \text{ kN·m}$, not the full plastic moment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Stiffness & T-Stub Mechanism*

**(a)** **[7 Marks — CO3, Applying Level]** *Derive the expression for the initial rotational stiffness $S_{j,ini}$ of an in-plane joint from first principles using the component method. Clearly state the components that contribute.*

**(b)** **[7 Marks — CO3, Evaluating Level]** *For a T-stub representing the column flange in an in-plane joint: end-plate thickness $t_p = 16 \text{ mm}$, $f_y = 355 \text{ MPa}$, $f_u = 470 \text{ MPa}$, two M20 grade 8.8 bolts per row, $m = 50 \text{ mm}$, $n = 35 \text{ mm}$, $\ell_{eff} = 120 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the governing T-stub resistance and identify the failure mode.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $S_{j,ini}$:**

**Step 1** — Define $S_j$ as moment per unit rotation: $S_j = M/\phi$.

**Step 2** — Express the joint rotation $\phi$ in terms of the lever arm $z$ and the joint deformation $\delta$:

$$\phi \;=\; \dfrac{\delta}{z}$$

**Step 3** — Express $\delta$ as the sum of elongations across all tension components and shortenings across all compression components (series assembly):

$$\delta \;=\; \sum_{i \in \text{tension}} \dfrac{F_T}{k_i} \;+\; \sum_{j \in \text{compression}} \dfrac{F_C}{k_j}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute $F_T = M/z$ and $F_C = M/z$, then $S_j = M/\phi$:

$$\boxed{\, S_{j,ini} \;=\; \dfrac{E \, z^{2}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i \in \text{active}} \dfrac{1}{k_i}} \,}$$

Active components include: **column web in tension, column flange in bending, end plate in bending, bolts in tension, column web in compression, beam web in compression, column web panel in shear**.

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Bolt Tension Capacity:**

$$F_{t,Rd} \;=\; \dfrac{0.9 \times 470 \times 245}{1.25} \;=\; 82\,926 \text{ N per bolt}$$

$$\Sigma F_{t,Rd} \;=\; 2 \times 82\,926 \;=\; 165\,852 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Plastic Moment of Plate:**

$$M_{pl,1,Rd} \;=\; \dfrac{0.25 \times 120 \times 16^{2} \times 355}{1.10} \;=\; 2\,478\,545 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Three Failure Modes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{T,1,Rd} \;&=\; \dfrac{4 \times 2\,478\,545}{50} \;=\; 198\,284 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{T,2,Rd} \;&=\; \dfrac{2 \times 2\,478\,545 + 35 \times 165\,852}{50 + 35} \;=\; 152\,069 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{T,3,Rd} \;&=\; 165\,852 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Governing Mode:**

$$F_{T,Rd} \;=\; \min(198\,284 \; ; \; 152\,069 \; ; \; 165\,852) \;=\; 152\,069 \text{ N}$$

**Result:** **Mode 2** (mixed — flange yielding combined with bolt failure) governs the design, with $F_{T,Rd} \approx 152 \text{ kN}$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Joint Parameter Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the partial safety factor $\gamma_{M0}$** in $M_{pl,1,Rd}$ — yields an un-conservative answer (deduct **1 mark**).
> 2. **Using the wrong $k_2$ factor** for bolt tension (use $k_2 = 0.9$ for grade 8.8, **0.63** for grade 10.9) — frequent error (deduct **1 mark**).
> 3. **Misidentifying Mode 1 vs Mode 3**: Mode 1 is *ductile* (preferred for seismic), Mode 3 is *brittle* (to be avoided). Examiners expect a clear identification.
> 4. **Confusing lever arm $z$ with beam depth $d$** — $z$ is the distance between **flange centroids**, not the total depth.
> 5. **Stiffness classification thresholds differ** for braced (≥25) and unbraced (≥8) frames. Read the question stem carefully.
> 6. **Not checking all components** — a single un-checked component (e.g., column web shear) can invalidate the entire joint design.
> 7. **Sign convention errors** in deriving the moment-rotation curve — write down both moment and rotation signs explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist for PECET864 – Module 2:**
> - ⭐ **Three primary parameters:** $M_{j,Rd}$, $S_{j,ini}$, $\phi_{Cd}$ — these completely define an in-plane joint.
> - ⭐ **Component Method:** Decompose joint into *basic components*; assemble using series-spring analogy for stiffness and weakest-link for resistance.
> - ⭐ **T-Stub is the central idealisation** — column flange in bending AND end plate in bending are both modelled as T-stubs.
> - ⭐ **Three T-stub failure modes:** Mode 1 (4 plastic hinges, ductile), Mode 2 (2 hinges + bolts, semi-ductile), Mode 3 (bolts only, brittle).
> - ⭐ **Lever arm $z$** ≈ distance between mid-thicknesses of beam flanges; never use total beam depth.
> - ⭐ **Strength classification thresholds:** Full $\geq 1.0$; Pinned $\leq 0.25$; in between = Partial.
> - ⭐ **Stiffness classification thresholds:** Rigid $\geq 25$ (braced) or $\geq 8$ (unbraced); Pinned $\leq 0.5$; in between = Semi-rigid.
> - ⭐ **Partial safety factors:** $\gamma_{M0} = 1.10$ for steel, $\gamma_{M2} = 1.25$ for bolts.
> - ⭐ **Bolt factors $k_2$:** $0.9$ for grade 8.8, $0.63$ for grade 10.9.
> - ⭐ **Always check ALL components:** column web panel shear, column web tension, column web compression, column flange bending, end plate bending, bolts.
> - ⭐ **Ductility matters:** A joint with high $M_{j,Rd}$ but low $\phi_{Cd}$ (Mode 3) is undesirable in seismic frames.
> - ⭐ **M–$\phi$ curve signature:** The full non-linear moment-rotation curve is the *true* joint characteristic; $S_{j,ini}$ is the initial slope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welds in Tubular Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welds in Tubular Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the design of **tubular structures** (offshore platforms, trusses, towers, and space frames), the members are predominantly **Hollow Structural Sections (HSS)** — either **Circular Hollow Sections (CHS)** or **Rectangular/Square Hollow Sections (RHS/SHS)**. The connections between these members are made almost exclusively by **arc welding**, because bolting is geometrically impossible on curved tube surfaces and on the hidden internal faces of closed sections.

**Welds in Tubular Joints** are the welded load-transferring elements that join a **branch member** (secondary tube) to a **chord member** (primary tube), and are classified according to:
- The **weld geometry** (fillet, partial-penetration butt, full-penetration butt, flare-bevel, flare-V),
- The **loading state** (axial, in-plane bending, out-of-plane bending, combined),
- The **joint configuration** (T, Y, X, K, KT, N-joint).

Per **IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.6)** and **Eurocode 3 — EN 1993-1-8 (Cl. 9)**, the design of welds in tubular joints is governed by the **effective throat thickness** `t_e` and the **effective weld length** `l_e`, since the actual weld profile is curved and cannot be directly measured on site.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECET864 Module 2):** Students must master (i) the computation of `t_e` for fillet, flare-bevel, flare-V and butt welds in tubes, (ii) the determination of `l_e` for CHS and RHS branch-to-chord configurations, and (iii) the verification of weld strength against the factored design load, with the partial safety factor $\gamma_{Mw} = 1.25$ (factory weld) or $\gamma_{Mw} = 1.50$ (field weld).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stitched Seam" Picture

Imagine two bamboo sticks used in scaffolding. A rope can be tied around the joint, but the rope is **curved** and the **contact length** depends on the circumference and the angle. A weld in a tubular joint behaves the **same way**:

- The **weld is curved** around the tube — its *real* length is greater than a straight line,
- The **load transfers** through the weld at the *shortest distance* from the chord surface to the branch surface — this shortest distance is the **effective throat thickness** `t_e`,
- Only a portion of the curved weld actually carries load uniformly — the rest is *"ineffective"*. The load-carrying portion is the **effective weld length** `l_e`,
- The weld has a **factory vs. field** quality — a shop weld is like a tailor stitching in a controlled room, a field weld is like sewing outdoors in wind. Hence the partial safety factor is different.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Default Code Parameters (IS 800:2007):**
> - Partial safety factor for weld (factory) $\gamma_{Mw} = 1.25$
> - Partial safety factor for weld (site) $\gamma_{Mw} = 1.50$
> - Minimum ultimate strength $f_u$ for structural steel **E250** = **410 MPa**, for **E300** = **440 MPa**, for **E350** = **490 MPa**, for **E410** = **540 MPa**.
> - Weld size (leg length) $s \geq$ thickness of the thinner connected part.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Welds in Tubular Joints

**A. By Geometry of the Connected Surfaces:**

1. **Fillet Weld** — A triangular cross-section weld deposited in the corner formed by two plates/tubes meeting at an angle (typically 60°–120°).
2. **Butt Weld (Full Penetration)** — The two member edges are prepared (e.g., single-V, double-V) and fused completely. The throat equals the plate thickness.
3. **Butt Weld (Partial Penetration)** — Only a portion of the plate depth is fused. Effective throat is reduced.
4. **Flare-Bevel Weld (FBW)** — Used when a flat plate is welded against the curved surface of a **round tube**. The weld follows the flare of the tube radius.
5. **Flare-V Weld (FVW)** — Used between two curved (round) surfaces, e.g., a CHS branch welded to a CHS chord.

**B. By Joint Configuration:**

- **T-joint** — single 90° branch,
- **Y-joint** — branch at an acute angle,
- **X-joint** — two branches crossing through the chord,
- **K-joint** — two branches balancing each other (truss-type),
- **N-joint / KT-joint** — multi-planar combinations.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Step

| Step | Engineering Logic ("Why") |
|------|---------------------------|
| Compute `t_e` | The actual rupture surface of a fillet weld is the **45° throat plane** between the weld root and the face. The 45° root gives the factor $0.7 = \sin 45° \cdot \cos 0°$. |
| Compute `l_e` | Only a portion of the curved weld around a tube perimeter transfers load. The rest is *"inactive"* due to non-uniform stress distribution and flexibility of the chord wall. |
| Apply $\gamma_{Mw}$ | A weld can have slag inclusions, under-cuts, and porosity — these are quality uncertainties. Factory control is better, hence $\gamma_{Mw}$ is lower. |
| Check $f_w$ from $f_u$ | Welds fail in shear along the throat. By **von-Mises** yield criterion, $f_y / \sqrt{3}$ for shear; in ultimate limit state, $f_u / \sqrt{3}$ is used. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values inside markdown tables. All equations are reproduced in LaTeX in §3.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applicable Weld Type | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|------|
| 1 | Effective throat (fillet) | $t_e = 0.7\,s$ | Fillet | mm |
| 2 | Effective throat (full-penetration butt) | $t_e = t$ | Full-Penetration Butt | mm |
| 3 | Effective throat (partial-penetration butt) | $t_e = 0.8\,t_w$ | Partial-Penetration Butt | mm |
| 4 | Effective throat (flare-bevel) | $t_e = (5/16)\,R$ | Plate-to-CHS branch | mm |
| 5 | Effective throat (flare-V) | $t_e = (3/8)\,R$ | CHS-to-CHS | mm |
| 6 | Weld design shear stress | $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{Mw}}$ | All | MPa |
| 7 | Design strength of weld | $F_{wd} = f_{wd}\, t_e\, l_e$ | All | N (kN) |
| 8 | Effective length (CHS branch to CHS chord) | $l_e = 2.5\,d_b$ (or $\pi d_b/2$ in some codes) | CHS-CHS | mm |
| 9 | Effective length (RHS branch to RHS chord) | $l_e = 2\,b_b$ | RHS-RHS | mm |
| 10 | Effective length (CHS branch to RHS chord) | $l_e = 2\,b_b$ | CHS-RHS | mm |
| 11 | Effective length (RHS branch to CHS chord) | $l_e = 1.57\,d_b$ (per EN 1993-1-8) | RHS-CHS | mm |
| 12 | Min. weld leg size | $s \geq t_{min}$ of members | All | mm |
| 13 | Min. effective length | $l_e \geq 4\,s$ and $\geq 40$ mm | All | mm |
| 14 | Chord diameter-to-thickness ratio | $2\gamma = d_0 / t_0$ | Joint classification | — |
| 15 | Branch-to-chord ratio | $\beta = d_b / d_0$ | Joint classification | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore jackets** (oil platforms) — the entire K-joint, X-joint, and KT-joint network is welded. The most fatigue-critical location is the weld **crown** and **saddle** of CHS branch-to-CHS chord.
- **Long-span roof trusses** (stadiums, airports) — RHS trusses use flare-bevel welds at the gusset-plate-to-CHS interface.
- **Transmission towers** (electrical pylons) — angle members are increasingly being replaced by CHS-RHS welded trusses for aerodynamic efficiency.
- **Crane jibs and booms** — lattice boom welds must satisfy both static strength (ULS) and fatigue (fatigue truck loading).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Throat of a Fillet Weld

A fillet weld of equal leg size $s$ is deposited as a right-isosceles triangle between two plates at 90°.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the rupture plane.**
The weakest section is the **45° throat plane**, which extends from the weld root to the hypotenuse. This plane is inclined at $45°$ to both legs.

**Step 2 — Length of the throat.**
The throat length is the perpendicular distance from the root to the hypotenuse.

$$
t_e = s \cdot \sin(45^\circ) = s \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.7071\,s \approx 0.7\,s
$$

> Conversion logic: The leg $s$ is the hypotenuse of an isosceles right triangle whose legs are both equal to $s/\sqrt{2}$. The altitude to the hypotenuse is therefore $s \cdot \sin(45^\circ) = 0.7071 s$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Throat of a Flare-Bevel Weld (Plate to CHS)

When a flat plate is butted against the curved surface of a round HSS of radius $R$, the natural surface profile of the tube flares outward. The effective throat is measured **perpendicular to the plate** at the apex of the flare.

The standard derivation (per **AWS D1.1** and adopted in IS 800 commentary) treats the curved surface as a circular arc. For a plate-to-CHS joint with the plate tangent to the tube:

**Step 1 — Identify the governing geometry.**
The arc of the tube is a portion of a circle of radius $R$. The plate touches the tube at the apex (top dead centre). The flare is symmetric about the vertical axis.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum flare height.**
Beyond a small angle, the tube surface deviates from the tangent plane by $\Delta h$. For a flare angle $\theta$ measured from the tangent line, $\Delta h = R (1 - \cos\theta)$.

**Step 3 — Effective throat empirical value.**
For routine design, IS 800 commentary (and AWS D1.1, Table 2.3) simplifies this to:

$$
t_e = \dfrac{5}{16}\,R = 0.3125\,R
$$

> Conversion logic: The empirical 5/16 factor arises because the *uniform* effective throat that produces the same volume of fused metal as the curved profile is approximately $0.3125 R$ when $\theta \approx 30°$ (a typical fabrication assumption).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Weld Length in a CHS-to-CHS Joint (Per IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.6.4)

For a **CHS branch** of diameter $d_b$ welded to a **CHS chord** of diameter $d_0$, the weld runs around the entire intersection curve of the two cylinders. Only a portion of this curve is "effective".

**Step 1 — Total geometric weld length.**
The intersection of two cylinders of diameters $d_b$ and $d_0$ meeting at an angle $\theta$ is a **space curve** of length approximately:

$$
L_{total} \approx \dfrac{\pi\, d_b}{2}
$$

for a 90° T-joint.

**Step 2 — Inactive portions at saddle and crown.**
The two **"saddle"** points and the two **"crown"** points experience stress concentrations. The **"side"** portions carry most of the load. IS 800:2007 conservatively takes:

$$
l_e = 2.5\, d_b
$$

> Conversion logic: $2.5 d_b$ is approximately $0.796 \times \pi d_b$, i.e., the equivalent of $144°$ out of the $180°$ half-circumference — this corresponds to the actively loaded side arcs.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Weld Design Shear Strength

The shear yield stress is derived from von-Mises:

$$
\tau_y = \dfrac{f_y}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

In ultimate limit state, the weld is assumed to reach ultimate stress $f_u$ along the throat:

$$
f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{Mw}}
$$

**Step 1 — Weld force capacity per unit length.**

$$
f_{v,wd} = (0.7 s) \cdot f_{wd} = 0.7 s \cdot \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{Mw}}
$$

**Step 2 — Total weld capacity.**

$$
F_{wd} = l_e \cdot t_e \cdot f_{wd} = l_e \cdot 0.7 s \cdot \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{Mw}}
$$

**Step 3 — ULS check.**

$$
F_{wd} \geq F_u \;\; (\text{factored applied load})
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem (modeled on a 14-mark university problem):**

> A **CHS branch of 88.9 mm OD × 4.0 mm thick** carries an axial factored tensile load of **180 kN** to a **CHS chord of 168.3 mm OD × 5.0 mm thick**. The branch is fillet-welded to the chord with a **6 mm leg size** factory weld. Check whether the weld is adequate. Use $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{Mw} = 1.25$.

**Solution Step 1 — Identify the effective throat.**

$$
t_e = 0.7 \cdot s = 0.7 \cdot 6 = 4.2 \text{ mm}
$$

Check: $t_e = 4.2 \text{ mm} \geq t_{min} = 4.0 \text{ mm}$ ✓ (tube thickness governs).

**Solution Step 2 — Effective length (CHS branch to CHS chord).**

$$
l_e = 2.5\, d_b = 2.5 \cdot 88.9 = 222.25 \text{ mm}
$$

Check: $l_e \geq 4 s = 24$ mm ✓ and $\geq 40$ mm ✓.

**Solution Step 3 — Weld design shear stress.**

$$
f_{wd} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} = \dfrac{410}{2.1651} = 189.37 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Solution Step 4 — Weld design capacity.**

$$
F_{wd} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e \cdot l_e = 189.37 \cdot 4.2 \cdot 222.25 = 176{,}762 \text{ N} \approx 176.76 \text{ kN}
$$

**Solution Step 5 — ULS Check.**

$$
F_{wd} = 176.76 \text{ kN} \;<\; F_u = 180 \text{ kN}
$$

**Verdict: Weld is INADEQUATE by 3.24 kN (≈ 1.8 % shortfall).**

**Remedy:** Increase leg size to $s = 7$ mm.

Recompute:
$$
t_e = 0.7 \cdot 7 = 4.9 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
F_{wd} = 189.37 \cdot 4.9 \cdot 222.25 = 206{,}235 \text{ N} \approx 206.24 \text{ kN} > 180 \text{ kN} \quad\checkmark
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Weld-Design in a Tubular Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Branch Force Fu, Material fu, γ_Mw] --> B{Identify Tube Type}
    B -->|CHS Branch| C[Compute db, tb]
    B -->|RHS Branch| D[Compute bb, hb, tb]
    C --> E{Identify Weld Geometry}
    D --> E
    E -->|Fillet| F[te = 0.7 s]
    E -->|Flare-Bevel| G[te = 5/16 R]
    E -->|Flare-V| H[te = 3/8 R]
    E -->|Full-Penetration Butt| I[te = t_plate]
    F --> J[Compute Effective Length le]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -->|CHS-CHS| K[le = 2.5 db]
    J -->|RHS-RHS| L[le = 2 bb]
    J -->|CHS-RHS| M[le = 2 bb]
    K --> N[Compute fwd = fu / sqrt3 γ_Mw]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Fwd = fwd × te × le]
    O --> P{Fwd ≥ Fu ?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[STOP: Weld SAFE]
    P -->|No| R[Increase s, Iterate]
    R --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Anatomical Location Map of Weld Zones in a CHS T-Joint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CHS Branch db] --> B{Crown Point — Top}
    A --> C{Saddle Point — Sides}
    A --> D{Crown Point — Bottom}
    B --> E[Tension Zone — max σ]
    C --> F[Shear-Dominant Zone]
    D --> G[Bearing Zone]
    E --> H[Crack Initiation Risk]
    F --> I[Effective Length Region]
    G --> J[Effective Length Region]
    H --> K[Fatigue Critical]
    I --> L[Load Carrying — Counts in le]
    J --> L
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Fillet Weld Throat Geometry (Per IS 800)

```
        Plate A
        ┌──────────┐
        │          │
════════╪══════════╪═══  ← Fusion face
        │╲        ╱│
        │  ╲    ╱  │     s = leg size
        │    ╲╱    │     t_e = 0.7 s
        │    ╱╲    │     (45° throat plane)
        │  ╱    ╲  │
        │╱        ╲│
════════╪══════════╪═══
        │          │
        └──────────┘
        Plate B
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Flare-Bevel Weld (Plate-to-CHS)

```
            ┌────────── Plate
            │          │
════════════╪══════════╪══
            │  ╲    ╱  │
            │    ╲╱    │ ← t_e = 5/16 R measured
            │   CHS    │   perpendicular to plate
            │  (R)     │   from fusion line
            │    ╱╲    │
            │  ╱    ╲  │
════════════╪══════════╪══
            │          │
            └──────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid "Block-Functional" instead of physical drawings?**
> True to-scale 3-D renderings of intersecting cylinders are beyond Mermaid's syntax. The block-diagram above captures **which zones of the weld curve participate in load transfer** (sides) and **which are stress raisers** (crown, saddle) — the exact information required for `l_e` estimation.
