## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set B]**
State and explain the **economic depth criterion** for proportioning a plate girder. How is the depth-factor $k$ selected for stiffened and unstiffened girders?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The economic depth of a plate girder is the depth that minimises the total weight (and hence the cost) of the web and flange combined. The Shorter–Spaen formula gives:

$$D = k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_b \cdot t_w}}$$

The depth-factor $k$ accounts for whether transverse stiffeners are provided:
- For **unstiffened** girders: $k = 0.3$ to $0.6$ (shallower depth, but thicker web needed to avoid buckling).
- For **stiffened** girders (transverse stiffeners provided): $k = 0.4$ to $0.8$ (deeper section, thinner web, post-buckling action mobilised).

**Mark Split:** Definition (1 m) + Formula (1 m) + Selection of $k$ (1 m).

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between the **theoretical cut-off** and **actual cut-off** of a flange plate in a plate girder. Why is an extension provided beyond the theoretical cut-off?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Theoretical cut-off** is the section on the girder where the bending moment $M_x$ equals the **reduced moment capacity** $M_r$ of the section remaining *after* the flange plate is removed. It is obtained by solving $M_x = M_r$.
- **Actual cut-off** is the section where the flange plate is **physically terminated**, which lies **beyond** the theoretical cut-off (closer to the support) by an extension distance.
- **Reason for extension:** A flange plate cannot develop its full stress abruptly at the cut-off section due to **shear lag** and the need to transfer forces gradually through the welds/bolts. IS 800:2007, Cl. 9.5.3 prescribes an extension of at least $1.5 \times b_{eff}$ for welded plates (where $b_{eff}$ is the effective width of the curtailed plate).

**Mark Split:** Definitions (1.5 m) + IS clause + reason (1.5 m).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Design Problems (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

A simply supported plate girder of span **$L = 24$ m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **$w = 80$ kN/m** (inclusive of self-weight). Design the girder using **E250 steel** ($f_y = 250$ MPa). The flanges consist of two plates (one outer, one inner). Carry out curtailment of the outer plate. Sketch the curtailment diagram. (7 + 7 = 14 marks)

---

**Part (a) — Preliminary Design of Cross-Section (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Internal Forces**
$$V_{max} = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{80 \times 24}{2} = 960 \text{ kN}$$
$$M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{80 \times 24^2}{8} = 5760 \text{ kN·m} = 5.76 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Assume Web Thickness**
Assume $\tau_b = 100$ MPa and overall depth $D = 1800$ mm (≈ $L/13$).

$$t_w = \dfrac{V_{max}}{D \cdot \tau_b} = \dfrac{960 \times 10^3}{1800 \times 100} = 5.33 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt **$t_w = 8$ mm** (standard rolled plate).

**Step 3: Economic Depth Check**
$$D = k \sqrt{\dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_b \cdot t_w}} = 0.6 \sqrt{\dfrac{5.76 \times 10^9}{250 \times 8}} = 0.6 \sqrt{2.88 \times 10^6}$$
$$D = 0.6 \times 1697 = 1018 \text{ mm}$$

A deeper section is more economical — adopt **$D = 1600$ mm**.

**Step 4: Flange Area**
Effective depth $d = D - 2 t_f \approx 1600 - 2(20) = 1560$ mm.

$$A_f = \dfrac{M_{max}}{f_y \cdot d} - \dfrac{t_w \cdot d}{6} = \dfrac{5.76 \times 10^9}{250 \times 1560} - \dfrac{8 \times 1560}{6}$$
$$A_f = 14769 - 2080 = 12689 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 127 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 5: Choose Flange Plates**
- **Outer plate:** $b_f = 400$ mm, $t_f = 25$ mm $\Rightarrow A_{f,out} = 10000$ mm²
- **Inner plate:** $b_f = 350$ mm, $t_f = 16$ mm $\Rightarrow A_{f,in} = 5600$ mm²
- **Total:** $15600$ mm² > $12689$ mm² ✓ (provides reserve)

**Step 6: Flange b/t Check**
$b_o = (400 - 8)/2 = 196$ mm
$$\dfrac{b_o}{t_f} = \dfrac{196}{25} = 7.84 \le 13.6 \times \sqrt{250/250} = 13.6 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7: Web d/t Check**
$$\dfrac{d}{t_w} = \dfrac{1560}{8} = 195$$

Since $195 > 67\varepsilon = 67$, **web is slender** — intermediate stiffeners are required.

---

**Part (b) — Curtailment of Outer Flange Plate (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Reduced Moment Capacity with Outer Plate Removed**
The inner plate (350 × 16 mm) alone is available. Calculate the moment of inertia of the **reduced section**:

$$I_{web} = \dfrac{t_w \cdot D^3}{12} = \dfrac{8 \times 1600^3}{12} = 2.73 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$
$$I_{inner} = 2 \times (350 \times 16) \times (1600/2 - 16/2)^2 = 2 \times 5600 \times (792)^2 = 7.024 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$
$$I_{red} = 2.73 \times 10^9 + 7.024 \times 10^9 = 9.754 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

Distance to extreme fibre $y_{max} = D/2 = 800$ mm.

$$Z_{red} = \dfrac{I_{red}}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{9.754 \times 10^9}{800} = 1.219 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$M_r = \dfrac{Z_{red} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{1.219 \times 10^7 \times 250}{1.10} = 2.771 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm} = 2771 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Theoretical Cut-off Distance**
$$x = \dfrac{L - \sqrt{L^2 - \dfrac{8M_r}{w}}}{2} = \dfrac{24 - \sqrt{24^2 - \dfrac{8 \times 2771}{80}}}{2}$$
$$x = \dfrac{24 - \sqrt{576 - 277.1}}{2} = \dfrac{24 - \sqrt{298.9}}{2} = \dfrac{24 - 17.29}{2} = 3.36 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Actual Cut-off with Extension**
Effective width of outer plate $b_{eff} = 400 - 8 = 392$ mm.

Extension $= 1.5 \times 392 = 588$ mm $\approx 0.59$ m.

$$x_{\text{actual}} = x_{\text{theoretical}} - 1.5 b_{eff} = 3.36 - 0.59 = 2.77 \text{ m from support}$$

**Step 4: Verification by Direct BM Check**
At $x = 3.36$ m, $M_x = 80 \times 3.36 \times (24 - 3.36)/2 = 80 \times 3.36 \times 20.64/2 = 2773$ kN·m ≈ $M_r = 2771$ kN·m ✓

**Final Answer:**
- **Web:** 1600 mm × 8 mm
- **Outer flange:** 400 mm × 25 mm (curtailed at $x_{\text{actual}} = 2.77$ m from support, then re-introduced symmetrically)
- **Inner flange:** 350 mm × 16 mm (full length, $L = 24$ m)
- **Length of outer plate** on one half = $(24/2 - 2.77) = 9.23$ m

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (2 marks lost):** Using $M_{max}$ instead of $M_r$ in the cut-off formula, leading to $x = 0$.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (2 marks lost):** Forgetting the **extension** beyond the theoretical cut-off. Always quote IS 800 Cl. 9.5.3 explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** Not checking the **discriminant** $L^2 \ge 8M_r/w$ to verify curtailment is feasible.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

A welded plate girder of **span $L = 20$ m** carries a UDL of **$w = 60$ kN/m** (DL + LL, including self-weight). Using $f_y = 250$ MPa and assuming the web is to be provided with intermediate transverse stiffeners, design the **cross-section** and the **bearing stiffener** at the support. Also calculate the **theoretical cut-off** of the flange plate. (7 + 7 = 14 marks)

---

**Part (a) — Design of Cross-Section (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Internal Forces**
$$V_{max} = \dfrac{60 \times 20}{2} = 600 \text{ kN}, \quad M_{max} = \dfrac{60 \times 20^2}{8} = 3000 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Web Thickness & Depth**
Adopt $t_w = 8$ mm. With stiffeners, $k = 0.65$:
$$D = 0.65 \sqrt{\dfrac{3000 \times 10^6}{250 \times 8}} = 0.65 \sqrt{1.5 \times 10^6} = 0.65 \times 1224.7 = 796 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt $D = 1200$ mm (within $L/10$ to $L/8$ range).

**Step 3: Flange Area**
$d \approx 1200 - 2(20) = 1160$ mm.
$$A_f = \dfrac{3000 \times 10^6}{250 \times 1160} - \dfrac{8 \times 1160}{6} = 10345 - 1547 = 8798 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Flange Selection**
- Single flange plate: $300 \times 32$ mm → $A_f = 9600$ mm² > 8798 mm² ✓
- Check outstand: $b_o = (300 - 8)/2 = 146$ mm; $b_o/t_f = 146/32 = 4.56 \le 13.6$ ✓

**Step 5: Curtailment** (we will curtail half-length, leaving central portion with full plate)
- Section at mid-span (full plate): $I_{full} = \dfrac{8 \times 1200^3}{12} + 2 \times 9600 \times (600 - 16)^2 = 1.152 \times 10^9 + 6.696 \times 10^9 = 7.848 \times 10^9$ mm⁴
- $Z_{full} = 7.848 \times 10^9 / 600 = 1.308 \times 10^7$ mm³
- $M_{full} = 1.308 \times 10^7 \times 250 / 1.10 = 2972$ kN·m (≈ $M_{max}$ ✓)

For curtailment, assume **half the plate** (central 8 m only) is kept. Then outside this central 8 m, the girder relies on the web only:
- $I_{web} = 1.152 \times 10^9$ mm⁴, $Z_{web} = 1.152 \times 10^9 / 600 = 1.92 \times 10^6$ mm³
- $M_{r,web} = 1.92 \times 10^6 \times 250 / 1.10 = 436$ kN·m

Theoretical cut-off (where $M_x = 436$ kN·m):
$$x = \dfrac{20 - \sqrt{400 - 8 \times 436/60}}{2} = \dfrac{20 - \sqrt{400 - 58.13}}{2} = \dfrac{20 - 18.49}{2} = 0.755 \text{ m from support}$$

So flange is required only from $x = 0.755$ m to $x = 19.245$ m (with extension to $x_{\text{actual}} \approx 0.75 - 0.45 = 0.3$ m).

---

**Part (b) — Design of Bearing Stiffener (7 marks)**

As per **IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.7.2**:
- Width of bearing stiffener: $b_s \ge (D/30) + 75 = (1200/30) + 75 = 115$ mm → adopt $b_s = 120$ mm.
- Thickness: $t_s \ge t_w = 8$ mm → adopt $t_s = 10$ mm.
- **Bearing stress check** on the web: $A_{bearing} = b_s \times t_w = 120 \times 8 = 960$ mm²
$$\sigma_{bearing} = \dfrac{600 \times 10^3}{960} = 625 \text{ MPa} > 0.75 f_y = 187.5 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{FAIL}$$

Increase $b_s$ to ensure $\sigma_b \le 187.5$ MPa:
$$b_s \ge \dfrac{600 \times 10^3}{187.5 \times 8} = 400 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt **$b_s = 120$ mm × 2 = 240 mm (pair of stiffeners)** on both sides of web — effective bearing width = 2 × 120 = 240 mm.
$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{600 \times 10^3}{240 \times 8} = 312.5 \text{ MPa (still high)}$$

Provide a **sole plate** of $b = 240$ mm, $t = 25$ mm at the support:
$$A_{bearing,sole} = 240 \times 25 = 6000 \text{ mm}^2, \quad \sigma_b = 100 \text{ MPa} \le 187.5 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

- **Column buckling check** of the stiffener pair acting as a column:
$$I_{stiff} = \dfrac{t_s (2 b_s)^3}{12} = \dfrac{10 \times 240^3}{12} = 1.152 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$
$$r = \dfrac{240}{\sqrt{12}} = 69.3 \text{ mm}$$
$$L_{eff} = 0.7 D = 0.7 \times 1200 = 840 \text{ mm}$$
$$\lambda = \dfrac{840}{69.3} = 12.1 \quad \text{(very low, safe — no buckling)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake (3 marks lost):** Forgetting the **crippling (bearing) check** on the web before relying on stiffeners. Always compute $\sigma_b = R / (b_s \times t_w)$ first.
> - **Common Mistake (2 marks lost):** Treating the bearing stiffener as a *web extension* rather than as a *column* with effective length $0.7D$.
> - **Common Mistake (1 mark lost):** Not providing a **sole/base plate** when the bearing stress exceeds the permissible limit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save Before Exam!):**

- ☐ A plate girder under UDL carries a **parabolic BM** ($M \propto x(L-x)$) — this governs curtailment.
- ☐ **Economic depth** $D = k\sqrt{M_{max}/(\sigma_b t_w)}$ with $k = 0.3$–$0.6$ (unstiffened) or $0.4$–$0.8$ (stiffened).
- ☐ **Flange area** $A_f = M_{max}/(f_y d) - t_w d/6$ — the web contributes $t_w d/6$ to moment capacity.
- ☐ **b/t outstand limit** for plastic flange = $13.6\varepsilon$ (Table 2, IS 800:2007).
- ☐ Web is **slender** if $d/t_w > 67\varepsilon$ — intermediate stiffeners mandatory.
- ☐ **Theoretical cut-off** (UDL): $x = \dfrac{L - \sqrt{L^2 - 8M_r/w}}{2}$.
- ☐ **Actual cut-off** = theoretical − $1.5 b_{eff}$ extension (welded) or full $b_{eff}$ (bolted) per Cl. 9.5.3.
- ☐ **Discriminant** $L^2 \ge 8M_r/w$ must be non-negative; otherwise plate is needed full-length.
- ☐ **Bearing stiffeners** designed as short columns, $L_{eff} = 0.7D$, with crippling check $\sigma_b \le 0.75 f_y$.
- ☐ **Bearing stiffener width** $b_s \ge D/30 + 75$ mm (Cl. 8.7.2.2).
- ☐ **Intermediate stiffener spacing** $0.5d \le c \le 1.5d$ (Cl. 8.7.3.1).
- ☐ **Curtailment pattern:** Outer plate curtailed first (nearer supports), inner plate next.
- ☐ Always maintain **symmetry** of curtailment about the mid-span of the girder.
- ☐ Curtailment saves **15–20%** of flange steel — hence mandatory in **economy-grade** design.
- ☐ For KTU exam: show the **parabolic BM sketch** with curtailment points marked — full credit awarded.
- ☐ Use **partial safety factor** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (yielding) and $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ (ultimate) consistently.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU answer sheets, draw the **parabola** first, then mark the **curtailment points** on it. Examiners award 2–3 marks (out of 14) just for a clear, labelled curtailment sketch. Always cite the **IS 800:2007 Clause number** with each design check (e.g., "as per Cl. 8.7.2") — this adds 1–2 marks of impression credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Stiffeners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design of Stiffeners # 🔩 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stiffeners** are secondary plate or section members attached to the web of a plate girder, beam, or column to prevent out-of-plane deformation, control local buckling, transfer concentrated loads, and enhance the load-carrying capacity of the slender parent element. They are mandatory components in thin-webbed flexural members and form a critical part of the **shear-buckling design philosophy** under **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel)** and the **Limit State Design** method. According to **IS 800:2007 (Cl. 8.6 & 8.7)**, stiffeners in plate girders are categorized into the following functional types: | Sl. No. | Stiffener Type | Primary Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Intermediate / Transverse Stiffeners** | Resists shear buckling of the web | | 2 | **Bearing Stiffeners** | Transmits concentrated / reaction loads to the web | | 3 | **Longitudinal Stiffeners** | Increases web buckling resistance by subdividing the web panel | | 4 | **Diagonal / Torsional Stiffeners** | Provides lateral restraint & torsional stiffness | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Knife Edge" Analogy Imagine a **flat sheet of paper** held vertically. Press your finger on its top edge. The paper instantly folds (buckles) sideways — it is too thin to resist the compressive force. Now tape a small cardboard strip (a **stiffener**) along the paper's height. The same finger pressure is now safely carried, because the stiffener has transformed the paper from a free sheet into a **supported slender column**. A **web of a plate girder** behaves exactly like that paper — under shear and bending it is highly prone to buckle. **Stiffeners act as the cardboard tape**, bracing the web at strategic locations and effectively shortening its unsupported buckling length. Just as you wouldn't trust a thin door to support a heavy bookshelf without a vertical wooden batten, an engineer cannot trust an unstiffened thin web to carry industrial-grade shear forces. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights) - **Modulus of Elasticity, E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200 GPa)** - **Poisson's ratio, ν = 0.3** (for steel) - **Yield Stress of steel, f_y = 250 MPa (Fe 410 grade)** unless otherwise specified - **Shear modulus, G = 76.9 × 10³ N/mm² ≈ 77 GPa** - **Partial safety factor for steel, γ_m = 1.10** (at ultimate limit state, IS 800:2007 Table 5) - **Imperfection factor, ε = √(250/f_y)** > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECET864 – Module 3):** Students must master the design of **intermediate stiffeners** (stiffness + strength check) and **bearing stiffeners** (as a compression member + bearing check) — these two form the bulk of university exam questions and carry direct numerical weightage. # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why" Behind Stiffeners The web of a plate girder has a very high **height-to-thickness ratio (d/t_w)** to economize material. However, thin webs fail prematurely by: 1. **Shear buckling** — diagonal waves forming between flanges 2. **Web crippling** — localized crushing under concentrated loads 3. **Vertical buckling** — like a column when height is excessive The web panel between two transverse stiffeners acts like a **plate supported on all four edges** under pure shear. Stiffeners break the long web into shorter panels, raising the **shear buckling coefficient (k_v)** and the **critical shear stress (τ_cr)**. ## 2.2 Behavioural Logic of Each Stiffener Type ### A. Intermediate (Transverse) Stiffeners - **Why provided?** → Resists shear buckling; not designed to carry external load. - **When omitted?** → If τ_b < τ_cr (allowable shear without buckling). - **When provided?** → If τ_b > 0.4 τ_cr (mandatory per IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.6.1.1). - **Geometry rule:** Projecting outstand of stiffener ≤ 20·ε·t_q (for outstanding leg). - **Stiffness check:** Moment of inertia of stiffener about web face, I_s ≥ (1.5·d³·t_w³) / a² (Cl. 8.6.1.2 of IS 800:2007), where 'a' is the stiffener spacing and 'd' is the web depth. ### B. Bearing Stiffeners - **Why provided?** → To prevent web crippling at points of application of large concentrated loads (column loads, reaction supports). - **Designed as:** A short column (effective section = outstanding legs of two stiffeners + a strip of web of width 20·t_w on each side). - **Bearing check:** Bearing stress on the contact area ≤ 0.75·f_y / γ_m. - **Buckling check:** Axial load on effective section ≤ P_d (design compressive strength). ### C. Longitudinal Stiffeners - **Why provided?** → To increase critical shear/bending buckling stress by subdividing web panel. - **Location:** At 0.2d or 0.25d from compression flange. - **Stiffness requirement:** I_sl ≥ d·t_w³ × (k_sl-function), per Cl. 8.7. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below is a **high-yield cheat sheet** capturing every formula a student will need in the KTU University Exam for the topic of Design of Stiffeners. | # | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Reference | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\lambda_w = \dfrac{d}{t_w}$ | Web slenderness ratio | IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.6.1 | | 2 | $\tau_b = \dfrac{V}{d \cdot t_w}$ | Average shear stress in web | Fundamental | | 3 | $k_v = 5.35$ (for $a/d \geq \sqrt{2}$) | Shear buckling coefficient (long panel) | IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.6.1(a) | | 4 | $k_v = 4.0 + \dfrac{5.35}{(a/d)^2}$ (for $a/d < \sqrt{2}$) | Shear buckling coefficient (short panel) | IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.6.1(a) | | 5 | $\tau_{cr} = \dfrac{k_v \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t_w}{d}\right)^2$ | Elastic critical shear stress | Cl. 8.6.1.1 | | 6 | $\tau_{cr,b} = \dfrac{f_y}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Plastic shear capacity (yielding) | Cl. 8.4.1 | | 7 | $\tau_{b,max} = \dfrac{f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}}$ | Design shear strength (yielding limit) | Cl. 9.2.2 | | 8 | $I_s \geq \dfrac{1.5 \cdot d^3 \cdot t_w^3}{a^2}$ | Minimum stiffness of intermediate stiffener | Cl. 8.6.1.2 | | 9 | $A_{sv} = (h_s \cdot t_s) - $ cut-out region | Gross area of intermediate stiffener | — | | 10 | $P \leq \dfrac{0.75 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sb}}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | Bearing strength on contact area | Cl. 10.4.4 | | 11 | $A_{eff} = 2 \cdot b_s \cdot t_s + 20 \cdot t_w^2$ (approx.) | Effective area of bearing stiffener section | Standard practice | | 12 | $P_d = A_e \cdot f_{cd}$ | Design compressive strength | Cl. 7.1 | | 13 | $f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda^2}}$ | Design compressive stress (column curve) | Cl. 7.1.2.1 | | 14 | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cc}}} = \dfrac{L_e / r}{93.9\varepsilon}$ | Non-dimensional slenderness | Cl. 7.1.2.1 | | 15 | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{\dfrac{250}{f_y}}$ | Imperfection factor | Cl. 7.1.2.1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Remember:** $| \lambda \vert$ must always be evaluated as $\sqrt{...}$ — **never** as absolute value in the lambda formula context. Use $\sqrt{\cdot}$ explicitly. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Stiffeners are **the lifeline of plate girder bridges, industrial crane girders, building transfer beams, and offshore platform jacket legs**. For example: - A **gantry girder** carrying a 100-ton EOT crane uses bearing stiffeners at every wheel load point — without them, the web would **cripple** (crush) within hours of operation. - A **highway bridge plate girder** in coastal Kerala may have intermediate stiffeners every 1.0–1.5 m along its depth to resist **shear buckling** from moving vehicle loads. The **economic implication** is enormous — without stiffeners, webs would need to be 2–3 times thicker, adding **30–40% weight penalty** to every steel structure. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Elastic Critical Shear Stress of Web Panel We derive the classical **Timoshenko plate buckling solution** for a simply-supported rectangular web panel under pure shear. ### Step 1 — Governing Equation of a Plate in Shear For a thin rectangular plate of dimensions $a \times d$ (width × depth) and thickness $t_w$, subjected to uniform edge shear $N_{xy}$, the equilibrium equation in **elastic plate theory** is: $$ D \left( \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^4} + 2 \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial y^4} \right) = 2 N_{xy} \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y} $$ where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity of the plate** per unit width: $$ D = \frac{E \cdot t_w^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)} $$ ### Step 2 — Assume Sinusoidal Buckled Shape A simply-supported plate under pure shear buckles in a **sinusoidal wave pattern**. The deflection is assumed as: $$ w(x, y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_{mn} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \cdot \sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{d}\right) $$ The integer $m$ is the number of half-waves horizontally and $n$ vertically. ### Step 3 — Apply the Galerkin/Ritz Energy Method Substituting the assumed mode shape into the governing equation, and applying the **orthogonality of sine functions**, the **critical shear force per unit length** is obtained as: $$ N_{xy,cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot D}{a^2} \left[ m^2 + \left(\frac{a}{d}\right)^2 n^2 \right]^2 \cdot \frac{1}{m \cdot n} $$ > *Conversion logic:* The terms $m^2$ and $(a/d)^2 n^2$ come from the second derivatives; the factor $1/(mn)$ arises from the antisymmetric cross-coupling of the shear stress field. ### Step 4 — Minimise w.r.t. m and n to Get the Lowest Critical Load For a given aspect ratio $\alpha = a/d$, the minimum of $N_{xy,cr}$ is achieved when: $$ \frac{m^2 + \alpha^2 n^2}{mn} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial m}[\cdots] = 0 \Rightarrow m = n \cdot \alpha $$ Substituting this back, the **non-dimensional critical shear coefficient** $k_v$ emerges: $$ N_{xy,cr} = k_v \cdot \frac{\pi^2 \cdot D}{d^2} $$ where: $$ k_v = \begin{cases} 5.35 & \text{when } \dfrac{a}{d} \geq \sqrt{2} \\ 4.0 + \dfrac{5.35}{(a/d)^2} & \text{when } \dfrac{a}{d} < \sqrt{2} \end{cases} $$ ### Step 5 — Convert to Critical Shear Stress Since $N_{xy,cr} = \tau_{cr} \cdot t_w$: $$ \tau_{cr} \cdot t_w = k_v \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{d^2} \cdot \frac{E \cdot t_w^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} $$ Solving for $\tau_{cr}$: $$ \boxed{\tau_{cr} = \frac{k_v \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{t_w}{d}\right)^2} $$ > *Conversion logic:* This is the **classical plate-buckling formula** for shear, derived from the differential equation of plate equilibrium combined with the assumed sinusoidal displacement field. It forms the foundation of the **tension-field theory** extension used in IS 800:2007. ## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Section of Bearing Stiffener (Column Model) A bearing stiffener is essentially a **short column built into the web**. IS 800 prescribes that only a strip of web of width **20·t_w** on either side of the stiffener pair acts compositely with the two outstanding legs. ### Step 1 — Define the Effective Cross-Section Consider a **double-stiffener arrangement** (two flat plates of size $b_s \times t_s$ on either side of the web): $$ A_{eff} = 2 \cdot b_s \cdot t_s + 20 \cdot t_w^2 $$ > *Conversion logic:* The web strip of width $20 t_w$ is included because **local buckling of the web in this region is prevented** by the stiffener; this is the same principle as for an "outstand-of-element" slenderness limit. ### Step 2 — Radius of Gyration About the Centroidal Axis Parallel to the Web The two stiffener plates lie on the web centre-line, so the moment of inertia about the web axis (centroidal axis through web) is: $$ I_{eff} = 2 \cdot \left[ \frac{t_s \cdot b_s^3}{12} \right] = \frac{t_s \cdot b_s^3}{6} $$ Radius of gyration: $$ r = \sqrt{\frac{I_{eff}}{A_{eff}}} $$ ### Step 3 — Effective Length of Bearing Stiffener Column Per IS 800:2007, the bearing stiffener column is treated as **fixed at the loaded flange and pinned at the opposite flange**. For this end condition: $$ L_e = 0.7 \cdot d $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Slenderness Ratio $$ \lambda = \frac{L_e / r}{93.9 \cdot \varepsilon} $$ > *Conversion logic:* The term $93.9 \cdot \varepsilon$ is the **squash limit slenderness** at which the column is at the transition point between yielding and Euler buckling. ### Step 5 — Buckling Class Selection Per **Table 11 of IS 800:2007** (Buckling Class of Cross-Sections), for a double-angle/-plate with web restraint on one side, buckling about the web axis corresponds to **Class 'c'** (for plates) or **Class 'b'** (for rolled sections). Choose $\phi = 0.49$ (Class 'b') or $\phi = 0.61$ (Class 'c'). ### Step 6 — Design Compressive Stress $$ f_{cd} = \frac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda^2}} \leq \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} $$ ### Step 7 — Design Strength Check $$ P \leq P_d = A_{eff} \cdot f_{cd} $$ > *Conversion logic:* This is the **Perry-Robertson formula** modified for Indian code calibration. The square-root denominator captures the **secant column formula** behaviour, while $\phi$ embeds the initial imperfection. ## 3.3 Numerical Validation — A Worked Check Let us verify a typical **intermediate stiffener** for a plate girder with $d = 1500$ mm, $t_w = 12$ mm, $a = 1500$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa. **Step 1 — Slenderness:** $$ \lambda_w = \frac{d}{t_w} = \frac{1500}{12} = 125 $$ **Step 2 — Buckling coefficient:** $$ a/d = 1.0 < \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow k_v = 4.0 + 5.35/(1.0)^2 = 9.35 $$ **Step 3 — Critical stress:** $$ \tau_{cr} = \frac{9.35 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^5}{12(1 - 0.09)} \cdot \left(\frac{12}{1500}\right)^2 \approx 65.4 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 4 — Plastic shear capacity:** $$ \tau_{p} = \frac{f_y}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{250}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 144.3 \text{ MPa} $$ Since $\tau_{cr} < \tau_p$, the web is **shear-buckling critical**, and **intermediate stiffeners are mandatory**. **Step 5 — Required stiffness of stiffener:** $$ I_{s,req} = \frac{1.5 \cdot 1500^3 \cdot 12^3}{1500^2} = 1.5 \cdot 1500 \cdot 1728 = 3.888 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$ Provide a flat of size $100 \times 12$ mm on either side, giving $I_s = t_s \cdot b_s^3/3 = 12 \cdot 100^3 / 3 = 4.0 \times 10^6$ mm⁴. **Stiffness is satisfied.** ✓ # 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid) ## 4.1 Classification Flow of Stiffeners in Plate Girders ```mermaid flowchart TD A[STIFFENERS IN PLATE GIRDERS]:::main A --> B1[Intermediate Stiffeners]:::type1 A --> B2[Bearing Stiffeners]:::type2 A --> B3[Longitudinal Stiffeners]:::type3 A --> B4[Diagonal Torsional Stiffeners]:::type4 B1 --> C1[Resist Shear Buckling] B1 --> C2[Stiffness check I_s] B1 --> C3[Strength check A_sv] B2 --> D1[Transmit Concentrated Load] B2 --> D2[Bearing check 0.75 fy] B2 --> D3[Column buckling check] B3 --> E1[Subdivide Web Panel] B3 --> E2[Located at 0.2d from flange] B4 --> F1[Provide Lateral Torsional Restraint] B4 --> F2[Use at Support Regions] classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef type1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef type2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef type3 fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef type4 fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Force-Transfer Architecture in a Bearing Stiffener Assembly ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Concentrated Load P]:::load A --> B[Top Flange]:::flange B --> C[Bearing Stiffener Pair]:::bs C --> D[Effective Column Section
2b_s t_s + 20 t_w sq]:::eff D --> E[Check Bearing Stress
0.75 fy]:::cb D --> F[Check Column Buckling
P d = A eff f cd]:::cc E --> G[Safely Transfer to Web]:::ok F --> G G --> H[Bottom Flange / Support]:::flange classDef load fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef flange fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef bs fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef eff fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef cb fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef cc fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef ok fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Decision Logic for Intermediate Stiffener Design ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Given d, t_w, a, f_y]:::start S2[Compute lambda_w = d / t_w]:::calc S3{tau_b vs 0.4 tau_cr}:::decision S4[No stiffener required]:::nos S5{tau_cr vs tau_p}:::decision S6[Inelastic buckling regime]:::inelastic S7[Elastic buckling regime]:::elastic S8[Compute I_s,req from Cl 8.6.1.2]:::calc S9[Choose b_s and t_s]:::choose S10[Check Outstand Limit 20 eps t_q]:::check S11[Provide Intermediate Stiffener]:::provide S12[Check Shear Capacity]:::final S1 --> S2 --> S3 S3 -- Less --> S4 S3 -- Greater --> S5 S5 -- Less --> S6 --> S8 S5 -- Greater --> S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9 --> S10 --> S11 --> S12 classDef start fill:#002060,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef calc fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef decision fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef nos fill:#a6a6a6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef inelastic fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef elastic fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef choose fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef check fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef provide fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef final fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** > > **Q1.** Differentiate clearly between **intermediate stiffeners** and **bearing stiffeners** in a plate girder. State at least two distinct design checks performed for each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Intermediate Stiffeners | Bearing Stiffeners | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Purpose** | Prevent shear buckling of the web | Transfer concentrated loads / reactions to web | | **External Load** | Designed for no external axial load | Designed to carry the full concentrated load | | **Design as** | A flexural element (stiffness) | A short column (axial + bearing) | | **Stiffness Check** | $I_s \geq 1.5 d^3 t_w^3 / a^2$ | Not required | | **Bearing Check** | Not required | $\sigma_b \leq 0.75 f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | | **Buckling Check** | Outstand slenderness limit | Effective column buckling check (Cl. 7.1) | | **Location** | Distributed along span | At points of load / reaction application | | **Connection to Flange** | Tight fit, often not welded to tension flange | Full weld to both flanges | --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** > > **Q2.** What is the **imperfection factor (ε)** as per IS 800:2007? Why is it used in the column-buckling formula? Compute ε for $f_y = 415$ MPa. **Model Answer:** The **imperfection factor** $\varepsilon$ accounts for unavoidable initial bow, residual stresses, and load eccentricities in real columns that are absent in an ideal Euler column. It is defined as: $$ \varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{f_y}} $$ For $f_y = 415$ MPa: $$ \varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{415}} = \sqrt{0.6024} = 0.776 $$ It enters the column-buckling formula as $\lambda = (L_e/r) / (93.9 \cdot \varepsilon)$, thus effectively **reducing the allowable slenderness** for higher-strength steels, ensuring consistent safety margins. --- ## 5.2 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, ESE Pattern]** > > **Q3(a) [Module Choice A] — Question A (14 Marks = 7 + 7)** A simply-supported **plate girder** of span **18 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **50 kN/m** (including self-weight) over its entire span. The section consists of: - Flanges: 400 mm × 20 mm (Fe 410 grade, $f_y = 250$ MPa) - Web: 1500 mm × 12 mm - Stiffener spacing: $a = 1500$ mm (transverse stiffeners provided) **Design the intermediate transverse stiffeners** for this plate girder. Check: **(i)** Whether stiffeners are required (Cl. 8.6.1.1 of IS 800:2007). **(7 marks)** **(ii)** Stiffness and outstand requirements, and propose the stiffener size. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### (i) — Check Whether Stiffeners Are Required **Step 1 — Maximum Shear Force at Support:** $$ V_{max} = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{50 \times 18}{2} = 450 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 2 — Average Shear Stress in Web:** $$ \tau_b = \frac{V_{max}}{d \cdot t_w} = \frac{450 \times 10^3}{1500 \times 12} = 25.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 25.0 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3 — Aspect Ratio and Buckling Coefficient:** Since $a/d = 1500/1500 = 1.0 < \sqrt{2} = 1.414$: $$ k_v = 4.0 + \frac{5.35}{(1.0)^2} = 9.35 $$ **Step 4 — Critical Shear Stress (Elastic):** $$ \tau_{cr} = \frac{9.35 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^5}{12(1 - 0.09)} \cdot \left(\frac{12}{1500}\right)^2 $$ Evaluating step by step: $$ \tau_{cr} = \frac{9.35 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{10.92} \times \left(8.0 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2 $$ $$ = \frac{1.846 \times 10^7}{10.92} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ = 1.6905 \times 10^6 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 108.2 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 5 — Plastic Shear Capacity:** $$ \tau_{p} = \frac{f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} = \frac{250}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.1} = 131.2 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 6 — Compare (per Cl. 8.6.1.1):** We must check: $\tau_b > 0.4 \cdot \tau_{cr}$? $$ 0.4 \cdot \tau_{cr} = 0.4 \times 108.2 = 43.3 \text{ MPa} $$ Since $\tau_b = 25.0 \text{ MPa} < 43.3 \text{ MPa}$, **intermediate stiffeners are NOT theoretically required** for shear-buckling resistance. > *However*, since $\tau_b = 25.0 \text{ MPa} > 0.4 \cdot \tau_p$ is also evaluated, and for the more rigorous design, the **Code recommends stiffeners** if the web is slender ($\lambda_w = 125 > 67\varepsilon$ for Fe 410, since $\varepsilon = 1.0$). **Practically, intermediate stiffeners are provided** to meet code recommendation for slender webs. #### (ii) — Stiffness & Outstand Requirements **Step 1 — Imperfection Factor:** $$ \varepsilon = \sqrt{250/250} = 1.0 $$ **Step 2 — Outstand Limit (Cl. 8.6.1.3.1):** For an outstanding leg: $$ b_s \leq 20 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot t_q = 20 \times 1.0 \times 12 = 240 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Required Stiffener Moment of Inertia (Cl. 8.6.1.2):** $$ I_{s,req} = \frac{1.5 \cdot d^3 \cdot t_w^3}{a^2} = \frac{1.5 \times 1500^3 \times 12^3}{1500^2} $$ $$ = 1.5 \times 1500 \times 1728 = 3.888 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$ **Step 4 — Trial Stiffener Size:** Provide flat of $90 \times 12$ mm on each side of the web. Moment of inertia about the web face: $$ I_{s,prov} = \frac{t_s \cdot b_s^3}{3} = \frac{12 \times 90^3}{3} = 2.916 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$ **Step 5 — Insufficient!** Try $100 \times 12$ mm: $$ I_{s,prov} = \frac{12 \times 100^3}{3} = 4.0 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 > 3.888 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 6 — Strength Check of Stiffener (Cl. 8.6.1.3.2):** Axial force in stiffener due to tension-field action: $$ F_{ts} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot (V - V_{cr}) \cdot \cot\theta $$ For simplicity, take $V_{cr} = \tau_{cr} \cdot d \cdot t_w = 108.2 \times 1500 \times 12 = 1947.6$ kN, which is greater than $V_{max} = 450$ kN, indicating no tension field. So axial force in stiffener ≈ 0; **strength is automatically OK** as long as stiffness is satisfied. **Step 7 — Final Selection:** > **Provide intermediate stiffeners of 100 mm × 12 mm flat on each side of the web, at a spacing of 1500 mm c/c, welded to the web continuously and to the compression flange only.** --- ### ✅ Alternative Sub-Question (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, ESE Pattern]** > > **Q3(b) [Module Choice B] — Question B (14 Marks = 7 + 7)** A plate girder of span **12 m** is supported on a column at one end. The end reaction is **600 kN**. The web dimensions are: $d = 1200$ mm, $t_w = 10$ mm. Use $f_y = 250$ MPa. **Design the bearing stiffener** for this plate girder. Show all steps including: **(i)** Selection of trial section and the bearing-stress check. **(7 marks)** **(ii)** Column-buckling check of the effective bearing section. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### (i) — Trial Section & Bearing Check **Step 1 — Bearing Area Required:** Bearing strength criterion (Cl. 10.4.4): $$ A_{sb,req} = \frac{P \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{0.75 \cdot f_y} = \frac{600 \times 10^3 \times 1.1}{0.75 \times 250} = 3520 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Trial Stiffener Size:** Assume bearing stiffener of size **100 mm × 12 mm** flat on each side of web. Bearing contact length (cut to clear flange roots): take as $b_s = 90$ mm effective. Total bearing area (two stiffeners): $$ A_{sb} = 2 \times 90 \times 12 = 2160 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Insufficient bearing area!** Try $150 \times 16$ mm: $$ A_{sb} = 2 \times 140 \times 16 = 4480 \text{ mm}^2 > 3520 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 4 — Bearing-Stress Check:** $$ \sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_{sb}} = \frac{600 \times 10^3}{4480} = 133.9 \text{ MPa} $$ Allowable: $0.75 f_y / \gamma_{m0} = 0.75 \times 250 / 1.1 = 170.5$ MPa. Since $133.9 < 170.5$ MPa, **bearing is safe**. ✓ #### (ii) — Column-Buckling Check **Step 1 — Effective Section:** A strip of web of width $20 t_w = 200$ mm on each side of the stiffener pair acts compositely: $$ A_{eff} = 2 \times 140 \times 16 + 200 \times 10 = 4480 + 2000 = 6480 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Moment of Inertia (about web axis):** $$ I_{eff} = 2 \times \left( \frac{16 \times 140^3}{12} \right) = 2 \times 3.659 \times 10^6 = 7.318 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$ **Step 3 — Radius of Gyration:** $$ r = \sqrt{\frac{7.318 \times 10^6}{6480}} = \sqrt{1129.4} = 33.6 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 4 — Effective Length (Fixed-Pinned, Cl. 7.1.2.2):** $$ L_e = 0.7 \cdot d = 0.7 \times 1200 = 840 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 5 — Imperfection Factor:** $$ \varepsilon = \sqrt{250/250} = 1.0 $$ **Step 6 — Slenderness Ratio:** $$ \lambda = \frac{L_e / r}{93.9 \cdot \varepsilon} = \frac{840 / 33.6}{93.9} = \frac{25.0}{93.9} = 0.266 $$ **Step 7 — Buckling Class & $\phi$:** For plates on one side of web (about web axis), **Class 'c'** from Table 11, $\phi = 0.61$. **Step 8 — Design Compressive Stress:** $$ f_{cd} = \frac{f_y/\gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda^2}} = \frac{227.27}{0.61 + \sqrt{0.61^2 - 0.266^2}} $$ $$ = \frac{227.27}{0.61 + \sqrt{0.3721 - 0.0708}} = \frac{227.27}{0.61 + 0.5497} = \frac{227.27}{1.1597} = 195.97 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 9 — Design Strength:** $$ P_d = A_{eff} \cdot f_{cd} = 6480 \times 195.97 = 1.270 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1270 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 10 — Safety Check:** Since $P = 600$ kN $< P_d = 1270$ kN, **the bearing stiffener is safe in column buckling**. ✓ > **Final Design:** Provide **2 stiffeners of 150 × 16 mm flats** on each side of web, **full-butt-welded to both flanges** and **continuous-fillet-welded to the web**, with **root gaps cut to clear flange-to-web welds (typically 25–40 mm)**. --- > [!WARNING] > ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > > 1. **Forgetting the 20·t_w web strip** in the effective column area of bearing stiffeners — this costs **2–3 marks** in almost every answer. > 2. **Wrong buckling class:** Treating a pair of flats as "Class 'a'" (rolled section) instead of **Class 'c'** (welded plate) — this inflates $P_d$ artificially and gives an unsafe (and incorrect) design. > 3. **Using the wrong slenderness limit** for outstand of intermediate stiffeners — students often write "16·ε·t_q" (which is for compression flanges) instead of the correct **20·ε·t_q** for stiffeners (Cl. 8.6.1.3.1). > 4. **Forgetting to weld stiffener to compression flange** — for intermediate stiffeners, the code restricts welding to the compression flange only to avoid fatigue cracks in the tension flange zone. > 5. **Mixing up $\gamma_{m0}$ (1.10) and $\gamma_{m1}$ (1.25)** — the former is for material strength at ULS, the latter for bolts. The bearing check uses $\gamma_{m0}$. > 6. **Skipping the outstand limit check** for intermediate stiffeners — even if stiffness is satisfied, outstand b_s ≤ 20·ε·t_q must be independently verified. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > **Bookmark this section for the night before your exam.** - [ ] **Stiffeners are mandatory** if $\tau_b > 0.4 \tau_{cr}$ (Cl. 8.6.1.1). - [ ] **Buckling coefficient $k_v$** switches from $4.0 + 5.35/(a/d)^2$ to $5.35$ at $a/d = \sqrt{2}$. - [ ] **Stiffness criterion:** $I_s \geq 1.5 d^3 t_w^3 / a^2$ — this is **non-negotiable**. - [ ] **Outstand limit:** $b_s \leq 20 \varepsilon t_q$ for intermediate stiffeners. - [ ] **Bearing stiffeners** are designed as **short columns** + **bearing stress check**. - [ ] **Effective bearing area** = 2 × (stiffener contact length) × thickness. - [ ] **Effective buckling area** = 2 × $b_s t_s$ + 20 $t_w^2$ (web strip). - [ ] **Effective length** of bearing stiffener column = **0.7·d** (fixed-pinned). - [ ] **Buckling class** for welded double-flat = **Class 'c'** (φ = 0.61). - [ ] **Design compressive stress** formula is Perry-Robertson (Cl. 7.1.2.1). - [ ] **Partial safety factor** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ for steel. - [ ] **Imperfection factor** $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/f_y}$ — decreases as $f_y$ increases. - [ ] **Longitudinal stiffeners** (if used) are placed at $h_1 = 0.2d$ from compression flange. - [ ] **Connect intermediate stiffeners** to web by continuous fillet weld, to **compression flange only**. - [ ] **Connect bearing stiffeners** to web by **intermittent or continuous fillet weld**, to **both flanges** by full strength weld. - [ ] **Always check** $f_{cd} \leq f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ to ensure no plastic over-strength is assumed. - [ ] **Units rule:** $E$ in N/mm², $I$ in mm⁴, $r$ in mm, $L_e$ in mm, $\sigma$ in N/mm² = MPa. - [ ] **In tension-field design (Annex G of IS 800)** — when used, intermediate stiffeners also carry axial force $F_{ts} = (\pi/2)(V - V_{cr})\cot\theta$. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, expect a **direct 14-mark question on bearing stiffener design** OR a **2-part question splitting intermediate vs bearing stiffener checks**. Practice **at least 3 numerical problems** covering both, and **memorize the formula sheet** in §2.3 above for the 3-mark short-answer section.

topics.title: Design of Gantry Girders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Gantry Girders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gantry Girder** (also termed a **crane girder** or **crane runway beam**) is a simply-supported, laterally-restrained flexural member spanning between the columns of an industrial building, specifically engineered to carry the rolling wheel loads of an overhead Electric Overhead Travelling (EOT) crane. The girder transfers the vertical wheel reactions, the lateral surge forces, and the longitudinal tractive forces of the moving crane to the supporting columns, while simultaneously providing a continuous running track for the crane rails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET864 – Module 3):**
> The course mandates a *complete working-design* of a gantry girder — including preliminary section selection, moment/shear capacity checks, deflection serviceability, lateral-torsional buckling (LTB) verification, and stiffener detailing — in strict compliance with **IS 800:2007** and **IS 875 (Part 2):1987** for crane loads.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **railway platform** in a large factory. Trains don't run on it; instead, a giant **movable workshop crane** does. The rails of this crane are not laid on the ground — they are mounted on **elevated horizontal beams** that run along the factory walls, supported by the **vertical columns** of the building. Those elevated beams are the *gantry girders*.

Every time the crane picks up a load, three things happen simultaneously:
1. The crane's wheels press **down** on the girder (vertical load).
2. When the crane **accelerates, decelerates, or swings** a load sideways, the girder is pushed **horizontally** across its weak axis (surge force).
3. When the crane moves **along the bay**, a horizontal force is transferred **along the girder's length** (longitudinal force).

So a gantry girder must resist **bending, torsion, and sideways shove** — all from a moving, swaying mass. This is why it is one of the most demanding flexural members in structural steelwork.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants / standard metrics to memorize:**
> - **Impact factor (electric cranes):** 1.25 (per IS 875 Part 2, for slow-moving cranes) and up to 1.5 for heavy dynamic operation.
> - **Lateral surge force (per wheel):** **5%** of the combined weight of (crane trolley + crab + lifted load) for electric cranes.
> - **Longitudinal force (per rail):** **5%** of the maximum static wheel load, applied at the rail level.

## 1.3 Classification of Gantry Girders

| Type | Description | Typical Span |
|---|---|---|
| **Plate Gantry Girder** | Built-up I-section with web + flanges welded/bolted | Spans > 25 m, heavy cranes (> 100 kN) |
| **Rolled Section Girder** | Hot-rolled ISMB / ISWB section used directly | Spans < 12 m, light cranes |
| **Trussed Gantry Girder** | Open-web lattice for very heavy cranes | Industrial heavy mills |
| **Box Gantry Girder** | Twin I-sections or box with high lateral stiffness | Very heavy EOT, asymmetric cranes |

> [!TIP]
> KTU university problems almost exclusively test **plate-type built-up gantry girders**, because it allows a full design exercise including flange curtailment, bearing/intermediate stiffeners, and buckling checks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Loading Philosophy — The Three Force Vectors

A gantry girder is unique because it must resist **three orthogonal force systems simultaneously**:

1. **Vertical (Gravity) Wheel Loads (W):** Maximum reaction from the crane wheel.
2. **Transverse Horizontal (Surge) Force (H):** Perpendicular to the runway, applied at the top of the rail.
3. **Longitudinal Horizontal (Traction) Force (F):** Parallel to the runway, applied at the rail level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **transverse surge** is what differentiates gantry design from ordinary beam design. It produces a *bending moment about the weak axis (z-z)* of the section, which in combination with the *strong-axis moment (y-y)* and *torsional effects* makes the section highly stressed.

## 2.2 Codal Load Magnitudes (IS 875 Part 2)

| Load Component | Magnitude | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertical Wheel Load, W** | Maximum static wheel reaction (from crane catalogue) | ↓ Downward |
| **Impact on Vertical Load** | Multiply W by **1.25** (electric, light duty) | ↓ Downward |
| **Transverse Surge, Hₜ** | **5 % × (Weight of crab + trolley + load lifted)** per wheel | ⊥ to rail, at rail level |
| **Longitudinal Force, Hₗ** | **5 % × Maximum static wheel load** per rail | ∥ to rail, at rail level |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU valuation pitfall: students **double-count** the impact factor. Apply the 1.25 multiplier **only to the vertical gravity loads**, NOT to the horizontal surge or longitudinal forces.

## 2.3 Critical Sectional Forces (Influence Line Logic)

For a simply-supported gantry girder of span **L**, carrying two moving wheel loads **W₁** and **W₂** separated by a fixed spacing **a**:

- **Maximum Bending Moment** occurs when the **resultant** of the wheel loads and the **mid-span** are equidistant from the **centre of the span** (i.e., the heavier wheel and the resultant are symmetric about mid-span).
- **Maximum Shear Force** occurs when one wheel is placed **infinitesimally close to the support**, on either side.

The **absolute maximum moment** under a wheel load system is:

$$M_{max} = \frac{R}{L} \cdot \left( \frac{L}{2} - \frac{x}{2} \right)^2 - M_{x}$$

where R is the resultant reaction and x is the distance of the resultant from the nearest wheel.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cram Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design Bending Moment (strong axis) | $M_{y,d} = \dfrac{1.25 \, W \cdot L}{a}$ (for single crane, simply supported) | — |
| 2 | Transverse surge moment (weak axis) | $M_{z,d} = H_t \cdot \dfrac{L}{a}$ (factor depends on bracing location) | IS 875 Pt. 2 |
| 3 | Design Shear Force | $V_d = 1.25 \, W \left( 1 + \dfrac{a}{L} \right)$ for two wheels | — |
| 4 | Section Modulus required (approx.) | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{y,d}}{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.2.1 |
| 5 | Permissible vertical deflection | $\delta_{perm} = \dfrac{L}{750}$ to $\dfrac{L}{1000}$ | IS 800 Table 6 |
| 6 | Permissible horizontal sway | $\delta_{H,perm} = \dfrac{L}{400}$ | IS 800 Table 6 |
| 7 | Shear capacity of web (no buckling) | $V_{d} = \dfrac{f_{yw} \cdot t_w \cdot h_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}}$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.4.1 |
| 8 | Shear buckling check (stiffened web) | $\tau_b = \dfrac{V_d}{t_w \cdot h_w} \le \dfrac{f_{yw}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{m0} \cdot \lambda_w^{0.5+...}}$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.4.2 |
| 9 | Combined Bending + Shear | $\dfrac{M_y}{M_{d,y}} + \dfrac{M_z}{M_{d,z}} \le 1.0$ | IS 800 Cl. 9.3.1 |
| 10 | Web crippling (bearing) | $F_{w} = (b + n_2) \cdot t_w \cdot f_{yw} / \gamma_{m0}$ | IS 800 Cl. 8.7.4 |
| 11 | Partial safety factor, $\gamma_{m0}$ | **1.10** | IS 800 Table 5 |
| 12 | Partial safety factor, $\gamma_{m1}$ | **1.25** | IS 800 Table 5 |
| 13 | Yield stress, $f_y$ (Fe 410) | **250 MPa** | IS 2062 |
| 14 | Yield stress, $f_y$ (Fe 500) | **340 MPa** (for crane girders, generally Fe 410 used) | IS 2062 |
| 15 | Modulus of Elasticity, E | **2.0 × 10⁵ MPa** | IS 800 |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical and horizontal deflections are checked using the **unfactored** (working) loads, while the **strength** checks use the **factored** loads (1.5 DL + 1.5 IL, or simplified as **1.5 × (W + impact + surge combined)** depending on load combination adopted).

## 2.5 Design Philosophy — Strength + Serviceability + Fatigue

The design of a gantry girder is governed by **three simultaneous criteria** that must *all* be satisfied:

> [!TIP]
> **Pillar 1 — Ultimate Strength:** Moment capacity, shear capacity, web buckling, web crippling, bearing stiffener design.
> **Pillar 2 — Serviceability (Deflection):** Vertical and horizontal deflections must be within code limits. For long spans, **deflection, not stress, often governs the section size**.
> **Pillar 3 — Fatigue (for heavy duty cranes):** Class of crane (light/medium/heavy/very heavy) determines the number of cycles. Fatigue checks apply for **> 20,000 cycles** (Medium) and **> 2,00,000 cycles** (Heavy). For light cranes (< 20,000 cycles), fatigue is exempted.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real Practice

Gantry girders are the **backbone of every steel-framed industrial plant** — power stations, steel mills, shipyards, warehouses, and assembly lines. Without an accurately designed gantry, the crane's safe operation (lifting loads of 5 tonnes to 500 tonnes) cannot be guaranteed. A failed gantry girder collapses catastrophically, endangering workers and halting industrial output — making this design exercise a **safety-critical activity**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Influence Line for Absolute Maximum Moment under Two Wheel Loads

### Statement of the Problem

Given a simply-supported gantry girder of span **L** carrying two crane wheel loads **W₁** and **W₂** at a fixed spacing **a** (W₁ > W₂, positioned left to right), derive the condition for **absolute maximum bending moment** and its magnitude.

### Step 1 — Define the Resultant

The resultant of the two wheel loads is:

$$R = W_1 + W_2$$

Let the resultant **R** be located at a distance **x̄** from **W₁** (measured to the right). Taking moments about W₁:

$$R \cdot \bar{x} = W_2 \cdot a$$

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{W_2 \cdot a}{R} = \dfrac{W_2 \cdot a}{W_1 + W_2}$$

### Step 2 — Position the Resultant at Mid-span

For the absolute maximum moment, place the resultant **R** and the **mid-span** of the girder at equal distances from the **centre of the span**. Equivalently, the average of the two wheel-load positions must equal **L/2**.

Let the resultant **R** be at a distance **c** from mid-span (to the right). Then the position of the heavier wheel **W₁** from the left support is:

$$x_1 = \dfrac{L}{2} - c - \bar{x} \cdot (\text{sign convention})$$

A more elegant form: position **W₁** such that the **centre of the beam bisects the distance between W₁ and R**.

### Step 3 — Compute the Maximum Moment

The reaction at the left support is:

$$R_A = \dfrac{R \cdot (L/2 + c)}{L} \cdot \text{(after geometry)}$$

The maximum moment under **W₁** equals the moment of the **left reaction** about **W₁** plus the moment of any other wheels to its left (none, in this case):

$$M_{max} = R_A \cdot x_1 = \dfrac{R}{L} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L}{2} - \dfrac{\bar{x}}{2} \right)^2 - M_{between}$$

The closed-form expression simplifies to:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{R}{L} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L - \bar{x}}{2} \right)^2 - W_2 \cdot \bar{x}$$

### Step 4 — Symmetric Special Case (W₁ = W₂ = W)

When the two wheels are equal and equally loaded:

$$R = 2W, \quad \bar{x} = \dfrac{a}{2}$$

Substituting into the general expression:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{2W}{L} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L - a/2}{2} \right)^2 - W \cdot \dfrac{a}{2}$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} = \dfrac{W}{2L} \cdot \left( L - \dfrac{a}{2} \right)^2 - \dfrac{W a}{2}}$$

This is the **standard KTU-board favourite** formula for two-wheel crane loading.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Design of a Plate Gantry Girder

### Problem Data (Standard KTU-style)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Span of gantry girder, **L** | **8.0 m** (centre-to-centre of columns) |
| Crane capacity (SWL) | **100 kN** |
| Self-weight of crane (excluding trolley) | **75 kN** |
| Self-weight of trolley + crab | **25 kN** |
| Weight of crane bridge | **125 kN** (so total moving weight = 25 + SWL = 125 kN) |
| Number of wheels per side rail | **2** (4-wheel crane) |
| Wheel base (spacing between wheels on same rail) | **3.5 m** |
| Steel grade | **Fe 410** ($f_y$ = **250 MPa**) |
| Stiffener spacing | Assume 1.0 m initially |

### Step 5 — Compute Maximum Static Wheel Load

The maximum load lifted is the **SWL = 100 kN**. The total weight transferred to **one rail** (assuming two rails) is half of the bridge weight plus the full trolley/load weight:

$$W_{rail} = \dfrac{125}{2} + (25 + 100) = 62.5 + 125 = 187.5 \text{ kN}$$

With **2 wheels per rail**, the maximum wheel load (assuming equal distribution):

$$W = \dfrac{187.5}{2} = 93.75 \text{ kN per wheel}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Impact Factor

For an electric crane (per IS 875):

$$W_{factored} = 1.25 \times 93.75 = 117.19 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 7 — Transverse Surge Force (per wheel)

The surge is **5% of (trolley + crab + lifted load)** = 5% of (25 + 100) = 6.25 kN per wheel.

$$H_t = 0.05 \times 125 = 6.25 \text{ kN per wheel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This force acts **horizontally**, perpendicular to the girder length, **at the level of the top of the rail** (typically 100–150 mm above the top flange of the girder).

### Step 8 — Maximum Bending Moment (Strong Axis)

Using the symmetric two-wheel formula with **W = 117.19 kN**, **L = 8.0 m**, **a = 3.5 m**:

$$M_{max,y} = \dfrac{117.19}{2 \times 8.0} \cdot \left( 8.0 - \dfrac{3.5}{2} \right)^2 - \dfrac{117.19 \times 3.5}{2}$$

Intermediate arithmetic:

$$8.0 - 1.75 = 6.25 \text{ m}$$

$$6.25^2 = 39.0625 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\dfrac{117.19 \times 39.0625}{16} = \dfrac{4577.73}{16} = 286.11 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$117.19 \times 1.75 = 205.08 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{max,y} = 286.11 - 205.08 = \boxed{81.03 \text{ kN·m}}$$

### Step 9 — Maximum Shear Force (Strong Axis)

Maximum shear occurs when a wheel is just inside the support. With two wheels (W each, spacing a), the absolute maximum shear is:

$$V_{max} = W \left( 1 + \dfrac{a}{L} \right) = 117.19 \times \left( 1 + \dfrac{3.5}{8.0} \right)$$

$$V_{max} = 117.19 \times 1.4375 = \boxed{168.46 \text{ kN}}$$

### Step 10 — Weak-Axis Moment due to Surge

The surge is resisted by the **lateral bracing system** (channel running along the compression flange). If the bracing is provided at the top flange, the weak-axis moment is taken by the entire unbraced length:

$$M_{z} = \dfrac{H_t \cdot L}{4} = \dfrac{6.25 \times 8.0}{4} = 12.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU convention: when the surge force is transferred at the **top flange** through a **horizontal channel (lateral bracing)**, the *lateral bending is assumed to be resisted by the channel and the top flange acting together*. The bottom flange is taken as a simple support laterally. Thus the lateral moment reduces to $H_t L/4$ in lieu of $H_t L/a$.

### Step 11 — Required Section Modulus (Strong Axis)

$$Z_{p,req} = \dfrac{M_{max,y} \times 10^6}{f_y / \gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{81.03 \times 10^6}{250 / 1.10}$$

$$Z_{p,req} = \dfrac{81.03 \times 10^6}{227.27} = \boxed{356{,}534 \text{ mm}^3} = 356.5 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 12 — Trial Section Selection

Try a **ISWB 400** rolled section (or design a built-up plate girder):
- For demonstration, assume a **built-up plate girder**:
  - Web: **600 mm × 10 mm**
  - Flanges: **2 × 200 mm × 20 mm** (one on each side of web)
  - Steel Fe 410

**Section Properties:**

$$I_y = \dfrac{t_w \cdot h_w^3}{12} + 2 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{b_f \cdot t_f^3}{12} + b_f \cdot t_f \cdot \left( \dfrac{h_w + t_f}{2} \right)^2 \right]$$

$$I_y = \dfrac{10 \times 600^3}{12} + 2 \times \dfrac{200 \times 20^3}{12} + 2 \times (200 \times 20) \times (310)^2$$

$$I_y = 1.8 \times 10^8 + 2.667 \times 10^5 + 2 \times 4000 \times 96100$$

$$I_y = 1.8 \times 10^8 + 2.667 \times 10^5 + 7.688 \times 10^8$$

$$I_y = 9.491 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$Z_{p,y} = \dfrac{I_y}{(h_w/2 + t_f)} = \dfrac{9.491 \times 10^8}{320} = 2.966 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$Z_{p,y} = 2966 \text{ cm}^3 \gg 356.5 \text{ cm}^3 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Section is grossly overdesigned — refine in practice)}$$

### Step 13 — Shear Capacity Check (Web)

$$V_{d,web} = \dfrac{f_{yw} \cdot t_w \cdot h_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{250 \times 10 \times 600}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10}$$

$$V_{d,web} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^6}{1.905} = 7.874 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 787.4 \text{ kN}$$

Since $V_d$ (787.4 kN) $\gg V_{max}$ (168.46 kN), the web is **safe in shear**.

### Step 14 — Deflection Check (Vertical)

For a simply-supported beam under two-point loads symmetric about the centre, the central deflection is:

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{W a (3L^2 - 4a^2)}{24 E I_y}$$

Plugging in $W = 93.75 \text{ kN}$ (unfactored, working), $a = 3.5 \text{ m}$, $L = 8.0 \text{ m}$:

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{93.75 \times 10^3 \times 3500 \times (3 \times 6400 \times 10^3 - 4 \times 12.25 \times 10^6)}{24 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 9.491 \times 10^8}$$

Working out:

$$3L^2 = 3 \times 64 = 192 \text{ m}^2 = 192 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$4a^2 = 4 \times 12.25 = 49 \text{ m}^2 = 49 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$3L^2 - 4a^2 = 143 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{93750 \times 3500 \times 143 \times 10^6}{24 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 9.491 \times 10^8}$$

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{4.692 \times 10^{16}}{4.556 \times 10^{15}} = 10.30 \text{ mm}$$

Permissible: $\dfrac{L}{750} = \dfrac{8000}{750} = 10.67 \text{ mm}$

Since 10.30 < 10.67, **vertical deflection is OK**. ✓

### Step 15 — Deflection Check (Horizontal)

The horizontal deflection at the top of the rail under surge force $H_t = 6.25$ kN:

$$\delta_H = \dfrac{H_t \cdot L^3}{48 E I_z}$$

For our built-up section, $I_z$ (about weak axis):

$$I_z = 2 \times \dfrac{t_f \cdot b_f^3}{12} = 2 \times \dfrac{20 \times 200^3}{12} = 2.667 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\delta_H = \dfrac{6250 \times 8.0^3 \times 10^9}{48 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 2.667 \times 10^7}$$

$$\delta_H = \dfrac{6250 \times 5.12 \times 10^{11}}{2.560 \times 10^{14}} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

Permissible: $\dfrac{L}{400} = \dfrac{8000}{400} = 20 \text{ mm}$

Since 12.5 < 20, **horizontal sway is OK**. ✓

### Step 16 — Web Buckling Check (Intermediate Stiffener Spacing)

Shear stress in the web:

$$\tau = \dfrac{V_{max}}{t_w \cdot h_w} = \dfrac{168.46 \times 10^3}{10 \times 600} = 28.08 \text{ MPa}$$

Assuming transverse stiffeners at **c/d = 1.0** (i.e., spacing = web depth), the slenderness ratio for shear buckling is checked. With $\lambda_w$ low enough, the web does **not buckle** in shear; only yielding check is needed. We already have $\tau = 28.08$ MPa $< \tau_{per} = 250/\sqrt{3} = 144.3$ MPa. ✓

### Step 17 — Bearing Stiffener Design (At Support)

Bearing force = $V_{max} = 168.46$ kN. Use 2 stiffeners, each of thickness 12 mm, projecting 100 mm beyond the web flange. Check bearing stress and outstanding length for local buckling (per IS 800 Cl. 8.7.4).

$$F_{w,allow} = (b + n_2) t_w \dfrac{f_{yw}}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

With $b = 100$ mm, $n_2 = 50$ mm (edge distance), $t_w = 10$ mm:

$$F_{w,allow} = (100 + 50) \times 10 \times \dfrac{250}{1.10} = 3.41 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 341 \text{ kN} \gg 168.46 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 18 — Combined Bending + Shear Check

Per IS 800 Cl. 9.3.1, when $V_d \le 0.6 V_{d,web}$, the full moment capacity is available. Since:

$$\dfrac{168.46}{787.4} = 0.214 < 0.6$$

Full moment capacity is available. The combined check is automatically satisfied. ✓

### Step 19 — Connection of Rail to Girder

Provide a **100 × 12 mm** sole plate welded to the top flange with **6 mm fillet welds** on either side of the rail. The rail is clamped using **φ 20 mm** hook bolts at 600 mm c/c. Hook bolts carry the **longitudinal force** of 5% of max wheel load:

$$F_L = 0.05 \times 93.75 = 4.69 \text{ kN per bolt}$$

Capacity of φ 20 mm bolt in single shear: $\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 \times 400 = 1.257 \times 10^5$ N = 125.7 kN. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Verified** — the plate girder is **safe** in moment, shear, deflection, and connection. In a KTU board exam, presenting the **summary table** of all checks is essential for full marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Final Summary of the Worked Example

| Check | Demand | Capacity | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moment Capacity (strong axis) | 81.03 kN·m | 2966 cm³ × 227.27 = 674 kN·m | ✅ Safe |
| Moment Capacity (weak axis) | 12.5 kN·m | $Z_z f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | ✅ Safe |
| Shear Capacity | 168.46 kN | 787.4 kN | ✅ Safe |
| Vertical Deflection | 10.30 mm | 10.67 mm | ✅ OK |
| Horizontal Sway | 12.5 mm | 20.0 mm | ✅ OK |
| Web Buckling | τ = 28.08 MPa | 144.3 MPa | ✅ Safe |
| Bearing (Support) | 168.46 kN | 341 kN | ✅ Safe |
| Combined B + S | — | Auto-pass (V < 0.6 Vd) | ✅ Safe |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Gantry Girder System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crane Bridge<br/>Carries Trolley and Load] --> B[Crane End Carriage]
    B --> C[Wheel Pair 1<br/>Left Rail]
    B --> D[Wheel Pair 2<br/>Right Rail]
    C --> E[Vertical Wheel Load W<br/>+ Impact 1.25]
    C --> F[Transverse Surge Ht<br/>5% of lift load]
    C --> G[Longitudinal Force Hl<br/>5% of wheel load]
    E --> H[Gantry Girder<br/>Plate I-Section]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Lateral Bracing Channel<br/>Resists Ht]
    H --> J[Intermediate Stiffeners<br/>Web Buckling]
    H --> K[Bearing Stiffeners<br/>at Supports]
    H --> L[Column<br/>Transfers to Foundation]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Load Transfer Hierarchy (Sequential Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Step 1<br/>Identify Wheel Loads<br/>W, W, a, L] --> L2[Step 2<br/>Apply Impact Factor<br/>1.25 x W]
    L2 --> L3[Step 3<br/>Compute Surge Force<br/>Ht = 0.05 x Lifted]
    L3 --> L4[Step 4<br/>Compute Max Moment<br/>Mmax y axis]
    L4 --> L5[Step 5<br/>Compute Max Shear<br/>Vmax]
    L5 --> L6[Step 6<br/>Trial Section<br/>Plate Girder]
    L6 --> L7[Step 7<br/>Check Moment Capacity]
    L7 --> L8{Step 8<br/>Pass?}
    L8 -- No --> L9[Revise Section<br/>Increase Depth]
    L9 --> L6
    L8 -- Yes --> L10[Step 9<br/>Check Shear Capacity]
    L10 --> L11[Step 10<br/>Check Deflection<br/>Vertical and Horizontal]
    L11 --> L12[Step 11<br/>Web Buckling Check]
    L12 --> L13[Step 12<br/>Stiffener Design]
    L13 --> L14[Step 13<br/>Connection Detailing]
    L14 --> L15[Final Design<br/>Complete]
```

## 4.3 Section Anatomy of a Built-Up Plate Gantry Girder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Top Flange bftf<br/>Resists Bending<br/>Compression Zone] --> S2[Web hwtw<br/>Resists Shear<br/>Buckling Critical]
    S2 --> S3[Bottom Flange bftf<br/>Resists Bending<br/>Tension Zone]
    S2 --> S4[Intermediate Vertical Stiffeners<br/>c/d = 1 to 2<br/>Prevents Web Buckling]
    S2 --> S5[Bearing Stiffeners at Supports<br/>Prevents Web Crippling]
    S2 --> S6[Lateral Bracing Channel<br/>Resists Ht Transverse Surge]
```

## 4.4 Free-Body Diagram — Forces on Gantry Girder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Wheel Load W<br/>Downward Arrow] --> F2[Impact W x 1.25<br/>Downward Arrow]
    F2 --> F3[Surge Ht<br/>Horizontal Arrow<br/>at Top of Rail]
    F3 --> F4[Longitudinal Hl<br/>Horizontal Arrow<br/>Along Rail]
    F4 --> F5[Reaction at Column<br/>Vertical + Horizontal]
    F5 --> F6[Self Weight of Girder<br/>UDL]
```
