# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three salient design requirements of a gantry girder that distinguish it from an ordinary floor beam.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Resistance to transverse horizontal surge force** ($H_t$): The girder must be designed for a lateral bending moment about its weak axis (z-z), caused by the 5% surge from the moving crane trolley. Ordinary beams carry only vertical gravity loads.

2. **Deflection control under moving wheel loads:** The vertical deflection limit is stricter — **L/750 to L/1000** (vs. L/360 for ordinary beams), because excessive sag would cause the crane to "climb" or "run downhill", endangering operations.

3. **Fatigue consideration:** Gantry girders supporting heavy-duty cranes are subject to a large number of load cycles. The section must be checked for **fatigue** (per IS 800 Cl. 13), classifying the detail category. This is not relevant for static floor beams.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define "transverse surge force" and "longitudinal force" in a gantry girder. State their design magnitudes per IS 875 Part 2.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Transverse Surge Force ($H_t$):** The horizontal force exerted by the crane trolley/crab on the gantry girder *perpendicular to the crane runway direction*, arising during acceleration, braking, and lateral sway of the lifted load. **Design magnitude = 5%** of the weight of (trolley + crab + maximum lifted load) for electric cranes, applied at the top of the rail.

- **Longitudinal Force ($H_l$):** The horizontal force exerted *along the runway direction*, caused by the longitudinal acceleration/braking of the crane bridge itself. **Design magnitude = 5%** of the maximum static wheel load, applied at the rail level.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Design a built-up plate gantry girder for the following data:**

- Span (centre-to-centre of columns): **L = 10.0 m**
- Crane capacity (SWL): **150 kN**
- Self-weight of crane bridge: **120 kN**
- Self-weight of trolley + crab: **30 kN**
- Number of wheels per rail: **2**
- Wheel spacing (on same rail): **a = 3.6 m**
- Steel grade: **Fe 410** ($f_y$ = 250 MPa)
- Use Fe 410 plates.

**Assume: Suitable lateral bracing is provided at the top flange to resist the surge force.**

#### Sub-part (a) (7 Marks): Compute the maximum bending moment, maximum shear force, and required section modulus for the gantry girder. Apply impact factor and surge force per IS 875.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum wheel load (static):**

$$\text{Load on one rail} = \dfrac{120}{2} + (30 + 150) = 60 + 180 = 240 \text{ kN}$$

$$W = \dfrac{240}{2} = 120 \text{ kN per wheel (static)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply impact factor:**

$$W_{factored} = 1.25 \times 120 = 150 \text{ kN per wheel (factored)}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending moment (strong axis):**

Using the symmetric two-wheel formula:

$$M_{max,y} = \dfrac{W_{factored}}{2L} \cdot \left( L - \dfrac{a}{2} \right)^2 - \dfrac{W_{factored} \cdot a}{2}$$

$$M_{max,y} = \dfrac{150}{2 \times 10} \cdot \left( 10 - 1.8 \right)^2 - \dfrac{150 \times 3.6}{2}$$

$$M_{max,y} = 7.5 \times (8.2)^2 - 270 = 7.5 \times 67.24 - 270$$

$$M_{max,y} = 504.3 - 270 = \boxed{234.3 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum shear force (strong axis):**

$$V_{max} = W_{factored} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{a}{L} \right) = 150 \times \left( 1 + \dfrac{3.6}{10} \right) = 150 \times 1.36$$

$$V_{max} = \boxed{204 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5 — Required plastic section modulus:**

$$Z_{p,req} = \dfrac{M_{max,y}}{f_y / \gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{234.3 \times 10^6}{250 / 1.10} = \dfrac{234.3 \times 10^6}{227.27}$$

$$Z_{p,req} = \boxed{1.031 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 = 1031 \text{ cm}^3}$$

#### Sub-part (b) (7 Marks): Select a trial plate girder section and verify its adequacy in (i) bending, (ii) shear, and (iii) vertical deflection.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Trial Section:**

Select a built-up plate girder:
- Web: **800 mm × 10 mm**
- Flanges: **2 × 250 mm × 20 mm** (one on each side of web)

**Step 2 — Section Properties:**

$$I_y = \dfrac{10 \times 800^3}{12} + 2 \times \left[ \dfrac{250 \times 20^3}{12} + 250 \times 20 \times (410)^2 \right]$$

$$I_y = 4.267 \times 10^8 + 2 \times \left[ 1.667 \times 10^5 + 8.405 \times 10^8 \right]$$

$$I_y = 4.267 \times 10^8 + 1.681 \times 10^9 = 2.108 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$Z_{p,y} = \dfrac{I_y}{h/2 + t_f/2} = \dfrac{2.108 \times 10^9}{410} = 5.141 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 = 5141 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Check (i) — Bending:**

$$M_{d,y} = Z_{p,y} \times \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = 5.141 \times 10^6 \times 227.27 = 1.169 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm} = 1169 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_{d,y}$ (1169 kN·m) $> M_{max,y}$ (234.3 kN·m), section is **SAFE in bending** with a utilisation ratio of $234.3/1169 = 0.20$. ✓

**Check (ii) — Shear:**

$$V_{d,web} = \dfrac{f_{yw} \cdot t_w \cdot h_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{250 \times 10 \times 800}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10} = 1050 \text{ kN}$$

Since $V_{d,web}$ (1050 kN) $> V_{max}$ (204 kN), section is **SAFE in shear**. ✓

**Check (iii) — Vertical Deflection:**

For a simply-supported girder with two equal symmetric loads at distance a from supports:

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{W \cdot a \cdot (3L^2 - 4a^2)}{24 E I_y}$$

With $W = 120 \text{ kN}$ (working/unfactored), $a = 3.6$ m, $L = 10$ m, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $I_y = 2.108 \times 10^9$ mm⁴:

$$3L^2 - 4a^2 = 3 \times 100 - 4 \times 12.96 = 300 - 51.84 = 248.16 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{120 \times 10^3 \times 3600 \times 248.16 \times 10^6}{24 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 2.108 \times 10^9}$$

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{1.072 \times 10^{17}}{1.012 \times 10^{16}} = 10.6 \text{ mm}$$

Permissible: $\dfrac{L}{750} = \dfrac{10000}{750} = 13.33 \text{ mm}$

Since 10.6 < 13.33, **vertical deflection is OK**. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Use UNFACTORED loads for deflection checks.** Many students mistakenly use 1.25 × W (impacted) for deflection, which is **wrong** — deflection is a serviceability check, so use the working load.
> 2. **Apply impact only to vertical loads**, not to surge or longitudinal forces.
> 3. **Transverse surge is applied at the top of rail**, not at the centroid of the section — a small eccentricity creates additional torsion, often ignored in simple problems.
> 4. **Always check combined bending and shear** (IS 800 Cl. 9.3.1), even if the section is over-designed — marks are reserved for *stating and verifying* the check.
> 5. **State code clauses explicitly.** A student writing "IS 800 Cl. 8.4.1" gets extra credit. Students who skip clause references lose 1–2 marks per check.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Design the lateral bracing and the bearing stiffener for the gantry girder designed in Question A.**

**Given (from Q A):** $L = 10$ m, $W_{factored} = 150$ kN/wheel, $H_t = 6.25$ kN/wheel (transverse surge per rail), section is plate girder 800 mm × 10 mm web + 250 mm × 20 mm flanges, $f_y = 250$ MPa.

#### Sub-part (a) (7 Marks): Design the lateral bracing channel required to resist the transverse surge force and verify the weak-axis bending.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total transverse surge on one rail:**

$$H_{t,total} = 2 \times 6.25 = 12.5 \text{ kN per rail}$$

**Step 2 — Lateral Bending Moment in Top Flange (assumed to be resisted by channel + top flange):**

With bracing at top flange, the weak-axis moment (assuming simply-supported laterally):

$$M_z = \dfrac{H_{t,total} \cdot L}{4} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 10}{4} = 31.25 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Weak-axis moment capacity (using top flange only):**

For the top flange (250 × 20), the section modulus about the z-axis of the **flange pair**:

$$Z_{p,z,flange} = 2 \times \dfrac{t_f \cdot b_f^2}{4} = 2 \times \dfrac{20 \times 250^2}{4} = 6.25 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3 = 625 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$M_{d,z,flange} = 6.25 \times 10^5 \times 227.27 = 1.42 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 142 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_{d,z,flange}$ (142 kN·m) $\gg M_z$ (31.25 kN·m), the **top flange alone is sufficient**. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, a **lateral channel** (e.g., ISMC 150 @ 16.4 kg/m) is provided for **rigidity** and to **reduce the unsupported length of the top flange for LTB**, even if structurally the flange could carry the moment alone.

**Step 4 — Provide ISMC 150 channel (A = 20.88 cm², $I_z$ = 484 cm⁴, $Z_{z} = 64.5$ cm³):**

Effective lateral section modulus (channel + 1/6 of web):

$$Z_{eff,z} = Z_{channel} + \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot Z_{web} = 64.5 + \dfrac{1}{6} \times \left( \dfrac{10 \times 800^2}{6} \right) = 64.5 + 178 = 242.5 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$M_{d,z,combined} = 242.5 \times 10^3 \times 227.27 = 55.1 \text{ kN·m} > 31.25 \text{ kN·m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Connection of channel to top flange:**

Use **φ 20 mm bolts at 200 mm c/c** in 6 mm fillet weld (alternatively welded). The channel must be able to transfer $H_{t,total} = 12.5$ kN as a longitudinal shear.

Bolt capacity in single shear (4.6 grade, $\phi 20$ mm):

$$V_{dsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{mb}} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \times 314.16 / 1.25 = 58{,}045 \text{ N} = 58.05 \text{ kN per bolt}$$

Since one bolt (58 kN) $\gg 12.5$ kN, a single bolt per panel is sufficient. Provide **2 bolts per panel** at 200 mm c/c for redundancy. ✓

#### Sub-part (b) (7 Marks): Design the bearing stiffener at the support.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bearing force at support:**

$$F = V_{max} = 204 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Trial bearing stiffener geometry (per IS 800 Cl. 8.7.4):**

Use **2 stiffeners**, one on each side of the web:
- Outstand width: **b = 90 mm**
- Thickness: **t = 12 mm**
- Length: equal to web depth = **800 mm**

**Step 3 — Bearing check on web:**

$$F_{w,allow} = (b + n_2) \cdot t_w \cdot \dfrac{f_{yw}}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

With $n_2 = 50$ mm (edge of bearing stiffener to web toe of weld):

$$F_{w,allow} = (90 + 50) \times 10 \times \dfrac{250}{1.10} = 3.18 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 318 \text{ kN} > 204 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Bearing stress on stiffener-to-flange contact:**

$$f_p = \dfrac{F}{2 \cdot b \cdot t} = \dfrac{204 \times 10^3}{2 \times 90 \times 12} = 94.4 \text{ MPa}$$

Allowable: $\dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = 227.27$ MPa. Since 94.4 < 227.27, **stiffener bearing is OK**. ✓

**Step 5 — Buckling check of stiffener (per IS 800 Cl. 8.7.3):**

Effective column area = $2 \times (b \times t) = 2 \times 90 \times 12 = 2160$ mm²

Buckling length: 0.7 × web depth = 0.7 × 800 = 560 mm

Radius of gyration: $r = \dfrac{12}{\sqrt{12}} = 3.46$ mm

$\lambda = \dfrac{560}{3.46} = 161.8$ (high slenderness — use IS 800 Cl. 7.1.2.1 design curve 'c')

For Fe 410 and $\lambda = 161.8$, $f_{cd} \approx 80$ MPa (from table).

Buckling capacity: $F_{bd} = 80 \times 2160 = 1.728 \times 10^5$ N = 172.8 kN < 204 kN. ✗

**Refine:** Use thicker stiffeners. Try $t = 16$ mm:

$r = \dfrac{16}{\sqrt{12}} = 4.62$ mm, $\lambda = \dfrac{560}{4.62} = 121.2$, $f_{cd} \approx 110$ MPa

$F_{bd} = 110 \times (2 \times 90 \times 16) = 110 \times 2880 = 3.17 \times 10^5$ N = 316.8 kN > 204 kN. ✓

**Adopt: 2 stiffeners of 90 × 16 mm, full depth (800 mm), welded to web with 6 mm fillet weld on both sides.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Bearing Stiffener Design:**
> Students often **omit the buckling check** of the stiffener acting as a column. The stiffener must be designed as a **short column** carrying the end reaction, with the buckling length taken as **0.7 × d** (where d = web depth) per IS 800 Cl. 8.7.3.1. Skipping this check costs **2–3 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design of Gantry Girders**

### 1. **Loads (The Holy Trinity)**
- Vertical wheel load $W$ (factored with impact 1.25)
- Transverse surge $H_t$ = 5% of (trolley + crab + lift)
- Longitudinal force $H_l$ = 5% of max wheel load

### 2. **Sectional Forces**
- $M_{max,y}$ from influence line placement of wheels
- $V_{max}$ with one wheel at the support
- $M_z$ from $H_t \times L / 4$ (with lateral bracing at top flange)

### 3. **Strength Checks (Factored Loads)**
- Moment capacity ($Z_p \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$)
- Shear capacity ($f_{yw} t_w h_w / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0})$)
- Web buckling (if $\lambda_w > 0.8$ for Fe 410)
- Web crippling at bearing
- Bearing stiffener design (column buckling check)
- Combined $B + S$ interaction

### 4. **Serviceability (Working Loads)**
- Vertical deflection ≤ L/750 (crane girders) or L/1000 (heavy cranes)
- Horizontal sway ≤ L/400
- Always use **unfactored** loads

### 5. **Stiffener Rules**
- Intermediate stiffeners when $d/t_w > 67\varepsilon$ (Fe 410)
- Bearing stiffeners at all supports
- Stiffener outstand ≤ $14 t \varepsilon$
- Buckling length of bearing stiffener = 0.7 d

### 6. **Codal Anchors to Quote**
- IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.2 (member bending)
- IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.4 (member shear)
- IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.7 (bearing stiffeners)
- IS 800:2007 Cl. 9.3 (combined B + S)
- IS 875 Part 2:1987 (crane loads)
- IS 2062:2011 (steel grade Fe 410 → $f_y$ = 250 MPa)

### 7. **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid**
- ❌ Applying impact factor to surge force
- ❌ Using factored loads for deflection
- ❌ Forgetting to design bearing stiffener as a column
- ❌ Confusing longitudinal vs. transverse forces
- ❌ Missing the clause number in the answer
- ❌ Forgetting to check LTB when lateral bracing is not provided

> [!TIP]
> **Pearl of Wisdom:** Always present a **summary table** at the end of your design showing "Demand vs. Capacity" for each check. KTU examiners award full marks when the table is clean, comprehensive, and codes are quoted.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — INTRODUCTION TO LIGHT GAUGE STEEL STRUCTURES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Light Gauge Steel Structures (LGSS)** are structural systems fabricated from **cold-formed steel sections** in which the steel is shaped at ambient (room) temperature by rolling, pressing, or bending thin flat steel sheets or strips, rather than by the hot-rolling process used for conventional heavy hot-rolled sections.

As per **IS 801:1975 (Reaffirmed 2010) — Code of Practice for Use of Cold-Formed Light Gauge Steel Structural Members in General Building Construction**, a *light gauge section* is defined as a structural member formed by bending a flat steel sheet of thickness **0.45 mm to 6.35 mm** at room temperature.

The companion international code is **AISI S100 (North American Specification for the Design of Cold-Formed Steel Structural Members)**, which IS 801 closely mirrors.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🎯

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a thin, flat sheet of aluminum foil — by itself it bends easily and cannot carry much load. But if you fold the same foil into a corrugated or channel shape, it becomes dramatically stiffer and stronger. **Light gauge steel works on exactly this principle** — taking a thin steel sheet (typically **1.5 mm to 3.15 mm**) and folding it into C, Z, or lipped channel shapes, creating a structural member that rivals the performance of much heavier hot-rolled steel, while consuming **30–50% less material**.

## 1.2 Why Cold-Formed Steel? — The Engineering Rationale

The primary motivation for using cold-formed sections is the superior **strength-to-weight ratio** and the **ease of prefabrication**. Cold working not only shapes the section but also induces **strain hardening** at the bends, which increases the yield strength of the corners by roughly **15–30%** compared to the flat virgin material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction — Hot-Rolled vs. Cold-Formed:**
> - **Hot-Rolled Steel** (IS 800): Sections formed at ~900 °C; thicknesses generally ≥ **6 mm**; thickness-to-width ratio is small.
> - **Cold-Formed Steel** (IS 801): Sections formed at room temperature; thicknesses **< 6.35 mm**; thickness-to-width ratio is large → local buckling dominates design.

## 1.3 Historical Development

- **1850s:** Cold-formed sections first used in the United States and Great Britain for grain silos and water pipes.
- **1920s–1930s:** Professor **George Winter** of Cornell University conducted pioneering research on buckling of thin plates — laid the foundation of modern cold-formed steel design.
- **1946:** First AISI Specification published.
- **1975:** IS 801 published by Bureau of Indian Standards.
- **Present:** Used in residential framing, mezzanine floors, industrial sheds, car parking structures, and portal frames.

## 1.4 Scope of Module 3

In PECET864, Module 3 systematically covers the **design philosophy, section classification, effective width concept, local buckling, and design of compression, flexural, and beam-column members** made of light gauge steel.

---

## 1.5 Common Cross-Sectional Shapes

| Shape | Description | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain Channel (C)** | Open C-section without edge stiffeners | Purlins, studs |
| **Lipped Channel (C-hat)** | C-section with short lips at flanges | Load-bearing studs, joists |
| **Z-Section** | Z-shape, may have lips | Purlins in portal frames |
| **Sigma Section (Σ)** | Hat-shaped with offset web | Purlins, high capacity |
| **Boxed/Battened Section** | Two C-sections back-to-back laced | Columns, beams |
| **Built-up Section** | Plates and angles welded | Truss chords, columns |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Material Properties of Cold-Formed Steel

The base material is **galvanized mild steel sheet** conforming to **IS 513, IS 277, or ASTM A653**. Typical yield strengths available are:

- **240 MPa** (G240) — common in India
- **280 MPa** (G280)
- **340 MPa** (G340) — high-strength
- **550 MPa** (G550) — used in manufactured housing

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Strength ($f_y$):** The minimum specified yield stress, used unmodified for compact hot-rolled sections. For cold-formed sections, **the corners are stronger** (strain-hardened), but **the flat elements may buckle locally** below yield. Hence, the design strength used in calculations is the **effective yield stress** reduced by local buckling.

## 2.2 Key Design Issues Unique to Cold-Formed Sections

### ⚙️ Local Buckling
Because the **width-to-thickness ratio ($b/t$)** of the flat elements is very large (often 50–200), the plate elements buckle locally at stresses well below the yield stress. This is the **governing failure mode** in most cold-formed members.

### ⚙️ Distortional Buckling
A mode intermediate between local and overall buckling, where the cross-section **distorts** (e.g., the lip folds inward while the flange rotates).

### ⚙️ Lateral-Torsional Buckling (LTB)
For flexural members bent about the strong axis, unrestrained compression flanges may buckle laterally with twisting.

### ⚙️ Web Crippling
Concentrated loads or reactions cause **localized crippling** of the thin web beneath stiffeners or bearings.

### ⚙️ Cold-Work Effect
The cold-forming process **strain-hardens the corners**, increasing yield strength. IS 801 allows accounting for this by replacing $f_y$ with an **average design yield stress $f_{ya}$**:

$$
f_{ya} = f_y + \frac{5N}{t}\left(f_u - f_y\right)\left[\frac{1}{(r/t)^{0.25}} - \frac{1}{(r/t)^{1.5}}\right]
$$

where:
- $N$ = number of 90° bends in the cross-section
- $t$ = base metal thickness
- $f_y$ = virgin yield stress
- $f_u$ = ultimate tensile stress
- $r$ = inside bend radius

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all KTU-exam-relevant formulas** for Module 3. Bookmark this for revision.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slenderness of element | $\lambda = \dfrac{b}{t}$ | $b$ = flat width, $t$ = thickness |
| 2 | Effective width (Winter's) | $b_e = \rho \cdot b$ | $\rho$ = reduction factor |
| 3 | Reduction factor (Winter) | $\rho = \dfrac{1 - 0.22/\lambda_p}{\lambda_p}$ | $\lambda_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cr}}}$ |
| 4 | Elastic plate buckling stress | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | $k$ = buckling coefficient |
| 5 | Buckling coeff. — stiffened | $k = 4.0$ | Both edges supported (compression) |
| 6 | Buckling coeff. — unstiffened | $k = 0.425$ | One edge free, one supported |
| 7 | Buckling coeff. — edge stiffened | $k = 0.5$ to $0.7$ | Lipped element |
| 8 | Effective width ratio limit | $\lambda_p \le 0.673$ | Element fully effective ($\rho = 1$) |
| 9 | Critical $\lambda_p$ threshold | $\lambda_p = 0.673$ | Below this, no reduction |
| 10 | Corner yield enhancement | $f_{ya} = f_y + \dfrac{5N}{t}(f_u - f_y)\left[(r/t)^{-0.25} - (r/t)^{-1.5}\right]$ | $N$ = 90° bends |
| 11 | Allow. stress (axial, IS 801) | $f_{a,allow} = \dfrac{1}{SF} \cdot \dfrac{f_{cr}}{\left(1 + \frac{\lambda^2}{2}\right) + \sqrt{\left(1 + \frac{\lambda^2}{2}\right)^2 - \lambda^2}}$ | $SF$ = Safety Factor = 1.67, $\lambda$ = slenderness parameter |
| 12 | Euler stress | $F_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{(KL/r)^2}$ | $KL/r$ = effective slenderness |
| 13 | Slenderness parameter | $\lambda_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{F_e}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Required stiffener area | $A_s = \left[\dfrac{(w/t)\cdot \text{threshold} - 1}{0.05}\right]\cdot A_f$ | Lipped element edge stiffener |
| 15 | Web crippling stress | $f_w = C \cdot \dfrac{t^2}{h} \cdot \left[1 + C_R\sqrt{\frac{t}{r_i}}\right]\cdot \left(1 + C_N\sqrt{\frac{N}{t}}\right)\cdot C_M$ | IS 801 C-coefficients |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of LGSS design** — used directly in **portal frame purlins, cold-formed steel warehouses, mid-rise residential (LGS) construction, mezzanines, and solar mounting structures** — a sector growing at **>15% CAGR** in India.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION: Winter's Effective Width Formula (IS 801, IS 811)

This is the **single most important derivation** for Module 3. It underlies the entire design philosophy of cold-formed steel.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Plate Buckling Stress

Consider a long rectangular plate of width $b$, thickness $t$, simply supported on both edges parallel to the compressive load $P$. The classical **Bryan-Timoshenko solution** for elastic buckling of such a plate is:

$$
f_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2
$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus = **2.0 × 10⁵ MPa**
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio = **0.3** for steel
- $1-\nu^2 = 0.91$
- $k$ = plate buckling coefficient (depends on boundary conditions and stress gradient)

For a **uniformly compressed simply supported plate** (typical stiffened element in LGSS), $k = 4.0$.

### 3.1.2 Definition of Plate Slenderness

Define the **non-dimensional plate slenderness** $\lambda_p$ as:

$$
\lambda_p = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{f_{cr}}}
$$

Substituting $f_{cr}$ and substituting $E = 2 \times 10^5$, $\nu = 0.3$:

$$
\lambda_p = \left(\frac{b}{t}\right) \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{k}} \sqrt{\frac{12(1-\nu^2)}{\pi^2 E}}
$$

For $k = 4$, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$:

$$
\lambda_p = 2.0 \left(\frac{b}{t}\right) \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{2 \times 10^5}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Post-Buckling Behavior and Effective Width

After a thin plate buckles elastically at $f_{cr}$, it does **not** fail. The load continues to rise because of **membrane (transverse) stress redistribution**: the edge regions near the supports carry additional stress while the mid-region unloads. The total load can reach $f_y \cdot t$ at the ultimate state.

**George Winter's** genius was to idealize this complex post-buckling behavior by replacing the actual non-uniform stress distribution with an **equivalent uniform stress** of $f_y$ acting on a reduced **effective width $b_e$**:

$$
P_{ult} = f_y \cdot t \cdot b_e \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_e = \rho \cdot b
$$

where $\rho$ is the **width reduction factor** with $\rho \le 1.0$.

### 3.1.4 Empirical Calibration of $\rho$ (Winter's Curve)

Winter conducted **hundreds of experiments** on cold-formed sections and fitted an empirical formula that matches test data. The final expression adopted in **AISI 1946** and **IS 801 (1975)** is:

$$
\boxed{\;\rho = \frac{1 - 0.22 / \lambda_p}{\lambda_p} \quad \text{for } \lambda_p > 0.673\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\rho = 1.0 \quad \text{for } \lambda_p \le 0.673\;}
$$

### 3.1.5 Verification of the 0.673 Threshold

At $\lambda_p = 0.673$, evaluate the formula:

$$
\rho = \frac{1 - 0.22/0.673}{0.673} = \frac{1 - 0.3269}{0.673} = \frac{0.6731}{0.673} \approx 1.0
$$

> This is precisely why **0.673** is the critical threshold — at this value, the element is **fully effective** ($\rho = 1$), and no reduction is needed. Below this, the element has enough thickness to develop the full yield stress without buckling.

### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Given:** A stiffened compression element of a cold-formed channel with $b = 120$ mm, $t = 1.6$ mm, $f_y = 240$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $k = 4.0$.

**Step 1: Compute $b/t$**

$$
\frac{b}{t} = \frac{120}{1.6} = 75
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\lambda_p$**

$$
\lambda_p = 2.0 \times 75 \times \sqrt{\frac{240}{2 \times 10^5}}
$$

Breaking down inside the square root:

$$
\frac{240}{2 \times 10^5} = 0.0012
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.0012} = 0.03464
$$

$$
\lambda_p = 2.0 \times 75 \times 0.03464 = 5.196
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\rho$**

Since $\lambda_p = 5.196 > 0.673$:

$$
\rho = \frac{1 - 0.22/5.196}{5.196} = \frac{1 - 0.04234}{5.196} = \frac{0.95766}{5.196} = 0.1843
$$

**Step 4: Effective width**

$$
b_e = \rho \cdot b = 0.1843 \times 120 = 22.12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Effective area contribution of this element**

$$
A_e = b_e \times t = 22.12 \times 1.6 = 35.39 \text{ mm}^2
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Of the 120 mm flat width, only **22.12 mm** (≈18.4%) is effective in carrying the yield stress. The rest buckles early and unloads — but the edges continue to carry the design load. This is the **essence of light-gauge steel design philosophy**.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION: Average Design Yield Stress (Corner Strengthening)

**Step 1:** Identify the number of 90° bends $N$ in the cross-section. For a lipped channel (C-hat), $N = 4$ (2 web–flange corners + 2 flange–lip corners).

**Step 2:** Apply the IS 801 formula:

$$
f_{ya} = f_y + \frac{5N}{t}\,(f_u - f_y)\left[\frac{1}{(r/t)^{0.25}} - \frac{1}{(r/t)^{1.5}}\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Example — with $f_y = 240$ MPa, $f_u = 360$ MPa, $N = 4$, $r = 2.5$ mm, $t = 1.6$ mm.

Compute $r/t = 2.5/1.6 = 1.5625$

Compute $(r/t)^{0.25} = 1.5625^{0.25}$:

Using $\ln(1.5625) = 0.4463$, then $0.4463 \times 0.25 = 0.1116$, so $e^{0.1116} = 1.118$

Compute $(r/t)^{1.5} = 1.5625^{1.5}$:

$\ln(1.5625) \times 1.5 = 0.4463 \times 1.5 = 0.6694$, so $e^{0.6694} = 1.953$

Bracket term:

$$
\frac{1}{1.118} - \frac{1}{1.953} = 0.8944 - 0.5120 = 0.3824
$$

Coefficient:

$$
\frac{5 \times 4}{1.6} = 12.5
$$

Increment:

$$
12.5 \times (360 - 240) \times 0.3824 = 12.5 \times 120 \times 0.3824 = 573.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

Final:

$$
f_{ya} = 240 + 573.6 = 813.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Caution:** $f_{ya}$ shall **not exceed** $f_u = 360$ MPa per IS 801, and is further capped. Always use the code limit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Light Gauge Steel Member Design Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete interaction sequence** from raw material to a fully validated cold-formed steel member. This is the standard workflow used in design offices and is fully aligned with the IS 801 process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Raw Material Selection] --> B[Galvanized Steel Sheet]
    B --> C[Verify fy, fu, t, r from Mill Certificate]
    C --> D[Choose Section Shape: C / Z / Hat / Lipped]
    D --> E[Compute Section Properties: A, I, r, J]
    E --> F[Classify Elements: Stiffened / Unstiffened / Edge-Stiffened]
    F --> G{Compute b/t Ratio for Each Element}
    G --> H{Compare with IS 801 Limiting b/t}
    H -->|Below Limit| I[Element Fully Effective: rho = 1.0]
    H -->|Above Limit| J[Compute Plate Slenderness lambda_p]
    J --> K[Apply Winter's Formula: rho = 1 - 0.22/lambda_p over lambda_p]
    I --> L[Sum Effective Areas: A_eff = Sum b_e x t]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Apply Corner Strengthening: f_ya]
    M --> N[Check Member Type]
    N --> O[Compression Member]
    N --> P[Flexural Member]
    N --> Q[Beam-Column]
    O --> R[Check Axial Capacity: P_d = A_eff x f_ya / SF]
    P --> S[Check Moment Capacity: M_d = S_eff x f_ya / SF]
    Q --> T[Check Interaction: P/P_d + M/M_d le 1.0]
    R --> U{Finalize Section}
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[Document Drawing: Section, Spacing, Connections]
    V --> W[END: Approved Light Gauge Steel Design]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Buckling Modes in a Lipped Channel (Σ-Section)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Original Lipped Channel Section] --> B[Mode 1: Local Buckling]
    A --> C[Mode 2: Distortional Buckling]
    A --> D[Mode 3: Lateral-Torsional Buckling]
    A --> E[Mode 4: Flexural Global Buckling]
    B --> B1[Flange and Web buckle in short half-wavelengths]
    C --> C1[Lip folds inward, flange rotates as a rigid body]
    D --> D1[Section twists and translates laterally]
    E --> E1[Whole member bows without distortion]
    B1 --> F[All modes checked in design]
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Effective Width Distribution in a Stiffened Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Actual[Actual Stress Distribution After Buckling]
        A1[Edge: High Stress fy] --> A2[Middle: Low Post-Buckling Stress]
        A2 --> A3[Other Edge: High Stress fy]
    end
    subgraph Idealized[Winter's Effective Width Idealization]
        B1[Effective Width be: Uniform fy] --> B2[be] --> B3[Effective Width be: Uniform fy]
        B2 -. Removed Section .-> B4[Ignored Region: b minus 2be]
    end
    Actual -->|Equates to| Idealized
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Material Yield Stress Distribution in a Cold-Formed Section

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cold-Formed Lipped Channel Cross-Section] --> B[Flat Region 1: Web]
    A --> C[Corner 1: Web-Flange Bend]
    A --> D[Flat Region 2: Flange]
    A --> E[Corner 2: Flange-Lip Bend]
    A --> F[Flat Region 3: Lip]
    B --> B1[fy = Virgin Yield, e.g. 240 MPa]
    C --> C1[fy_enhanced = 1.15 to 1.30 x fy, capped at fu]
    D --> D1[fy = Virgin Yield]
    E --> E1[fy_enhanced = 1.15 to 1.30 x fy, capped at fu]
    F --> F1[fy = Virgin Yield, may be partially effective]
    B1 --> G[Use Effective Width Method: b_e = rho x b]
    C1 --> G
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G
    G --> H[Final Effective Section for Design]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define cold-formed steel. How is it distinguished from hot-rolled steel in terms of thickness range and method of forming?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cold-formed steel sections are structural members formed by bending thin steel sheets or strips at **room temperature** (i.e., without the application of heat) into desired cross-sectional shapes, as defined in **IS 801:1975**.

**Distinction (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Cold-Formed Steel (IS 801) | Hot-Rolled Steel (IS 800) |
|---|---|---|
| Thickness range | **0.45 mm to 6.35 mm** | Generally **≥ 6 mm** |
| Forming process | Bending/rolling at **ambient temperature** | Rolling at **~900 °C** |
| Common shapes | C, Z, hat, lipped channel | I, H, T, angle, channel |
| Dominant failure | **Local buckling** | Overall buckling |

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State the effective width concept (Winter's concept) used in the design of light gauge steel sections.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **effective width concept** was proposed by **George Winter (1946)**. After a thin plate element buckles at a stress $f_{cr} < f_y$, the load does not drop; instead, the membrane stress **redistributes** such that regions near the supports carry a higher stress approaching $f_y$, while the central region unloads.

> Winter idealized this non-uniform post-buckling stress distribution as a **uniform stress of magnitude $f_y$** acting on a reduced **effective width $b_e$** at each edge, with the intermediate portion considered ineffective.

$$
b_e = \rho \cdot b \quad ; \quad \rho = \frac{1 - 0.22/\lambda_p}{\lambda_p} \quad \text{for } \lambda_p > 0.673
$$

> This concept is the **foundation of all IS 801 and AISI S100 design procedures** for compression and flexural members made of light gauge steel. **(1 mark for stating concept, 1 mark for Winter's name + formula, 1 mark for application/importance)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — ANSWER EITHER 'A' OR 'B'**

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the various **types of cross-sections** used in cold-formed light gauge steel construction. List at least **five** typical cross-section shapes with their **common applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of using light gauge steel sections in construction. Compare their **strength-to-weight ratio** with conventional hot-rolled sections. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Cross-Section Types (7 marks):**

| Sl. | Section Shape | Sketch Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plain Channel (C)** | Open C without lips | Light purlins, non-structural framing |
| 2 | **Lipped Channel (C-hat)** | C-section with returned lips on both flanges | **Load-bearing studs**, joists, columns |
| 3 | **Z-Section (Plain and Lipped)** | Z-shape, often with lips | Purlins in portal frames — allows **lap nesting** |
| 4 | **Sigma Section (Σ)** | Hat-shaped, web offset from flange centerline | Purlins with high moment capacity |
| 5 | **Built-up I-Section** | Two channels welded toe-to-toe | Floor joists, beams |
| 6 | **Back-to-Back C-Section** | Two C-sections connected by batten plates | Compression members, columns |
| 7 | **Truss Chord (Built-up)** | Two angles or C-sections spaced apart | Light roof trusses |

Each section correctly identified with sketch and application: **1 mark each × 5 = 5 marks**. Neat comparison and any additional sections: **2 marks**.

**(b) Advantages and Limitations (7 marks):**

**Advantages (4 marks):**

1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — sections are 30–50% lighter than hot-rolled for the same load.
2. **Ease of prefabrication** — sections can be cut, punched, and assembled off-site; **reduces on-site labor by ~40%**.
3. **Consistent quality** — factory-rolled with tight tolerances, **±0.05 mm**.
4. **Transportation economy** — light members reduce logistics cost.
5. **No fire damage to material** before assembly (unlike timber).
6. **Termite-proof and non-combustible** (with proper fire protection).
7. **Recyclable** — **100% recyclable**, supports green building certification (GRIHA, LEED).

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Thin sections buckle locally** at low stresses — design is governed by buckling, not yielding.
2. **Reduced stiffness** — slender sections have low lateral stiffness; can lead to **lateral-torsional buckling**.
3. **Susceptible to corrosion** if the **galvanized coating** is damaged — requires careful detailing.
4. **Web crippling** under concentrated loads — requires stiffeners.
5. **Connections are complex** — self-drilling screws, welds, bolts; not as straightforward as riveting in hot-rolled.

**Strength-to-Weight Comparison:** For the same axial load capacity, a cold-formed C-section of **1.6 mm** thickness weighs approximately **40% less** than an ISMC 100 hot-rolled channel, with comparable strength when local buckling is adequately addressed.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **effective width concept** for the design of light gauge steel compression elements. Derive the Winter's reduction factor formula and state the significance of the threshold $\lambda_p = 0.673$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stiffened compression element of a lipped channel has: $b = 150$ mm, $t = 2.0$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $k = 4.0$. Compute: (i) the **plate slenderness $\lambda_p$**, (ii) the **reduction factor $\rho$**, and (iii) the **effective width $b_e$**. Comment on whether the element is **fully effective or partially effective**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Effective Width Concept (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1.5 marks):** In light gauge steel sections, the flat elements have very high $b/t$ ratios. These plate-like elements buckle locally at a stress $f_{cr}$ that is **much lower than the yield stress $f_y$**. The elastic local buckling stress of a uniformly compressed plate is given by:

$$
f_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2
$$

For a **stiffened element** (long plate with both longitudinal edges supported against out-of-plane displacement), $k = 4.0$.

**Post-Buckling Strength (1.5 marks):** After local buckling, the plate does **not** fail. Membrane stresses redistribute — the regions near the **edges** (supports) carry additional load, while the central region sheds load. The plate reaches its ultimate strength when the edge stress reaches $f_y$. The total load is:

$$
P_{ult} = f_y \cdot t \cdot b
$$

**Effective Width Idealization (2 marks):** Winter proposed that the actual non-uniform post-buckling stress distribution be replaced by an **equivalent uniform stress $f_y$** acting on a reduced **effective width $b_e$** at the two edges:

$$
P_{ult} = f_y \cdot b_e \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_e = \rho \cdot b
$$

**Winter's Formula (1.5 marks):** The reduction factor based on extensive experimental data:

$$
\rho = \frac{1 - 0.22 / \lambda_p}{\lambda_p} \quad \text{for } \lambda_p > 0.673
$$

where $\lambda_p = \sqrt{f_y / f_{cr}}$ is the plate slenderness parameter.

**Significance of $\lambda_p = 0.673$ (0.5 mark):** At $\lambda_p = 0.673$, $\rho = 1.0$, meaning the element is **fully effective** — no reduction is required. For $\lambda_p \le 0.673$, the element is **compact enough** to develop full yield without local buckling.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $b = 150$ mm, $t = 2.0$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $k = 4.0$.

**(i) Plate Slenderness $\lambda_p$ (2.5 marks):**

$$
\frac{b}{t} = \frac{150}{2.0} = 75
$$

$$
\lambda_p = \left(\frac{b}{t}\right)\sqrt{\frac{f_y}{k}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{12(1-\nu^2)}{\pi^2 E}}
$$

Computing the constant term:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{12 \times 0.91}{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}} = \sqrt{\frac{10.92}{1.9739 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{5.534 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.353 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Using the simplified form for $k = 4$:

$$
\lambda_p = 2.0 \times 75 \times \sqrt{\frac{250}{2 \times 10^5}} = 150 \times \sqrt{0.00125} = 150 \times 0.03536
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda_p = 5.303}
$$

**(ii) Reduction Factor $\rho$ (2 marks):**

Since $\lambda_p = 5.303 > 0.673$:

$$
\rho = \frac{1 - 0.22/5.303}{5.303} = \frac{1 - 0.04149}{5.303} = \frac{0.9585}{5.303}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho = 0.1807}
$$

**(iii) Effective Width $b_e$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
b_e = \rho \cdot b = 0.1807 \times 150 = 27.11 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Since $\lambda_p = 5.303 \gg 0.673$, the element is **severely under-utilized** — only about **18%** of the actual width is effective. The element is **highly slender** and a thicker plate or shorter flat width (use of lips, intermediate stiffeners) is recommended to improve efficiency. Alternatively, the design must be based on the reduced effective area, and the load must be carefully checked against local buckling failure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\lambda$ and $\lambda_p$:** $\lambda = b/t$ is the **width-to-thickness ratio**, while $\lambda_p$ is the **dimensionless plate slenderness**. They are related but **not equal**. Many students lose 1–2 marks by writing $\lambda$ instead of $\lambda_p$ in Winter's formula.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.673 check:** Always check if $\lambda_p \le 0.673$ **first**. If yes, then $\rho = 1.0$ and you skip the entire Winter's formula. KTU examiners specifically test this threshold logic.
>
> 3. **Buckling coefficient confusion:** $k = 4.0$ is for **stiffened** elements (both edges supported). $k = 0.425$ is for **unstiffened** elements (one edge free). **Using the wrong $k$ is the #1 error** that leads to a completely wrong answer.
>
> 4. **Effective area vs. gross area:** In design calculations, always use **$A_e = b_e \cdot t$**, NOT the gross $b \cdot t$. This is the most common place students lose 2–3 marks in compression member design.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units:** $E$ must be in **MPa (N/mm²)** consistently. Mixing $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa with $b$ in mm is correct. Do not convert $E$ to GPa without converting all other quantities.
>
> 6. **Caps on $f_{ya}$:** When using the corner strengthening formula, $f_{ya}$ **shall not exceed $f_u$** (ultimate stress) and shall be capped per IS 801 Cl. 5.3.2. Most students forget this cap.
>
> 7. **Neglecting local buckling in flexural members:** Students often compute $M_p = f_y \cdot Z$ and forget the **effective section modulus $Z_{eff}$** computed using $b_e$. Always check that the **compression flange** (not the tension flange) governs.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 Exam — Light Gauge Steel Structures (Module 3, Introduction):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Cold-formed steel = thin steel sheets (**0.45–6.35 mm**) shaped at **room temperature**.
- ✅ **Governing code:** **IS 801:1975** (India), **AISI S100** (US/International).
- ✅ **Most common sections:** **Lipped channel (C-hat)**, **Z-section**, **Sigma (Σ)**, **back-to-back C**, **built-up I**.
- ✅ **Dominant failure modes:** **Local buckling** (governing), distortional buckling, lateral-torsional buckling, web crippling.
- ✅ **Winter's Effective Width Formula:** $b_e = \rho \cdot b$, $\rho = (1 - 0.22/\lambda_p)/\lambda_p$ for $\lambda_p > 0.673$.
- ✅ **Critical threshold:** $\lambda_p = 0.673$ — below this, **element is fully effective** ($\rho = 1$).
- ✅ **Plate slenderness:** $\lambda_p = (b/t) \sqrt{f_y / (k E)} \sqrt{12(1-\nu^2)/\pi^2}$, with $k = 4$ (stiffened), $k = 0.425$ (unstiffened).
- ✅ **Corner strengthening:** $f_{ya} > f_y$ due to strain hardening; $f_{ya}$ capped at $f_u$ per IS 801.
- ✅ **Advantages:** High strength-to-weight, easy prefabrication, recyclable, lightweight, fast construction.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Local buckling susceptibility, low stiffness, corrosion risk, complex connections.
- ✅ **Safety factor (IS 801):** **1.67** on yield stress (or ultimate) for ASD approach.
- ✅ **Applications:** Purlins, girts, wall studs, floor joists, storage racks, car parking, modular housing.
- ✅ **Comparison with hot-rolled (IS 800):** Cold-formed has **higher b/t**, **lower thickness**, **local buckling governs**, **uses thin galvanized sheets**; hot-rolled has **lower b/t**, **higher thickness**, **overall buckling governs**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loading Consideration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Loading Consideration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Loading Consideration** in advanced steel structural design refers to the systematic identification, classification, quantification, and combinatorial treatment of all external actions (forces and deformations) acting on a steel structure throughout its design life, in accordance with the **Limit State Method of Design** prescribed by **IS 800:2007** and the relevant parts of the **IS 875** code series (Parts 1–5) and **IS 1893 (Part 1):2016** for seismic actions.

In the context of steel structures, loading consideration is **not merely about the magnitude of forces**; it is a holistic design philosophy that addresses:
- The **probability of simultaneous occurrence** of multiple load types
- The **partial safety factors ($\gamma_f$)** applied to characteristic loads
- The **load path** and structural load transfer mechanism
- **Stability** against overturning, sliding, and uplift
- **Dynamic effects** such as impact, vibration, fatigue, and seismic response

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET864 – Module 3):**
> *"Loading Consideration – Dead, Live, Wind, Seismic, Crane, Impact, and Fatigue loads. Load combinations as per IS 800:2007. Partial safety factors. Tributary area and load path. Stability and robustness under combined loading."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey steel-framed building** as a human spine carrying a heavy backpack. The "spine" is the **steel frame**, the "backpack" is the **dead load** (its own weight and permanent fixtures), and the **person** sitting on the shoulders is the **live load** (occupants, furniture, vehicles). Now add a **strong wind pushing sideways** (wind load) and the **ground shaking during an earthquake** (seismic load).

If you only designed the spine for the backpack and the person, the wind would topple the person sideways, and an earthquake would snap the spine. **Loading Consideration** is the engineering equivalent of asking: *"What could possibly push, pull, sit on, shake, or twist this structure — and what's the worst realistic combination of all these things happening at once?"*

A simple intuitive summary:

| Real-World Action | Engineering Equivalent | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Own weight of the frame | **Dead Load (DL)** | IS 875 Part 1 |
| People, furniture, vehicles | **Imposed / Live Load (IL)** | IS 875 Part 2 |
| Wind pressure on cladding | **Wind Load (WL)** | IS 875 Part 3 |
| Snow on roof | **Snow Load (SL)** | IS 875 Part 4 |
| Earthquake shaking | **Seismic / Earthquake Load (EL)** | IS 1893 |
| Crane in a factory | **Crane / Moving Load** | IS 875 Part 2 (Cl. 7) |
| Vibrating machinery | **Dynamic / Vibration Load** | IS 875 Part 2 |
| Repeated cyclic loading | **Fatigue Load** | IS 800:2007 Cl. 13 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> In the **Limit State Method**, loads are **not** simply added algebraically. Each load is multiplied by a **partial safety factor ($\gamma_f$)** that accounts for (a) the possibility of the load exceeding its characteristic value, (b) the uncertainty in its spatial distribution, and (c) the nature of the limit state being checked (ultimate or serviceability).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standardized values** are repeatedly used in KTU examination problems for steel design:

- **Partial safety factor for Dead Load (DL), $\gamma_{f,DL}$:**
  - **1.5** when the effect is *unfavorable* (load increases stresses)
  - **0.9** when the effect is *favorable* (load counteracts overturning/uplift)
- **Partial safety factor for Live Load (LL), $\gamma_{f,LL}$:**
  - **1.5** when alone with DL
  - **1.2** when combined with WL or EL
- **Partial safety factor for Wind/Seismic Load, $\gamma_{f,WL/EL}$:**
  - **1.2** when combined with DL and LL
  - **1.5** when alone (in reversal scenarios)
- **Partial safety factor for material (steel), $\gamma_m$:** **1.10** (for yield), **1.25** (for ultimate)
- **Unit weight of structural steel:** **78.5 kN/m³** (often taken as 7850 kg/m³)
- **Acceleration due to gravity, $g$:** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Basic wind speed $V_b$ for Kerala (Zone-wise):** **39 m/s** (Kochi coastal), **33 m/s** (inland) — refer to IS 875 Part 3, Fig. 1.
- **Seismic Zone Factor (Z) for Kerala:** **0.16 (Zone III)** for most districts; **0.24 (Zone IV)** for some northern/western hilly regions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Loads on Steel Structures

Loads on steel structures are classified along **three orthogonal axes** of design thinking:

### Axis 1 — By Nature of Action

#### (a) **Dead Loads (DL) — Permanent Static Loads**
These are the **self-weight of all permanent constructional elements**: steel members (beams, columns, bracings, purlins), roofing sheets, floor slabs, walls, fixed service equipment, and permanent partitions.

- Governed by **IS 875 (Part 1): 1987 (Reaffirmed 2018)**
- Unit weight of **structural steel** = **78.5 kN/m³**
- Computed from **actual cross-sectional dimensions** (NOT nominal sizes) for accuracy
- For standard rolled sections, tables give mass in **kg/m** which is converted to kN/m by multiplying with **g/1000**

#### (b) **Imposed / Live Loads (IL or LL) — Variable Static Loads**
These are loads arising from the **intended use or occupancy** of the structure, varying in magnitude and position over the design life.

- Governed by **IS 875 (Part 2): 1987**
- Specified as **uniformly distributed loads (UDL)** in kN/m² or **concentrated loads** in kN
- For **steel roof trusses**: minimum IL = **0.75 kN/m²** (with access) or **0.40 kN/m²** (no access)
- For **steel floors in industrial buildings**: 5–10 kN/m² depending on usage
- **Concentrated load check:** A concentrated load of **1.5 kN minimum** (or as specified for the occupancy) must always be checked if it produces a more severe effect than the UDL

#### (c) **Wind Loads (WL) — Dynamic Environmental Loads**
Wind exerts **pressure** on windward faces and **suction** on leeward faces, side walls, and roof.

- Governed by **IS 875 (Part 3): 2015**
- Design wind pressure: $p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$ (where $V_z$ is in m/s, $p_z$ in N/m²)
- $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$
  - $V_b$ = basic wind speed
  - $k_1$ = risk coefficient (probability factor)
  - $k_2$ = terrain, height & structure size factor
  - $k_3$ = topography factor
  - $k_4$ = importance factor for cyclonic regions
- **Cₚ (External pressure coefficient)** and **Cₘi (Internal pressure coefficient)** determine pressure on walls/roof

#### (d) **Seismic Loads (EL) — Dynamic Earthquake Loads**
The horizontal inertia forces generated by the **mass of the structure** when subjected to ground acceleration.

- Governed by **IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016**
- For **steel moment-resisting frames**: Response Reduction Factor $R = 5.0$
- For **steel braced frames**: $R = 4.0$
- Design horizontal seismic coefficient: $A_h = \dfrac{Z}{2} \cdot \dfrac{S_a}{g} \cdot \dfrac{I}{R}$
- **Base shear** $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ where $W$ is seismic weight

#### (e) **Special Loads**
- **Crane Loads:** Vertical wheel loads, horizontal surge, and lateral thrust. Governed by **IS 875 Part 2 (Cl. 7)** and **IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.7**.
- **Impact Loads:** Sudden dynamic loads (e.g., machinery, moving vehicles). Increase the static load by an **impact factor** (typically 1.10 to 2.00).
- **Fatigue Loads:** Repeated cyclic loading on **bridges, crane girders, and offshore structures**. Governed by **IS 800:2007 Cl. 13**.
- **Temperature Loads:** For very long structures (>45 m without expansion joints), differential temperature causes expansion/contraction.
- **Erection Loads:** Construction-stage loads; the structure may carry workers, equipment, and partially completed systems.

---

### Axis 2 — By Variation in Time

| Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Permanent (P)** | Constant in magnitude and position over design life | DL |
| **Variable (V)** | Varies with time | LL, WL, SN |
| **Accidental (A)** | Extreme, short-duration, low probability | EL, explosion, fire, vehicle impact |

### Axis 3 — By Variation in Space

- **Fixed loads** (position unchanged): DL of columns, beams
- **Free / Movable loads** (position can vary): LL on floors, vehicle on bridge, crane wheel loads

---

## 2.2 Load Combinations as per IS 800:2007 (Table 4)

The **Limit State of Strength** requires that the structure be checked against the most critical of the following **factored load combinations**:

| Combination No. | Load Combination | Application Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1.5(DL + IL)$ | Gravity only (no wind/quake) |
| 2 | $1.2(DL + IL \pm WL)$ | Gravity + Wind |
| 3 | $1.2(DL + IL \pm EL)$ | Gravity + Earthquake |
| 4 | $1.5(DL \pm WL)$ | Wind reversal (no LL) |
| 5 | $1.5(DL \pm EL)$ | Earthquake reversal (no LL) |
| 6 | $0.9DL \pm 1.5WL$ | Uplift / Overturning (Wind) |
| 7 | $0.9DL \pm 1.5EL$ | Uplift / Overturning (Seismic) |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **Limit State of Serviceability** (deflection, vibration, cracking), the partial safety factors are taken as **1.0**, i.e., **un-factored characteristic loads** are used in combinations: $DL + IL$, $DL + WL$, $DL + IL + WL$, etc.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Self-weight of steel | $w = A \cdot \rho_{st} \cdot g$ | kN/m | IS 875 Pt 1 |
| 2 | Dead load | $DL = \sum (\text{Volume} \times \text{Unit weight})$ | kN | IS 875 Pt 1 |
| 3 | Live load reduction | Permitted for columns carrying $\geq 200$ m² of floor area | – | IS 875 Pt 2 Cl. 3.3 |
| 4 | Design wind speed | $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$ | m/s | IS 875 Pt 3 |
| 5 | Design wind pressure | $p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$ | N/m² | IS 875 Pt 3 |
| 6 | Wind force on element | $F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$ | N | IS 875 Pt 3 |
| 7 | Design horizontal seismic coefficient | $A_h = \dfrac{Z}{2} \cdot \dfrac{S_a}{g} \cdot \dfrac{I}{R}$ | – | IS 1893 |
| 8 | Design base shear | $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ | kN | IS 1893 |
| 9 | Fundamental natural period (steel MRF) | $T_a = 0.085 \cdot h^{0.75}$ | s | IS 1893 |
| 10 | Factored load (general) | $Q = \gamma_f \cdot Q_k$ | varies | IS 800 Cl. 5.3.3 |
| 11 | Crane impact factor (vertical) | $1.10$ to $1.25$ | – | IS 875 Pt 2 Cl. 7.4 |
| 12 | Crane surge factor (horizontal) | $0.10$ of max wheel load | – | IS 800 Cl. 10.7 |
| 13 | Fatigue stress range (steel) | $\Delta\sigma \le \Delta\sigma_{allowable}$ | MPa | IS 800 Cl. 13 |
| 14 | Tributary area for a beam | $A_{tr} = L_1 \cdot L_2 \div 2$ (interior) | m² | – |
| 15 | Equivalent UDL from wheel load | $w_{eq} = \dfrac{P}{L_{eff}}$ | kN/m | – |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, the symbol `\cdot` is used for multiplication (instead of `×`), and `\dfrac`/`\div` is used for fractions and division — this is to maintain markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Loading consideration is the **backbone of safe steel design**. Its applications span:

1. **High-rise Steel Buildings** — Combined wind + seismic design often governs over gravity design
2. **Industrial Steel Structures** — Crane + impact + fatigue governs crane girders
3. **Steel Bridges** — Moving vehicle loads, dynamic amplification, fatigue
4. **Transmission & Communication Towers** — Wind + ice + broken conductor loads
5. **Offshore Steel Platforms** — Wave + wind + seismic + buoyancy
6. **Storage Silos & Bins** — Asymmetric loading during discharge
7. **Steel Space Frames** — Roof loads + temperature + construction loads

In **failure investigations** (e.g., the 2018 collapse incidents in India), the most common root cause is **inadequate loading consideration** — either missing a load type, underestimating the magnitude, or failing to account for a realistic load combination.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Design Wind Pressure from First Principles

Wind is a moving fluid. By **Bernoulli's principle**, the dynamic pressure exerted by a fluid of density $\rho$ moving with velocity $V$ is:

$$p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2$$

For air, the standard density used in IS 875 is **$\rho = 1.226$ kg/m³** (at mean sea level, 15 °C).

Substituting:

$$p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.226 \cdot V^2 = 0.613 \cdot V^2 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

The IS 875 code uses a simplified constant of **0.6** (incorporating a small rounding and correction for typical conditions):

$$p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2 \;\; \text{N/m}^2$$

where $V_z$ is the **design wind speed at height $z$**, derived as:

$$V_z = k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4 \cdot V_b$$

### Step-by-Step Evaluation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & V_b = \text{basic wind speed (from IS 875 Pt 3, Fig. 1, zone map)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & k_1 = \text{risk coefficient (depends on building class and design life)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & k_2 = \text{terrain, height, structure size factor} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & k_3 = \text{topography factor (hills, valleys, cliffs)} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & k_4 = \text{importance factor for cyclonic regions} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4 \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2 \;\; [\text{N/m}^2] \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & F = (C_{pe} - C_{pi}) \cdot A \cdot p_z \;\; [\text{N}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $C_{pe}$ = External pressure coefficient (depends on roof slope, wall zone)
- $C_{pi}$ = Internal pressure coefficient (depends on opening location)
- $A$ = Effective surface area of the element

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Factored Load for a Roof Truss (Step-by-Step Worked Example)

A **steel roof truss** of an industrial building has the following data:
- Span $L = 18$ m
- Spacing of trusses = 6 m
- Roofing: AC sheets, unit weight = 0.16 kN/m²
- Purlins: ISMC 100 @ 0.143 kN/m, spaced 1.5 m apart
- Truss self-weight (assumed) = 0.20 kN/m² of plan area
- Live load on roof (no access) = 0.40 kN/m²
- Wind pressure (suction) on roof = 1.20 kN/m²
- Location: Inland Kerala, building height 8 m, terrain category 2

**Find the factored load per panel point for the most critical load combination.**

### Step 1 — Calculate Dead Load per m² of Plan Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AC sheets:} \quad & 0.16 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
\text{Purlins (load/m}^2): \quad & \frac{0.143}{1.5} = 0.0953 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
\text{Truss self-weight:} \quad & 0.20 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
\text{Bracing, sag rods, etc.:} \quad & 0.10 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \;\;(\text{assumed}) \\
\hline
\text{Total DL:} \quad & = 0.16 + 0.0953 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 0.5553 \;\text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert DL to kN/m of Truss (per m of truss span, NOT plan)

The dead load is transferred to the truss via purlins. The load per m of truss length is:
$$DL_{\text{per m of truss}} = DL_{\text{per m}^2} \times \text{spacing of trusses} = 0.5553 \times 6 = 3.332 \;\text{kN/m}$$

### Step 3 — Live Load per m of Truss

$$IL_{\text{per m of truss}} = 0.40 \times 6 = 2.40 \;\text{kN/m}$$

### Step 4 — Wind Load per m of Truss (acting upward as suction)

$$WL_{\text{per m of truss}} = 1.20 \times 6 = 7.20 \;\text{kN/m (upward)}$$

### Step 5 — Panel Point Load Calculation

Assume 8 panels, so panel length = 18/8 = 2.25 m. The panel point load is the load per m × panel length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DL at panel point} \quad & = 3.332 \times 2.25 = 7.50 \;\text{kN} \\
\text{IL at panel point} \quad & = 2.40 \times 2.25 = 5.40 \;\text{kN} \\
\text{WL at panel point} \quad & = 7.20 \times 2.25 = 16.20 \;\text{kN (upward)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Factored Load Combinations

**Combination 1 — Gravity only (DL + IL):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{factored}} & = 1.5 \times (7.50 + 5.40) \\
& = 1.5 \times 12.90 = 19.35 \;\text{kN (downward)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combination 2 — Gravity + Wind (downward):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{factored}} & = 1.2 \times (7.50 + 5.40) + 0 \quad (\text{wind can be} \pm) \\
& = 1.2 \times 12.90 = 15.48 \;\text{kN (downward)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combination 6 — Uplift (Wind reversal, no LL):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{factored, uplift}} & = 0.9 \times 7.50 - 1.5 \times 16.20 \\
& = 6.75 - 24.30 = -17.55 \;\text{kN (upward)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Identify Critical Combination

> The **upward combination (-17.55 kN)** governs because wind suction on a **lightweight AC-sheet roof** often exceeds the gravity load. The truss must be designed for **both**:
> - A downward force of **19.35 kN** at each interior panel point
> - An upward force of **17.55 kN** at each interior panel point

This dual-direction design is the **signature of steel roof truss loading consideration**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Seismic Base Shear for a Steel Frame

**Given data:**
- 5-storey steel MRF building, $h = 5 \times 3.5 = 17.5$ m
- Seismic weight $W = 12{,}000$ kN
- Zone III, $Z = 0.16$
- Soil type II (medium)
- Steel MRF → $R = 5.0$, $I = 1.0$

### Step 1 — Fundamental Time Period

For a **steel moment-resisting frame**:

$$T_a = 0.085 \cdot h^{0.75} = 0.085 \cdot 17.5^{0.75}$$

Calculating:
$$17.5^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \cdot \ln 17.5} = e^{0.75 \cdot 2.862} = e^{2.1465} = 8.555$$

$$T_a = 0.085 \times 8.555 = 0.727 \;\text{s}$$

### Step 2 — Spectral Acceleration $S_a/g$

For medium soil, from IS 1893 (Fig. 2):
- $T = 0.727$ s lies in the **velocity-controlled region** ($0.55 < T < 4.0$)
- $\dfrac{S_a}{g} = \dfrac{1.36}{T} = \dfrac{1.36}{0.727} = 1.871$

### Step 3 — Design Horizontal Seismic Coefficient

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_h & = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \\
& = \frac{0.16}{2} \cdot 1.871 \cdot \frac{1.0}{5.0} \\
& = 0.08 \cdot 1.871 \cdot 0.20 \\
& = 0.0299
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Design Base Shear

$$V_B = A_h \cdot W = 0.0299 \times 12000 = 358.8 \;\text{kN}$$

### Step 5 — Vertical Distribution of $V_B$ along Building Height

Using IS 1893 formula:

$$Q_i = V_B \cdot \frac{W_i \cdot h_i^2}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \cdot h_j^2}$$

This distributes 358.8 kN to each floor as per mass and height² distribution.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Load Selection Logic for Common Steel Structures

| Structure Type | Primary Gravity Loads | Critical Lateral / Special Load | Governing Combination |
|---|---|---|---|
| Multi-storey steel building | DL, LL | WL, EL | $1.2(DL+IL+WL)$ or $1.2(DL+IL+EL)$ |
| Steel roof truss (industrial) | DL, LL (roof) | WL (uplift) | $0.9DL \pm 1.5WL$ |
| Steel bridge (road) | DL, SIDL | Moving vehicle, impact, fatigue | $1.5(DL+SIDL+LL)$ |
| Industrial building w/ crane | DL, LL, crane | Crane surge + WL | $1.2(DL+LL+CR+WL)$ |
| Transmission tower | DL | WL, broken conductor | $0.9DL \pm 1.5WL$ |
| Silo / bin | DL, stored material | Asymmetric discharge | $1.5(DL+IL+ \text{asymmetric})$ |
