# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Characteristic Load" as per IS 800:2007. Explain how the partial safety factor ($\gamma_f$) modifies this to obtain the "Design Load".**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Characteristic Load** is the value of a load that has an **accepted probability of not being exceeded** during the design life of the structure. It is determined statistically — for loads with sufficient data, the characteristic value corresponds to a **95% probability of not being exceeded** (i.e., the mean plus 1.65 standard deviations). For loads where statistical data is unavailable (e.g., wind, earthquake), the characteristic value is the **specified value in the relevant IS code**.
>
> The **Design Load** (or **Factored Load**) is obtained by multiplying the characteristic load by the **partial safety factor for loads ($\gamma_f$)**:
> $$Q_d = \gamma_f \cdot Q_k$$
> where $Q_k$ = characteristic load, $\gamma_f$ = partial safety factor (1.2 to 1.5 depending on load type and combination).
>
> The factor $\gamma_f$ accounts for:
> 1. **Possibility of unfavorable deviation** of the load
> 2. **Uncertainties in the assessment** of the load effect
> 3. **Nature of the limit state** being checked (ultimate or serviceability)
>
> [3 Marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for explanation of $\gamma_f$ significance]

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the seven standard load combinations of IS 800:2007 (Table 4) used for the Limit State of Strength. Why is the combination $0.9DL + 1.5WL$ important for steel roof trusses?**

**Model Answer:**

> The seven standard load combinations per **IS 800:2007 Table 4** for the Limit State of Strength are:
>
> 1. $1.5(DL + IL)$
> 2. $1.2(DL + IL \pm WL)$
> 3. $1.2(DL + IL \pm EL)$
> 4. $1.5(DL \pm WL)$
> 5. $1.5(DL \pm EL)$
> 6. $0.9DL \pm 1.5WL$
> 7. $0.9DL \pm 1.5EL$
>
> **Importance of combination 6 for roof trusses:**
> Lightweight steel roof trusses (e.g., with AC sheet roofing) have **low self-weight**. Strong wind creates **upward suction** on the roof (negative pressure). In this scenario, the dead load that normally resists uplift is small, while the wind suction is large. Combination 6 with **$\gamma_f = 0.9$ on DL** (reduced DL — favorable effect) and **$\gamma_f = 1.5$ on WL** (increased WL — unfavorable) gives the **maximum possible uplift** that the truss connections, anchorage, and members must resist. Failure to check this combination can lead to **roof blow-off failures** during cyclones.
>
> [3 Marks: 1 mark for combinations list, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for practical significance]

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Convention:** Two questions from the same module with internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question PECET864]`

**(a)** For an industrial steel-framed building located in **Kerala (Zone III, terrain category 2, building height 10 m, basic wind speed 39 m/s, cyclonic region factor applicable)**, compute the **factored load on a typical interior column** for the following data:

- **Roof area tributary to column:** 6 m × 6 m = 36 m²
- **Dead load on roof (excluding column self-weight):** 0.55 kN/m²
- **Live load on roof (no access):** 0.40 kN/m²
- **Wind suction on roof:** 1.80 kN/m² (acting upward)
- **Column self-weight:** 4.0 kN
- **Risk coefficient $k_1 = 1.0$, $k_2 = 0.98$, $k_3 = 1.0$, $k_4 = 1.0$**

**Determine the design load for combinations 1, 2, and 6, and identify the critical combination.**

**(b)** A simply supported **steel roof purlin** of span 6 m carries AC sheeting. The dead load (sheeting + self-weight) is 0.20 kN/m² and live load is 0.40 kN/m². Purlins are spaced at 1.5 m. Compute the **maximum bending moment and shear force** under the governing load combination as per IS 800:2007.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION A(a)**

**Step 1 — Characteristic Loads on Column (tributary area = 36 m²):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DL_{\text{roof}} & = 0.55 \times 36 = 19.80 \;\text{kN} \\
IL_{\text{roof}} & = 0.40 \times 36 = 14.40 \;\text{kN} \\
WL_{\text{suction}} & = 1.80 \times 36 = 64.80 \;\text{kN (upward)} \\
DL_{\text{column}} & = 4.0 \;\text{kN} \\
\hline
\text{Total } DL & = 19.80 + 4.0 = 23.80 \;\text{kN} \\
\text{Total } IL & = 14.40 \;\text{kN} \\
\text{Total } WL & = 64.80 \;\text{kN (upward)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Factored Load Combinations:**

**Combination 1 — $1.5(DL + IL)$:**

$$P_1 = 1.5 \times (23.80 + 14.40) = 1.5 \times 38.20 = 57.30 \;\text{kN (compression, downward)}$$

**Combination 2 — $1.2(DL + IL + WL)$ (downward, WL favorable):**

$$P_2 = 1.2 \times (23.80 + 14.40 + 0) = 1.2 \times 38.20 = 45.84 \;\text{kN (compression)}$$

**Combination 2 — $1.2(DL + IL - WL)$ (upward, WL unfavorable):**

$$P_{2,up} = 1.2 \times (23.80 + 14.40 - 64.80) = 1.2 \times (-26.60) = -31.92 \;\text{kN (uplift)}$$

**Combination 6 — $0.9DL - 1.5WL$ (uplift reversal):**

$$P_6 = 0.9 \times 23.80 - 1.5 \times 64.80 = 21.42 - 97.20 = -75.78 \;\text{kN (uplift)}$$

**Step 3 — Critical Combination Identification:**

| Combination | Factored Load (kN) | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | +57.30 | Maximum Compression |
| 2 (DL+IL+WL) | +45.84 | Compression (with favorable WL) |
| 2 (DL+IL-WL) | -31.92 | Uplift |
| **6** | **-75.78** | **Maximum Uplift** |

> **Critical Combination = Combination 6** with **75.78 kN uplift**.
>
> **Note:** For the column base, this uplift must be resisted by **anchor bolts and base plate weight**. Designers must verify that **$P_6 \le W_{base\ plate\ + \ anchors}$** to prevent column pull-out.

**Marks Distribution: [7 marks]**
- 1.5 marks — Load calculations
- 2 marks — Combination 1 and 2 evaluation
- 2 marks — Combination 6 evaluation
- 1.5 marks — Critical identification and design implication

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION A(b)**

**Step 1 — UDL on Purlin (per m of purlin length):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DL_{\text{purlin}} & = 0.20 \times 1.5 = 0.30 \;\text{kN/m} \\
IL_{\text{purlin}} & = 0.40 \times 1.5 = 0.60 \;\text{kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Governing Factored UDL (Combination 1):**

$$w_u = 1.5 \times (0.30 + 0.60) = 1.5 \times 0.90 = 1.35 \;\text{kN/m}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Moment (simply supported, span L = 6 m):**

$$M_u = \frac{w_u \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{1.35 \times 6^2}{8} = \frac{1.35 \times 36}{8} = 6.075 \;\text{kN·m}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Shear Force:**

$$V_u = \frac{w_u \cdot L}{2} = \frac{1.35 \times 6}{2} = 4.05 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Serviceability Check (deflection under DL+IL, $\gamma_f = 1.0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_s & = 0.30 + 0.60 = 0.90 \;\text{kN/m} \\
\delta_{max} & = \frac{5 \cdot w_s \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I} \\
& \le \frac{L}{150} \;\; (\text{IS 800:2007 Cl. 11.4.1 for purlins})
\end{aligned}
$$

> The designer should check that the purlin's moment of resistance $\ge M_u$ and its section modulus is sufficient.

**Marks Distribution: [7 marks]**
- 1.5 marks — UDL calculation
- 1.5 marks — Factored load identification
- 2 marks — Bending moment
- 1 mark — Shear force
- 1 mark — Serviceability check

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question PECET864]` — ALTERNATIVE

**(a)** A **gantry girder** in a steel industrial building supports an EOT crane with the following data:
- Crane capacity: 200 kN
- Self-weight of crane (excluding hoist): 80 kN
- Self-weight of crab: 30 kN
- Number of wheels per side: 2
- Minimum hook approach: 1.2 m
- Wheel spacing: 3.0 m
- Gantry girder span: 18 m
- Crane girder span: 16 m

**Compute the maximum static and impact-loaded wheel load and the maximum bending moment in the gantry girder under vertical loading.**

**(b)** Explain the **load path** in a steel-framed multi-storey building subjected to (i) gravity loads and (ii) lateral seismic loads. Illustrate with a clear **free-body-diagram equivalent description** and state why **bracing systems** are critical for seismic resistance.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION B(a) — Vertical Wheel Load and Bending Moment**

**Step 1 — Total Crane Load on All Wheels (2 sides × 2 wheels = 4 wheels):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total vertical load} & = \text{Crane capacity} + \text{Crane self-weight} + \text{Crab self-weight} \\
& = 200 + 80 + 30 = 310 \;\text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Static Wheel Load:**

When the crab is closest to one side of the crane, two wheels on that side carry the maximum load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum wheel load (static)} & = \frac{(W_{crane}/2) + W_{cap} \cdot (L_{girder} - a)/L_{girder}}{2} \\
\text{where } a & = \text{min hook approach} = 1.2 \;\text{m} \\
\text{Per-side load (no crab offset)} & = 310/2 = 155 \;\text{kN} \\
\text{Maximum load on 2 wheels of one side} & = 80/2 + 200 \cdot (16 - 1.2)/16 \\
& = 40 + 200 \times 14.8/16 \\
& = 40 + 185.0 = 225.0 \;\text{kN on 2 wheels} \\
\text{Max static wheel load} & = 225.0/2 = 112.5 \;\text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Impact Factor:**
Per IS 875 Part 2 (Cl. 7.4), for an EOT crane of 200 kN capacity, impact factor = **1.10**

$$
P_{wheel,\text{impact}} = 1.10 \times 112.5 = 123.75 \;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Moment in Gantry Girder (18 m span):**

For a simply supported gantry girder with **two point loads** at wheel spacing 3.0 m, the maximum moment occurs when the **mid-span coincides with the center of the wheel group**. By influence line:

$$
M_{max} = P \cdot \left( \frac{a}{L} \right) \cdot (L - a) \cdot \text{geometry factor}
$$

For two equal point loads $P$ spaced $c$ apart on a simply supported span $L$, with the wheel group centered at mid-span:

$$
M_{max} = P \cdot \left( \frac{L}{2} - \frac{c}{4} \right) \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{1}{1} = P \cdot (L - c/2)
$$

Using the **impacted wheel load** $P = 123.75$ kN and $c = 3.0$ m:

$$
M_{max} = 2 \times 123.75 \times \left( \frac{18}{2} - \frac{3.0}{4} \right) = 247.5 \times (9.0 - 0.75) = 247.5 \times 8.25 = 2041.875 \;\text{kN·m}
$$

Wait — this is a simplification. The more rigorous approach: place **one wheel at distance $x$ from left support**, second at $x + 3.0$ m. Maximum moment under one of the loads when both loads and the resultant are properly placed. For symmetric placement, max moment under inner wheel:

$$
M_{max} = P \cdot \left( \frac{L}{2} - \frac{c}{4} \right) \cdot 1 = 2P \cdot \left( \frac{L}{2} - \frac{c}{4} \right)
$$

Recomputing carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{max} & = 2 \times 123.75 \times (9.0 - 0.75) \\
& = 247.5 \times 8.25 \\
& = 2041.88 \;\text{kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marks Distribution: [7 marks]**
- 1.5 marks — Load distribution calculation
- 1.5 marks — Maximum wheel load (static)
- 1 mark — Impact factor application
- 2 marks — Maximum bending moment derivation
- 1 mark — Final answer with units

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION B(b) — Load Path Explanation**

**(i) Gravity Load Path:**

> Loads flow in a **vertical, hierarchical sequence**:
>
> **Slab → Beam → Column → Foundation → Soil**
>
> - **Slab** receives DL + LL, transfers to supporting **beams** via bending
> - **Beams** transfer reactions to **columns** at beam-column joints
> - **Columns** carry accumulated gravity to **footing/foundation**
> - **Foundation** distributes load to **soil** over a contact area such that bearing pressure $\le$ SBC
>
> **Free-body concept:** Imagine cutting a horizontal plane at any floor level — the column section above this plane carries the sum of all DL + LL from all floors above. This **cumulative gravity** is why **lower-floor columns** are progressively larger in cross-section.

**(ii) Lateral Seismic Load Path:**

> Seismic loads are **inertial forces** proportional to mass. They flow through:
>
> **Mass → Floor Diaphragm → Vertical Lateral-Force-Resisting System (LFRS) → Foundation**
>
> The LFRS in a steel building can be:
> - **Moment-Resisting Frames (MRF):** Beams and columns resist lateral load through **flexural action** (bending). Beam-column joints develop **moments** to transfer horizontal shear.
> - **Braced Frames:** Diagonal braces carry **axial tension/compression** to transfer horizontal shear to the foundation. Highly efficient and stiffer than MRF.
> - **Shear Walls (in composite steel construction):** Concrete cores provide major lateral stiffness.
>
> **Why bracing is critical:**
> 1. **Stiffness:** Braced frames are 2–3 times stiffer than unbraced MRF, reducing **drift** (lateral displacement) under design earthquake.
> 2. **Strength:** Diagonal braces carry large axial forces economically.
> 3. **Damage control:** Concentrically braced frames (CBF) absorb energy through **bracing buckling/yielding** in severe events, protecting beams and columns.
> 4. **Code compliance:** IS 1893 limits inter-storey drift to $0.004 \times h$ per storey; bracing helps meet this.

**Marks Distribution: [7 marks]**
- 2 marks — Gravity load path with hierarchy
- 2 marks — Seismic load path with FBD description
- 2 marks — Role of bracing (4 key points)
- 1 mark — Code reference and conclusion

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting partial safety factors** — Writing $DL + IL$ instead of $1.5(DL + IL)$ in the answer. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Mixing up wind load direction** — Forgetting that wind on a roof creates **uplift/suction** and using it as downward. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 3. **Ignoring Combination 6** — Skipping the $0.9DL \pm 1.5WL$ check for roof trusses. (Loss: 3–4 marks — examiners specifically look for this!)
> 4. **Wrong tributary area** — For interior beams, tributary area = $\frac{L_1 \times L_2}{2}$ (NOT $L_1 \times L_2$). (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Confusing limit states** — Using $\gamma_f$ values for serviceability (which should be **1.0**). (Loss: 1 mark)
> 6. **No units in final answer** — Always write kN, kN/m, kN·m, or MPa with the numerical value.
> 7. **Crane impact factor omitted** — Forgetting to multiply wheel load by 1.10 (or as applicable). (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 8. **Seismic weight wrong** — Including LL with full value. Per IS 1893, only **25% to 50% of LL** is included in seismic weight (depending on occupancy).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ✅ **Definition Essentials**
- [ ] **Loading consideration** = identification + classification + quantification + combination of all forces/deformations on a steel structure per IS 800:2007 and IS 875 series
- [ ] **Limit State Method** uses **factored loads** and **factored strengths**

### ✅ **Load Types**
- [ ] **DL** — IS 875 Pt 1, unit weight of steel = 78.5 kN/m³
- [ ] **IL/LL** — IS 875 Pt 2 (e.g., 0.40 kN/m² for roof without access)
- [ ] **WL** — IS 875 Pt 3, $p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$, $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$
- [ ] **EL** — IS 1893, $A_h = \dfrac{Z}{2} \cdot \dfrac{S_a}{g} \cdot \dfrac{I}{R}$, $V_B = A_h \cdot W$
- [ ] **Crane** — IS 875 Pt 2 Cl. 7 + IS 800 Cl. 10.7
- [ ] **Fatigue** — IS 800 Cl. 13

### ✅ **Partial Safety Factors ($\gamma_f$) — Must Memorize**
- [ ] DL alone (unfavorable) = 1.5
- [ ] DL with WL/EL (favorable) = 0.9
- [ ] IL with DL alone = 1.5
- [ ] IL combined with WL/EL = 1.2
- [ ] WL/EL combined with DL+IL = 1.2
- [ ] WL/EL reversal (no LL) = 1.5

### ✅ **Seven Standard Combinations (IS 800:2007 Table 4)**
- [ ] $1.5(DL + IL)$
- [ ] $1.2(DL + IL \pm WL)$
- [ ] $1.2(DL + IL \pm EL)$
- [ ] $1.5(DL \pm WL)$
- [ ] $1.5(DL \pm EL)$
- [ ] $0.9DL \pm 1.5WL$
- [ ] $0.9DL \pm 1.5EL$

### ✅ **Critical Loading Scenarios**
- [ ] **Roof trusses** → Uplift (Combination 6) usually governs
- [ ] **Multi-storey buildings** → Combinations 2 or 3
- [ ] **Crane girders** → Crane + impact + crane surge + WL
- [ ] **Bridges** → Moving load + impact + fatigue

### ✅ **Load Path Hierarchy**
- [ ] **Gravity:** Slab → Beam → Column → Foundation → Soil
- [ ] **Lateral:** Mass → Diaphragm → MRF/Braces → Foundation

### ✅ **Serviceability Limit State**
- [ ] $\gamma_f = 1.0$ for all loads
- [ ] Checks: deflection, drift, vibration, crack width

### ✅ **Key Numerical Values to Remember**
- [ ] Steel density = 7850 kg/m³ = 78.5 kN/m³
- [ ] $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- [ ] Wind constant = 0.6 (in $p_z = 0.6 V_z^2$)
- [ ] $R$ for steel MRF = 5.0
- [ ] $R$ for steel braced frame = 4.0
- [ ] Zone III (Kerala) Z = 0.16
- [ ] Zone IV (parts of Kerala) Z = 0.24
- [ ] Crane impact factor = 1.10 to 1.25
- [ ] $\gamma_m$ for steel = 1.10 (yield), 1.25 (ultimate)

### ✅ **Tributary Area Quick Reference**
- [ ] Interior column: full panel area ($L_1 \times L_2$)
- [ ] Edge column: half panel area
- [ ] Corner column: quarter panel area
- [ ] Interior beam: half panel width on each side
- [ ] Edge beam: half panel on one side

---
