# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three salient design requirements of a gantry girder that distinguish it from an ordinary floor beam.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Resistance to transverse horizontal surge force** ($H_t$): The girder must be designed for a lateral bending moment about its weak axis (z-z), caused by the 5% surge from the moving crane trolley. Ordinary beams carry only vertical gravity loads.

2. **Deflection control under moving wheel loads:** The vertical deflection limit is stricter — **L/750 to L/1000** (vs. L/360 for ordinary beams), because excessive sag would cause the crane to "climb" or "run downhill", endangering operations.

3. **Fatigue consideration:** Gantry girders supporting heavy-duty cranes are subject to a large number of load cycles. The section must be checked for **fatigue** (per IS 800 Cl. 13), classifying the detail category. This is not relevant for static floor beams.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define "transverse surge force" and "longitudinal force" in a gantry girder. State their design magnitudes per IS 875 Part 2.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Transverse Surge Force ($H_t$):** The horizontal force exerted by the crane trolley/crab on the gantry girder *perpendicular to the crane runway direction*, arising during acceleration, braking, and lateral sway of the lifted load. **Design magnitude = 5%** of the weight of (trolley + crab + maximum lifted load) for electric cranes, applied at the top of the rail.

- **Longitudinal Force ($H_l$):** The horizontal force exerted *along the runway direction*, caused by the longitudinal acceleration/braking of the crane bridge itself. **Design magnitude = 5%** of the maximum static wheel load, applied at the rail level.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Design a built-up plate gantry girder for the following data:**

- Span (centre-to-centre of columns): **L = 10.0 m**
- Crane capacity (SWL): **150 kN**
- Self-weight of crane bridge: **120 kN**
- Self-weight of trolley + crab: **30 kN**
- Number of wheels per rail: **2**
- Wheel spacing (on same rail): **a = 3.6 m**
- Steel grade: **Fe 410** ($f_y$ = 250 MPa)
- Use Fe 410 plates.

**Assume: Suitable lateral bracing is provided at the top flange to resist the surge force.**

#### Sub-part (a) (7 Marks): Compute the maximum bending moment, maximum shear force, and required section modulus for the gantry girder. Apply impact factor and surge force per IS 875.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum wheel load (static):**

$$\text{Load on one rail} = \dfrac{120}{2} + (30 + 150) = 60 + 180 = 240 \text{ kN}$$

$$W = \dfrac{240}{2} = 120 \text{ kN per wheel (static)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply impact factor:**

$$W_{factored} = 1.25 \times 120 = 150 \text{ kN per wheel (factored)}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending moment (strong axis):**

Using the symmetric two-wheel formula:

$$M_{max,y} = \dfrac{W_{factored}}{2L} \cdot \left( L - \dfrac{a}{2} \right)^2 - \dfrac{W_{factored} \cdot a}{2}$$

$$M_{max,y} = \dfrac{150}{2 \times 10} \cdot \left( 10 - 1.8 \right)^2 - \dfrac{150 \times 3.6}{2}$$

$$M_{max,y} = 7.5 \times (8.2)^2 - 270 = 7.5 \times 67.24 - 270$$

$$M_{max,y} = 504.3 - 270 = \boxed{234.3 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum shear force (strong axis):**

$$V_{max} = W_{factored} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{a}{L} \right) = 150 \times \left( 1 + \dfrac{3.6}{10} \right) = 150 \times 1.36$$

$$V_{max} = \boxed{204 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5 — Required plastic section modulus:**

$$Z_{p,req} = \dfrac{M_{max,y}}{f_y / \gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{234.3 \times 10^6}{250 / 1.10} = \dfrac{234.3 \times 10^6}{227.27}$$

$$Z_{p,req} = \boxed{1.031 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 = 1031 \text{ cm}^3}$$

#### Sub-part (b) (7 Marks): Select a trial plate girder section and verify its adequacy in (i) bending, (ii) shear, and (iii) vertical deflection.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Trial Section:**

Select a built-up plate girder:
- Web: **800 mm × 10 mm**
- Flanges: **2 × 250 mm × 20 mm** (one on each side of web)

**Step 2 — Section Properties:**

$$I_y = \dfrac{10 \times 800^3}{12} + 2 \times \left[ \dfrac{250 \times 20^3}{12} + 250 \times 20 \times (410)^2 \right]$$

$$I_y = 4.267 \times 10^8 + 2 \times \left[ 1.667 \times 10^5 + 8.405 \times 10^8 \right]$$

$$I_y = 4.267 \times 10^8 + 1.681 \times 10^9 = 2.108 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$Z_{p,y} = \dfrac{I_y}{h/2 + t_f/2} = \dfrac{2.108 \times 10^9}{410} = 5.141 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 = 5141 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Check (i) — Bending:**

$$M_{d,y} = Z_{p,y} \times \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = 5.141 \times 10^6 \times 227.27 = 1.169 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm} = 1169 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_{d,y}$ (1169 kN·m) $> M_{max,y}$ (234.3 kN·m), section is **SAFE in bending** with a utilisation ratio of $234.3/1169 = 0.20$. ✓

**Check (ii) — Shear:**

$$V_{d,web} = \dfrac{f_{yw} \cdot t_w \cdot h_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{250 \times 10 \times 800}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10} = 1050 \text{ kN}$$

Since $V_{d,web}$ (1050 kN) $> V_{max}$ (204 kN), section is **SAFE in shear**. ✓

**Check (iii) — Vertical Deflection:**

For a simply-supported girder with two equal symmetric loads at distance a from supports:

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{W \cdot a \cdot (3L^2 - 4a^2)}{24 E I_y}$$

With $W = 120 \text{ kN}$ (working/unfactored), $a = 3.6$ m, $L = 10$ m, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $I_y = 2.108 \times 10^9$ mm⁴:

$$3L^2 - 4a^2 = 3 \times 100 - 4 \times 12.96 = 300 - 51.84 = 248.16 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{120 \times 10^3 \times 3600 \times 248.16 \times 10^6}{24 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 2.108 \times 10^9}$$

$$\delta_v = \dfrac{1.072 \times 10^{17}}{1.012 \times 10^{16}} = 10.6 \text{ mm}$$

Permissible: $\dfrac{L}{750} = \dfrac{10000}{750} = 13.33 \text{ mm}$

Since 10.6 < 13.33, **vertical deflection is OK**. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Use UNFACTORED loads for deflection checks.** Many students mistakenly use 1.25 × W (impacted) for deflection, which is **wrong** — deflection is a serviceability check, so use the working load.
> 2. **Apply impact only to vertical loads**, not to surge or longitudinal forces.
> 3. **Transverse surge is applied at the top of rail**, not at the centroid of the section — a small eccentricity creates additional torsion, often ignored in simple problems.
> 4. **Always check combined bending and shear** (IS 800 Cl. 9.3.1), even if the section is over-designed — marks are reserved for *stating and verifying* the check.
> 5. **State code clauses explicitly.** A student writing "IS 800 Cl. 8.4.1" gets extra credit. Students who skip clause references lose 1–2 marks per check.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Design the lateral bracing and the bearing stiffener for the gantry girder designed in Question A.**

**Given (from Q A):** $L = 10$ m, $W_{factored} = 150$ kN/wheel, $H_t = 6.25$ kN/wheel (transverse surge per rail), section is plate girder 800 mm × 10 mm web + 250 mm × 20 mm flanges, $f_y = 250$ MPa.

#### Sub-part (a) (7 Marks): Design the lateral bracing channel required to resist the transverse surge force and verify the weak-axis bending.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total transverse surge on one rail:**

$$H_{t,total} = 2 \times 6.25 = 12.5 \text{ kN per rail}$$

**Step 2 — Lateral Bending Moment in Top Flange (assumed to be resisted by channel + top flange):**

With bracing at top flange, the weak-axis moment (assuming simply-supported laterally):

$$M_z = \dfrac{H_{t,total} \cdot L}{4} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 10}{4} = 31.25 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Weak-axis moment capacity (using top flange only):**

For the top flange (250 × 20), the section modulus about the z-axis of the **flange pair**:

$$Z_{p,z,flange} = 2 \times \dfrac{t_f \cdot b_f^2}{4} = 2 \times \dfrac{20 \times 250^2}{4} = 6.25 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3 = 625 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$M_{d,z,flange} = 6.25 \times 10^5 \times 227.27 = 1.42 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 142 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_{d,z,flange}$ (142 kN·m) $\gg M_z$ (31.25 kN·m), the **top flange alone is sufficient**. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, a **lateral channel** (e.g., ISMC 150 @ 16.4 kg/m) is provided for **rigidity** and to **reduce the unsupported length of the top flange for LTB**, even if structurally the flange could carry the moment alone.

**Step 4 — Provide ISMC 150 channel (A = 20.88 cm², $I_z$ = 484 cm⁴, $Z_{z} = 64.5$ cm³):**

Effective lateral section modulus (channel + 1/6 of web):

$$Z_{eff,z} = Z_{channel} + \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot Z_{web} = 64.5 + \dfrac{1}{6} \times \left( \dfrac{10 \times 800^2}{6} \right) = 64.5 + 178 = 242.5 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$M_{d,z,combined} = 242.5 \times 10^3 \times 227.27 = 55.1 \text{ kN·m} > 31.25 \text{ kN·m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Connection of channel to top flange:**

Use **φ 20 mm bolts at 200 mm c/c** in 6 mm fillet weld (alternatively welded). The channel must be able to transfer $H_{t,total} = 12.5$ kN as a longitudinal shear.

Bolt capacity in single shear (4.6 grade, $\phi 20$ mm):

$$V_{dsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{mb}} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \times 314.16 / 1.25 = 58{,}045 \text{ N} = 58.05 \text{ kN per bolt}$$

Since one bolt (58 kN) $\gg 12.5$ kN, a single bolt per panel is sufficient. Provide **2 bolts per panel** at 200 mm c/c for redundancy. ✓

#### Sub-part (b) (7 Marks): Design the bearing stiffener at the support.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bearing force at support:**

$$F = V_{max} = 204 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Trial bearing stiffener geometry (per IS 800 Cl. 8.7.4):**

Use **2 stiffeners**, one on each side of the web:
- Outstand width: **b = 90 mm**
- Thickness: **t = 12 mm**
- Length: equal to web depth = **800 mm**

**Step 3 — Bearing check on web:**

$$F_{w,allow} = (b + n_2) \cdot t_w \cdot \dfrac{f_{yw}}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

With $n_2 = 50$ mm (edge of bearing stiffener to web toe of weld):

$$F_{w,allow} = (90 + 50) \times 10 \times \dfrac{250}{1.10} = 3.18 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 318 \text{ kN} > 204 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Bearing stress on stiffener-to-flange contact:**

$$f_p = \dfrac{F}{2 \cdot b \cdot t} = \dfrac{204 \times 10^3}{2 \times 90 \times 12} = 94.4 \text{ MPa}$$

Allowable: $\dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = 227.27$ MPa. Since 94.4 < 227.27, **stiffener bearing is OK**. ✓

**Step 5 — Buckling check of stiffener (per IS 800 Cl. 8.7.3):**

Effective column area = $2 \times (b \times t) = 2 \times 90 \times 12 = 2160$ mm²

Buckling length: 0.7 × web depth = 0.7 × 800 = 560 mm

Radius of gyration: $r = \dfrac{12}{\sqrt{12}} = 3.46$ mm

$\lambda = \dfrac{560}{3.46} = 161.8$ (high slenderness — use IS 800 Cl. 7.1.2.1 design curve 'c')

For Fe 410 and $\lambda = 161.8$, $f_{cd} \approx 80$ MPa (from table).

Buckling capacity: $F_{bd} = 80 \times 2160 = 1.728 \times 10^5$ N = 172.8 kN < 204 kN. ✗

**Refine:** Use thicker stiffeners. Try $t = 16$ mm:

$r = \dfrac{16}{\sqrt{12}} = 4.62$ mm, $\lambda = \dfrac{560}{4.62} = 121.2$, $f_{cd} \approx 110$ MPa

$F_{bd} = 110 \times (2 \times 90 \times 16) = 110 \times 2880 = 3.17 \times 10^5$ N = 316.8 kN > 204 kN. ✓

**Adopt: 2 stiffeners of 90 × 16 mm, full depth (800 mm), welded to web with 6 mm fillet weld on both sides.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Bearing Stiffener Design:**
> Students often **omit the buckling check** of the stiffener acting as a column. The stiffener must be designed as a **short column** carrying the end reaction, with the buckling length taken as **0.7 × d** (where d = web depth) per IS 800 Cl. 8.7.3.1. Skipping this check costs **2–3 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design of Gantry Girders**

### 1. **Loads (The Holy Trinity)**
- Vertical wheel load $W$ (factored with impact 1.25)
- Transverse surge $H_t$ = 5% of (trolley + crab + lift)
- Longitudinal force $H_l$ = 5% of max wheel load

### 2. **Sectional Forces**
- $M_{max,y}$ from influence line placement of wheels
- $V_{max}$ with one wheel at the support
- $M_z$ from $H_t \times L / 4$ (with lateral bracing at top flange)

### 3. **Strength Checks (Factored Loads)**
- Moment capacity ($Z_p \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$)
- Shear capacity ($f_{yw} t_w h_w / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0})$)
- Web buckling (if $\lambda_w > 0.8$ for Fe 410)
- Web crippling at bearing
- Bearing stiffener design (column buckling check)
- Combined $B + S$ interaction

### 4. **Serviceability (Working Loads)**
- Vertical deflection ≤ L/750 (crane girders) or L/1000 (heavy cranes)
- Horizontal sway ≤ L/400
- Always use **unfactored** loads

### 5. **Stiffener Rules**
- Intermediate stiffeners when $d/t_w > 67\varepsilon$ (Fe 410)
- Bearing stiffeners at all supports
- Stiffener outstand ≤ $14 t \varepsilon$
- Buckling length of bearing stiffener = 0.7 d

### 6. **Codal Anchors to Quote**
- IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.2 (member bending)
- IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.4 (member shear)
- IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.7 (bearing stiffeners)
- IS 800:2007 Cl. 9.3 (combined B + S)
- IS 875 Part 2:1987 (crane loads)
- IS 2062:2011 (steel grade Fe 410 → $f_y$ = 250 MPa)

### 7. **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid**
- ❌ Applying impact factor to surge force
- ❌ Using factored loads for deflection
- ❌ Forgetting to design bearing stiffener as a column
- ❌ Confusing longitudinal vs. transverse forces
- ❌ Missing the clause number in the answer
- ❌ Forgetting to check LTB when lateral bracing is not provided

> [!TIP]
> **Pearl of Wisdom:** Always present a **summary table** at the end of your design showing "Demand vs. Capacity" for each check. KTU examiners award full marks when the table is clean, comprehensive, and codes are quoted.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Light Gauge Steel Structures: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Light Gauge Steel Structures: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Light Gauge Steel (LGS) Structures** are structural assemblies fabricated from **cold-formed steel sections** — thin steel sheets, strips, plates, or flat bars of uniform thickness — generally between **0.4 mm and 6.4 mm** — shaped by press-braking, cold-rolling, or bending at ambient room temperature (without the application of heat).

According to the **Indian Standard IS 801:1975 (Reaffirmed 2010)** — *Code of Practice for Use of Cold-Formed Light Gauge Steel Structural Members in General Building Construction* — these members are predominantly used where the **yield stress (f_y) of steel does not exceed 410 MPa**, although modern codes such as **AISI S100** and **Eurocode 3 Part 1-3** permit higher grades up to **550 MPa**.

The corresponding international standards are:
- **AISI S100 (North American Specification – NAS)**
- **AS/NZS 4600 (Australia/New Zealand Standard)**
- **Eurocode 3: Part 1-3 (EN 1993-1-3)**
- **IS 801:1975 (Indian Standard)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it as "Origami for Steel."**
>
> Just as an artist folds a flat sheet of paper into a strong crane, structural engineers fold thin, flat steel coils at room temperature into sturdy **C-purlins, Z-sections, sigma sections, and lipped channels**. The thickness is so small that you could crush the empty section between two fingers — yet, when properly formed and connected, the same member can carry roof loads of an entire warehouse spanning 15 m or more.

The strength of a **Light Gauge Steel Section (LGSS)** does not depend on raw material thickness but on the **geometry, corner radii, intermediate stiffeners (lips), and buckling resistance** of the cold-formed cross-section. The cold-working process during manufacturing also **strain-hardens** the steel at the corners, locally increasing its yield strength by up to **20–30%** — a phenomenon absent in hot-rolled sections.

### 1.3 Hot-Rolled vs. Cold-Formed (Light Gauge) — Key Distinctions

| Parameter | Hot-Rolled Steel Section (HRS) | Cold-Formed / Light Gauge (LGS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Hot rolling at ~1100 °C | Cold rolling / press braking at ambient temperature |
| **Thickness range** | 5 mm to 50+ mm | **0.4 mm to 6.4 mm** |
| **Typical yield stress (f_y)** | 250 MPa (Fe 410) | **210–550 MPa** |
| **Corner strain-hardening** | Negligible | Significant (up to +30 % at corners) |
| **Section shapes** | I, H, C, T, L, channels, angles | C, Z, Sigma, Lipped channel, Hat, Decking, Folded plate |
| **Governing failure mode** | Yielding, lateral-torsional buckling | **Local buckling, distortional buckling, web crippling** |
| **Design Code (India)** | IS 800:2007 | **IS 801:1975** |
| **Section slenderness (b/t)** | Low (compact) | **High (slender)** |
| **Residual stresses** | High (thermal) | Low to moderate (cold work) |

### 1.4 Applications (Industry Use)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Roof and wall cladding (profile sheets, trapezoidal sheets)**
> - **Purlins and girts (Cold-formed Z & C sections)**
> - **Floor decking (composite slabs)**
> - **Industrial buildings, warehouses, godowns**
> - **Modular prefabricated housing (LGSF – Light Gauge Steel Framing)**
> - **Cold storage structures, mezzanine floors**
> - **Trusses with battened or welded thin-walled chords**
> - **Transmission line towers, conveyor galleries**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Cold-Formed Steel Behaves Differently

The classical **theory of thin-walled members** governs the design of light gauge steel. Unlike hot-rolled compact sections, cold-formed members are inherently **slender** — their **width-to-thickness (b/t) ratios are high**, making them susceptible to **local plate buckling** before reaching yield.

#### 2.1.1 The Five Buckling Modes in Cold-Formed Members

1. **Local Buckling (Mode 1):** Short-wave, plate-like buckling of individual elements (flange, web, lip) interacting at the junction. The half-wavelength is roughly equal to the largest stiffened element width.
2. **Distortional Buckling (Mode 2):** Rotation of the lip and flange assembly about the web-flange junction. Half-wavelength is intermediate (typically 500–2000 mm). Introduced by **Lau and Hancock (1987)**.
3. **Global (Euler / Lateral-Torsional) Buckling (Mode 3):** Overall member flexural/torsional buckling analogous to classical column theory.
4. **Shear Buckling of Web:** Important in deep decking sections.
5. **Interactive / Coupled Buckling:** Combinations of the above three primary modes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Mode Framework (Hancock, 1994)** is the analytical backbone of modern cold-formed steel codes. KTU examiners frequently test the **identification and distinction** of these three primary modes.

#### 2.1.2 Effective Width Concept (Winter's Formulation)

Because slender compression elements cannot develop the full yield stress over their entire width (local buckling intervenes), only a portion of the width — the **effective width (b_eff)** — is assumed to carry the design stress. The remaining portion is structurally ineffective.

The classic **Winter's Formula (1947)** for stiffened elements under uniform compression:

$$
\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_{\max}}{f_{cr}}}
$$

$$
\rho = \dfrac{b_{eff}}{b} =
\begin{cases}
1 - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda} & \text{for } \lambda \geq 0.673 \quad \text{(slender)} \\[2mm]
1.0 & \text{for } \lambda < 0.673 \quad \text{(compact / non-slender)}
\end{cases}
$$

where the **elastic local buckling stress** of a stiffened compression element is given by:

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot E}{12\,(1 - \nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}
$$

with the **buckling coefficient** $k$ taking the following values:

| Boundary Condition | $k$ |
|---|---|
| Both edges simply supported (plate) | 4.0 |
| One edge fixed, one free (stiffened) | 0.425 |
| One edge simply supported, one fixed (web in flexure) | 5.42 |
| One edge fixed, one free (unstiffened) | 0.43 |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Cheat-Code Edition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this table — these are the high-frequency formulas for Module 4 problems.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slenderness of element | $\lambda = \sqrt{f_{\max} / f_{cr}}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Reduction factor (slender) | $\rho = 1 - 0.22 / \lambda$ | $\lambda \geq 0.673$ |
| 3 | Reduction factor (compact) | $\rho = 1.0$ | $\lambda < 0.673$ |
| 4 | Effective width | $b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$ | mm |
| 5 | Elastic local buckling stress | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^{2} E}{12(1 - \nu^{2})} (t/b)^{2}$ | MPa; $\nu = 0.30$ |
| 6 | Modulus of Elasticity (Steel) | $E = \mathbf{2.0 \times 10^{5}}$ **MPa** | IS 801 / IS 800 |
| 7 | Poisson's ratio (Steel) | $\nu = \mathbf{0.30}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Shear modulus (Steel) | $G = \mathbf{0.769 \times 10^{5}}$ **MPa** | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ |
| 9 | **Maximum b/t limits (IS 801:1975)** | | |
| | (a) Stiffened compression element | $b/t \leq \mathbf{60}$ | (Grade 340) |
| | (b) Unstiffened compression element | $b/t \leq \mathbf{30}$ | (Grade 340) |
| 10 | Increased yield at corners | $f_{yc} = f_y + \dfrac{5 \cdot f_u \cdot (b/t)}{(B/p)^{0.5}}$ | AISI S100 formula |
| 11 | Web crippling nominal strength | Per IS 801 Tables 6, 7 (4 cases) | depends on $N/h$, $t$, $r/t$ |
| 12 | Effective area | $A_{eff} = \Sigma \,(b_{eff} \cdot t)$ | mm² |
| 13 | Design compressive stress | $F_a = 0.6 \cdot f_y$ (allowable) | ASD basis |
| 14 | Design bending strength | $M_n = S_e \cdot F_b$ | N·mm |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The effective width concept, despite being introduced in 1947, remains the **workhorse of cold-formed steel design worldwide**. It is used in:

- **Design of purlin and girt sections** for industrial sheds.
- **Load-bearing stud walls** in modular LGSF housing (LGSF = Light Gauge Steel Framing).
- **Cold-formed trusses** for long-span airports, sports halls.
- **Steel storage rack uprights** (pallet racks, drive-in racks).
- **Transmission line towers** in extra-high-voltage networks (e.g., 765 kV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern codes (AISI S100-16, Eurocode 3-1-3) have supplemented the effective width method with the **Direct Strength Method (DSM)**, but for KTU 2024 scheme exams the **Effective Width Method (EWM)** — codified in IS 801 — remains the primary expected approach.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Why the Winter Reduction Factor Equals $\rho = 1 - 0.22/\lambda$

The effective width concept originated with **von Kármán (1932)** who proposed that a thin plate of total width $b$ loaded in uniform compression can be replaced (for load-carrying capacity) by two strips of total effective width $b_{eff}$ placed at the supported edges, both stressed to the yield value $f_y$.

**Step 1 — Plate behavior under uniform compression**

Consider a simply supported thin plate of width $b$, thickness $t$, compressed uniformly along its edges. The elastic critical (Euler-type) plate buckling stress is:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^{2} E}{12(1 - \nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}
$$

For a long simply supported plate under uniform compression, $k = 4.0$, giving the classical **Bryan/Levy solution**.

**Step 2 — Von Kármán's postulate**

Von Kármán assumed that the actual load-carrying width of the buckled plate equals the effective width of two strips at the supports, each carrying the yield stress. Equating the actual load (inelastic) to the effective load (elastic) at yield:

$$
\sigma_{\max} \cdot b = f_y \cdot b_{eff}
$$

Solving:

$$
b_{eff} = b \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{\max}}{f_y} = b \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Winter's empirical refinement (1947)**

The von Kármán solution over-predicts strength because the **post-buckled plate has non-uniform stress distribution**. George Winter (Cornell University) ran 200+ experiments on cold-formed sections and proposed the following empirically-fitted formula:

$$
\rho = \dfrac{b_{eff}}{b} = \dfrac{1 - 0.22 / \lambda}{\lambda} \quad (\text{original Winter form, 1947})
$$

**Step 4 — Simplified form (modification for code use)**

Because the original Winter form was cumbersome, codes adopted the simplified linear form (introduced in AISI 1968, retained in IS 801):

$$
\rho = 1 - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda} \quad \text{when } \lambda \geq 0.673
$$

**Step 5 — Continuity at $\lambda = 0.673$**

The transition point $\lambda = 0.673$ is obtained by setting $\rho = 1.0$ in the Winter formula:

$$
1.0 = 1 - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 0.673
$$

For $\lambda < 0.673$, the element is "compact" and the full width is effective ($\rho = 1.0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Converged Form — adopted in IS 801 and AISI S100:**
>
> $$
> \rho = \max\left(1.0, \ 1 - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \sqrt{f_{\max} / f_{cr}}
> $$

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — Demonstration

> **Problem:** A stiffened compression element of a cold-formed lipped channel has the following properties: width $b = 100$ mm, thickness $t = 1.6$ mm, yield stress $f_y = 250$ MPa, maximum applied stress $f_{\max} = 0.6 f_y = 150$ MPa. Calculate the **effective width** per IS 801. Take $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $k = 4.0$ (stiffened element, simply supported).

**Step 1 — Compute the elastic local buckling stress**

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \times \pi^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12 \times (1 - 0.30^{2})} \left(\dfrac{1.6}{100}\right)^{2}
$$

Expanding the numerical sub-expressions:

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12 \times 0.91} \times (0.016)^{2}
$$

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{78.9568 \times 10^{5}}{10.92} \times 2.56 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
f_{cr} = 72,308.6 \times 2.56 \times 10^{-4} = 18.51 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the slenderness parameter**

$$
\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_{\max}}{f_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{150}{18.51}} = \sqrt{8.103} = 2.847
$$

**Step 3 — Reduction factor**

Since $\lambda = 2.847 > 0.673$, the element is slender:

$$
\rho = 1 - \dfrac{0.22}{2.847} = 1 - 0.0773 = 0.9227
$$

**Step 4 — Effective width**

$$
b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b = 0.9227 \times 100 = 92.27 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Reduction in capacity**

The **ineffective width** is:

$$
b_{i} = b - b_{eff} = 100 - 92.27 = 7.73 \text{ mm} \approx 7.7 \text{ mm}
$$

This $7.7$ mm of width (about $7.7\%$) is structurally assumed ineffective for design and is typically ignored in section property calculations or conservatively carried forward.

### 3.3 Tabulated Logic Matrix — Decision Flow in IS 801

| Step | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Classify element as **stiffened** or **unstiffened** (parallel to load) | Determines $k$ value |
| 2 | Compute **$f_{cr}$** using plate-buckling equation | MPa |
| 3 | Compute **$\lambda$** | dimensionless |
| 4 | If $\lambda \leq 0.673$ | $\rho = 1.0$ (full width effective) |
| 5 | If $\lambda > 0.673$ | $\rho = 1 - 0.22/\lambda$ |
| 6 | Compute **$b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$** | mm |
| 7 | Compute **$A_{eff} = \Sigma (b_{eff} \cdot t)$** | mm² |
| 8 | Use $A_{eff}$ in column / beam / beam-column design | per IS 801 Cl. 5–7 |

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Workflow

The following diagram maps the **iterative interaction** between the major analysis blocks in the **IS 801 design methodology**, illustrating the data flow from geometry input to final section verification.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[INPUT GEOMETRY AND MATERIALS] --> CLASSIFY[CLASSIFY SECTION TYPE]
    CLASSIFY --> IDENTIFY[IDENTIFY STIFFENED AND UNSTIFFENED ELEMENTS]
    IDENTIFY --> COMPUTEFCR[COMPUTE ELASTIC BUCKLING STRESS FCR]
    COMPUTEFCR --> COMPUTELAM[COMPUTE SLENDERNESS LAMBDA]
    COMPUTELAM --> DECIDE{LAMBDA LESS THAN 0.673}
    DECIDE -->|YES| FULLWIDTH[FULL WIDTH EFFECTIVE RHO EQUALS 1]
    DECIDE -->|NO| WINTER[APPLY WINTER FORMULA RHO EQUALS 1 MINUS 0.22 BY LAMBDA]
    FULLWIDTH --> AEFF[COMPUTE EFFECTIVE AREA AEFF]
    WINTER --> AEFF
    AEFF --> AEFFP[COMPUTE EFFECTIVE SECTION MODULUS SEFF]
    AEFFP --> CHECK[CHECK LOCAL BUCKLING WEB CRIPPLING DEFLECTION]
    CHECK --> OK{ALL CHECKS PASS}
    OK -->|YES| APPROVE[APPROVE SECTION DESIGN]
    OK -->|NO| REVISE[REVISE GEOMETRY OR THICKNESS]
    REVISE --> INPUT

```
