## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define the term "Light Gauge Steel Section". State any two applications of such sections in building construction.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** A **Light Gauge Steel Section (LGSS)** is a structural member formed from thin steel sheets, plates, strips, or flat bars, generally of thickness between **0.4 mm and 6.4 mm**, shaped by cold-forming processes (press braking, cold rolling, or bending) at ambient room temperature without the application of heat. The yield stress typically does not exceed **410 MPa** (per IS 801:1975), though modern international standards permit up to **550 MPa**.
>
> **Applications (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. **Purlins and girts** in industrial roof/wall systems.
> 2. **Load-bearing studs and joists** in modular LGSF housing.
> 3. **Roof and wall cladding / profile sheets.**
> 4. **Floor decking for composite slabs.**
> 5. **Cold-formed steel storage racks and mezzanine floors.**

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Distinguish between "stiffened" and "unstiffened" compression elements as per IS 801.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Stiffened Element | Unstiffened Element |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | A compression element supported along **both longitudinal edges** by a web, flange, or stiffener | A compression element supported along **only one longitudinal edge**; the other edge is free |
> | **Buckling coefficient $k$** | $k = 4.0$ | $k = 0.425$ (lower) |
> | **Examples** | Web of a channel, flange of a lipped channel | Outstand flange of a plain channel, leg of an angle |
> | **Capacity** | Higher effective width / less reduction | Lower effective width / more reduction |
>
> The fundamental difference is that **stiffened elements can develop post-buckling reserve strength** (per Winter), whereas **unstiffened elements buckle locally and fail** at or near the elastic critical stress with minimal post-buckling capacity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the **"Effective Width Concept"** used in the design of cold-formed steel members. State Winter's formula and define each term.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A stiffened compression element of a cold-formed section has the following properties: width $b = 120$ mm, thickness $t = 2.0$ mm, yield stress $f_y = 250$ MPa. The element is subjected to a uniform compressive stress $f_{\max} = 0.6 f_y$. Determine the **effective width** of the element as per IS 801. Take $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $k = 4.0$.
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q3(A)(a) — Effective Width Concept (7 marks)

The **Effective Width Concept** was introduced by **Theodore von Kármán (1932)** and later refined by **George Winter (1947)** at Cornell University.

In a thin compression element, the **average in-plane stress** cannot reach the yield stress because **local buckling intervenes at stresses well below yield**. The plate develops a non-uniform stress distribution — maximum at the supported edges and minimum at the centre. The element, however, continues to carry additional load beyond buckling (the **post-buckling reserve**), primarily through membrane action.

**Von Kármán's idealization (2 marks):**

Replace the actual non-uniform stress block with an equivalent uniform stress block of width $b_{eff}$ stressed to yield $f_y$, such that the **total compressive force is identical**:

$$
\int_{0}^{b} \sigma_{actual}(x) \, dx = f_y \cdot b_{eff}
$$

**Winter's empirical formula (3 marks):**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{b_{eff}}{b} = 1 - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda} \quad \text{when } \lambda \geq 0.673
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = **reduction factor** (ratio of effective to actual width)
- $b_{eff}$ = **effective width** (mm)
- $b$ = **actual (flat) width** of element (mm)
- $\lambda$ = **slenderness parameter** = $\sqrt{f_{\max} / f_{cr}}$
- $f_{\max}$ = **maximum edge stress** (MPa)
- $f_{cr}$ = **elastic local buckling stress** (MPa) = $\dfrac{k \pi^{2} E}{12(1-\nu^{2})} (t/b)^{2}$

**Physical interpretation (2 marks):**

For $\lambda \geq 0.673$ (slender), only a fraction $\rho$ of the actual width is structurally effective. For $\lambda < 0.673$ (compact/non-slender), the full width is effective. The reduction factor accounts for the loss of stiffness due to local buckling and is the central concept upon which **all IS 801 design provisions** for axial compression and flexure of cold-formed members are built.

#### Model Solution to Q3(A)(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $b = 120$ mm
- $t = 2.0$ mm
- $f_y = 250$ MPa
- $f_{\max} = 0.6 \times 250 = 150$ MPa
- $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $k = 4.0$

**Step 1 — Compute $f_{cr}$** (2 marks):

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \times \pi^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12 \times (1 - 0.30^{2})} \times \left(\dfrac{2.0}{120}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{10.92} \times (0.01667)^{2}
$$

$$
f_{cr} = 7,228,571.4 \times 2.778 \times 10^{-4} \approx 20.07 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\lambda$** (1 mark):

$$
\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{150}{20.07}} = \sqrt{7.473} = 2.734
$$

**Step 3 — Compute reduction factor $\rho$** (2 marks):

Since $\lambda = 2.734 > 0.673$, the element is slender:

$$
\rho = 1 - \dfrac{0.22}{2.734} = 1 - 0.0805 = 0.9195
$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective width** (2 marks):

$$
b_{eff} = 0.9195 \times 120 = 110.34 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The element has an effective width of **$b_{eff} \approx 110.34$ mm** out of the actual width of $120$ mm, i.e., about $9.66$ mm (or $8.05\%$) is structurally ineffective.

---

> **Q3(B) [Alternative Choice].** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the **three modes of buckling** in cold-formed steel members with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A **lipped channel section** is cold-formed from a steel sheet of thickness $t = 1.6$ mm, with web depth $h = 150$ mm, flange width $b = 60$ mm, and lip width $d = 20$ mm. Calculate the **elastic local buckling stress** of the **flange** (assumed as a stiffened element with one longitudinal edge supported by the web and the other by the lip). Take $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $k = 4.0$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q3(B)(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $b_{flange} = 60$ mm
- $t = 1.6$ mm
- $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $k = 4.0$

**Step 1 — Geometry ratio** (1 mark):

$$
\dfrac{b}{t} = \dfrac{60}{1.6} = 37.5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply plate buckling formula** (3 marks):

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \times \pi^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12 \times (1 - 0.30^{2})} \times \left(\dfrac{1.6}{60}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{7.8957 \times 10^{6}}{10.92} \times (0.02667)^{2}
$$

$$
f_{cr} = 722,857.14 \times 7.111 \times 10^{-4} \approx 51.40 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation** (3 marks):

The **elastic local buckling stress of the flange is only 51.40 MPa**, far below the yield stress of typical structural steel (250 MPa). This confirms the **fundamental premise of cold-formed steel design**: the element buckles locally long before yielding, justifying the use of the **Effective Width Method** to safely account for the post-buckling reserve.

For design, the ratio $b/t = 37.5$ is well within the **IS 801 limit of 60 for stiffened elements** in Grade 340 steel, so the section is geometrically admissible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\nu^2$ term:** Many students write $f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^{2} E}{12} (t/b)^2$, omitting the $(1 - \nu^2)$ denominator. This **overestimates $f_{cr}$ by ~9%** and triggers cascading errors. **Cost: ~1 mark.**
> 2. **Wrong $k$ value:** Using $k = 4.0$ for an unstiffened element (where $k = 0.425$) drastically changes the result. **Cost: ~1 mark.**
> 3. **Mixing up $\lambda$ and $\rho$:** Some students compute $\lambda$ but then forget to check the boundary $\lambda \geq 0.673$. **Cost: ~1 mark.**
> 4. **Units inconsistency:** Always use **MPa for stresses, mm for lengths, MPa for modulus** — not mixing kN/mm² and N/mm². **Cost: ~0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Not stating the assumption** that the maximum edge stress $f_{\max}$ is used (not the average stress) in Winter's formula. **Cost: ~0.5 mark.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Light Gauge Steel Structures: Introduction**
>
> ☐ **Definition:** Cold-formed members, thickness 0.4–6.4 mm, $f_y \leq 410$ MPa (IS 801).
> ☐ **Codes:** IS 801:1975 (India), AISI S100, Eurocode 3 Part 1-3, AS/NZS 4600.
> ☐ **Manufacturing:** Cold rolling, press braking, bending — at room temperature.
> ☐ **Section types:** C, Z, Sigma, Hat, Lipped Channel, Lipped Z, Corrugated/Decking.
> ☐ **Stiffened vs. Unstiffened:** Stiffened = supported on both edges ($k = 4.0$); Unstiffened = supported on one edge only ($k = 0.425$).
> ☐ **Three Buckling Modes:** Local (Mode 1), Distortional (Mode 2), Global/Euler (Mode 3).
> ☐ **Winter's Formula:** $b_{eff}/b = \rho = 1 - 0.22/\lambda$ for $\lambda \geq 0.673$; else $\rho = 1.0$.
> ☐ **Slenderness:** $\lambda = \sqrt{f_{\max}/f_{cr}}$; Transition at $\lambda = 0.673$.
> ☐ **Critical stress:** $f_{cr} = k\pi^2 E / [12(1-\nu^2)] \cdot (t/b)^2$.
> ☐ **Material:** $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $G = 0.769 \times 10^5$ MPa.
> ☐ **b/t limits (IS 801, Grade 340):** Stiffened ≤ 60; Unstiffened ≤ 30.
> ☐ **Strain hardening at corners:** $f_{yc}$ may exceed $f_y$ by 20–30% due to cold working.
> ☐ **Effective Width Method (EWM)** is the primary expected approach for KTU examinations.
> ☐ **Direct Strength Method (DSM)** is the modern alternative used in AISI S100-16 — supplementary knowledge.
> ☐ **Real-world use:** Purlins, girts, decking, modular LGSF housing, racks, mezzanines, transmission towers.
> ☐ **Why it differs from hot-rolled:** Slender sections, local buckling dominates, geometry-not-thickness governs strength.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Cross Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types of Cross Sections # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the design of **steel structures** as per **IS 800:2007** (the Indian Standard for General Construction in Steel), cross-sections are classified into **four behavioural categories** based on the **width-to-thickness (b/t) ratios** of their plate elements and the magnitude of **local buckling** relative to the yield strength of the material. The classification governs the **moment-rotation (M-θ) capacity** of the section and dictates the **design philosophy** (plastic, compact, semi-compact, or slender) that may be adopted. The four classes prescribed by **Clause 3.7 of IS 800:2007** are: | Class | Nomenclature | Behavioural Characteristic | |:---:|:---|:---| | **Class 1** | Plastic | Can develop **full plastic moment (M_p)** with sufficient **rotation capacity** to allow plastic hinge formation and moment redistribution. | | **Class 2** | Compact | Can develop **full plastic moment (M_p)** but has **limited rotation capacity** before local buckling. | | **Class 3** | Semi-Compact | Can develop **yield moment (M_y)** only; extreme fibres reach yield before local buckling. | | **Class 4** | Slender | **Local buckling occurs before the yield stress** is reached; the effective width concept must be applied. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cross-section classification is a **prerequisite topic** that influences every subsequent design calculation — from flexural member design (Module 4 onwards) to column design and plate girder design. Always classify the section **first** before selecting the design approach. ## 1.2 The Governing Parameter: ε (Epsilon) The single most important parameter in classification is: $$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{f_y}}$$ where **f_y** is the **yield strength of steel in MPa**. This non-dimensional factor (a **square root ratio**) scales all the limiting b/t ratios and reflects that **higher-strength steel is more susceptible to local buckling** for a given geometry, because its yield plateau occurs at a higher stress. | **f_y (MPa)** | **250** | **300** | **350** | **410** | **450** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **ε** | **1.000** | **0.913** | **0.845** | **0.781** | **0.745** | > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Outstanding Element:** A plate element that is **connected along only one longitudinal edge** (e.g., the half-flange of an I-section sticking out from the web). > **Definition — Internal Element:** A plate element that is **supported along both longitudinal edges** (e.g., the web of an I-section or a side of a hollow box section). ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Soda Can vs. Thick Pipe" Principle Imagine two cylinders under axial compression: - A **thin soda can** crumples sideways almost the instant you press on it — long before the aluminium reaches its yield stress. The walls are too slender to resist the inward buckling. - A **heavy-duty cast iron pipe** of the same diameter resists the load until the steel actually yields; only then does it deform. The soda can is a **Class 4 (Slender)** section; the thick pipe behaves as **Class 1 (Plastic)**. The **wall thickness relative to the diameter (the b/t ratio)** determines which behaviour governs — exactly as the **b/t ratio of plate elements** decides the class of a steel cross-section in IS 800. In real buildings: - **Universal beams (UB / ISMB)** are typically rolled with **compact proportions** — they're Class 1 or Class 2 under normal flexure. - **Long-span plate girders** with deep, thin webs are often **Class 3 or Class 4**, requiring careful stiffener design and effective-width treatment. > [!TIP] > **Real-world engineering rule of thumb:** Most **standard rolled I-sections** (ISMB 200 to ISMB 600) made of **Fe 410 steel** (f_y = 250 MPa) are **Class 1 (Plastic)** when used in pure bending. This is why plastic design is permitted for routine building frames in India. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Theory — Why Does Local Buckling Reduce Capacity? When a plate element is compressed, it does not fail solely by yielding — **thin plates undergo a sudden out-of-plane deflection (buckling)** governed by the **elastic plate-buckling stress**: $$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k_{\sigma} \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$ **Where:** - $k_{\sigma}$ = **buckling coefficient** (depends on edge support & stress distribution; ≈ 4.0 for an internal element in pure compression, ≈ 0.425 for an outstanding element) - $E$ = **Young's Modulus** = **2.0 × 10⁵ MPa** for steel - $\nu$ = **Poisson's ratio** = **0.30** for steel - $t$ = **plate thickness**, $b$ = **plate width** - The ratio $\sqrt{k_{\sigma}}$ explains why **internal elements are far more efficient** than outstanding elements at the same b/t. The **b/t limits in IS 800:2007 Table 2 are derived by equating $\sigma_{cr}$ to fractions of $f_y$** for each class boundary: - **Class 1 / Class 2 boundary:** $\sigma_{cr} = f_y$ (full yield before buckling) - **Class 3 boundary:** $\sigma_{cr}$ corresponds to the extreme fibre reaching yield in bending - **Class 4 boundary:** $\sigma_{cr} < f_y$ — the effective width approach (Clause 7.4.2) is required. > [!IMPORTANT] > The parameter **ε = √(250/f_y)** emerges naturally from substituting the **plate-buckling formula** with **E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa** and **ν = 0.3**. It normalises the limits to a reference steel of f_y = 250 MPa. ## 2.2 The Hierarchy of Element Behaviour | Element Type | Example | Buckling Coefficient $k_{\sigma}$ | Most Restrictive b/t | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | **Internal (both edges supported)** | Web of I-section; side of box | **≈ 4.0 (pure compression)** | **Most lenient limits** | | **Internal (bending stress)** | Web of I-section in flexure | **≈ 23.9 (pure bending)** | **Even more lenient** | | **Outstanding (one edge free)** | Half-flange of I-section | **≈ 0.425 (free edge)** | **Strictest limits** | This is **why flange b/t ratios are far more critical** than web b/t ratios in I-section classification. ## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula & Limiting Ratios Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All limits below are from **IS 800:2007 Table 2 (Clause 3.7.2 & 3.7.3)**. Multiply the given constant by **ε** to obtain the actual limit for any $f_y$. For **rolled I-sections** the flange outstand $b$ is taken as $b_f / 2$ (half the flange width). For **welded I-sections** the outstand is $b_f / 2$ measured from the **web-to-flange weld**. ### Table 2A — I-Section Flange (Outstanding Element of Compression Flange) | Section Type | Class 1 | Class 2 | Class 3 | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Rolled Section** (flange thickness $t_f$) | $\frac{b}{t_f} \leq 9.4\varepsilon$ | $\frac{b}{t_f} \leq 10.5\varepsilon$ | $\frac{b}{t_f} \leq 15.7\varepsilon$ | | **Welded Section** | $\frac{b}{t_f} \leq 8.4\varepsilon$ | $\frac{b}{t_f} \leq 9.4\varepsilon$ | $\frac{b}{t_f} \leq 13.6\varepsilon$ | ### Table 2B — I-Section Web (Internal Element, Bending or Axial Compression) | Loading Type | Class 1 | Class 2 | Class 3 | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Pure Bending** (web $d/t_w$) | $\frac{d}{t_w} \leq 84\varepsilon$ | $\frac{d}{t_w} \leq 105\varepsilon$ | $\frac{d}{t_w} \leq 126\varepsilon$ | | **Pure Axial Compression** | $\frac{d}{t_w} \leq 40\varepsilon$ | $\frac{d}{t_w} \leq 50\varepsilon$ | $\frac{d}{t_w} \leq 42\varepsilon^2$ | | **Combined Bending + Axial** | **Linear interpolation** between pure bending and pure compression limits based on axial stress ratio $r_1$ | — | — | ### Table 2C — Rectangular Hollow Section (Internal Element b/t) | Loading | Class 1 | Class 2 | Class 3 | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Flexure / Compression** | $\frac{b}{t} \leq 29.3\varepsilon$ | $\frac{b}{t} \leq 35.3\varepsilon$ | $\frac{b}{t} \leq 42\varepsilon$ | ### Table 2D — Circular Hollow Section (Diameter d, Thickness t) | Class 1 | Class 2 | Class 3 | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | $\frac{d}{t} \leq 40\varepsilon^2$ | $\frac{d}{t} \leq 52\varepsilon^2$ | $\frac{d}{t} \leq 80\varepsilon^2$ | ### Table 2E — Single Angle (Outstanding Element) and Channels | Section | Class 3 (only) | |:---|:---:| | **Single Angle, both edges connected to gusset** | $\frac{(b+h)}{2t} \leq 11.5\varepsilon$ | | **Single Angle, one edge free** | $\frac{b}{t} \leq 15.7\varepsilon$ | | **Channel flange (outstand)** | $\frac{b}{t_f} \leq 15.7\varepsilon$ | ## 2.4 The Overall Section Classification Rule > [!IMPORTANT] > **The section class = the WORST class of ALL its individual plate elements.** > - If the flange is Class 1 and the web is Class 2, **the section is Class 2**. > - A single Class 4 element makes the **entire section Class 4**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why Classification Matters | |:---|:---| | **Plastic Design of Portal Frames** | Requires **Class 1** sections for full plastic hinge rotation; dictates section selection. | | **Seismic Design** | Class 1 / Class 2 sections are required in **moment-resisting frames** for energy dissipation. | | **Plate Girder Webs** | Deep webs are often **Class 3 or 4**; governs web stiffener spacing (Clause 8) and shear buckling. | | **Cold-Formed Steel** | Almost always **Class 4**; effective width method (Clause 7.4.2) **must be used** — cross-section area is reduced to an "effective" area. | | **Composite Construction** | Steel beam in negative moment regions (over supports) is typically in **Class 1 or 2** for plastic redistribution. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Parameter ε Starting from the **classical elastic plate-buckling stress** (Bryan, 1891): $$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k_{\sigma}\,\pi^2\,E}{12\,(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$ For an **internal element in pure compression** (e.g., web of axially-loaded column), $k_{\sigma} = 4.0$. Substituting the standard values for **structural steel**: $$E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}\,\text{MPa}, \quad \nu = 0.3$$ $$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \times \pi^2 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12 \times (1 - 0.3^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$ $$= \frac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12 \times 0.91} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$ $$= \frac{7.8957 \times 10^{6}}{10.92} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 \approx 723{,}000 \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 \text{ MPa}$$ The **Class 3 boundary** for the web under pure compression is set at $\sigma_{cr} = f_y$: $$f_y = 723{,}000 \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{b}{t}\right)_{\text{Class 3}} = \sqrt{\frac{723{,}000}{f_y}} = 850.3 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{f_y}}$$ Writing this in the **standard form** by dividing both numerator and denominator by 250: $$\left(\frac{b}{t}\right)_{\text{Class 3}} = 850.3 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{250}} \times \sqrt{\frac{250}{f_y}} = 850.3 \times 0.0632 \times \varepsilon \approx 53.7\,\varepsilon$$ IS 800:2007 simplifies this to **$42\varepsilon$** (Class 3, axial compression) using more refined stress-distribution assumptions and empirical calibration against test data. The same procedure — applied with different $k_{\sigma}$ values for outstanding elements, bending stress distributions, and other boundary conditions — yields **all the limits** in Table 2 of IS 800:2007. The factor **ε** is therefore a **mathematical normalisation constant**, not a fitted parameter. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Classification of a Welded I-Section **Given:** Welded I-section, **overall depth D = 600 mm**, **flange width b_f = 250 mm**, **flange thickness t_f = 16 mm**, **web thickness t_w = 10 mm**, **steel grade E 350 (f_y = 350 MPa)**. **Loading: Pure bending** about the major axis. **Step 1 — Calculate ε:** $$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{350}} = \sqrt{0.7143} = 0.845$$ **Step 2 — Identify element types:** - **Flange (welded)**: outstanding element → use Table 2A, **welded** row. - **Web**: internal element under bending → use Table 2B, **pure bending** row. **Step 3 — Classify the flange:** Outstand of welded flange: $b = (b_f - t_w)/2 = (250 - 10)/2 = 120$ mm. $$\left(\frac{b}{t_f}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{120}{16} = 7.50$$ **Limiting ratios** (multiply by ε = 0.845): $$\text{Class 1 limit} = 8.4 \times 0.845 = 7.10$$ $$\text{Class 2 limit} = 9.4 \times 0.845 = 7.94$$ $$\text{Class 3 limit} = 13.6 \times 0.845 = 11.49$$ **Comparison:** | Limit | Value | Actual $b/t_f$ | Verdict | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Class 1 | **7.10** | 7.50 | **FAILS** (7.50 > 7.10) | | Class 2 | **7.94** | 7.50 | **PASSES** (7.50 < 7.94) | | Class 3 | **11.49** | 7.50 | **PASSES** | **Flange classification: Class 2 (Compact).** **Step 4 — Classify the web:** Clear depth of web: $d = D - 2t_f = 600 - 2(16) = 568$ mm. $$\left(\frac{d}{t_w}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{568}{10} = 56.80$$ **Limiting ratios** (pure bending): $$\text{Class 1 limit} = 84 \times 0.845 = 71.0$$ $$\text{Class 2 limit} = 105 \times 0.845 = 88.7$$ $$\text{Class 3 limit} = 126 \times 0.845 = 106.5$$ **Comparison:** | Limit | Value | Actual $d/t_w$ | Verdict | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Class 1 | **71.0** | 56.80 | **PASSES** | | Class 2 | **88.7** | 56.80 | **PASSES** | | Class 3 | **106.5** | 56.80 | **PASSES** | **Web classification: Class 1 (Plastic).** **Step 5 — Overall section classification:** The **worst** of the two elements governs: $$\text{Flange: Class 2} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Web: Class 1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\text{Section is Class 2 (Compact)}}$$ **Design implication:** The section can develop the **full plastic moment $M_{pd}$** as per **Clause 8.2.1.2 of IS 800:2007**, but plastic hinge rotation is limited (no plastic design permissible). ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Quick Reference) | Step | Action | Output | |:---:|:---|:---| | **1** | Identify material grade → Compute $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/f_y}$ | Non-dimensional factor | | **2** | List all compression plate elements → Tag as **internal** or **outstanding** | Element inventory | | **3** | For each element, measure the relevant **b and t** | Geometric data | | **4** | Compute actual $b/t$ ratio | Single number | | **5** | Multiply IS 800 limit constants by $\varepsilon$ → Obtain Class 1, 2, 3 limits | Numerical limits | | **6** | Compare actual $b/t$ against limits → **Highest class satisfied** | Element class | | **7** | Take **worst** class across all elements | **Section class** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Cross-Section Classification Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Given cross-section and f_y]) --> stepA1[Step 1: Compute epsilon = sqrt 250 / f_y] stepA1 --> stepA2[Step 2: Identify all compression elements in the section] stepA2 --> stepA3{Is element internal
or outstanding?} stepA3 --> branchInt1[Internal Element] stepA3 --> branchOut1[Outstanding Element] branchInt1 --> stepA4a[Note: web of I, side of box, web of channel] branchOut1 --> stepA4b[Note: half flange of I, flange of channel, leg of single angle] stepA4a --> stepA5[Step 3: Measure b and t for the element] stepA4b --> stepA5 stepA5 --> stepA6[Step 4: Compute actual b divided by t] stepA6 --> stepA7[Step 5: Look up Class 1, 2, 3 limits from IS 800 Table 2 and multiply by epsilon] stepA7 --> stepA8{Compare actual b/t with limits} stepA8 --> condA1[b/t less than or equal to 9.4 epsilon or 84 epsilon etc]:::classnode stepA8 --> condA2[b/t greater than 9.4 epsilon but less than 10.5 epsilon]:::classnode stepA8 --> condA3[b/t greater than 10.5 epsilon but less than 15.7 epsilon]:::classnode stepA8 --> condA4[b/t greater than 15.7 epsilon]:::classnode condA1 --> labelA1[CLASS 1 Plastic] condA2 --> labelA2[CLASS 2 Compact] condA3 --> labelA3[CLASS 3 Semi-Compact] condA4 --> labelA4[CLASS 4 Slender] labelA1 --> stepA9[Step 6: Repeat for all elements] labelA2 --> stepA9 labelA3 --> stepA9 labelA4 --> stepA9 stepA9 --> stepA10[Step 7: Section class equals WORST element class] stepA10 --> outputA[OUTPUT: Section Class and Design Philosophy]:::outputnode classDef classnode fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef outputnode fill:#A8E6A3,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Behavioural Comparison of the Four Classes ```mermaid graph LR subgraph BLOCK_CLASS1[CLASS 1 PLASTIC] nodeC1A[Full Plastic Moment Mp] --> nodeC1B[Rotation Capacity theta greater than 6 theta_y] --> nodeC1C[Plastic Design Allowed] end subgraph BLOCK_CLASS2[CLASS 2 COMPACT] nodeC2A[Full Plastic Moment Mp] --> nodeC2B[Rotation Less than 6 theta_y] --> nodeC2C[Elastic Design with Mp] end subgraph BLOCK_CLASS3[CLASS 3 SEMI COMPACT] nodeC3A[Yield Moment My only] --> nodeC3B[No Plastic Hinge] --> nodeC3C[Elastic Design with My] end subgraph BLOCK_CLASS4[CLASS 4 SLENDER] nodeC4A[Buckling before Yield] --> nodeC4B[Effective Width Used] --> nodeC4C[Reduced Effective Area] end BLOCK_CLASS1 -.->|b/t increases| BLOCK_CLASS2 BLOCK_CLASS2 -.->|b/t increases| BLOCK_CLASS3 BLOCK_CLASS3 -.->|b/t increases| BLOCK_CLASS4 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Element Type Identification ``` +-----------------------+ +-----------------------+ | FLANGE (top) | | FLANGE (top) | +-----+------------+-----+ +-----+------------+-----+ | t_f | | t_f | | (outstand | | (outstand | | element) | | element) | +-----+-----------+ +---+-----------+---+ | | | t_w | | WEB | Internal Element | (internal | | d (depth) | (both edges | element) | | | supported) | | +------------------+ +---------------+ I-SECTION FLANGE+WEB BOX / HSS (All Internal) >>> Classification Rule: <<< >>> Section Class = WORST of all elements <<< ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define cross-section classification as per IS 800:2007. List the four classes with their key characteristics.** **Model Answer:** Cross-section classification is a systematic procedure in IS 800:2007 (Clause 3.7) that categorises rolled and welded steel sections into **four classes** based on the **width-to-thickness (b/t) ratios** of their plate elements. The classification determines the **moment-rotation capacity** of the section and dictates the design approach (plastic vs. elastic). The four classes are: - **Class 1 (Plastic):** Can develop full plastic moment $M_p$ with rotation capacity $\theta \geq 6\theta_y$. Permits plastic design. - **Class 2 (Compact):** Can develop $M_p$ but with limited rotation capacity; $M_p$ is the design strength. - **Class 3 (Semi-Compact):** Only the yield moment $M_y$ can be developed; extreme fibres yield before local buckling. - **Class 4 (Slender):** Local buckling occurs before yielding; the **effective width method** (Clause 7.4.2) is mandatory. The governing non-dimensional parameter is $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/f_y}$. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Always state the **b/t ratio basis** of classification and mention the **ε parameter** to score full marks. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between an "outstanding element" and an "internal element" with one example each. Why is the b/t ratio of an outstanding element more critical?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Outstanding Element | Internal Element | |:---|:---|:---| | **Edge Support** | Connected along **one longitudinal edge**; other edge is **free** | Connected along **both longitudinal edges** | | **Buckling Coefficient $k_{\sigma}$** | **≈ 0.425** (lower — buckles more easily) | **≈ 4.0** (higher — buckles less easily) | | **b/t Limit** | Stricter (e.g., $9.4\varepsilon$ for flange Class 1) | More lenient (e.g., $84\varepsilon$ for web Class 1 in bending) | | **Example** | Half-flange of an I-section; flange of a channel; leg of an unstiffened angle | Web of an I-section; sides of a box section; flanges of an H-section | The **outstanding element is more critical** because the free edge has no transverse support; it can deflect outward easily under compression, triggering local buckling at **much lower stresses** than an internal element. This is reflected in the lower $k_{\sigma}$ value, which makes the $\sigma_{cr}$ formula yield a much smaller critical stress for the same b/t ratio. Consequently, IS 800 prescribes **stricter b/t limits** for outstanding elements (e.g., 9.4ε vs. 84ε — a factor of nearly **9 times**). --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** A **welded I-section** has the following properties: overall depth $D = 500$ mm, flange width $b_f = 200$ mm, flange thickness $t_f = 14$ mm, web thickness $t_w = 10$ mm. The steel grade is **E 300 (f_y = 300 MPa)**. Classify the section under: - **(i) Pure bending about the major axis** - **(ii) Pure axial compression** State the design implications in each case. #### **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute ε:** $$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{300}} = \sqrt{0.8333} = 0.9129$$ **Step 2 — Geometrical data:** - Flange outstand (welded): $b = (b_f - t_w)/2 = (200 - 10)/2 = 95$ mm - Clear web depth: $d = D - 2t_f = 500 - 2(14) = 472$ mm **Step 3 — Flange classification (outstanding element, welded):** $$\left(\frac{b}{t_f}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{95}{14} = 6.786$$ | Class | Limit Formula | Limit Value | Verdict | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $8.4\varepsilon$ | $8.4 \times 0.9129 = 7.668$ | 6.786 < 7.668 → **PASSES** | | 2 | $9.4\varepsilon$ | $9.4 \times 0.9129 = 8.581$ | PASSES | | 3 | $13.6\varepsilon$ | $13.6 \times 0.9129 = 12.42$ | PASSES | **Flange: Class 1 (Plastic)** ✓ **Step 4 — (i) Web classification under pure bending (internal element):** $$\left(\frac{d}{t_w}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{472}{10} = 47.2$$ | Class | Limit Formula | Limit Value | Verdict | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $84\varepsilon$ | $84 \times 0.9129 = 76.68$ | 47.2 < 76.68 → **PASSES** | | 2 | $105\varepsilon$ | $95.85$ | PASSES | | 3 | $126\varepsilon$ | $115.0$ | PASSES | **Web (bending): Class 1** ✓ **Section class under pure bending:** $$\text{Flange: Class 1} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Web: Class 1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\text{Section: Class 1 (Plastic)}}$$ **Design implication (pure bending):** Plastic design is permissible. The **full plastic moment** $M_{pd} = \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_{yd}$ governs the design. The section can undergo plastic hinge rotation suitable for **plastic analysis** (Clause 8 of IS 800:2007). --- **Step 5 — (ii) Web classification under pure axial compression (internal element):** $$\left(\frac{d}{t_w}\right)_{\text{actual}} = 47.2 \text{ (unchanged)}$$ | Class | Limit Formula | Limit Value | Verdict | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $40\varepsilon$ | $40 \times 0.9129 = 36.52$ | 47.2 > 36.52 → **FAILS** | | 2 | $50\varepsilon$ | $45.64$ | 47.2 > 45.64 → **FAILS** | | 3 | $42\varepsilon^2$ | $42 \times 0.9129^2 = 35.00$ | 47.2 > 35.00 → **FAILS** | **Web (axial compression): Class 4 (Slender)** ✗ **Section class under pure axial compression:** $$\text{Flange: Class 1} \quad \text{Web: Class 4} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\text{Section: Class 4 (Slender)}}$$ **Design implication (pure axial compression):** The section is **Slender**; the **effective width method** (Clause 7.4.2 of IS 800:2007) must be applied. The web's effective area is reduced ($A_{eff} < A_g$), and column strength is governed by **Clause 7.1** with reduction factors. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Using the **bending limit** ($84\varepsilon$) for the web when the loading is **pure compression** — this gives an *incorrect* Class 1 verdict. The bending and compression limits are **different**. Always re-classify the section when the **loading type changes**. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Explain the effective width concept for Class 4 sections. How is it used in design as per IS 800:2007?** **Model Answer:** In **Class 4 (slender)** sections, local buckling occurs at stresses **below the yield stress** $f_y$. The post-buckling behaviour, however, is not catastrophic: the **central portion of the plate sheds load** to the **edge strips**, which continue to carry stress up to $f_y$. The plate is therefore assumed to behave as if it has a **reduced effective width** $b_{eff}$ carrying the full yield stress, while the **ineffective middle portion** is ignored. **IS 800:2007 Clause 7.4.2.1** gives the effective width for an **internal element in pure compression**: $$b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$$ where the **reduction factor** $\rho$ is: $$\rho = \frac{\bar{\lambda}_p - 0.22}{\bar{\lambda}_p^2} \quad \text{with} \quad \bar{\lambda}_p = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr}}}$$ For an **outstanding element** the formula is: $$b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b \quad \text{with} \quad \rho = \frac{\bar{\lambda}_p - 0.188}{\bar{\lambda}_p^2}$$ **Where:** - $\bar{\lambda}_p$ = **plate slenderness ratio** (non-dimensional) - $\sigma_{cr}$ = **elastic local buckling stress** from the plate-buckling equation - $\rho$ = **reduction factor** (always $\leq 1.0$) - $b_{eff}$ is then distributed: typically **$0.4 b_{eff}$ on each edge** with the central portion **disregarded** for internal elements. **Design use:** The **effective cross-sectional area** $A_{eff} = \sum b_{eff} \cdot t$ is used in all subsequent calculations — axial capacity $N_{Rd} = A_{eff} \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ and moment capacity $M_{Rd} = f_y \cdot Z_{eff} / \gamma_{m0}$ — with $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ as per IS 800:2007 Table 5. This approach is essential for **cold-formed steel sections**, **plate girder webs**, and **slender welded I-sections** under axial load. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** A standard **hot-rolled ISMB 450** section has the following properties: - Depth $D = 450$ mm, flange width $b_f = 150$ mm - Flange thickness $t_f = 17.4$ mm, web thickness $t_w = 9.4$ mm - Yield strength $f_y = 250$ MPa Classify the section for **(i) pure flexure** and **(ii) pure axial compression**. Comment on the **moment capacity** in each case. #### **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute ε (f_y = 250 MPa):** $$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{250}} = 1.000$$ **Step 2 — Geometrical data (rolled section, so outstand is $b_f/2$):** - Flange outstand: $b = b_f/2 = 150/2 = 75$ mm - Clear web depth: $d = D - 2t_f = 450 - 2(17.4) = 415.2$ mm **Step 3 — Flange classification (rolled):** $$\left(\frac{b}{t_f}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{75}{17.4} = 4.31$$ | Class | Limit Formula | Limit Value | Verdict | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $9.4\varepsilon$ | $9.4$ | 4.31 < 9.4 → **PASSES** | | 2 | $10.5\varepsilon$ | $10.5$ | PASSES | | 3 | $15.7\varepsilon$ | $15.7$ | PASSES | **Flange: Class 1 (Plastic)** ✓ **Step 4 — (i) Web under pure flexure (internal element, bending):** $$\left(\frac{d}{t_w}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{415.2}{9.4} = 44.17$$ | Class | Limit Formula | Limit Value | Verdict | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $84\varepsilon$ | $84$ | 44.17 < 84 → **PASSES** | | 2 | $105\varepsilon$ | $105$ | PASSES | | 3 | $126\varepsilon$ | $126$ | PASSES | **Web (bending): Class 1** ✓ **Section class under pure flexure: Class 1 (Plastic).** The design moment is the **full plastic moment**: $$M_{pd} = \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$ For ISMB 450, $Z_p \approx 935.7$ cm³ (from steel tables); with $f_y = 250$ MPa and $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$: $$M_{pd} = 1.0 \times 935.7 \times 10^3 \times \frac{250}{1.10} = 212.66 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 5 — (ii) Web under pure axial compression (internal element):** $$\left(\frac{d}{t_w}\right)_{\text{actual}} = 44.17 \text{ (unchanged)}$$ | Class | Limit Formula | Limit Value | Verdict | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $40\varepsilon$ | $40$ | 44.17 > 40 → **FAILS** | | 2 | $50\varepsilon$ | $50$ | 44.17 < 50 → **PASSES** | | 3 | $42\varepsilon^2$ | $42$ | 44.17 > 42 → **FAILS** | **Web (axial compression): Class 2 (Compact).** **Section class under pure axial compression:** $\text{Flange: Class 1} \quad \text{Web: Class 2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\text{Section: Class 2 (Compact)}}$ **Design implication:** As the section is **Class 2** (not Class 4), the full gross area is used. The column capacity is computed using Clause 7.1 of IS 800: $$N_{Rd} = A_g \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$$ (with buckling curve reduction if $L/r$ is high). **Comment on moment capacity change:** The moment capacity **does not change** with class alone — the **full plastic moment $M_p$** can still be developed (since Class 2 allows $M_p$). However, the **section cannot be used in a fully plastic mechanism** because of its limited rotation capacity. Hence, plastic analysis (moment redistribution) is restricted, but **plastic section design** is still permitted. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"Class 2 = Elastic design"** — this is WRONG. Class 2 still allows the **full plastic moment $M_p$** as the design strength; only the **rotation capacity is limited**. Plastic analysis (mechanism method) is the restriction, not the moment value. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A circular hollow section (CHS) has outer diameter D = 273 mm and thickness t = 6.3 mm, with f_y = 355 MPa. Classify the section for flexure and compression. State the design moment expression.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute ε:** $$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{355}} = 0.839$$ **Step 2 — d/t ratio:** $$\frac{d}{t} = \frac{273}{6.3} = 43.33$$ **Step 3 — Class limits for CHS (Table 2D):** | Class | Limit Formula | Limit Value | Verdict | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $40\varepsilon^2$ | $40 \times (0.839)^2 = 28.16$ | 43.33 > 28.16 → **FAILS** | | 2 | $52\varepsilon^2$ | $52 \times 0.704 = 36.62$ | 43.33 > 36.62 → **FAILS** | | 3 | $80\varepsilon^2$ | $80 \times 0.704 = 56.32$ | 43.33 < 56.32 → **PASSES** | **Section class: Class 3 (Semi-Compact) for BOTH flexure and compression.** **Design implication:** - For **flexure**: Design moment is the **yield moment** $M_{yd} = Z_e \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ (NOT the plastic moment). - For **compression**: Full gross area is used; design strength is **$f_y / \gamma_{m0}$** for short columns (subject to buckling reduction per Clause 7.1). > [!TIP] > **Key learning:** Thin circular tubes are often **Class 3** in flexure because of the higher d/t² sensitivity. Designers frequently thicken the wall or use a higher-grade steel (E 410 or E 450) to push the section into Class 1 or 2 for **seismic applications**. --- ## 🧠 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Mistakes KTU Students Make in Cross-Section Classification:** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by ε** — comparing raw actual b/t against the constant 9.4 instead of $9.4\varepsilon$. This is the **#1 mark killer** in this topic. > 2. **Using welded limits on a rolled section** (or vice versa) — welded flanges have **stricter** limits (8.4ε) than rolled (9.4ε). > 3. **Wrong outstand measurement** — for welded sections, the outstand is measured from the **weld toe**, not the centre of the web. > 4. **Using bending limits for compression** — the **web under axial load** has much tighter limits ($40\varepsilon$ vs. $84\varepsilon$). > 5. **Forgetting to take the worst-case element** — the section class is the **WORST** of all elements, not the average or the best. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) - 🔑 **Classification governs design philosophy** — Plastic (Class 1), Compact (Class 2), Semi-Compact (Class 3), Slender (Class 4). - 🔑 **Parameter ε = √(250/f_y)** scales all b/t limits; **higher f_y → smaller ε → stricter limits**. - 🔑 **Outstanding elements** (e.g., flange) have **stricter** b/t limits than **internal elements** (e.g., web) due to lower buckling coefficient. - 🔑 **Welded I-section flanges** are governed by **stricter limits** (8.4ε Class 1) than **rolled sections** (9.4ε Class 1). - 🔑 **Web under axial compression** has tighter limits (40ε, 50ε, 42ε²) than **web under pure bending** (84ε, 105ε, 126ε). - 🔑 **Overall section class = WORST individual element class.** - 🔑 **Class 4 sections** require the **effective width method** (Clause 7.4.2 of IS 800:2007) — actual area is reduced to an effective area. - 🔑 **For combined bending + axial load**, the web classification uses **linear interpolation** based on the axial stress ratio $r_1 = N_d / N_{d,\text{web}}$. - 🔑 **Most standard rolled ISMB/ISHB sections in Fe 410** are **Class 1** in pure flexure — making plastic design widely applicable in routine Indian practice. - 🔑 **Memorise the four limit constants for flange (rolled): 9.4, 10.5, 15.7** and **welded: 8.4, 9.4, 13.6** — multiply by ε to obtain actual limits.

topics.title: Materials - Local and Post Buckling of Thin Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Materials - Local and Post Buckling of Thin Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Local Buckling of Thin Elements** is a mode of instability that occurs in individual *plate-type components* (webs, flanges, lips, lips of lips) of a steel cross-section when the width-to-thickness ratio (b/t) is sufficiently large. Unlike *overall* or *Euler* buckling of the entire member axis, local buckling involves out-of-plane transverse deflection of plate elements *between* adjacent stiffeners or supports, while the line junctions between the plates (corners of the section) remain essentially straight.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme (PECET864 Module 4), the relevant steel families in which this phenomenon governs design are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECET864 M4):**
> - **Light-gauge / Cold-formed steel sections** (IS 801 : 1975 reaffirmed 2010; AISI S100)
> - **Plate girders with slender webs** (IS 800 : 2007, Cl. 8 — Webs under flexure, shear, and patch loading)
> - **Welded built-up sections with thin outstanding flanges**

**Post-buckling strength** is the *additional load-carrying capacity* of a slender plate element *beyond* its theoretical elastic local buckling load. Because of the development of *transverse membrane (tensile) stresses* at the plate edges after buckling, the load is redistributed to the stiffer, edge-supported regions. The plate does not fail at the bifurcation load; the collapse load can be several times higher.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Post-it Note on the Desk"**
> 
> Imagine pressing vertically down on the long edge of a *Post-it note* lying flat on a smooth desk. Theoretically, the note should "pop" into a wavy shape the moment a critical load is reached — this is the **buckling load**. But in reality, the note still holds your finger for a long time afterwards because the *two long edges stay flat* (they're supported by the desk), and only the *middle of the note* bulges. The central bulge no longer carries compression; the load is transferred to the two flat "edge strips" along the supported boundaries. Those two flat strips are the **effective width** of the plate. Even after the wavy shape forms, the post-it is still a usable "beam" — that extra strength is **post-buckling reserve**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Stiffened element** — a plate element supported along **two parallel edges** parallel to the direction of compressive stress (e.g., the web of an I-section in bending).
- **Unstiffened element** — a plate element supported along **only one edge** parallel to the compressive stress (e.g., the outstanding half of an I-flange).
- **Width-to-thickness ratio (b/t)** — the governing geometric parameter. Larger b/t → more slender → lower local buckling stress.
- **Plate buckling coefficient (k)** — a non-dimensional factor (Bryan, 1891) that depends on edge support and stress distribution.
- **Effective width (b_eff)** — the reduced, *equivalent uniform-stress* width of a buckled plate that gives the same axial capacity as the actual non-uniformly stressed plate.
- **Slenderness parameter (λ)** — λ = √(f_y / f_cr), the ratio of squash stress to elastic local buckling stress.

## 1.4 Standard Constants (to be memorised)

| Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| E | Young's Modulus of Steel | **2.0 × 10⁵** | N/mm² (MPa) |
| ν | Poisson's Ratio | **0.3** | — |
| 1 − ν² | Plane-strain factor | **0.91** | — |
| f_y | Yield Stress (typical grade E 250) | **250** | N/mm² |
| π² | Architectural constant | **9.8696** | — |
| √(E / (1 − ν²)) | Plate stiffness modulus | **4694.6** | N/mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> The plane-strain modulus of elasticity used in plate buckling theory is **E / (1 − ν²)** ≈ **2.0 × 10⁵ / 0.91 ≈ 2.197 × 10⁵ N/mm²**. Always use this value, not E alone, in plate-buckling formulas.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Local Buckling in a Plate Element

Consider a flat rectangular plate, dimensions **b × t × L** (width × thickness × length between stiffeners), simply supported on all four edges, loaded by a uniform in-plane compressive stress **f** along the short edges.

The sequence of events is:

1. **Pre-buckling phase (f < f_cr):** The plate remains perfectly flat. Compressive stress is uniform.
2. **Bifurcation (f = f_cr):** At the critical value, a *small transverse deflection* w(x,y) appears. The plate is in neutral equilibrium between flat and deflected states.
3. **Post-buckling phase (f > f_cr):** The deflection grows. The plate develops **transverse membrane tensile stresses** (sometimes called *stress redistribution* or *membrane action*). The *maximum* stress at the supported edges continues to rise, while the *central region* unloads.
4. **Collapse:** When the *edge stress* reaches the yield stress f_y, the section cannot carry further load.

The classical **Bryan (1891) result** for a long, simply supported plate in uniform compression gives the elastic critical stress as:

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{k \, \pi^{2} \, E}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})} \left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^{2}
$$

where **k = 4.0** for a long simply supported plate under uniform compression. For other boundary conditions, **k** changes (see Section 2.3 below).

## 2.2 Why the Plate Does *Not* Fail at f_cr

In Euler column buckling, the member loses all stiffness at the bifurcation load (it has no post-buckling reserve). **A plate is different**:

- After buckling, the *transverse deflections* set up membrane stretching, which is a *tensile* stress in the transverse direction.
- This transverse tension *stiffens* the plate against further deflection.
- The plate can therefore carry additional in-plane load, with the load *redistributing* toward the stiffer supported edges.
- The edge regions (of width approximately b/2 each) carry most of the load; the central region is comparatively stress-free.

This "redistribution to the edges" is the physical basis of the **Effective Width Concept** (von Kármán, 1932).

## 2.3 Plate Buckling Coefficient (k) — Reference Table

| Loading & Edge Condition | k |
| :--- | :--- |
| Long plate, simply supported, **uniform compression** (SS-SS-SS-SS) | **4.00** |
| Long plate, **one edge free, one simply supported**, uniform compression (S-F-SS-SS) | **0.425** |
| Long plate, **both edges simply supported, in pure bending** | **23.90** |
| Long plate, **all four edges clamped, uniform compression** | **10.07** |
| Long plate, simply supported, **pure shear** | **5.34** |
| Long web in patch / bearing loading | **≈ 2.0 – 3.0** |

> [!NOTE]
> For an **unstiffened element** (one edge free), use k = 0.425. For a **stiffened element** (both edges supported), use k = 4.0. These two values govern the majority of KTU problems on cold-formed steel.

## 2.4 The Effective Width Concept (von Kármán)

**Idea (von Kármán, 1932):** Replace the buckled, non-uniformly stressed plate of total width **b** by an *equivalent* fictitious plate of width **b_eff** that:

- Is **uniformly stressed** at the yield stress f_y,
- Carries the **same total axial force** as the actual buckled plate at collapse.

The original (ideal) von Kármán formula was:

$$
b_{eff} = b \, \sqrt{\frac{f_{cr}}{f_{y}}}
$$

This was modified by **Winter, Hancock & Penka (1950)** after a long series of experiments at Cornell on cold-formed steel sections, and Winter proposed:

$$
\frac{b_{eff}}{b} \;=\; \rho \;=\; 1.0 \quad \text{for} \quad \lambda \leq 0.673
$$

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.22 / \lambda}{\lambda} \quad \text{for} \quad \lambda > 0.673
$$

where the **plate slenderness parameter** is:

$$
\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{f_{y}}{f_{cr}}} \;=\; \frac{1.052}{\sqrt{k}} \left( \frac{b}{t} \right) \sqrt{\frac{f_{y}}{E}} \quad \text{(IS 801 / AISI form)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise:** The Winter reduction factor ρ depends on λ = √(f_y / f_cr). Two regimes: (i) **stocky** (λ ≤ 0.673, ρ = 1.0, no reduction), (ii) **slender** (λ > 0.673, ρ decreases as 1/λ²). This is the single most-tested formula in PECET864 Module 4.

## 2.5 IS 800 : 2007 — Slenderness Limits for Plate Elements

The Indian code **IS 800 : 2007, Cl. 3.7** defines three classes of plate elements based on b/t (internal compression element):

| Class | b/t Limit (Hot-rolled, I/H) | b/t Limit (Rolled, outstanding) | Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Class 1 (Plastic) | ≤ 9.4 ε | ≤ 9.4 ε | Plastic rotation, no local buckling |
| Class 2 (Compact) | ≤ 10.5 ε | ≤ 10.5 ε | Yield, no local buckling |
| Class 3 (Semi-compact) | ≤ 15.7 ε | ≤ 13.6 ε | Elastic, no local buckling |
| Class 4 (Slender) | > 15.7 ε | > 13.6 ε | **Local buckling before yield** |

where **ε = √(250 / f_y)**, with f_y in MPa.

> [!WARNING]
> Section 9 of IS 800 (Cold-Formed Light Gauge Steel) uses the **effective width method** of IS 801 / AISI S100, *not* the Table 2.1 of Cl. 3.7. Students must not mix the two classification systems.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (One-Page Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Print and Pin — every formula below is examinable under PECET864 M4.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| F1 | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^{2} E}{12(1-\nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ | Elastic local buckling stress of a plate | Bryan (1891) |
| F2 | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{1898}{\left(b/t\right)^{2}} \cdot k$ (MPa, with E = 2×10⁵) | Numerical shortcut | Derived from F1 |
| F3 | $\lambda = \sqrt{f_{y}/f_{cr}}$ | Plate slenderness parameter | IS 801 |
| F4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1.052(b/t)}{\sqrt{k}} \sqrt{f_{y}/E}$ | λ in terms of b/t | IS 801 (alternative) |
| F5 | $\rho = 1.0$ for $\lambda \leq 0.673$ | Stocky regime | Winter (IS 801) |
| F6 | $\rho = \dfrac{1 - 0.22/\lambda}{\lambda}$ for $\lambda > 0.673$ | Slender regime | Winter (IS 801) |
| F7 | $b_{eff} = \rho \, b$ | Effective width | IS 801 |
| F8 | $A_{eff} = \sum \rho_{i} b_{i} \, t_{i}$ | Effective area of section | IS 801 Cl. 7.3.1 |
| F9 | $P_{d,eff} = A_{eff} \, f_{y} / \gamma_{m0}$ | Axial capacity, slender section | IS 801 |
| F10 | $M_{d,eff} = Z_{eff} \, f_{y} / \gamma_{m0}$ | Moment capacity, slender section | IS 801 |
| F11 | $b/t \leq 30$ for shear in web (unstiffened) | Web slenderness, no stiffener | IS 800 Cl. 8.4.1 |
| F12 | $\tau_{cr} = \dfrac{k_{v} \pi^{2} E}{12(1-\nu^{2})} (t/d)^{2}$, k_v = 5.35 | Shear buckling of web | IS 800 Cl. 8.4.2 |
| F13 | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250 / f_{y}}$ | Yield-factor (IS 800) | IS 800 Cl. 2.2.4.2 |

**Where:**
- $b$ = full width of the plate element (clear of curves),
- $t$ = thickness of plate,
- $k$ = plate buckling coefficient (Table in §2.3),
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio = 0.3,
- $\gamma_{m0}$ = partial safety factor for material = **1.10** (IS 800), **1.20** (IS 801, AISI).

## 2.7 Why This Topic Matters in Real Engineering

1. **Cold-formed steel construction** (steel racks, purlins, wall studs, deck profiles): The whole point of CFS is that sections are *thin* (t = 0.5 – 3.0 mm) and thus *Class 4* by hot-rolled standards. Effective width governs the design.
2. **Plate girders** in bridges and industrial buildings: Slender webs carry shear by *tension-field action* after shear buckling — the post-buckling reserve.
3. **Aluminium and stainless sections** in architecture: Even "thick" sections are class 4 because E is low.
4. **Crashworthiness of thin-walled automotive rails**: Controlled local buckling *absorbs* energy (it's desired, not avoided).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Elastic Local Buckling Stress of a Plate (Bryan / Timoshenko)

We will derive the critical buckling stress for a *long*, *simply supported* plate of width **b**, thickness **t**, carrying uniform compression **N_x** per unit length on the two short edges. The plate is assumed infinitely long in the y-direction (so w is uniform along y).

**Step 1 — Governing equation.** The transverse deflection w(x) of the plate is governed by the small-deflection equilibrium equation of a plate under in-plane stress N_x. For uniform in-plane compression and uniform transverse deflection (long-plate assumption), the differential equation reduces to:

$$
D \, \frac{d^{4} w}{dx^{4}} \;+\; N_{x} \, \frac{d^{2} w}{dx^{2}} \;=\; 0
$$

*Text:* $D$ is the flexural rigidity of the plate per unit width. $N_x$ is the compressive force per unit length. The first term is the elastic restoring bending stiffness; the second is the destabilising effect of the in-plane compression.

**Step 2 — Flexural rigidity per unit width.**

$$
D \;=\; \frac{E \, t^{3}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})}
$$

*Text:* This is the same form as the second moment of area $I = b h^3 / 12$ per unit width, with $E$ replaced by $E/(1-\nu^2)$ because the plate is in plane strain (constrained laterally by Poisson's effect).

**Step 3 — Trial solution.** The assumed deflected shape that satisfies the simply-supported boundary conditions $w = 0$ and $M_x = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = b$ is:

$$
w(x) \;=\; w_{0} \, \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right)
$$

*Text:* A single half-sine wave with one node at each edge — the lowest buckling mode for a simply supported plate.

**Step 4 — Differentiate.**

$$
\frac{d^{2} w}{dx^{2}} = -\,\left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2} \, w_{0} \, \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{d^{4} w}{dx^{4}} = \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{4} \, w_{0} \, \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right)
$$

*Text:* Standard derivative identities; both are proportional to $\sin(\pi x / b)$.

**Step 5 — Substitute into the governing equation.**

$$
D \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{4} w_{0} \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right) \;-\; N_{x} \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2} w_{0} \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right) \;=\; 0
$$

*Text:* The sign in the second term is negative because the buckling term enters as $-N_x \cdot w''$, not $+N_x \cdot w''$. We have $D \cdot (+)(\pi/b)^4 \cdot w - N_x \cdot (-)(\pi/b)^2 \cdot w = 0$, which simplifies as below.

**Step 6 — Cancel common non-zero factors** (assuming $w_0 \neq 0$, since otherwise we have the trivial pre-buckling solution).

$$
D \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{4} \;-\; N_{x} \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for N_x,cr.**

$$
N_{x,cr} \;=\; D \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2}
$$

*Text:* The critical compressive force per unit length is the plate stiffness times the square of the buckling-mode wave number.

**Step 8 — Substitute the expression for D.**

$$
N_{x,cr} \;=\; \frac{E \, t^{3}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})} \cdot \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 9 — Convert to average compressive stress** (divide by thickness t).

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{N_{x,cr}}{t} \;=\; \frac{E \, t^{2} \, \pi^{2}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2}) \, b^{2}}
$$

**Step 10 — Rearrange into the standard form with k.**

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{k \, \pi^{2} \, E}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})} \left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^{2}
$$

*Text:* Setting k = 4.0 recovers the case of a long simply-supported plate in uniform compression (one half-wave across the width). For other boundary conditions, the trial function is changed to include the appropriate edge conditions, and the corresponding k emerges from the same derivation. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The student must understand **that the derived k = 4.0 is for a *simply supported* long plate in uniform compression**. Other k values are *not* derived here but must be memorised from the Table in §2.3.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Effective Width of a Stiffened Element

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A cold-formed channel section has a web of width **b = 100 mm** and thickness **t = 1.0 mm**. The yield stress is **f_y = 250 MPa**. The web is *stiffened* (supported on both edges). Compute the effective width and effective area for axial compression. (Use E = 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa, ν = 0.3.)

**Step 1 — Identify k.** Stiffened element, uniform compression → k = 4.0.

**Step 2 — Compute f_cr.**

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{4.0 \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot 2 \times 10^{5}}{12 \cdot (1 - 0.3^{2})} \left( \frac{1.0}{100} \right)^{2}
$$

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{4.0 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 200000}{12 \cdot 0.91} \cdot 10^{-4}
$$

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{7895680}{10.92} \cdot 10^{-4} \;=\; 723046 \cdot 10^{-4} \;=\; 72.30 \; \text{MPa}
$$

*Text:* We are keeping 4 significant figures in intermediate steps to avoid round-off accumulation.

**Step 3 — Compute slenderness λ.**

$$
\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{f_{y}}{f_{cr}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{250}{72.30}} \;=\; \sqrt{3.4578} \;=\; 1.8595
$$

**Step 4 — Choose the correct Winter branch.** Since λ = 1.8595 > 0.673, we use the slender formula.

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.22 / \lambda}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.22 / 1.8595}{1.8595} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.1183}{1.8595} \;=\; \frac{0.8817}{1.8595} \;=\; 0.4741
$$

**Step 5 — Effective width.**

$$
b_{eff} \;=\; \rho \, b \;=\; 0.4741 \times 100 \;=\; 47.41 \;\text{mm}
$$

*Text:* The web, although 100 mm wide, behaves as a 47.4 mm wide plate uniformly stressed at f_y.

**Step 6 — Effective area contribution of web.**

$$
A_{eff,web} \;=\; b_{eff} \cdot t \;=\; 47.41 \times 1.0 \;=\; 47.41 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

*Text:* Compared with the full 100 mm², we have lost more than half the area — confirming that slender webs are highly inefficient for axial load.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Effective Width of an Unstiffened Element

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For the same section, the outstanding flange (unstiffened) has b = 50 mm, t = 1.0 mm, f_y = 250 MPa, E = 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa, ν = 0.3. Find b_eff and A_eff,flange.

**Step 1 — Identify k.** Unstiffened element in uniform compression → k = 0.425.

**Step 2 — Compute f_cr.**

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{0.425 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 200000}{12 \cdot 0.91} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{50} \right)^{2}
$$

*Text:* Note that (b/t) = 50 for the flange, not 100. The b/t is *per element*, not for the whole section.

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{838918}{10.92} \cdot 4 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 76824 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 30.73 \;\text{MPa}
$$

*Text:* Lower f_cr for the flange than for the web because (a) k is smaller, and (b) the element is relatively more slender (smaller denominator, larger b/t). This is *unfair* in a way — the flange is smaller, but slenderness is higher. We accept this.

**Step 3 — Compute λ.**

$$
\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{250}{30.73}} \;=\; \sqrt{8.135} \;=\; 2.852
$$

**Step 4 — ρ.** λ > 0.673, so use the slender formula.

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.22 / 2.852}{2.852} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.0771}{2.852} \;=\; \frac{0.9229}{2.852} \;=\; 0.3236
$$

**Step 5 — Effective width.**

$$
b_{eff,flange} \;=\; 0.3236 \times 50 \;=\; 16.18 \;\text{mm}
$$

*Text:* Almost 70 % of the outstanding flange is *ineffective* in carrying axial load. The student should appreciate why cold-formed steel sections prefer **stiffened** geometries (e.g., a hollow tube, a channel with edge stiffeners) over *unstiffened* outstanding flanges.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Effect on Axial Capacity

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Continuing the example: the cold-formed channel has one web (100×1.0 mm) and two flanges (50×1.0 mm). Use the effective widths from §3.2 and §3.3. Find the design axial capacity P_d. Take γ_m0 = 1.20 (IS 801).

**Step 1 — Total effective area.**

$$
A_{eff} \;=\; A_{eff,web} + 2 \times A_{eff,flange}
$$

$$
A_{eff} \;=\; 47.41 + 2 \times 16.18 \;=\; 47.41 + 32.36 \;=\; 79.77 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross area for reference.**

$$
A_{gross} \;=\; 1.0 \times 100 + 2 \times 1.0 \times 50 \;=\; 200 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

*Text:* We have lost 60 % of the gross area. Without effective-width consideration, the design would be wildly unsafe (over-estimate by 2.5×).

**Step 3 — Design capacity.**

$$
P_{d} \;=\; \frac{A_{eff} \cdot f_{y}}{\gamma_{m0}} \;=\; \frac{79.77 \times 250}{1.20} \;=\; \frac{19942.5}{1.20} \;=\; 16618.75 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 16.62 \;\text{kN}
$$

*Text:* The capacity of this small (200 mm²) channel is only ~16.6 kN at yield. It is a thin section; massive design is impossible.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Stress Distribution Affects k

| Stress State on Plate | Boundary | k | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Uniform compression | SSSS | 4.00 | All edges straight, edges free to wave in-plane |
| Uniform compression | SCSF (one edge free) | 0.425 | Outstand much weaker |
| Pure bending (linear gradient) | SSSS | 23.90 | Compression half much stiffer, more waves |
| Pure shear | SSSS | 5.34 | Diagonal tension field |
| Patch loading (length c) | SSSS | ≈ 2–6 | Localised compression strip |
| Uniaxial compression with one clamped edge | CSCS | 6.97 | Stiffer than SSSS |

*Text:* In KTU examinations, the **two values that must be memorised are 4.0 and 0.425** (stiffened vs. unstiffened compression elements). For bending and shear, the corresponding k values (23.9 and 5.34) are typically provided in the question.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Local Buckling of a Stiffened vs. Unstiffened Element (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram maps the *failure mode pathway* of a single plate element from service load to collapse, showing the role of the k-value and effective width.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Steel Plate Element b x t under uniform compression] --> B{b/t ratio and edge support?}
    B -- Stiffened SS-SS, k = 4.0 --> C[Elastic Local Buckling at f_cr = 1898 x 4.0 divided by b over t squared MPa]
    B -- Unstiffened SS-Free, k = 0.425 --> D[Elastic Local Buckling at f_cr = 1898 x 0.425 divided by b over t squared MPa]
    C --> E{lambda = sqrt f_y / f_cr greater than 0.673?}
    D --> F{lambda = sqrt f_y / f_cr greater than 0.673?}
    E -- No --> G[Stocky: rho = 1.0, full area effective]
    E -- Yes --> H[Slender: rho = 1 - 0.22 over lambda divided by lambda]
    F -- No --> I[Stocky: rho = 1.0, full area effective]
    F -- Yes --> J[Slender: rho = 1 - 0.22 over lambda divided by lambda]
    H --> K[Effective Width b_eff = rho x b, Stress redistributes to edges]
    J --> L[Effective Width b_eff = rho x b, Stress redistributes to edges]
    K --> M[Edge stress reaches f_y, collapse]
    L --> M[Edge stress reaches f_y, collapse]
    G --> M
    I --> M
    M --> N[Post-buckling reserve fully utilised]
```

## 4.2 Stress Redistribution Schematic

The following diagram describes how stress redistributes across the width of a buckled plate, with the **effective width concept** mapped in a clear, learner-friendly way.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Physical plate b wide] --> P2[Pre-buckling: uniform stress f everywhere]
    P2 --> P3[Post-buckling: transverse membrane tension develops]
    P3 --> P4[Central region unloads, stress drops below f_y]
    P4 --> P5[Edge regions load up to f_y each]
    P5 --> P6[Equivalent effective width b_eff carrying full f_y]
    P6 --> P7[Fictitious plate of width b_eff at uniform stress f_y]
    P7 --> P8[Same axial capacity as physical buckled plate]
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree of Thin-Element Behaviour

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Slenderness of plate element] --> R1{Class 1 Plastic}
    R --> R2{Class 2 Compact}
    R --> R3{Class 3 Semi-Compact}
    R --> R4{Class 4 Slender}
    R1 --> R1A[Uses full plastic moment]
    R2 --> R2A[Uses full plastic moment]
    R3 --> R3A[Uses yield moment]
    R4 --> R4A[Local buckling governs, use effective width method]
    R4A --> R4B[Winter effective width formula]
    R4B --> R4C[Effective area A_eff]
    R4C --> R4D[P_d = A_eff x f_y divided by gamma_m0]
```

## 4.4 Concept Map — Relationship Between Concepts

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1[Width-thickness ratio b/t] --> C2[Plate slenderness lambda]
    C2 --> C3[Reduction factor rho via Winter]
    C3 --> C4[Effective width b_eff]
    C4 --> C5[Effective section properties]
    C5 --> C6[Design capacity P_d or M_d]
    C7[Plate buckling coefficient k] --> C2
    C8[Edge conditions: stiffened or unstiffened] --> C7
    C9[Material: E, f_y, nu] --> C2
    C10[Stress distribution: uniform, bending, shear] --> C7
```

> **Reading Guide for the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram is a *decision flowchart* that you can re-trace during an exam.
> - The second shows the *physical mechanism* of post-buckling redistribution.
> - The third ties local buckling to the four IS 800 classes.
> - The fourth is a *concept dependency graph* showing which input controls which output.
