# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Apr 2024, Model Qn]**

**Q1.** Define *effective width* of a plate element as used in the design of cold-formed steel sections. Why is it required in place of the actual width?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Effective width (b_eff) of a plate element is the reduced equivalent width of a buckled, non-uniformly stressed plate which, when assumed to be uniformly stressed at the design yield stress f_y, carries the same total axial force as the actual buckled plate at collapse. It is *required* in place of the actual width because slender plate elements (Class 4, b/t > IS 800 limits) buckle locally at a stress f_cr far below f_y. Beyond buckling, the plate continues to carry load due to *post-buckling membrane action* — the load redistributes from the buckled central region to the supported edge strips. The effective width concept (von Kármán 1932, refined by Winter 1950) replaces the actual non-uniform post-buckling stress distribution by an *equivalent uniform* stress distribution over b_eff at f_y. Per IS 801 / AISI S100, b_eff = ρ b, with ρ defined by the Winter formula depending on λ = √(f_y / f_cr). (3 marks distributed: 1 for definition, 1 for post-buckling mechanism, 1 for IS 801 reference.)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Qn]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between *stiffened* and *unstiffened* plate elements. Why is the plate-buckling coefficient k different for the two cases?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **stiffened element** has both edges parallel to the direction of compressive stress supported (e.g., the web of an I-section, the flat of a hollow rectangular section). An **unstiffened element** has only one such edge supported, with the other edge free (e.g., the outstanding half of an I-flange, the lip of a single-fold section). The plate-buckling coefficient k differs because buckling is the *in-plane* deflection of the plate. A plate that is free to translate (or wave) in the in-plane direction at one edge cannot develop the same membrane restraint as a plate that is rigidly supported on both edges. Per Bryan / Timoshenko, for a long simply-supported plate in uniform compression, k = 4.0 when both edges are supported (S-S-S-S) and k = 0.425 when one edge is free (S-S-F-S). Hence unstiffened elements have a *lower* elastic critical stress f_cr for the same b/t, and are correspondingly less efficient in compression. (3 marks: 1 for distinction, 1 for values, 1 for physical reasoning.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice Question]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the elastic local buckling stress f_cr of a long, simply supported plate of width b and thickness t, uniformly compressed in one direction, starting from the governing differential equation. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a cold-formed steel lipped channel section, the web is 120 mm wide and 1.2 mm thick, the flanges are 60 mm wide and 1.2 mm thick, and the lips are 20 mm wide and 1.2 mm thick. The yield stress f_y = 250 MPa, E = 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa, ν = 0.3. Determine the **effective area** of the section in axial compression using the Winter effective-width formula per IS 801. (7 marks)

> **Model Solution for Q.A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

We use the long-plate assumption (deflection uniform in the longitudinal direction). The governing equation for small transverse deflection w(x) of a plate under uniform in-plane compression N_x per unit length is:

$$
D \, \frac{d^{4} w}{dx^{4}} \;+\; N_{x} \, \frac{d^{2} w}{dx^{2}} \;=\; 0 \quad \quad (1)
$$

where the flexural rigidity per unit width is:

$$
D \;=\; \frac{E \, t^{3}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})} \quad \quad (2)
$$

*Text:* The first term is the elastic bending stiffness; the second is the destabilising effect of in-plane compression (geometric stiffness). Two unknowns: w(x) and N_x.

For a *simply supported* plate at x = 0 and x = b, the boundary conditions are w = 0 and bending moment M_x = 0. The trial solution satisfying both is:

$$
w(x) \;=\; w_{0} \, \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right) \quad \quad (3)
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{d^{2} w}{dx^{2}} = -w_{0} \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2} \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right) \quad \quad (4)
$$

$$
\frac{d^{4} w}{dx^{4}} = w_{0} \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{4} \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right) \quad \quad (5)
$$

Substituting (4) and (5) into (1):

$$
D \, w_{0} \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{4} \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right) \;-\; N_{x} \, w_{0} \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2} \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi \, x}{b} \right) \;=\; 0
$$

Since w_0 ≠ 0 (post-buckling configuration) and sin(πx/b) is not identically zero:

$$
D \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2} \;=\; N_{x} \quad \quad (6)
$$

Substituting (2) into (6):

$$
N_{x,cr} \;=\; \frac{E \, \pi^{2} \, t^{3}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2}) \, b^{2}} \quad \quad (7)
$$

The average compressive stress at the critical state is:

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{N_{x,cr}}{t} \;=\; \frac{E \, \pi^{2}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})} \left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^{2} \quad \quad (8)
$$

For a long simply-supported plate, the constant factor in the bracket is 4.0. To unify across all support and loading conditions, we write:

$$
f_{cr} \;=\; \frac{k \, \pi^{2} \, E}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})} \left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^{2} \quad \quad (9)
$$

where k = 4.0 for the case derived. **QED.** (7 marks distributed: 1 for governing equation, 1 for D, 1 for trial solution, 1 for differentiation, 1 for substitution, 1 for simplification, 1 for final form.)

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify element types and k.**

| Element | b (mm) | t (mm) | Type | k |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Web | 120 | 1.2 | Stiffened (SS-SS) | 4.0 |
| Flange | 60 | 1.2 | Stiffened at one edge (corner to web), the other free; or we use the lip to provide stiffening | 0.425 (use of lip stiffener may qualify as **partially stiffened**, but conservatively take unstiffened) |
| Lip | 20 | 1.2 | Unstiffened outstanding | 0.425 |

**Step 2 — Compute f_cr for the web (k = 4.0, b/t = 100).**

$$
f_{cr,web} \;=\; \frac{1898 \times 4.0}{(120/1.2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{7592}{10000} \;=\; 0.7592 \;\text{MPa}
$$

*Text:* A very small f_cr, because (b/t)² = 10⁴. The web is highly slender.

**Step 3 — λ and ρ for the web.**

$$
\lambda_{web} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{250}{0.7592}} \;=\; \sqrt{329.30} \;=\; 18.146
$$

$$
\rho_{web} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.22/18.146}{18.146} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.01213}{18.146} \;=\; \frac{0.98787}{18.146} \;=\; 0.05444
$$

$$
b_{eff,web} \;=\; 0.05444 \times 120 \;=\; 6.533 \;\text{mm}
$$

*Text:* More than 94 % of the web is *ineffective*. This is a very slender web indeed.

**Step 4 — Compute f_cr for the flange (k = 0.425, b/t = 50).**

$$
f_{cr,flange} \;=\; \frac{1898 \times 0.425}{(60/1.2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{806.65}{2500} \;=\; 0.3227 \;\text{MPa}
$$

*Text:* Even lower f_cr than the web. The (b/t) = 50 unstiffened flange is exceptionally slender.

**Step 5 — λ and ρ for the flange.**

$$
\lambda_{flange} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{250}{0.3227}} \;=\; \sqrt{774.71} \;=\; 27.83
$$

$$
\rho_{flange} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.22/27.83}{27.83} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.00791}{27.83} \;=\; \frac{0.99209}{27.83} \;=\; 0.03565
$$

$$
b_{eff,flange} \;=\; 0.03565 \times 60 \;=\; 2.139 \;\text{mm}
$$

*Text:* Almost 96 % of the flange is ineffective.

**Step 6 — Compute f_cr for the lip (k = 0.425, b/t = 16.67).**

$$
f_{cr,lip} \;=\; \frac{1898 \times 0.425}{(20/1.2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{806.65}{277.78} \;=\; 2.904 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 7 — λ and ρ for the lip.**

$$
\lambda_{lip} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{250}{2.904}} \;=\; \sqrt{86.09} \;=\; 9.279
$$

$$
\rho_{lip} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.22/9.279}{9.279} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.02371}{9.279} \;=\; \frac{0.97629}{9.279} \;=\; 0.1052
$$

$$
b_{eff,lip} \;=\; 0.1052 \times 20 \;=\; 2.104 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Effective area.**

$$
A_{eff} \;=\; b_{eff,web} \, t + 2 \, b_{eff,flange} \, t + 2 \, b_{eff,lip} \, t
$$

$$
A_{eff} \;=\; (6.533 \times 1.2) + 2 \times (2.139 \times 1.2) + 2 \times (2.104 \times 1.2)
$$

$$
A_{eff} \;=\; 7.840 + 5.134 + 5.050 \;=\; 18.02 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 9 — Gross area for reference.**

$$
A_{gross} \;=\; (120 + 2 \times 60 + 2 \times 20) \times 1.2 \;=\; 280 \times 1.2 \;=\; 336 \;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

*Text:* Only 18.02 / 336 ≈ **5.4 %** of the gross area is effective. This is a *very* inefficient section for axial load. The student should conclude that such slender lips and flanges are appropriate for **flexural** members (purlins, girts, studs) where bending governs, *not* for axial columns. **(7 marks distributed: 1 for k identification, 4 for f_cr / λ / ρ / b_eff computations, 1 for A_eff sum, 1 for design commentary.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain with a clear sketch how *transverse membrane stresses* develop in a plate element after local buckling. How is this incorporated in the *effective width* concept for design? (7 marks)

**(b)** A plate girder has a web of depth d_w = 1500 mm and thickness t_w = 6 mm, simply supported on all four edges, subjected to a uniform shear stress τ. Compute the **elastic shear buckling stress τ_cr** using k_v = 5.35, E = 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa, ν = 0.3. Comment on whether the web is *class 4* in shear per IS 800 (limit λ_w ≤ 67ε for unstiffened web). (7 marks)

> **Model Solution for Q.B:**

**(a) Post-buckling mechanism (7 marks):**

When a plate element loaded in uniform compression reaches f_cr, the smallest transverse deflection w(x,y) appears. In the *post-buckling* regime, the deflected shape is no longer flat. Because the plate is constrained at its boundaries (in-plane restraint), the out-of-plane deflection of the central region *stretches* the plate in the transverse (y) direction. This stretching is balanced by an in-plane **membrane tension** in the transverse direction.

These transverse tensile stresses are not destabilising — they *stiffen* the plate against further buckling. As a result, the plate can continue to carry increased in-plane compression even after the bifurcation. The total in-plane compression is no longer uniform across the width: it is *high* near the supported edges and *low* in the central region. The edge regions of width approximately b/2 each carry the lion's share of the load.

[Sketch description — full text-based figure]: the diagram shows a horizontal plate of full width b. The original flat shape is shown as a horizontal line. The post-buckled shape is shown as a wavy line with one half-wave across the width. The arrows in the figure are: (i) vertical arrows at left and right edges showing the *applied* compression N_x, (ii) horizontal arrows at the supports showing the *transverse membrane tension* developing at the edges, (iii) a small horizontal double-headed arrow in the middle of the plate showing the *transverse compression / reduction* in the central region, (iv) a non-uniform stress distribution f(y) across the width showing peak values at the two edges and a dip in the middle.

The **effective width concept** (von Kármán, 1932) replaces this non-uniform distribution by an *equivalent* uniform distribution at f_y over a reduced width b_eff. The two areas under the actual f(y) and the fictitious f_y are equal, both equal to the same total axial force. Winter (1950) modified von Kármán's ideal formula $b_{eff} = b \sqrt{f_{cr}/f_y}$ to account for *initial imperfections* and gave the IS 801 / AISI S100 form $b_{eff} = \rho b$ with $\rho$ from the Winter formula. (7 marks: 2 for the mechanism, 2 for the sketch description, 2 for the von Kármán/Winter concept, 1 for the IS 801 reference.)

**(b) Shear buckling of web (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Elastic critical shear stress.**

$$
\tau_{cr} \;=\; \frac{k_{v} \, \pi^{2} \, E}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})} \left( \frac{t_{w}}{d_{w}} \right)^{2}
$$

With k_v = 5.35:

$$
\tau_{cr} \;=\; \frac{5.35 \times 9.8696 \times 200000}{12 \times 0.91} \times \left( \frac{6}{1500} \right)^{2}
$$

*Text:* Note 6/1500 = 0.004, so (6/1500)² = 1.6 × 10⁻⁵.

$$
\tau_{cr} \;=\; \frac{10562270}{10.92} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\tau_{cr} \;=\; 967243 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \;=\; 15.476 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with yield shear stress.**

For E 250 grade steel, f_y = 250 MPa, and nominal yield shear stress is τ_y = f_y / √3 = 250 / 1.732 = 144.34 MPa.

Since τ_cr = 15.48 MPa << τ_y, **the web is slender in shear and will buckle before yielding**.

**Step 3 — Compute web slenderness parameter λ_w (per IS 800 Cl. 8.4.2).**

$$
\lambda_{w} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\tau_{y}}{\tau_{cr}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{144.34}{15.476}} \;=\; \sqrt{9.327} \;=\; 3.054
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with limit per IS 800.**

For E 250 steel, ε = √(250/250) = 1.0. The limit per IS 800 Cl. 8.4.2 for shear yielding without buckling is:

$$
\lambda_{w,\text{limit}} \;=\; 67 \, \varepsilon \;=\; 67 \times 1.0 \;=\; 67
$$

Since λ_w = 3.054 < 67, the criterion is *not* a direct violation. However, the *intent* of IS 800 is that **when τ_cr < τ_y**, the web should be designed using tension-field action (Cl. 8.5) and intermediate transverse stiffeners should be provided. As per Cl. 8.4.1, if d_w / t_w = 1500/6 = 250 > 67ε = 67, **the web needs intermediate stiffeners**.

**Step 5 — Design implication.**

A web with d_w / t_w = 250 is a *very* slender web. The design should use the tension-field method (Cl. 8.5.1 of IS 800) for shear capacity. We do not have buckling control by web alone; transverse stiffeners are *mandatory*. (7 marks: 2 for τ_cr calculation, 1 for comparison with τ_y, 2 for λ_w and limit, 2 for design commentary.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU 2024 Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use E / (1 − ν²) instead of E alone** in the plate buckling formula. This single error under-estimates f_cr by ~9 % and gives the *wrong* effective width. (*1 mark lost in Part A; 1-2 marks lost in Part B.*)
> 2. **Confusing k = 4.0 (stiffened) and k = 0.425 (unstiffened)**. A wrong k gives wrong f_cr, wrong λ, wrong ρ, wrong A_eff. (*Cascading errors worth 3-4 marks.*)
> 3. **Using the wrong branch of the Winter formula** (i.e., ρ = 1.0 when λ > 0.673, or vice versa). *1 mark lost per element type.*
> 4. **Mixing IS 800 (hot-rolled, Class 1-4) with IS 801 / AISI (cold-formed, effective width)**. They are *separate* design philosophies. KTU Module 4 focuses on IS 801 for the *cold-formed* content. (*2 marks if confused in a Part B question.*)
> 5. **Neglecting the *post-buckling* nature of the problem.** The question may explicitly say "post-buckling" — do not write only about elastic buckling. Mention *transverse membrane tension*, *load redistribution to edges*, and *effective width* explicitly. (*2 marks lost for missing the keyword in Part A definitions.*)
> 6. **Not stating units** of intermediate values like f_cr, λ, b_eff. Always write N/mm² or mm explicitly. (*0.5 mark per missing unit.*)
> 7. **Rounding intermediate steps to 2-3 figures.** Carry 4 figures in f_cr, λ, ρ to avoid round-off at the end. The Part B mark distribution allots 1 mark for "correctness to 4 sig. fig."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-minute revision before the exam:**

- [ ] **f_cr formula** is $f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^{2} E}{12(1-\nu^{2})} (t/b)^{2}$ with $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, ν = 0.3.
- [ ] **Numerical shortcut** $f_{cr} \approx 1898 \, k / (b/t)^{2}$ MPa — saves 30 seconds in the exam.
- [ ] **k = 4.0 for stiffened**, **k = 0.425 for unstiffened** in *uniform compression*. Memorise these two.
- [ ] **k = 23.9 for bending**, **k = 5.35 for shear** — provided in the question typically, but memorise as backup.
- [ ] **Plate slenderness** $\lambda = \sqrt{f_{y}/f_{cr}}$. Two regimes: **λ ≤ 0.673 (stocky, ρ = 1.0)** and **λ > 0.673 (slender)**.
- [ ] **Winter formula**: $\rho = (1 - 0.22/\lambda)/\lambda$ for λ > 0.673. **Do not use the von Kármán ideal $b_{eff} = b\sqrt{f_{cr}/f_y}$** — it does not account for initial imperfections and is not in IS 801.
- [ ] **Effective width**: $b_{eff} = \rho \, b$. **Effective area**: $A_{eff} = \sum \rho_i b_i t_i$.
- [ ] **γ_m0**: **1.10 for hot-rolled (IS 800)**, **1.20 for cold-formed (IS 801 / AISI)**. Mix-up is a common error.
- [ ] **Post-buckling** mechanism: transverse membrane tension at the edges → load redistribution to edges → collapse when edge stress reaches f_y.
- [ ] **IS 800 Class 4** elements (slender plates in hot-rolled sections) also use effective width, but the formulation is different from IS 801 (the *inelastic* reserve of hot-rolled Class 4 is partial; for cold-formed it is the *elastic* post-buckling reserve).
- [ ] **Plate girder webs**: d_w / t_w > 67ε ⇒ intermediate stiffeners required. d_w / t_w > 200ε ⇒ use tension-field method.
- [ ] **Effective width is *always* less than or equal to the full width**. Verify numerically: ρ ∈ [0, 1].
- [ ] **Stiffened sections (tubes, channels with lips) are MORE efficient than unstiffened sections** in compression, because k = 4.0 >> 0.425.
- [ ] **Cold-formed steel sections** are Class 4 almost by design — *every* KTU problem on CFS must use the effective-width approach.
- [ ] **Always state assumptions**: "long plate", "simply supported", "uniform compression", "initial imperfections ignored except via Winter".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word:** The PECET864 M4 question on *Local and Post Buckling of Thin Elements* is almost always a *derivation* (Part a) plus a *numerical* (Part b). Memorise the Bryan / Timoshenko derivation of f_cr, and the Winter formula for ρ. With these two plus the values k = 4.0 and k = 0.425, you can solve 90 % of the KTU Module 4 questions in this area. Do not confuse *elastic local buckling* (the theoretical f_cr) with *collapse* (which happens at f_y at the edge, using ρ).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stiffened and Multiple Stiffened Compression Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Stiffened and Multiple Stiffened Compression Elements # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (As per IS 801:1975 & KTU 2024 Syllabus) A **compression element** of a cold-formed steel section is any portion of the cross-section that is subjected to axial compressive stress when the member carries load. Based on edge support conditions and buckling behavior, these elements are classified as **stiffened**, **unstiffened**, and **multiple stiffened** elements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stiffened Compression Element:** A flat compression element (e.g., the web of a plain channel, the flange of a hat section) whose two longitudinal edges are each connected to a web, flange, stiffener, or other element that provides lateral support preventing local edge rotation/displacement. Both edges are **effectively supported against out-of-plane buckling**. **Multiple Stiffened Compression Element:** A flat compression element that is stiffened **between its two supported edges by means of one or more intermediate stiffeners** (longitudinal stiffeners/edge stiffeners), thereby dividing the parent plate into a series of smaller sub-elements. Each sub-element behaves as an individual stiffened element with reduced width. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long, thin, flat ruler held at both ends and pressed along its length (compression). If you push hard, the ruler will **bow out sideways** — this is buckling. The unsupported middle waves up and down. Now imagine the *same ruler* but you place a thin vertical **rib** at its middle, gluing it firmly to the ruler. The ruler is now restrained at the center too. It can no longer buckle freely as one big wave — instead it must form **two smaller waves**, each hugging the rib. By splitting one long wave into many small ones, the plate becomes much stiffer. - **Stiffened element** = plate supported at both edges (like the ruler held at both ends). - **Multiple stiffened element** = plate with extra ribs in between (like the ruler with the middle glue-rib). This is the **fundamental trick** behind cold-formed steel design — we don't add material; we redistribute the unsupported length using **intermediate stiffeners** to delay local buckling and gain strength. ## 1.3 Code Reference (IS 801:1975 – Cold-Formed Steel Structures) > [!NOTE] > Per **IS 801:1975 (Reaffirmed 2010)** and **IS 811:1987**, the design of cold-formed steel members is governed by the following element classification: > - **Clause 4.1.1** defines stiffened and unstiffened elements. > - **Clause 5.2** provides the **effective width formula** for stiffened elements. > - **Clause 6.1.1** provides the **limiting width-to-thickness ratios (b/t)**. > - Multiple stiffened elements are treated per **Clause 5.2.2** (Edge Stiffened Elements). ## 1.4 Classification of Compression Elements | Type | Definition | Typical Example | |------|------------|-----------------| | **Stiffened Element** | Supported along **both** longitudinal edges | Web of plain channel, flange between two webs of I-section | | **Unstiffened Element** | Supported along **only one** longitudinal edge | Flange tip of a plain channel, outstanding leg of angle | | **Edge Stiffened / Multiple Stiffened** | Stiffened element with **intermediate stiffeners** | Flange of lipped channel, deck profile with longitudinal ribs | | **Unstiffened with Edge Stiffener** | An unstiffened element reinforced with a simple lip | Lipped flange of a lipped channel | ## 1.5 Real-World Applications - **Lipped channels** (C-sections with edge stiffeners) used in **purlins and girts** of pre-engineered buildings. - **Profiled steel decking** used as permanent formwork and composite slabs — these are **multi-stiffened** elements. - **Cold-formed steel storage racks** — upright frames with multiple intermediate stiffeners in the web. - **Solar PV mounting structures** — high strength-to-weight ratio achieved by stiffened compression flanges. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Local Buckling — The Underlying Phenomenon When a thin plate element of a steel section is subjected to **uniform compressive stress** ($f_c$), it does not fail catastrophically at the classical Euler buckling load. Instead: 1. The plate reaches its **critical local buckling stress** ($f_{cr}$). 2. It does **not** collapse — the **post-buckling strength** is mobilized as the stress redistributes toward the supported edges. 3. Only a portion of the width, called the **effective width ($b_{eff}$)** adjacent to the supported edges, is considered fully effective. 4. The central portion, $b - b_{eff}$, is assumed to carry no stress. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the famous **von Kármán effective width concept** (1932), adopted universally by AISI, IS 801, Eurocode 3 Part 1-3, and AS/NZS 4600. ## 2.2 The Effective Width Formula (IS 801 — Clause 5.2.1) For a uniformly compressed stiffened element, the **effective width** is computed as: $$b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$$ where $b$ is the actual flat width, and $\rho$ is the **reduction factor (≤ 1.0)**. The reduction factor is determined by the **slenderness parameter $\lambda$**: $$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cr}}}$$ where $f_y$ is the **yield stress of steel** and $f_{cr}$ is the **elastic local buckling stress** of the plate element. $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \mu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$$ with: - $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²** (Young's Modulus) - $\mu = 0.3$ (Poisson's ratio for steel) - $t$ = plate thickness - $b$ = flat width of element - $k$ = **buckling coefficient** (depends on edge conditions and stress distribution) For **uniformly compressed stiffened element** with both edges simply supported: $k = 4.0$. For **uniformly compressed unstiffened element** with one edge free: $k = 0.425$. The reduction factor $\rho$: $$\rho = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda^2} \quad \text{for } \lambda \geq 0.673$$ $$\rho = 1.0 \quad \text{for } \lambda < 0.673$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes **all formulas** tested in KTU University Examinations for this topic. Memorize them and their boundary conditions. | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---------|-------------------| | 1 | $b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$ | Effective width for stiffened element | | 2 | $\lambda = \sqrt{f_y / f_{cr}}$ | Slenderness parameter | | 3 | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\mu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | Elastic local buckling stress | | 4 | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda^2}$ | Reduction factor ($\lambda \geq 0.673$) | | 5 | $\rho = 1.0$ | For stocky elements ($\lambda < 0.673$) | | 6 | $\lambda_p = 0.673$ | Cut-off slenderness (yielding vs. buckling) | | 7 | $(b/t)_{lim} = \dfrac{0.644}{\sqrt{f_y/k_E}} \cdot \sqrt{k}$ | Limiting b/t ratio for $\rho = 1.0$ | | 8 | $A_{eff} = \sum b_{eff,i} \cdot t$ | Effective cross-sectional area | | 9 | $P_d = A_{eff} \cdot f_y$ | Design axial strength | | 10 | $b_e = b$ for $\lambda \leq 0.673$ | Fully effective condition | ## 2.4 Buckling Coefficient ($k$) — Critical Values | Element Type | Stress Pattern | k-value | |--------------|----------------|---------| | Stiffened element — both edges SS | Uniform compression | 4.0 | | Stiffened element — both edges SS | Pure bending (compression) | 23.9 | | Unstiffened element — one edge free | Uniform compression | 0.425 | | Unstiffened element — one edge free | Pure bending (compression) | 0.85 | | Edge stiffened element with simple lip | Uniform compression | 0.85 (treated as effective) | | Multiple stiffened element | Uniform compression | 4.0 per sub-element | ## 2.5 Limiting Width-to-Thickness Ratio (b/t) The **limiting slenderness** below which the element is **fully effective** ($\rho = 1.0$): $$(b/t)_{lim} = \dfrac{0.644 \cdot \lambda_p \cdot \sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{12(1-\mu^2)/(\pi^2)}} \cdot \sqrt{E/f_y}$$ For **stiffened element under uniform compression** ($k = 4.0$), the simplified form is: $$(b/t)_{lim} = \dfrac{0.644 \cdot \sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{f_y/E}} \quad \text{(for $f_y$ in MPa)}$$ Substituting $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa: $$(b/t)_{lim} = \dfrac{0.644 \cdot \sqrt{4}}{\sqrt{f_y/2 \times 10^5}} = \dfrac{1.288}{\sqrt{f_y/2 \times 10^5}}$$ > [!WARNING] > If actual $b/t$ exceeds $(b/t)_{lim}$, the element undergoes **local buckling** and effective width must be calculated. The most common KTU mistake is forgetting this check. ## 2.6 Multiple Stiffened Elements — Stiffener Design Rules For a multiple stiffened element (e.g., deck profile), the intermediate stiffener must have adequate **flexural rigidity** and **axial rigidity** to function as a node of support. IS 801 specifies: **Required moment of inertia ($I_s$) of the stiffener about its own centroidal axis parallel to the stiffened element:** $$I_{s,min} = \dfrac{3.66 \cdot t^4 \cdot [\lambda - 0.136 \cdot (b/t)]^3}{(b/t)} \quad \text{— when } \lambda > 0.673$$ $$I_{s,min} = 0 \quad \text{— when } \lambda \leq 0.673 \text{ (no stiffener needed)}$$ **Required cross-sectional area ($A_s$) of the stiffener:** $$A_{s,min} = \dfrac{0.205 \cdot t^2 \cdot [\lambda - 0.136 \cdot (b/t)]}{(b/t)} \quad \text{— when } \lambda > 0.673$$ ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Design of cold-formed steel purlins** — lipped channels are typical; web is stiffened; flange with lip is multiple-stiffened. - **Storage rack uprights** — perforated webs behave as multiple-stiffened elements. - **Decking in composite construction** — trapezoidal deck profiles are multiple-stiffened elements, with intermediate stiffeners in the flange and web. - **Automotive chassis & truck bodies** — high-strength thin-gauge cold-formed sections rely heavily on this concept for crashworthiness and stiffness. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Width Reduction Factor $\rho$ ### Starting Point: Plate Buckling Theory (Bryan/Timoshenko) For a simply supported rectangular plate of width $b$, thickness $t$, length $L$, under uniform in-plane compression $N_x$ per unit length, the **critical buckling load** is: $$N_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot D}{b^2}$$ where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity per unit width** of the plate: $$D = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1 - \mu^2)}$$ Substituting $D$: $$N_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot b^2 \cdot (1 - \mu^2)}$$ The **elastic critical buckling stress** is $f_{cr} = N_{cr}/t$: $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\mu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2 \quad \text{— (Eq. 1)}$$ ### Von Kármán's Effective Width Concept Post-buckling, the stress distribution becomes non-uniform. The actual stress is concentrated near the supported edges and is approximated as a uniform stress $f_y$ acting over an **effective width** $b_{eff}$ that gives the same total load capacity as the actual non-uniform distribution: $$f_y \cdot b_{eff} = \int_0^b f_{actual}(x) \, dx \quad \text{— (Eq. 2)}$$ Von Kármán's classical solution yields: $$b_{eff} = 0.95 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot E}{f_y}}$$ Winter (1947) modified this with empirical fit to test data: $$b_{eff} = 0.95 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot E}{f_y}} \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{0.209}{(b/t)} \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot E}{f_y}}\right]$$ Defining the **slenderness parameter**: $$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cr}}} = \dfrac{1.052}{\sqrt{k}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{b}{t}\right) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E}}$$ The effective width can be written in compact form: $$\dfrac{b_{eff}}{b} = \rho = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left(1 - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda^2} \quad \text{— (Eq. 3)}$$ > **Physical meaning:** $\rho$ is the fraction of the original width that is "active" in carrying load. When $\lambda$ is small (stocky element), $\rho \to 1$ (fully effective). When $\lambda$ is large (slender), $\rho \to 0$ (most of the width is ineffective). ### The Cut-off Slenderness $\lambda_p = 0.673$ Setting $\rho = 1.0$ in Eq. 3: $$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda^2} = 1 \implies \lambda^2 - \lambda + 0.22 = 0$$ Solving the quadratic: $$\lambda = \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 0.88}}{2} = \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{0.12}}{2} = \dfrac{1 \pm 0.3464}{2}$$ Taking the **larger root** (the upper limit below which element is fully effective): $$\lambda_p = 0.673$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For $\lambda \leq 0.673$: **Fully effective**, no reduction required ($b_{eff} = b$). > For $\lambda > 0.673$: **Ineffective portion** exists, use $b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Effective Width of a Stiffened Web > **Problem:** A cold-formed lipped channel section has a web of flat width $b = 200$ mm and thickness $t = 2$ mm. The yield stress of steel is $f_y = 250$ MPa. Calculate the effective width of the web assuming it acts as a stiffened compression element under uniform compression. **Step 1:** Identify the element type. The web of a channel is connected to two flanges along both longitudinal edges → **Stiffened element** → $k = 4.0$. **Step 2:** Compute the actual $b/t$ ratio. $$\dfrac{b}{t} = \dfrac{200}{2} = 100$$ **Step 3:** Compute the elastic local buckling stress $f_{cr}$. $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\mu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$$ $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}{12 \cdot (1-0.3^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{100}\right)^2$$ $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}{12 \cdot 0.91} \cdot 10^{-4}$$ $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{7.8957 \times 10^6}{10.92} \cdot 10^{-4} = 723{,}047 \cdot 10^{-4} = 72.30 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the slenderness parameter $\lambda$. $$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{250}{72.30}} = \sqrt{3.457} = 1.859$$ **Step 5:** Check whether $\lambda > 0.673$. Since $1.859 > 0.673$, the element undergoes local buckling. **Step 6:** Compute the reduction factor $\rho$. $$\rho = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{1}{1.859} - \dfrac{0.22}{(1.859)^2}$$ $$\rho = 0.5379 - \dfrac{0.22}{3.456} = 0.5379 - 0.0636 = 0.4743$$ **Step 7:** Compute the effective width. $$b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b = 0.4743 \cdot 200 = 94.86 \text{ mm} \approx 95 \text{ mm}$$ The **ineffective width** = $200 - 95 = 105$ mm, located at the mid-width of the web. > [!NOTE] > This result reveals a critical engineering insight: **a slender web carries only ~47% of its full geometric area effectively** in pure compression. Stiffeners or thicker webs are required to mobilize full strength. ## 3.3 Worked Example — Multiple Stiffened Element > **Problem:** A profiled steel deck has a top flange of total width $b = 300$ mm, thickness $t = 1.2$ mm, with two equally spaced intermediate stiffeners. Yield stress $f_y = 350$ MPa. Each sub-element width = 100 mm. Compute the effective width. **Step 1:** With $k = 4.0$ and sub-element $b_s = 100$ mm, $t = 1.2$ mm: $$\dfrac{b_s}{t} = \dfrac{100}{1.2} = 83.33$$ **Step 2:** Compute $f_{cr}$. $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{4 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}{12 \cdot 0.91} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1.2}{100}\right)^2$$ $$f_{cr} = 723{,}047 \cdot 1.44 \times 10^{-4} = 104.12 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $\lambda$. $$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{350}{104.12}} = \sqrt{3.361} = 1.833$$ **Step 4:** Compute $\rho$. $$\rho = \dfrac{1}{1.833} - \dfrac{0.22}{1.833^2} = 0.5456 - 0.0655 = 0.4801$$ **Step 5:** Effective width of each sub-element. $$b_{eff,s} = 0.4801 \cdot 100 = 48.01 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6:** Total effective width of the multiple stiffened flange. $$b_{eff,total} = 3 \times 48.01 = 144.03 \text{ mm} \approx 144 \text{ mm}$$ > Compare with the unstiffened 300 mm flange: $b_{eff,unstiff}$ would be $0.425$ $k$ value, which yields a much smaller effective width. This shows the **effectiveness of intermediate stiffeners** — they reduce the slenderness of each sub-element and **increase total effective width by ~3 times** in this case. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree | Condition | Element Type | Design Action | |-----------|--------------|---------------| | $b/t \leq (b/t)_{lim}$ | Stiffened — **stocky** | $b_{eff} = b$ (fully effective) | | $b/t > (b/t)_{lim}$ | Stiffened — **slender** | Calculate $b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$ | | Edge stiffener present | Multiple stiffened | Treat each sub-element as stiffened | | $\lambda \leq 0.673$ | — | $\rho = 1$, no buckling reduction | | $\lambda > 0.673$ | — | Use Winter's formula for $\rho$ | | Stiffener $I_s < I_{s,min}$ | Multiple stiffened | Treat as **unstiffened** (worse case) | | Stiffener $A_s < A_{s,min}$ | Multiple stiffened | Same — treat as unstiffened | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stiffened vs. Unstiffened vs. Multiple Stiffened ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Compression Element Classification] --> B[Stiffened Element] A --> C[Unstiffened Element] A --> D[Multiple Stiffened Element] B --> B1[Both edges supported
k equals 4.0] B --> B2[Web of channel
Flange between two webs] C --> C1[One edge free
k equals 0.425] C --> C2[Flange tip of plain channel
Outstanding leg of angle] D --> D1[Both edges plus
intermediate stiffeners] D --> D2[Lipped channel flange
Profiled decking] B1 --> E[Compute slenderness lambda] C1 --> E D1 --> E E --> F{lambda greater than 0.673} F -->|Yes| G[Calculate rho using Winter formula] F -->|No| H[Fully effective rho equals 1.0] G --> I[Effective width beff equals rho times b] H --> J[Effective width beff equals b] I --> K[Design capacity equals A eff times fy] J --> K D2 --> L{Stiffener rigidity check} L -->|Inadequate| M[Treat as unstiffened
k equals 0.425] L -->|Adequate| N[Treat each sub-element
k equals 4.0] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Stress Distribution in Stiffened Compression Element ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Uniform compression applied fc] --> B[Slenderness check
b over t] B --> C{lambda} C -->|less than 0.673| D[Uniform stress retained
b effective equals b] C -->|greater than 0.673| E[Post-buckling redistribution] E --> F[Stress concentrated at edges] F --> G[Effective width at edges
beff equals rho times b] F --> H[Central portion ineffective
width b minus beff] G --> I[Equivalent uniform stress fy] H --> I ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Multiple Stiffened Element Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Parent Compression Plate
width b, thickness t] --> B[Add intermediate stiffener] B --> C[Divide into sub-elements
b1, b2, b3] C --> D{Each sub-element
behaves as stiffened} D --> E[Sub-element 1: k equals 4.0] D --> F[Sub-element 2: k equals 4.0] D --> G[Sub-element 3: k equals 4.0] E --> H[Total effective width
equals sum of bi effective] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Compare with single unstiffened
beff for width b] I --> J[Stiffeners reduce slenderness
Increase load capacity] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Flow — Design of Stiffened Element (IS 801 Procedure) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify element type] --> B[Determine flat width b and thickness t] B --> C[Compute b over t ratio] C --> D[Compute fcr using k value] D --> E[Compute slenderness lambda] E --> F{lambda compared with 0.673} F -->|less than or equal| G[Element fully effective
beff equals b] F -->|greater than| H[Compute rho equals 1 over lambda
minus 0.22 over lambda squared] H --> I[Compute beff equals rho times b] G --> J[Sum effective widths
equals A effective] I --> J J --> K[Compute design strength
equals A effective times fy] K --> L[Apply partial safety factor gamma m equals 1.10] L --> M[Compare with applied load] ``` ## 4.5 Comparative Effectiveness Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original plate width b equals 300 mm] --> B[Single stiffened element
k equals 4.0] A --> C[Single unstiffened element
k equals 0.425] A --> D[Multiple stiffened element
2 intermediate stiffeners
k equals 4.0 per sub-element] B --> E[Effective width approximately 142 mm] C --> F[Effective width approximately 65 mm] D --> G[Effective width approximately 144 mm total] E --> H[Stiffener strategy is highly effective] F --> H G --> H ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** > **Define a stiffened compression element. Give one example.** **Model Answer:** A **stiffened compression element** is a flat element of a cold-formed steel section in which **both longitudinal edges are connected to other elements** (webs, flanges, or stiffeners) that provide support against out-of-plane displacement and rotation. The **buckling coefficient** is $k = 4.0$ for uniform compression. **Example:** The **web of a plain channel section** — connected to two flanges along both longitudinal edges. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** > **What is meant by effective width of a compression element? Why is it used in design?** **Model Answer:** The **effective width** ($b_{eff}$) is the reduced width of a compression element that, when assumed to carry **uniform stress equal to the yield stress** $f_y$, has the **same load-carrying capacity** as the actual element with its non-uniform post-buckling stress distribution. It is computed as $b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$, where $\rho$ is the reduction factor from Winter's formula. **Why used:** - Accounts for **local buckling** in slender thin-walled elements. - Enables the use of simple **linear elastic** design procedures. - Reflects the real **post-buckling reserve** of strength. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 4 Model Question]** > **A cold-formed steel channel section has a web of flat width 180 mm, thickness 2.5 mm, with both edges stiffened by flanges. The yield stress of steel is $f_y = 250$ MPa and $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa. Determine:** > **(a) The elastic local buckling stress $f_{cr}$ of the web. (7 marks)** > **(b) The effective width of the web, and comment on whether the web is fully effective. (7 marks)** #### Solution to (a): **Given:** $b = 180$ mm, $t = 2.5$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\mu = 0.3$. For a **stiffened element under uniform compression**, $k = 4.0$. $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \mu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$$ Substituting: $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}{12 \cdot (1 - 0.3^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2.5}{180}\right)^2$$ $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}{10.92} \cdot (0.01389)^2$$ $$f_{cr} = 723{,}047 \cdot 0.0001929 = 139.5 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\boxed{f_{cr} \approx 139.5 \text{ MPa}}$$ #### Solution to (b): **Slenderness parameter:** $$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{250}{139.5}} = \sqrt{1.792} = 1.339$$ Since $\lambda = 1.339 > 0.673$, the web **undergoes local buckling** and is **not fully effective**. **Reduction factor (Winter's formula):** $$\rho = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} - \dfrac{0.22}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{1}{1.339} - \dfrac{0.22}{(1.339)^2}$$ $$\rho = 0.7468 - \dfrac{0.22}{1.7930} = 0.7468 - 0.1227 = 0.6241$$ **Effective width:** $$b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b = 0.6241 \cdot 180 = 112.3 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{b_{eff} \approx 112.3 \text{ mm, with 67.7 mm ineffective}}$$ **Comment:** The web carries only **62.4%** of its geometric width effectively. The middle ~38% of the web is ineffective in uniform compression. To make it fully effective, either reduce $b/t$ (thicker web or smaller web depth) or add intermediate stiffeners. ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 4 Model Question]** > **A lipped channel section is used as a compression member. The top flange has flat width 120 mm, thickness 1.6 mm, with two intermediate stiffeners creating three equal sub-elements. Yield stress $f_y = 350$ MPa. Calculate:** > **(a) The slenderness parameter $\lambda$ for each sub-element. (7 marks)** > **(b) The total effective width of the multiple stiffened flange and the minimum required moment of inertia $I_s$ of each intermediate stiffener. (7 marks)** #### Solution to (a): **Given:** Sub-element width $b_s = 120/3 = 40$ mm, $t = 1.6$ mm, $f_y = 350$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\mu = 0.3$. **$b/t$ ratio:** $$\dfrac{b_s}{t} = \dfrac{40}{1.6} = 25$$ **$f_{cr}$ (stiffened sub-element, $k = 4.0$):** $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{4.0 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}{10.92} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1.6}{40}\right)^2$$ $$f_{cr} = 723{,}047 \cdot (0.04)^2 = 723{,}047 \cdot 0.0016 = 1156.9 \text{ MPa}$$ **Slenderness parameter:** $$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{350}{1156.9}} = \sqrt{0.3026} = 0.550$$ $$\boxed{\lambda \approx 0.550}$$ Since $\lambda = 0.550 < 0.673$, each sub-element is **fully effective** — no reduction needed. #### Solution to (b): **Total effective width:** $$b_{eff,total} = b = 120 \text{ mm (since } \lambda < 0.673\text{)}$$ **Minimum required stiffener moment of inertia:** Since $\lambda < 0.673$, by IS 801, **no minimum $I_s$ is required**: $$I_{s,min} = 0$$ However, **for practical edge-stiffened lipped channels**, the lip must satisfy: $$I_{s,min} = 1.83 \cdot t^4 \cdot \left[\dfrac{(b/t)}{60} - 0.286\right] \geq 9.2 \cdot t^4$$ $$\dfrac{b}{t} = \dfrac{120}{1.6} = 75$$ $$I_{s,min} = 1.83 \cdot (1.6)^4 \cdot \left[\dfrac{75}{60} - 0.286\right]$$ $$I_{s,min} = 1.83 \cdot 6.5536 \cdot [1.25 - 0.286] = 1.83 \cdot 6.5536 \cdot 0.964$$ $$I_{s,min} = 11.56 \text{ mm}^4$$ **Total effective width** = **120 mm** (fully effective). $$\boxed{b_{eff,total} = 120 \text{ mm}, \quad I_{s,min} \approx 11.56 \text{ mm}^4}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting Poisson's ratio term $(1-\mu^2)$** — many students compute $f_{cr}$ as $k\pi^2 E/12 \cdot (t/b)^2$ without the $(1-\mu^2)$ denominator. This causes **~5–8% error** and the examiner will deduct marks. **Always include** $12(1-\mu^2) = 12(0.91) = 10.92$. > > 2. **Wrong $k$-value selection** — using $k = 4.0$ for an **unstiffened** element (should be $0.425$) is a **fatal error** that doubles the effective width incorrectly. **Identify the element type FIRST.** > > 3. **Mixing up formula for $\rho$** — students often write $\rho = 1 - 0.22/\lambda$ instead of $\rho = 1/\lambda - 0.22/\lambda^2$. The **denominator is $\lambda$, not $1$**. This single mistake changes $\rho$ by 50%+. > > 4. **Skipping the $\lambda > 0.673$ check** — if you blindly apply Winter's formula when $\lambda < 0.673$, you get a **negative or absurdly large** $\rho$ value. The cut-off exists precisely to avoid this. > > 5. **Effective width location** — for a stiffened element, the **ineffective width is centered** at the plate middle. For an unstiffened element, the **ineffective width is on the free edge side**. Examiners explicitly check this. (2 marks) > > 6. **Stiffener inadequacy clause** — failing to mention that if $I_s < I_{s,min}$, the multiple-stiffened element is **re-classified as unstiffened** ($k = 0.425$) — a critical IS 801 requirement worth 2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Fire for KTU Exam):** - [x] **Stiffened element** = both edges supported → $k = 4.0$ (uniform compression). - [x] **Unstiffened element** = one edge free → $k = 0.425$ (uniform compression). - [x] **Multiple stiffened element** = stiffeners divide the plate into sub-elements; each sub-element treated as stiffened **if** stiffener has adequate $I_s$ and $A_s$. - [x] **Effective width formula:** $b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$. - [x] **Slenderness parameter:** $\lambda = \sqrt{f_y / f_{cr}}$. - [x] **Elastic local buckling stress:** $f_{cr} = k\pi^2 E / [12(1-\mu^2)] \cdot (t/b)^2$. - [x] **Winter's formula** (for $\lambda \geq 0.673$): $\rho = 1/\lambda - 0.22/\lambda^2$. - [x] **Cut-off slenderness:** $\lambda_p = 0.673$ — below this, $\rho = 1.0$. - [x] **Key constants:** $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\mu = 0.3$, $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$, $12(1-0.3^2) = 10.92$. - [x] **For lipped channel flange** (edge-stiffened): $k$ taken as $0.85$ if stiffener is adequate, else $0.425$. - [x] **Post-buckling** is the key — element doesn't fail at $f_{cr}$; redistribution gives reserve strength. - [x] **Ineffective width location** = middle for stiffened; free-edge side for unstiffened. - [x] **Effective area** $A_{eff}$ is used to compute design axial strength $P_d = A_{eff} \cdot f_y / \gamma_m$. - [x] **IS 801 / IS 811** is the governing code for cold-formed steel design in India. - [x] **Always check stiffener adequacy** for multiple stiffened elements — otherwise classify as unstiffened. - [x] **The effective width is located adjacent to the supported edges** in equal portions for stiffened elements (i.e., $b_{eff}/2$ on each side).

topics.title: Tension Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tension Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tension Member** is a structural steel element that is primarily subjected to **axial tensile forces** along its longitudinal axis, inducing uniform tensile stress across its cross-section. In the framework of **Limit State Method of Design** as codified in **IS 800:2007**, tension members are designed to satisfy two limit states: the **Limit State of Strength (Ultimate Tensile Strength)** and the **Limit State of Serviceability (Deflection and Slenderness)**.

Mathematically, a tension member is characterized by an axial force $T$ applied at its centroidal axis, generating a uniform normal stress distribution:

$$\sigma = \frac{T}{A_g}$$

where $A_g$ is the **gross cross-sectional area** of the member. The member is said to be in pure tension when the load passes through the **centroid of the section**, ensuring uniform stress distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per the PECET864 syllabus under Module 4, students must master the design philosophy, net section computations, shear lag phenomenon, block shear mechanics, and the application of **IS 800:2007 clauses (Section 6 — Design of Tension Members)**.

## 1.2 Common Examples of Tension Members in Practice

| Sl. No. | Tension Member Type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tie rods** | Truss bottom chords, bracing systems |
| 2 | **Single/Double angles** | Lattice girders, towers, roof trusses |
| 3 | **Channels (C-sections)** | Crane girders, truss verticals |
| 4 | **I-sections and H-sections** | Heavy industrial trusses, suspension bridges |
| 5 | **Flat bars / Plates** | Gusset plates, sag rods, hangers |
| 6 | **Wire ropes / Cables** | Suspension systems, cable-stayed bridges |
| 7 | **Built-up sections** | Heavy bracing, long-span trusses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Code:** All numerical constants, partial safety factors, and design strengths in this document follow **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel — Code of Practice, Third Revision)**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are holding a piece of **chocolate-bar-shaped rubber** with both hands and pulling it apart. The force you apply tries to **stretch** the rubber uniformly. Every tiny cross-section of the rubber is being pulled equally — this is exactly what happens in a tension member!

Now, imagine you **poke a hole** through the middle of the rubber with a pin. The cross-section where the hole exists is **weaker** because there is less material to carry the load. This is precisely the **net section** concept in steel design. Furthermore, when you pull the rubber, the part of the rubber *around* the hole does not stretch as efficiently as the parts away from the hole — this is the **shear lag** phenomenon.

In real engineering:
- **The chord of a roof truss** is a tension member (pulled at the supports).
- **The diagonal bracing in a building** acts in tension to resist lateral wind loads.
- **The cable in a suspension bridge** is the ultimate tension member carrying thousands of kilonewtons.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Physical Insight:** Unlike a compression member (which can **buckle** suddenly), a tension member usually gives visible warning (necking, elongation) before failure. This makes it a **ductile, forgiving** structural element — but its connection design is where most failures actually occur!

## 1.4 Fundamental Design Requirements (IS 800:2007 Compliance)

A properly designed tension member must satisfy the following criteria:

1. **Strength Criterion:** The design tensile force $T_d$ must not exceed the design strength $T_{dg}$ computed using the gross section yielding and rupture of net section criteria.
2. **Serviceability Criterion:** The slenderness ratio $L/r$ must remain within prescribed limits to prevent inadvertent sag, vibration, or self-weight-induced bending.
3. **Connection Integrity:** The end connections (bolted/welded) must be capable of developing the full tensile capacity of the member without premature failure.
4. **Block Shear Safety:** The tearing-out of a block of material at the connection must be prevented.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Philosophy (Limit State Method):**
> $$\text{Design Action} \leq \text{Design Capacity}$$
> $$\gamma_m \cdot T_u \leq R_d$$
> where $\gamma_m$ is the **partial safety factor for material** ($\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ for yielding, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ for ultimate stress).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Tension Members

Tension members are classified based on **geometry**, **connection type**, and **cross-sectional behavior**.

### A. Based on Cross-Section Geometry
- **Solid Sections:** Round bars, flat bars, rectangular bars.
- **Built-up Sections:** Star angles, cruciform sections, laced/battened built-up members.
- **Open Sections:** Single angles, double angles, channels, I-sections.
- **Hollow Sections:** Circular Hollow Sections (CHS), Rectangular Hollow Sections (RHS).

### B. Based on Stiffness Against Bending
- **Stiff Tension Members:** L/r ≤ 400 (those designed to resist small bending and self-weight sag).
- **Flexible Tension Members:** Cables and wire ropes (carrying only axial tension).

### C. Based on Connection Methodology
- **Bolted Tension Members:** Connected using bearing-type or friction-type bolts.
- **Welded Tension Members:** Connected using fillet welds or butt welds.

## 2.2 Modes of Failure of Tension Members

A tension member can fail in the following distinct modes:

1. **Excessive yielding of the gross section** — uniform yielding across the entire cross-section.
2. **Rupture of the net section** — fracture at the weakest plane (where holes are located).
3. **Block shear failure** — a combination of shear yielding/rupture along a perimeter and tensile rupture across a perpendicular plane, causing a "block" of material to tear out.
4. **Failure of the connection** — bolt shear, bolt bearing, weld fracture, or end-plate bending.
5. **Excessive deformation** — exceeding serviceability limits due to high slenderness.

## 2.3 Net Sectional Area — Detailed Logic Steps

The **net sectional area ($A_n$)** is the gross area minus the area removed by bolt holes or other openings. For a section with multiple holes staggered along the load direction, the **critical net section** is found by trial.

### Step 1: Gross Area Calculation
$$A_g = \text{Total geometric area of the cross-section}$$

For a standard equal-leg angle of leg width $b$ and thickness $t$:
$$A_g = 2bt - t^2 = t(2b - t)$$
(Subtracting the corner overlap area $t^2$ for cleaner computation; some textbooks ignore it for simplicity, but IS 800:2007 is precise.)

### Step 2: Net Area (Single Path)
$$A_n = A_g - n \cdot d_h \cdot t$$

where:
- $n$ = number of bolt holes in the failure path
- $d_h$ = diameter of the bolt hole = nominal bolt diameter $d + \text{clearance}$ (typically **+2 mm** for $d \leq 24$ mm, **+3 mm** for $d > 24$ mm)
- $t$ = thickness of the plate/element

### Step 3: Staggered Holes — The Pitch Correction
When bolt holes are staggered (zig-zag), the failure path may not be a straight transverse line. The **s-path** failure passes through holes diagonally, and we must add a correction term:

$$A_n = A_g - \sum d_h \cdot t + \sum \frac{p^2}{4g} \cdot t$$

where:
- $p$ = **pitch** (longitudinal spacing between consecutive staggered holes)
- $g$ = **gauge** (transverse spacing between staggered holes)
- $\frac{p^2}{4g}$ = correction per staggered hole

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Net Section Rule:** The **net area must be checked for ALL possible failure paths** through the bolt group. The path with the **least net area** governs the design.

## 2.4 Effective Net Area — The Shear Lag Phenomenon

When a tension member is connected through **only part of its cross-section** (e.g., one leg of an angle connected by bolts, or a channel connected only through its web), the stress distribution across the section is **non-uniform**. The connected portion reaches the yield stress first, while the unconnected portion lags behind. This is the **shear lag effect**.

To account for this, IS 800:2007 introduces the **Effective Net Area ($A_e$)**:

$$A_e = \alpha \cdot A_n$$

where $\alpha$ is the **shear lag factor** (also called the connection efficiency factor), which depends on the connection type.

### Shear Lag Factor Table (Clause 6.3.1, IS 800:2007)

| Sl. No. | Connection Type | $\alpha$ (Shear Lag Factor) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Welded connection (all elements connected) | 1.00 |
| 2 | Welded connection (transverse welds only) | 0.85 |
| 3 | Bolted connection (all elements connected) | 0.80 |
| 4 | Bolted connection (only one leg/flange connected) | 0.60 |
| 5 | Tie plates / gusset connections (transverse) | 0.70 |
| 6 | End connection with $\geq 2$ fasteners (angles) | 0.70 |
| 7 | End connection with 1 fastener (angles) | 0.60 |
| 8 | Lug angle connection | 0.80 |

## 2.5 Design Strength of Tension Members (Clause 6.1, IS 800:2007)

The design strength of a tension member is the **minimum** of the following:

### 1. Design Strength Due to Yielding of Gross Section
$$T_{dg} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

where:
- $f_y$ = **yield stress of steel** (e.g., **250 MPa for Fe 410**, **350 MPa for Fe 490**)
- $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (partial safety factor against yielding)

### 2. Design Strength Due to Rupture of Critical Section
$$T_{dn} = \frac{\alpha \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = shear lag factor
- $A_n$ = net effective area
- $f_u$ = **ultimate tensile stress** of steel
- $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ (partial safety factor against ultimate stress)

### 3. Design Strength Due to Block Shear
$$T_{db} = \frac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

or, whichever governs, see Section 2.6 below.

## 2.6 Block Shear Failure — Detailed Mechanics

**Block shear failure** occurs when a block of material at the connection is torn out, involving:
- **Shear yielding/rupture** along a longitudinal shear plane (area $A_{vg}$ or $A_{vn}$).
- **Tensile yielding/rupture** along a transverse tension plane (area $A_{tg}$ or $A_{tn}$).

### IS 800:2007 Block Shear Formulas (Clause 6.4.1)

The block shear strength is the **smaller** of:

$$\text{Mode 1 (Shear Yielding + Tension Rupture):} \quad T_{db1} = \frac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

$$\text{Mode 2 (Shear Rupture + Tension Yielding):} \quad T_{db2} = \frac{A_{vn} \cdot f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}} + \frac{A_{tg} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

where:
- $A_{vg}$ = gross shear area
- $A_{vn}$ = net shear area
- $A_{tg}$ = gross tension area
- $A_{tn}$ = net tension area
- $f_y$ = yield stress, $f_u$ = ultimate stress
- $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$

## 2.7 Slenderness Ratio Limits (Clause 3.8, IS 800:2007)

To control excessive vibration, sag, and self-weight-induced bending:

| Sl. No. | Member Type | Maximum L/r |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tension members in general | 350 |
| 2 | Tension members carrying dynamic loads | 250 |
| 3 | Cables (wire ropes) carrying tension | Not applicable |
| 4 | Pre-tensioned members | No specific limit |

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are guaranteed to appear in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) Module 4 questions.**

| Sl. No. | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gross area (angle) | $A_g = t(2b - t)$ | mm² |
| 2 | Net area (single line) | $A_n = A_g - n \cdot d_h \cdot t$ | mm² |
| 3 | Net area (staggered) | $A_n = A_g - \sum d_h t + \sum \frac{p^2}{4g} t$ | mm² |
| 4 | Effective net area | $A_e = \alpha \cdot A_n$ | mm² |
| 5 | Design strength (gross yielding) | $T_{dg} = A_g f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | N |
| 6 | Design strength (net rupture) | $T_{dn} = 0.9 \cdot A_n \cdot f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ (for plates) | N |
| 7 | Design strength (with $\alpha$) | $T_{dn} = \alpha A_n f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ | N |
| 8 | Block shear (Mode 1) | $T_{db1} = A_{vg} f_y / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0}) + A_{tn} f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ | N |
| 9 | Block shear (Mode 2) | $T_{db2} = A_{vn} f_u / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m1}) + A_{tg} f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | N |
| 10 | Bolt hole diameter | $d_h = d_{bolt} + \text{clearance}$ | mm |
| 11 | Slenderness ratio | $\lambda = L_{eff} / r_{min}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Design capacity | $T_d = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn}, T_{db})$ | N |

> [!TIP]
> **Why 0.9 factor in the design of plates per IS 800?**
> The factor 0.9 (Clause 6.3.1) is applied to $A_n$ for plates to account for **unequal distribution of stress** across the net section. The full equation is: $T_{dn} = 0.9 \cdot A_n \cdot f_u / \gamma_{m1} \leq T_{dg}$.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tension members are ubiquitous in:
- **Roof trusses** (bottom chord, diagonals in tension).
- **Transmission towers** (leg members, cross-arms in tension).
- **Cable-stayed bridges** (stay cables carry pure tension).
- **Industrial cranes** (tie rods in jib systems).
- **Earthquake-resistant structures** (diagonal bracing — designed to yield in tension and dissipate energy).
- **Railway/roadway bridges** (suspension hangers, anchor ties).
- **Offshore platforms** (mooring lines, riser tensioners).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fun Fact:** The longest tension members in the world are the **stay cables of the Millau Viaduct (France)** and the **Akashi Kaikyō Bridge (Japan)** — the latter has main span suspension cables carrying approximately **300,000 kN** of tension each!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Sectional Area for Staggered Bolt Holes

### Given:
A flat plate of width $b$ and thickness $t$ subjected to axial tension $T$, with bolt holes arranged in a staggered (zig-zag) pattern as shown in the Mermaid diagram (see Section 4).

### Derivation Goal:
To derive the expression for the **net sectional area** considering both **direct** and **staggered (s-path)** failure modes.

### Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Consider a section cut along a straight transverse line passing through two holes (say, holes 1 and 2):

$$A_{n,\text{straight}} = (b - 2 \cdot d_h) \cdot t$$

This is the net area when failure occurs along a straight perpendicular path.

**Step 2:** Consider a section cut along a staggered path (say, holes 1, 3, 2 in a zig-zag pattern). The total material removed includes:
- Three bolt holes: total hole area = $3 \cdot d_h \cdot t$
- BUT the path is **longer** than the straight path by horizontal distance $p$ and vertical distance $g$.

The actual length of the staggered path is:
$$L_s = \sqrt{p^2 + g^2}$$

The **stagger** adds material back into the net area because the path traverses a longer geometric distance. The incremental material contribution per staggered hole is:

$$\Delta A = t \cdot \left[ \sqrt{p^2 + g^2} - (g + \text{some baseline}) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Using the simplification (Codified in IS 800:2007), when $\sqrt{p^2 + g^2} \geq g$:

$$\Delta A \approx \frac{p^2}{4g} \cdot t \quad \text{(for one stagger)}$$

This is the famous **"p²/4g" rule** for net area calculation.

**Step 4:** Combining both contributions, the net area along the staggered path is:

$$A_{n,\text{staggered}} = A_g - \sum_{i=1}^{n_h} (d_h \cdot t) + \sum_{j=1}^{n_s} \left( \frac{p_j^2}{4 g_j} \cdot t \right)$$

where $n_h$ = total holes in path, $n_s$ = number of staggers.

**Step 5:** The **critical net section** is the path that gives the **minimum** $A_n$:

$$A_{n,\text{critical}} = \min_{k} \left[ A_g - \sum d_{h,k} t + \sum \frac{p_{k}^2}{4g_{k}} t \right]$$

**Step 6:** Substituting into the design strength equation:

$$T_{dn} = \frac{\alpha \cdot A_{n,\text{critical}} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Convention:** The pitch $p$ and gauge $g$ are measured between **centers** of consecutive staggered holes along the path of failure.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Tension Member Design

### Problem Statement:
A single unequal angle tension member $100 \times 75 \times 8$ mm (longer leg connected) carries an axial tensile factored load of $T_u = 350$ kN. The member is connected to a gusset plate using **4 numbers of 16 mm diameter bolts** of grade 4.6, arranged in a single row along the longer leg. Steel is of grade **Fe 410** ($f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa). Verify the adequacy of the section.

### Given Data:
- Section: $100 \times 75 \times 8$ mm unequal angle
- Load: $T_u = 350$ kN
- Bolt diameter: $d = 16$ mm
- Steel: Fe 410 ($f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa)
- $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$
- Longer leg (100 mm) is connected.

### Step 1: Properties of the Section (from Steel Tables)
- Gross area: $A_g = 13.5$ cm² = $1350$ mm²
- Centroid: $C_x = 32.4$ mm (from back of shorter leg), $C_y = 16.5$ mm
- Radius of gyration: $r_{min} = r_{vv} = 15.7$ mm (about weak axis)

### Step 2: Bolt Hole Diameter
$$d_h = d + \text{clearance} = 16 + 2 = 18 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3: Net Area (Single Line of 4 Bolts in One Row)
Since all 4 bolts are in a single line:
$$A_n = A_g - 4 \cdot d_h \cdot t = 1350 - 4 \cdot 18 \cdot 8$$
$$A_n = 1350 - 576 = 774 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 4: Shear Lag Factor
For an unequal angle connected through its **longer leg** with multiple bolts (≥ 2 fasteners):
$$\alpha = 0.80$$

### Step 5: Design Strength Due to Yielding of Gross Section
$$T_{dg} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{1350 \cdot 250}{1.10}$$
$$T_{dg} = \frac{337500}{1.10} = 306818 \text{ N} = 306.82 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 6: Design Strength Due to Rupture of Net Section
$$T_{dn} = \frac{\alpha \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}} = \frac{0.80 \cdot 774 \cdot 410}{1.25}$$
$$T_{dn} = \frac{253872}{1.25} = 203098 \text{ N} = 203.10 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 7: Design Strength (Governing Value)
$$T_d = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn}) = \min(306.82, 203.10) = 203.10 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 8: Check Against Applied Load
$$T_u = 350 \text{ kN} \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_d = 203.10 \text{ kN}$$

Since $T_u > T_d$, the section is **NOT ADEQUATE**.

**Required minimum $A_n$:**
$$A_{n,\text{req}} = \frac{T_u \cdot \gamma_{m1}}{\alpha \cdot f_u} = \frac{350 \times 10^3 \cdot 1.25}{0.80 \cdot 410}$$
$$A_{n,\text{req}} = \frac{437500}{328} = 1333.84 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Required gross area (from yielding):**
$$A_{g,\text{req}} = \frac{T_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_y} = \frac{350 \times 10^3 \cdot 1.10}{250} = 1540 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 9: Conclusion
The section $100 \times 75 \times 8$ mm is **inadequate**; the designer must select a **larger angle** or use a **double angle** section. For instance, a **double angle $90 \times 90 \times 10$ mm** back-to-back (long legs connected to gusset) should be tried.

## 3.3 Derivation: Block Shear Capacity (Tear-Out)

### Setup:
A gusset connection has a plate of thickness $t_p$ connected to a tension member with bolts arranged such that the failure surface forms a rectangular "block" of dimensions $L_v$ (shear length) and $L_t$ (tension length).

### Block Shear Resistance:

**Step 1:** Identify the **shear plane area** (parallel to load):
$$A_{vg} = L_v \cdot t_p \quad \text{(gross shear area)}$$
$$A_{vn} = (L_v - n_s \cdot d_h) \cdot t_p \quad \text{(net shear area)}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the **tension plane area** (perpendicular to load):
$$A_{tg} = L_t \cdot t_p \quad \text{(gross tension area)}$$
$$A_{tn} = (L_t - n_t \cdot d_h) \cdot t_p \quad \text{(net tension area)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Mode 1 failure (shear yielding + tension rupture):

$$T_{db1} = \frac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

Derivation of the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ factor:
The shear yield stress is related to tensile yield by **von Mises yield criterion**:
$$f_{vy} = \frac{f_y}{\sqrt{3}}$$
This arises from the **distortion energy theory** in 3D stress states.

**Step 4:** Apply Mode 2 failure (shear rupture + tension yielding):

$$T_{db2} = \frac{A_{vn} \cdot f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}} + \frac{A_{tg} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

**Step 5:** The block shear design strength is the **minimum** of the two modes:

$$T_{db} = \min(T_{db1}, T_{db2})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Clarification (IS 800:2007, Clause 6.4.1):**
> When the net shear area $A_{vn} \leq 0.3 A_{vg}$, use Mode 2 equation. Otherwise, Mode 1 is conservative. Always check both and select the smaller value.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Block Shear

### Problem:
A 12 mm thick gusset plate is connected to a tension member with **3 bolts in a row** along the load direction and **2 bolts in a column** perpendicular to the load. Bolt diameter = 20 mm, edge distance = 40 mm, pitch = 50 mm, gauge = 60 mm. Steel is Fe 410. Calculate the block shear strength.

### Given:
- $t_p = 12$ mm
- Bolt diameter = 20 mm, $d_h = 22$ mm
- Shear length $L_v = 2 \cdot \text{edge} + 1 \cdot \text{pitch} = 40 + 50 + 40 = 130$ mm (for 3 bolts: 2 edges + 1 pitch)
  - Actually, for 3 bolts in a row: $L_v = 2 \cdot e + 2p$ where $e$ = edge distance, $p$ = pitch
  - $L_v = 2(40) + 2(50) = 80 + 100 = 180$ mm
- Tension length $L_t = 60$ mm (gauge)
- 2 bolts in tension line: $n_t = 2$
- 3 bolts in shear line: $n_s = 3$

### Calculation:

**Step 1: Areas**
$$A_{vg} = 2 \cdot L_v \cdot t_p = 2 \cdot 180 \cdot 12 = 4320 \text{ mm}^2$$

(Gusset has 2 shear planes — one on each side of the tension block)

$$A_{vn} = A_{vg} - n_s \cdot d_h \cdot t_p = 4320 - 3 \cdot 22 \cdot 12 = 4320 - 792 = 3528 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{tg} = L_t \cdot t_p = 60 \cdot 12 = 720 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{tn} = A_{tg} - n_t \cdot d_h \cdot t_p = 720 - 2 \cdot 22 \cdot 12 = 720 - 528 = 192 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Mode 1 Strength**
$$T_{db1} = \frac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$
$$T_{db1} = \frac{4320 \cdot 250}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.10} + \frac{192 \cdot 410}{1.25}$$
$$T_{db1} = \frac{1080000}{1.905} + \frac{78720}{1.25}$$
$$T_{db1} = 566929 + 62976 = 629905 \text{ N} = 629.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Mode 2 Strength**
$$T_{db2} = \frac{A_{vn} \cdot f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}} + \frac{A_{tg} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$
$$T_{db2} = \frac{3528 \cdot 410}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} + \frac{720 \cdot 250}{1.10}$$
$$T_{db2} = \frac{1446480}{2.165} + \frac{180000}{1.10}$$
$$T_{db2} = 668181 + 163636 = 831817 \text{ N} = 831.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Block Shear Design Strength**
$$T_{db} = \min(629.9, 831.8) = 629.9 \text{ kN}$$

This is the **governing block shear strength** of the connection.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU 2024 scheme ESE, students must present **both** Mode 1 and Mode 2 calculations and clearly state which mode governs. Skipping this step results in a 1-2 mark penalty.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Bolted Tension Plate with Holes"] --> Q1{"Are Holes Staggered ?"}
    Q1 -->|No| P1["Path 1: Straight Line Failure"]

    P1 --> F1["An = Ag minus n times dh times t"]

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block Shear Failure Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bolted Gusset Connection"] --> B["Identify Failure Block"]
    B --> B1["Shear Planes: 2 longitudinal surfaces"]
    B --> B2["Tension Planes: 1 or 2 transverse surfaces"]

    B1 --> C1["Avg = Shear Gross Area = Lv times t"]
    B1 --> C2["Avn = Shear Net Area = Avg minus ns times dh times t"]
    B2 --> C3["Atg = Tension Gross Area = Lt times t"]
    B2 --> C4["Atn = Tension Net Area = Atg minus nt times dh times t"]

    C1 --> M1["Mode 1: Shear Yielding plus Tension Rupture"]
    C2 --> M2["Mode 2: Shear Rupture plus Tension Yielding"]
    C3 --> M1
    C3 --> M2
    C4 --> M1
    C4 --> M2

    M1 --> Eq1["Tdb1 = Avg fy by sqrt 3 gamma m0 PLUS Atn fu by gamma m1"]
    M2 --> Eq2["Tdb2 = Avn fu by sqrt 3 gamma m1 PLUS Atg fy by gamma m0"]

    Eq1 --> Final["Tdb = min Tdb1 and Tdb2"]
    Eq2 --> Final
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    S2a --> Step3["STEP 3: NET AREA COMPUTATION"]
    S2b --> Step3
    Step3 --> S3a["Check all possible failure paths"]
    Step3 --> S3b["Apply p squared by 4g rule for staggers"]
    Step3 --> S3c["Identify An critical"]

    S3b --> Step4
    S3c --> Step4

    S5a --> Step6["STEP 6: DESIGN STRENGTH"]

```
