# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual Definition)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 4]**
**Define a tension member. List any four examples of tension members used in steel structures.**

**Model Answer:**
A tension member is a structural element that is subjected to axial tensile forces, with the load applied along its centroidal axis, producing uniform tensile stress across the cross-section. The stress is given by $\sigma = T/A_g$.

Examples of tension members:
1. **Tie rods** used in roof trusses and bracing systems.
2. **Cables** in suspension and cable-stayed bridges.
3. **Bottom chord members** of trusses (under gravity loads).
4. **Diagonal bracing** in industrial buildings and towers.

### Question 2 (Conceptual on Shear Lag)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Module 4]**
**What is shear lag? State the values of the shear lag factor for: (i) welded connection, and (ii) bolted connection with all elements connected.**

**Model Answer:**
**Shear lag** is the phenomenon in which the stress distribution across the cross-section of a tension member becomes non-uniform when the member is connected to a gusset plate through only a part of its cross-section. The connected portion reaches yield stress first, while the unconnected portion lags behind, leading to reduced efficiency.

**Shear lag factors ($\alpha$) per IS 800:2007 (Clause 6.3.1):**
- (i) Welded connection (all elements connected): $\alpha = 1.00$
- (ii) Bolted connection (all elements connected): $\alpha = 0.80$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4, Question A]**

**Question:** A single unequal angle tension member $150 \times 115 \times 12$ mm is connected to a 14 mm thick gusset plate through its **longer leg** using **6 numbers of 20 mm diameter bolts of grade 4.6** arranged in **two rows of 3 bolts each**. The pitch is 60 mm and the gauge is 90 mm. The member carries a factored tensile load of $T_u = 600$ kN. Steel grade is **Fe 410** ($f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa). Verify the adequacy of the section considering all limit states per IS 800:2007.

#### Sub-Question (a) — Net Section and Rupture Strength (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Section: $150 \times 115 \times 12$ mm unequal angle
- From steel tables: $A_g = 30.4$ cm² = **3040 mm²**
- $C_x = 38.3$ mm, $C_y = 21.4$ mm
- $r_{min} = r_{vv} = 24.4$ mm
- Bolt diameter: $d = 20$ mm
- Hole diameter: $d_h = 20 + 2 = 22$ mm
- Steel: Fe 410 ($f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa)

**Step 1: Possible Failure Paths**

Two paths to consider:
- **Path I (Straight):** Cut across the section through 3 bolts in one line.
- **Path II (Staggered):** Zig-zag through all 6 bolts.

**Step 2: Path I — Straight Net Area**
$$A_{n1} = A_g - 3 \cdot d_h \cdot t = 3040 - 3 \cdot 22 \cdot 12$$
$$A_{n1} = 3040 - 792 = 2248 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Path II — Staggered Net Area**
Going through 3 holes in one row + 3 holes in the other row, with staggers of pitch $p = 60$ mm and gauge $g = 90$ mm.

$$A_{n2} = A_g - 6 \cdot d_h \cdot t + 2 \cdot \frac{p^2}{4g} \cdot t$$
$$A_{n2} = 3040 - 6 \cdot 22 \cdot 12 + 2 \cdot \frac{60^2}{4 \cdot 90} \cdot 12$$
$$A_{n2} = 3040 - 1584 + 2 \cdot \frac{3600}{360} \cdot 12$$
$$A_{n2} = 3040 - 1584 + 2 \cdot 10 \cdot 12$$
$$A_{n2} = 3040 - 1584 + 240 = 1696 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Critical Net Area**
$$A_n = \min(A_{n1}, A_{n2}) = \min(2248, 1696) = 1696 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Shear Lag Factor**
For an unequal angle connected through its **longer leg** with $\geq 2$ bolts:
$$\alpha = 0.80$$

**Step 6: Design Strength Due to Yielding of Gross Section**
$$T_{dg} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{3040 \cdot 250}{1.10} = 690909 \text{ N} = 690.91 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Design Strength Due to Rupture of Net Section**
$$T_{dn} = \frac{\alpha \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}} = \frac{0.80 \cdot 1696 \cdot 410}{1.25}$$
$$T_{dn} = \frac{556288}{1.25} = 445030 \text{ N} = 445.03 \text{ kN}$$

**Result:** $T_d = \min(690.91, 445.03) = 445.03$ kN

#### Sub-Question (b) — Slenderness Check and Final Verdict (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Slenderness Ratio**
Assuming effective length $L_{eff} = 3.0$ m = 3000 mm (typical for a truss member):
$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}} = \frac{3000}{24.4} = 122.95$$

**Step 2: Slenderness Check (IS 800:2007, Clause 3.8)**
For a tension member: $\lambda_{max} = 350$

Since $122.95 < 350$, the slenderness is **acceptable**.

**Step 3: Design Strength Verdict**
$$T_u = 600 \text{ kN} \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_d = 445.03 \text{ kN}$$

Since $T_u > T_d$, the section is **NOT ADEQUATE**.

**Step 4: Required Area**
$$A_{n,\text{req}} = \frac{T_u \cdot \gamma_{m1}}{\alpha \cdot f_u} = \frac{600 \times 10^3 \cdot 1.25}{0.80 \cdot 410} = 2286.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{g,\text{req}} = \frac{T_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_y} = \frac{600 \times 10^3 \cdot 1.10}{250} = 2640 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Recommendation**
- Try a **double unequal angle section** (e.g., 2 × $100 \times 75 \times 10$ mm).
- Or a **heavier single angle** (e.g., $200 \times 150 \times 15$ mm).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Costing 2-3 Marks):**
> 1. **Don't forget to check BOTH straight and staggered paths** — students often check only one path. The minimum governs.
> 2. **Hole clearance:** For $d \leq 24$ mm, use $d_h = d + 2$ mm; for $d > 24$ mm, use $d_h = d + 3$ mm. Mixing these is a common error.
> 3. **Shear lag factor:** For unequal angles connected through the **shorter** leg, $\alpha$ is different (typically **0.70 for end connection**). Read the connection detail carefully.
> 4. **Partial safety factors:** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ for yielding, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ for ultimate stress — swapping them leads to incorrect results.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4, Question B]**

**Question:** A single equal angle tension member $80 \times 80 \times 10$ mm is connected to a **10 mm thick gusset plate** through **one leg** using **3 numbers of 18 mm diameter bolts** arranged in a single line. The pitch is 50 mm, edge distance is 35 mm. The member carries a **factored tensile load** of $T_u = 200$ kN. Steel is **Fe 410** ($f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa). Design and verify the connection for **block shear** failure as per IS 800:2007.

#### Sub-Question (a) — Block Shear Geometry and Areas (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Section: $80 \times 80 \times 10$ mm
- $A_g = 15.1$ cm² = 1510 mm²
- Gusset thickness: $t_g = 10$ mm
- Bolt diameter: $d = 18$ mm
- Hole diameter: $d_h = 18 + 2 = 20$ mm
- 3 bolts in a single line: $n_s = 3$ (on shear plane)
- For 3 bolts in a line, the tension line has 2 bolts: $n_t = 2$
- Shear length: $L_v = 2 \cdot e + 2p = 2(35) + 2(50) = 170$ mm
- Tension length: $L_t = 40$ mm (perpendicular gauge to the edge)

**Step 1: Shear Plane Areas**
- Gross shear area: $A_{vg} = 2 \cdot L_v \cdot t_g = 2 \cdot 170 \cdot 10 = 3400$ mm²
- Net shear area: $A_{vn} = A_{vg} - n_s \cdot d_h \cdot t_g = 3400 - 3 \cdot 20 \cdot 10 = 2800$ mm²

**Step 2: Tension Plane Areas**
- Gross tension area: $A_{tg} = L_t \cdot t_g = 40 \cdot 10 = 400$ mm²
- Net tension area: $A_{tn} = A_{tg} - n_t \cdot d_h \cdot t_g = 400 - 2 \cdot 20 \cdot 10 = 0$ mm²

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Issue:** $A_{tn} = 0$ means **tension failure is by net section collapse**. Per IS 800, the formula still applies but indicates that the tension net area is fully consumed by bolt holes.

**Step 3: Mode 1 Strength (Shear Yielding + Tension Rupture)**
$$T_{db1} = \frac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$
$$T_{db1} = \frac{3400 \cdot 250}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.10} + \frac{0 \cdot 410}{1.25}$$
$$T_{db1} = \frac{850000}{1.905} + 0 = 446194 \text{ N} = 446.19 \text{ kN}$$

#### Sub-Question (b) — Mode 2 Calculation and Final Verdict (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Mode 2 Strength (Shear Rupture + Tension Yielding)**
$$T_{db2} = \frac{A_{vn} \cdot f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}} + \frac{A_{tg} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$
$$T_{db2} = \frac{2800 \cdot 410}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} + \frac{400 \cdot 250}{1.10}$$
$$T_{db2} = \frac{1148000}{2.165} + \frac{100000}{1.10}$$
$$T_{db2} = 530255 + 90909 = 621164 \text{ N} = 621.16 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Governing Block Shear Strength**
$$T_{db} = \min(T_{db1}, T_{db2}) = \min(446.19, 621.16) = 446.19 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Verification Against Applied Load**
$$T_u = 200 \text{ kN} \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_{db} = 446.19 \text{ kN}$$

Since $T_{db} > T_u$, the block shear strength is **ADEQUATE**. The connection is safe against block shear failure.

**Step 4: Supplementary Check — Net Section Rupture of the Angle**
For completeness, the rupture strength of the angle itself:
- $A_n = A_g - 3 \cdot d_h \cdot t = 1510 - 3 \cdot 20 \cdot 10 = 910$ mm²
- $\alpha = 0.80$ (single angle, one leg connected)
- $T_{dn} = 0.80 \cdot 910 \cdot 410 / 1.25 = 238784$ N = 238.78 kN

Since $T_{dn} = 238.78$ kN $> T_u = 200$ kN ✓
Gross yielding: $T_{dg} = 1510 \cdot 250 / 1.10 = 343.18$ kN $> 200$ kN ✓

**Design is SAFE in all respects.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Block Shear Questions):**
> 1. **Always calculate BOTH Mode 1 and Mode 2** — selecting only one mode (whichever seems "obvious") is incomplete and loses 3-4 marks.
> 2. **Two shear planes exist for the gusset plate** (one on each side of the tension block) — so $A_{vg} = 2 L_v t_g$, not $L_v t_g$.
> 3. **The factor $\sqrt{3}$** comes from the von Mises yield criterion; do not omit it.
> 4. **The net tension area can be ZERO** in some configurations — do not panic; the equation is still valid.
> 5. **Always verify the governing strength of the member itself** in addition to block shear, because block shear may not be the only failure mode.

---

## 5.3 KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Tension Members**

- [ ] **Definition:** Tension members carry axial tensile loads; stress is $\sigma = T/A_g$.
- [ ] **Hole diameter:** $d_h = d + 2$ mm (for $d \leq 24$ mm); $d_h = d + 3$ mm (for $d > 24$ mm).
- [ ] **Net area (straight path):** $A_n = A_g - n d_h t$.
- [ ] **Net area (staggered path):** $A_n = A_g - \sum d_h t + \sum (p^2/4g) t$ — always check both paths and choose the minimum.
- [ ] **Shear lag factor ($\alpha$):** 1.00 (welded all), 0.80 (bolted all), 0.60 (single leg, 1 bolt), 0.70 (lug angle / end connection).
- [ ] **Effective net area:** $A_e = \alpha A_n$.
- [ ] **Yielding strength:** $T_{dg} = A_g f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ with $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$.
- [ ] **Rupture strength:** $T_{dn} = \alpha A_n f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ with $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$.
- [ ] **Design strength:** $T_d = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn}, T_{db})$.
- [ ] **Block shear (Mode 1):** $T_{db1} = A_{vg} f_y / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0}) + A_{tn} f_u / \gamma_{m1}$.
- [ ] **Block shear (Mode 2):** $T_{db2} = A_{vn} f_u / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m1}) + A_{tg} f_y / \gamma_{m0}$.
- [ ] **Block shear design strength:** $T_{db} = \min(T_{db1}, T_{db2})$ — always compute both modes.
- [ ] **Slenderness limit:** $L/r \leq 350$ for general tension members; $\leq 250$ for dynamic loads.
- [ ] **Steel grades:** Fe 410 ($f_y = 250$, $f_u = 410$ MPa) and Fe 490 ($f_y = 350$, $f_u = 490$ MPa) are most common.
- [ ] **Code reference:** All provisions are from **IS 800:2007**, Section 6 (Clauses 6.1 to 6.4).
- [ ] **Connection eccentricity:** For single angle connections, eccentricity induces bending, but IS 800:2007 accounts for it via the shear lag factor $\alpha$.
- [ ] **Staggered hole rule:** The term $p^2/4g$ is added **only when** the failure path goes diagonally between staggered holes.
- [ ] **Block shear is critical** for short, compact connections with closely spaced bolts.
- [ ] **Net section rupture is critical** for slender members with few, widely spaced bolts.
- [ ] **Gross yielding is critical** when there are no holes (welded connections) or when holes are minimal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examination Mantra for Module 4:**
> "**Check all paths. Use correct $\alpha$. Verify all three strengths. Always confirm slenderness. Block shear — both modes.**"

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommended Study Resources (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **IS 800:2007** — Clauses 6.1, 6.3, 6.4 (most important).
> 2. **"Design of Steel Structures" by S.K. Duggal** — Chapter on Tension Members.
> 3. **"Limit State Design of Steel Structures" by S. Ramchandra & V. Gehlot** — Standard reference.
> 4. **KTU Previous Year Question Papers** (Dec 2018 to May 2024) — Available on the KTU student portal.
> 5. **Steel Tables** (S.P. Gupta or V.N. Vazirani) — Mandatory for section properties during exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beams and Deflection of Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Beams and Deflection of Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Steel Beams

A **beam** in steel structural engineering is a structural member primarily subjected to **transverse loads** and **bending moments**, designed to transfer loads to columns, walls, or other supporting elements. In steel construction, beams are typically fabricated using hot-rolled sections such as **I-sections (ISMB, ISWB)**, **Channels (ISMC, ISLC)**, **T-sections**, or built-up plate girders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET864 Module 4):**
> The module specifically addresses the **design philosophy**, **strength criteria**, and **serviceability limits** (deflection control) of steel beams as governed by **IS 800:2007** — the *General Construction in Steel — Code of Practice*.

### 1.2 Classification of Steel Beams (IS 800:2007 Perspective)

Under the **Limit State Method** of design adopted by IS 800:2007, beams are classified based on **lateral restraint conditions**:

| Classification | Description | Design Implications |
|----------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Laterally Supported Beam** | Compression flange is restrained against lateral-torsional buckling (LTB) by positive connections to decking, slabs, or purlins. | Full plastic/elastic moment capacity may be utilized. |
| **Laterally Un-Supported Beam** | Compression flange is free to displace laterally and twist over a portion of its length. | Capacity is reduced by bending stress reduction factor $\chi_{LT}$ to account for LTB. |

### 1.3 What is Beam Deflection?

**Deflection** is the vertical displacement of a beam under applied loads, measured from its original unloaded position. It is a **serviceability criterion** — it does not typically cause collapse but affects:

- Aesthetic appearance (sagging, cracking of finishes)
- Functionality (door operation, vibrations)
- Damage to non-structural elements (partitions, cladding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Diving Board Effect":**
> Imagine a diving board at a swimming pool. When you stand at its tip, it bends downward — that visible bending is *deflection*. A *steel beam* supporting a floor behaves similarly: it bends (deflects) under the weight of people, furniture, and its own self-weight. The code (IS 800:2007) limits this "bending" to prevent the floor from feeling bouncy or causing the glass windows above to crack — even though the beam itself isn't breaking. This is why *deflection* is checked separately from *strength* in design.

### 1.4 Design Philosophy — Dual Requirement

Every steel beam in KTU exam problems must satisfy **TWO independent checks**:

1. **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** → Beam must NOT fail (yielding, buckling, fracture).
2. **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** → Beam must NOT deflect excessively under *service loads* (unfactored loads, generally working loads).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Solving only for *strength* and ignoring *deflection*. In KTU board exams, marks are explicitly awarded in Module 4 for demonstrating both ULS and SLS verification.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (IS 800:2007)

- **Partial Safety Factor for Material ($\gamma_{m0}$)** = **1.10**
- **Partial Safety Factor for Material ($\gamma_{m1}$)** = **1.25** (for ultimate stress checks)
- **Modulus of Elasticity, $E$** = **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa**
- **Poisson's Ratio, $\nu$** = **0.30**
- **Shear Modulus, $G$** = **76.92 kN/mm² ≈ 77 GPa**
- **Standard Deflection Limit (general beams)** = **L/300** (under imposed loads only)
- **Deflection Limit (beams carrying brittle finishes)** = **L/360**
- **Deflection Limit (cantilevers)** = **L/150**
- **Total deflection (DL+LL) limit** = **L/250** (typically)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Deflection Limits:**
> *"Brittle = Big"*: For brittle materials, the limit is tighter (L/360) because cracks are dangerous.
> *"Cantilever = Critical"*: Cantilevers are most restrictive (L/150) because of amplified tip movement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Bending Strength of Laterally Supported Beams (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.1)

For a beam whose compression flange is **effectively restrained against lateral-torsional buckling**, the **design bending moment capacity** depends on the **section classification** (compact, semi-compact, slender).

### 2.1.1 Section Classification (Recall from Module 1)

| Class | Behavior at ULS | Design Bending Strength $M_d$ |
|-------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| **Plastic / Compact** | Can develop full plastic moment, rotation ≥ 3θ_y | $M_d = \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ |
| **Semi-Compact** | Yielding initiates, no plastic hinge | $M_d = Z_e \cdot \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ |
| **Slender** | Local buckling before yielding | $M_d = Z_e \cdot \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ (with effective $Z_e$ reduced) |

Where:
- $Z_p$ = **Plastic Section Modulus** (mm³)
- $Z_e$ = **Elastic Section Modulus** (mm³)
- $f_y$ = **Yield Stress of Steel** (N/mm²)
- $\beta_b$ = **Factor for the type of section** (depends on cross-section symmetry)

### 2.1.2 Values of $\beta_b$ (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.1.2)

| Section Type | $\beta_b$ |
|--------------|-----------|
| I and H sections (plastic/neutral axis in web) | **1.0** |
| Hollow rectangular sections, Box sections | **1.0** |
| Solid rectangular sections | **$\dfrac{Z_e}{Z_p}$** ≤ 1.0 |
| Circular hollow sections | **1.0** (with $Z_p = Z_e$ effectively) |
| I, H, channel sections bent about minor axis | **1.0** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-tested in KTU:** For standard ISMB/ISWB sections bent about the **major axis**, $\beta_b = 1.0$ and $M_d = Z_p \cdot f_{yd}$ for compact sections, where $f_{yd} = f_y / \gamma_{m0}$.

## 2.2 Design Bending Strength — Laterally Un-Supported Beams (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.2)

When lateral support is inadequate, **lateral-torsional buckling (LTB)** governs:

$$
M_d = \chi_{LT} \cdot \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \quad \text{(for compact sections)}
$$

The **reduction factor $\chi_{LT}$** is determined from:
- **$\lambda_{LT}$** = Non-dimensional slenderness ratio
- **Tables 14 & 15** of IS 800:2007 (depending on section type and steel grade)

For rolled I-sections (with $r_y / r_x$ as per section properties):
- **$\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y / M_{cr}}$**, where $M_{cr}$ is the **elastic critical moment**.

## 2.3 Design Shear Strength (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.4)

The **nominal shear strength** $V_n$ for a beam web:

$$
V_n = A_v \cdot \dfrac{f_{yw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}}
$$

The **design shear strength**:

$$
V_d = V_n \quad (\text{when } V_d \leq 0.6 V_d)
$$

For **rolled I-sections**, the shear area $A_v$:

$$
A_v = h \cdot t_w
$$

Where $h$ is the **overall depth of the section** and $t_w$ is the **web thickness**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick check**: For a standard ISMB 500 ($h = 500$ mm, $t_w = 10.2$ mm):
> $A_v = 500 \times 10.2 = 5100$ mm²
> $V_d = 5100 \times 250 / (\sqrt{3} \times 1.10) ≈ 668$ kN (for Fe 410).

## 2.4 Web Buckling Strength (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.4.2)

When the web is **slender** (high $d/t_w$), shear buckling controls:

$$
V_{db} = A_v \cdot \dfrac{f_{yw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} \cdot \chi_v
$$

Where $\chi_v$ depends on **$\lambda_w$** (web slenderness for shear):

$$
\lambda_w = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_{yw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \tau_{cr}}}
$$

## 2.5 Web Crippling Strength (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.4.3)

At points of **concentrated loads** (beam-column connections, bearing stiffeners), the web can cripple:

$$
F_w = (b_1 + n_2) \cdot t_w \cdot \dfrac{f_{yw}}{\gamma_{m0}}
$$

Where:
- $b_1$ = **Stiff bearing length**
- $n_2$ = **Length obtained by dispersion at 45°** (limited to $1.5 \cdot d$ from face of bearing)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Beams & Deflection

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **all high-yield formulas** for Module 4. Bookmark this — these are the equations that appear in **90% of KTU board problems**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Design Yield Stress | $f_{yd} = f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | N/mm², with $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ |
| 2 | Plastic Moment Capacity | $M_p = Z_p \cdot f_y$ | N·mm |
| 3 | Design Bending Strength (Compact) | $M_d = \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_{yd}$ | N·mm |
| 4 | Design Bending Strength (Semi-Compact) | $M_d = Z_e \cdot f_{yd}$ | N·mm |
| 5 | Design Shear Strength | $V_d = A_v \cdot f_{yw} / (\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0})$ | N |
| 6 | Shear Area (I-section) | $A_v = h \cdot t_w$ | mm² |
| 7 | Maximum Shear Stress (Rectangular) | $\tau_{max} = 1.5 \cdot V/A$ | N/mm² |
| 8 | Elastic Deflection (SS Beam, UDL) | $\delta = 5wL^4 / (384EI)$ | mm |
| 9 | Elastic Deflection (Cantilever, UDL) | $\delta = wL^4 / (8EI)$ | mm |
| 10 | Elastic Deflection (SS Beam, Point Load) | $\delta = PL^3 / (48EI)$ | mm |
| 11 | Deflection Limit (Simply Supported) | $L/300$ | under imposed load only |
| 12 | Deflection Limit (Cantilever) | $L/150$ | under imposed load only |
| 13 | Section Modulus Check | $Z_{req} = M_u / f_{yd}$ | mm³ (for trial section) |
| 14 | Self-Weight (ISMB) | $w_{sw} ≈ 0.0785 \cdot A$ | kN/m (with $A$ in cm²) |
| 15 | E (Modulus of Elasticity) | $2.0 \times 10^5$ | N/mm² |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building Floor Beams**: Must satisfy both strength (ULS) and deflection (SLS) to prevent cracking of floor finishes.
- **Crane Girders**: Heavy point loads → high shear → web crippling & shear buckling checks are critical.
- **Bridge Girders**: Long spans → deflection often governs; camber is provided to offset dead load deflection.
- **Industrial Mezzanines**: Vibration serviceability is a major concern → deflection and dynamic checks dominate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deflection of a Simply Supported Beam Under UDL

This is the most-tested derivation in KTU Module 4. We derive it using the **Double Integration Method** from first principles.

### Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation

For an Euler-Bernoulli beam, the curvature relationship is:

$$
EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)
$$

For a simply supported beam of span $L$ under uniformly distributed load $w$ (force per unit length):

**Bending moment at distance $x$ from left support:**

$$
M(x) = \dfrac{w \cdot x}{2} \cdot (L - x)
$$

Expanding:

$$
M(x) = \dfrac{w \cdot L \cdot x}{2} - \dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — First Integration (Slope Equation)

Integrating once with respect to $x$:

$$
EI \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x) \, dx + C_1
$$

$$
EI \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{w \cdot L \cdot x^2}{4} - \dfrac{w \cdot x^3}{6} + C_1
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Boundary Condition 1 (Slope Symmetry)

At mid-span ($x = L/2$), by symmetry, the **slope is zero**: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$.

$$
0 = \dfrac{w \cdot L}{4} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^2 - \dfrac{w}{6} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1
$$

$$
0 = \dfrac{w \cdot L^3}{16} - \dfrac{w \cdot L^3}{48} + C_1
$$

Finding common denominator (48):

$$
0 = \dfrac{3 w L^3}{48} - \dfrac{w L^3}{48} + C_1
$$

$$
C_1 = -\dfrac{2 w L^3}{48} = -\dfrac{w L^3}{24}
$$

### Step 4 — Second Integration (Deflection Equation)

$$
EI \cdot y = \int \left( \dfrac{w L x^2}{4} - \dfrac{w x^3}{6} + C_1 \right) dx + C_2
$$

$$
EI \cdot y = \dfrac{w L x^3}{12} - \dfrac{w x^4}{24} + C_1 \cdot x + C_2
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Boundary Condition 2 (Zero Deflection at Support)

At $x = 0$, $y = 0$:

$$
0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = 0
$$

### Step 6 — Maximum Deflection at Mid-Span ($x = L/2$)

$$
EI \cdot \delta_{max} = \dfrac{w L}{12} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^3 - \dfrac{w}{24} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^4
$$

$$
EI \cdot \delta_{max} = \dfrac{w L^4}{96} - \dfrac{w L^4}{384}
$$

Common denominator 384:

$$
EI \cdot \delta_{max} = \dfrac{4 w L^4}{384} - \dfrac{w L^4}{384} = \dfrac{3 w L^4}{384}
$$

$$
EI \cdot \delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384}
$$

Dividing by $EI$:

$$
\boxed{\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}}
$$

This is the **standard KTU result** for max deflection of a simply supported beam under UDL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Same formula appears in Part A (3 marks) as "State the formula" and Part B (14 marks) as "Derive using Macaulay's/Double Integration method."**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Deflection Formula

The following decision matrix is critical for KTU problem-solving:

| Loading & Support Condition | Maximum Deflection | Location of Max |
|------------------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| Simply Supported (SS) + UDL $w$ | $5wL^4 / 384EI$ | Mid-span ($x = L/2$) |
| Simply Supported (SS) + Point Load $P$ at center | $PL^3 / 48EI$ | Mid-span ($x = L/2$) |
| Cantilever (fixed-free) + UDL $w$ | $wL^4 / 8EI$ | Free end ($x = L$) |
| Cantilever + Point Load $P$ at free end | $PL^3 / 3EI$ | Free end ($x = L$) |
| Fixed-Fixed Beam + UDL $w$ | $wL^4 / 384EI$ | Mid-span |
| SS Beam + Moment $M$ at one end | $ML^2 / 16EI$ (at other end if $M$ is sagging) | Depends on sign |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Verification: "Beam Adequate in Deflection" Procedure

This is the KTU-mark-winning sequence for serviceability check:

**Step 1:** Compute **service moment** $M_s$ under *unfactored working loads* (NOT factored).

**Step 2:** Compute **service load** $w_s$ or $P_s$ in kN/m or kN.

**Step 3:** Calculate **maximum deflection** using appropriate formula. For SS beam + UDL:
$$
\delta = \dfrac{5 w_s L^4}{384 E I_{xx}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute **permissible deflection** from IS 800:2007 / IS 875 (Part 2):
$$
\delta_{perm} = \dfrac{L}{300} \quad \text{(or as specified)}
$$

**Step 5:** Check: $\delta \leq \delta_{perm}$.
- If **YES** → Beam is **OK in deflection**.
- If **NO** → Increase section size and **recheck**.

## 3.4 Numerical Illustration: Full Deflection Check

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Given:** A simply supported steel beam of span $L = 6$ m, section ISMB 300.
> Service load: $w_s = 20$ kN/m (including self-weight of beam already accounted for).
> Check deflection per IS 800:2007.

**Step 1 — Section Properties of ISMB 300** (from IS Hand-Book/SP-6):
- $I_{xx} = 8606$ cm⁴ = $8606 \times 10^4$ mm⁴
- $I_{yy} = 453.9$ cm⁴

**Step 2 — Maximum Service Deflection:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot w_s \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I_{xx}}
$$

Convert units consistently:
- $w_s = 20$ kN/m = **20 N/mm**
- $L = 6$ m = **6000 mm**
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $I_{xx} = 8606 \times 10^4$ mm⁴

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \times 20 \times (6000)^4}{384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 8606 \times 10^4}
$$

Numerator:
$$
5 \times 20 \times 1.296 \times 10^{15} = 1.296 \times 10^{17}
$$

Denominator:
$$
384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 8.606 \times 10^7 = 6.609 \times 10^{15}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{1.296 \times 10^{17}}{6.609 \times 10^{15}} = 19.61 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Permissible Deflection:**

$$
\delta_{perm} = \dfrac{L}{300} = \dfrac{6000}{300} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$
\delta_{max} = 19.61 \text{ mm} \leq \delta_{perm} = 20 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Conclusion**: The ISMB 300 beam is **SAFE in deflection** at the serviceability limit state.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Beam Design Workflow (ULS + SLS)

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **complete design process** for steel beams as taught in PECET864 Module 4:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Span, Loads, Support Conditions] --> B[Compute Factored Load wu or Pu]
    B --> C[Compute Factored Moment Mu and Shear Vu]
    C --> D[Compute Required Section Modulus Zreq = Mu / fyd]
    D --> E[Select Trial Section from Steel Table ISMB/ISWB]
    E --> F[Classify Section: Compact / Semi-Compact / Slender]
    F --> G{Check Section Classification}
    G -->|Compact| H[Compute Zp and fyd]
    G -->|Semi-Compact| I[Use Ze and fyd]
    G -->|Slender| J[Use Effective Ze]
    H --> K[Compute Design Bending Strength Md]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Md greater or equal Mu ?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Strength Check Passed]
    L -->|No| N[Select Larger Section]
    N --> E
    M --> O[Compute Shear Area Av = h x tw]
    O --> P[Compute Design Shear Strength Vd]
    P --> Q{Vd greater or equal Vu ?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Shear Check Passed]
    Q -->|No| S[Add Web Stiffeners or Larger Section]
    S --> E
    R --> T[Serviceability Check: Compute Service Load ws]
    T --> U[Compute Service Deflection delta using EI method]
    U --> V{Permissible Deflection delta perm}
    V -->|delta less or equal delta perm| W[DEFLECTION OK - Design Complete]
    V -->|No| X[Increase Section Modulus or Depth]
    X --> E
    W --> Y[End: Final Section Selected and Verified]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The flowchart begins at top with **load definition** and proceeds through **strength checks (ULS)** before ending with **deflection check (SLS)**. Each "diamond" is a *decision gate* — if a check fails, we must loop back and select a larger section.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Steel Beams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steel Beams] --> B[Based on Lateral Support]
    A --> C[Based on Section Geometry]
    B --> D[Laterally Supported]
    B --> E[Laterally Un-Supported]
    C --> F[Plastic / Compact]
    C --> G[Semi-Compact]
    C --> H[Slender]
    D --> I[Use Full Zp x fyd]
    E --> J[Use chi LT x Zp x fyd]
    F --> K[Plastic Hinge Forms]
    G --> L[Yielding Initiates No Plastic Hinge]
    H --> M[Local Buckling Before Yield]
    I --> N[Design Bending Strength Md]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.3 Stress Distribution Schematic — Deflected Beam Profile

The following schematic illustrates the **bending moment diagram**, **shear force diagram**, and **deflected shape** for a simply supported beam under UDL — the most common KTU exam setup.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Beam_SS_UDL["Simply Supported Beam - UDL"]
        S1[Support A: x equals 0] --- S2[Mid-Span: x equals L by 2] --- S3[Support B: x equals L]
    end
    subgraph BMD["Bending Moment Diagram Parabolic"]
        M1[Zero at A] --> M2[Maximum wL2 by 8 at Mid] --> M3[Zero at B]
    end
    subgraph SFD["Shear Force Diagram Linear"]
        V1[+wL by 2 at A] --> V2[Zero at Mid] --> V3[-wL by 2 at B]
    end
    subgraph DEF["Deflected Shape"]
        D1[Zero at A] --> D2[Maximum 5wL4 by 384EI DOWN at Mid] --> D3[Zero at B]
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Limit State Design Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Limit State Design of Steel Beam] --> B[Ultimate Limit State ULS]
    A --> C[Serviceability Limit State SLS]
    B --> B1[Bending Strength Check]
    B --> B2[Shear Strength Check]
    B --> B3[Web Buckling Check]
    B --> B4[Web Crippling Check]
    B --> B5[Bearing Stiffener Design]
    C --> C1[Deflection Check]
    C --> C2[Vibration Check]
    C --> C3[Fatigue Check for Crane Girders]
    B1 --> D[IS 800 Clause 8.2]
    B2 --> E[IS 800 Clause 8.4]
    B3 --> F[IS 800 Clause 8.4.2]
    B4 --> G[IS 800 Clause 8.4.3]
    B5 --> H[IS 800 Clause 8.7]
    C1 --> I[IS 800 Clause 8.6 and IS 875 Part 2]
    C2 --> J[IS 800 Clause 8.6 and IS 875]
    C3 --> K[IS 800 Clause 13]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "lateral-torsional buckling" of a steel beam. When does it occur?**

**Model Answer:**

Lateral-torsional buckling (LTB) is a **mode of instability** in which a beam subjected to bending about its major axis suddenly undergoes a **lateral displacement** combined with **twisting** of the cross-section. It occurs when the **compression flange**, which is not adequately restrained, buckles sideways between points of lateral support, while the tension flange remains relatively straight.

**Conditions for LTB to occur:**
1. The beam is **bent about its major (strong) axis**.
2. The **compression flange is laterally unsupported** (or inadequately supported).
3. The **slenderness ratio** of the unsupported length is high.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for LTB:** *"Lateral + Twist"* → LTB = Lateral displacement **AND** Twist.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the deflection limits as per IS 800:2007 for (a) simply supported beams, (b) cantilevers, and (c) beams carrying brittle finishes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Member Type | Deflection Limit |
|-------------|------------------|
| (a) Simply supported beams (general) | **L/300** (under imposed load only) |
| (b) Cantilevers | **L/150** (under imposed load only) |
| (c) Beams carrying brittle finishes | **L/360** (under imposed load) |

Where $L$ is the **effective span** of the member.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The deflection is calculated using **unfactored (service) loads** and the **moment of inertia of the gross cross-section**. Live load (imposed load) deflection is checked separately from total load deflection in some specifications.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU ESE convention, students are given an **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Design of a Laterally Supported Beam with Deflection Check**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, PECET864]**

A simply supported steel beam of **effective span 7.5 m** carries a **uniformly distributed imposed load of 25 kN/m** (office floor). The compression flange is laterally supported throughout. Design the beam using ISMB section in **Fe 410 grade steel**. Verify the section in:
- **(a)** Bending (ULS) — 7 marks
- **(b)** Deflection (SLS) — 7 marks

**Section properties of ISMB 300 (Fe 410):**
- Depth $h = 300$ mm, $t_w = 7.5$ mm, $b_f = 140$ mm
- $I_{xx} = 8606$ cm⁴, $Z_{e,xx} = 573.8$ cm³
- $Z_{p,xx} = 654.9$ cm³, $A_v = h \cdot t_w = 300 \times 7.5 = 2250$ mm²
- Self-weight ≈ 0.46 kN/m

---

#### **Part (a) Solution — Bending Check (ULS)**

**Step 1 — Compute Factored Load:**

Factored UDL including self-weight:
$$
w_u = 1.5 \cdot (w_{LL} + w_{DL}) = 1.5 \cdot (25 + 0.46) = 1.5 \cdot 25.46 = 38.19 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Factored Bending Moment:**

$$
M_u = \dfrac{w_u \cdot L^2}{8} = \dfrac{38.19 \times 7.5^2}{8}
$$

$$
M_u = \dfrac{38.19 \times 56.25}{8} = \dfrac{2148.19}{8} = 268.52 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Design Yield Stress:**

$$
f_{yd} = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{250}{1.10} = 227.27 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Section Classification:**

For ISMB 300 in Fe 410 (rolled I-section), it is a **compact section**. Hence $\beta_b = 1.0$.

**Step 5 — Design Bending Strength:**

$$
M_d = \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_{yd}
$$

Convert $Z_p$ to mm³: $Z_p = 654.9 \text{ cm}^3 = 654.9 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3$

$$
M_d = 1.0 \times 654.9 \times 10^3 \times 227.27
$$

$$
M_d = 1.488 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 148.85 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Oops — Strength is INSUFFICIENT!**

We need $M_d \geq M_u = 268.52$ kN·m. So **ISMB 300 is not adequate**.

**Step 6 — Try ISMB 500:**

Section properties of ISMB 500:
- $Z_p = 2093$ cm³, $I_{xx} = 45218$ cm⁴, $t_w = 10.2$ mm

$$
M_d = 1.0 \times 2093 \times 10^3 \times 227.27 = 4.756 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 475.7 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
M_d = 475.7 \text{ kN·m} \geq M_u = 268.52 \text{ kN·m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** **ISMB 500 is adequate in bending.**

---

#### **Part (b) Solution — Deflection Check (SLS)**

**Step 1 — Service Load:**

$$
w_s = 25 + 0.46 = 25.46 \text{ kN/m} = 25.46 \text{ N/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Service Moment (for reference):**

$$
M_s = \dfrac{w_s \cdot L^2}{8} = \dfrac{25.46 \times 56.25}{8} = 179.0 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Deflection:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot w_s \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I_{xx}}
$$

Convert: $L = 7500$ mm, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $I_{xx} = 45218 \times 10^4$ mm⁴

Numerator:
$$
5 \times 25.46 \times (7500)^4 = 5 \times 25.46 \times 3.164 \times 10^{15}
$$
$$
= 4.028 \times 10^{17}
$$

Denominator:
$$
384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 45218 \times 10^4 = 3.475 \times 10^{16}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{4.028 \times 10^{17}}{3.475 \times 10^{16}} = 11.59 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Permissible Deflection:**

$$
\delta_{perm} = \dfrac{L}{300} = \dfrac{7500}{300} = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$
\delta_{max} = 11.59 \text{ mm} \leq \delta_{perm} = 25 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Conclusion:** **ISMB 500 is SAFE in deflection.** Section is accepted.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — Laterally Un-Supported Beam with Web Crippling Check**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Module Choice]**

A steel beam of **effective span 5 m** is **not laterally supported** over its full length. It carries a **factored point load of 200 kN at mid-span** and a **factored UDL of 30 kN/m** (including self-weight). Design the section in:
- **(a)** Bending strength (laterally un-supported) — 7 marks
- **(b)** Shear strength and check for web crippling at support — 7 marks

Use **Fe 410** grade steel. Section properties of ISMB 450:
- $h = 450$ mm, $b_f = 150$ mm, $t_w = 9.4$ mm, $t_f = 17.4$ mm
- $Z_p = 1533$ cm³, $I_{xx} = 30450$ cm⁴
- $r_y = 3.18$ cm, $h_f = 424.6$ mm (inner depth between flanges)
- Effective length for LTB, $L_{LT} = 5$ m (assume full span)

---

#### **Part (a) Solution — Bending (Laterally Un-Supported)**

**Step 1 — Factored Loads & Moments:**

$w_u = 30$ kN/m, $P_u = 200$ kN

$$
M_u = \dfrac{w_u L^2}{8} + \dfrac{P_u L}{4} = \dfrac{30 \times 25}{8} + \dfrac{200 \times 5}{4}
$$

$$
M_u = 93.75 + 250 = 343.75 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Elastic Critical Moment for LTB:**

Using the simplified formula for rolled I-sections (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.2.1):

$$
M_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I_y \cdot h_f}{2 \cdot L_{LT}^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I_w}{L_{LT}^2 \cdot G \cdot I_t}}
$$

For a first estimate, the simplified **Perry-Robertson** form can be used. For typical rolled sections, the **non-dimensional slenderness** is:

$$
\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y / M_{cr}}
$$

For this problem, assume (from IS 800 Table 14 for rolled section) $\chi_{LT} = 0.65$ (typical for $L/r_y \approx 100$ to 150 in Fe 410).

**Step 3 — Design Bending Strength:**

$$
M_d = \chi_{LT} \cdot \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_{yd}
$$

With $\beta_b = 1.0$:

$$
M_d = 0.65 \times 1.0 \times 1533 \times 10^3 \times 227.27
$$

$$
M_d = 0.65 \times 3.483 \times 10^8 = 2.264 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 226.4 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$
M_d = 226.4 \text{ kN·m} \leq M_u = 343.75 \text{ kN·m} \quad \times
$$

**ISMB 450 is INADEQUATE in bending.** Need a larger section (e.g., **ISMB 500 or ISWB**).

> **Conclusion:** Select a heavier section, e.g., **ISWB 550** with $Z_p ≈ 2610$ cm³, and recheck.

---

#### **Part (b) Solution — Shear Strength and Web Crippling**

**Step 1 — Design Shear Force at Support:**

$$
V_u = \dfrac{w_u L}{2} + \dfrac{P_u}{2} = \dfrac{30 \times 5}{2} + \dfrac{200}{2} = 75 + 100 = 175 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Design Shear Strength of ISMB 450:**

Shear area:
$$
A_v = h \cdot t_w = 450 \times 9.4 = 4230 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
V_d = \dfrac{A_v \cdot f_{yw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{4230 \times 250}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10}
$$

$$
V_d = \dfrac{1.0575 \times 10^6}{1.905} = 5.55 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 555.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**

$$
V_d = 555 \text{ kN} \geq V_u = 175 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Conclusion:** Section is **safe in shear**.

**Step 4 — Web Crippling Check at Support:**

Assume bearing length $b_1 = 100$ mm (column flange width).

Dispersion length:
$$
n_2 = 2.5 \cdot (t_f + R_1)
$$

For rolled I-section, take $n_2 \approx 1.5 \cdot (d - 2 t_f)$ limited — typically use $n_2 = 75$ mm (standard assumption for rolled sections with column flange).

$$
F_w = (b_1 + n_2) \cdot t_w \cdot \dfrac{f_{yw}}{\gamma_{m0}}
$$

$$
F_w = (100 + 75) \times 9.4 \times \dfrac{250}{1.10}
$$

$$
F_w = 175 \times 9.4 \times 227.27 = 3.737 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 373.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$
F_w = 373.7 \text{ kN} \geq V_u = 175 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Conclusion:** Web crippling is safe. **Provide bearing stiffener if web crippling was inadequate** (not required here, but is the typical KTU follow-up question).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$** → Loss of 1-2 marks per sub-part. Always show the partial safety factor explicitly.
> 2. **Not converting units consistently** (kN·m to N·mm or cm³ to mm³) → Loss of 1 mark in numerical problems.
> 3. **Using factored loads in deflection check** → Wrong! Deflection is checked at **SLS with unfactored service loads**. Examiner's pet peeve.
> 4. **Ignoring section classification** → Always state if section is compact/semi-compact/slender before applying the relevant $M_d$ formula.
> 5. **Not stating the deflection limit clearly** → Always write "$\delta_{perm} = L/300$ for simply supported beam per IS 800:2007" before comparing.
> 6. **Forgetting the $\chi_{LT}$ factor for laterally un-supported beams** → A full-mark answer MUST mention lateral support conditions.
> 7. **Not checking the trial section adequacy** — if a section fails, you must explicitly state "section inadequate, try larger section" and show the iteration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist for last-day exam revision!**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] A **beam** is a flexural member; the **limit state method** requires BOTH ULS and SLS checks.
- [ ] **ULS** = strength (bending, shear, web buckling, web crippling).
- [ ] **SLS** = serviceability (deflection, vibration).
- [ ] IS 800:2007 uses $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ and $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².

### ✅ Section Classification Logic
- [ ] **Compact** → use $Z_p$ with $\beta_b$ factor.
- [ ] **Semi-Compact** → use $Z_e$ only.
- [ ] **Slender** → use effective $Z_e$ (with local buckling effects).
- [ ] For ISMB/ISWB bent about major axis: $\beta_b = 1.0$.

### ✅ Bending Strength Formulae
- [ ] Laterally Supported Compact: $M_d = \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_{yd}$
- [ ] Laterally Un-Supported: $M_d = \chi_{LT} \cdot \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_{yd}$
- [ ] $\chi_{LT}$ < 1.0 always for un-supported beams; obtained from IS 800 Tables 14/15.

### ✅ Shear Strength Formulae
- [ ] $V_d = A_v \cdot f_{yw} / (\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0})$
- [ ] $A_v = h \cdot t_w$ for rolled I-sections.
- [ ] Web buckling → use $\chi_v$ (Cl. 8.4.2).
- [ ] Web crippling → $F_w = (b_1 + n_2) \cdot t_w \cdot f_{yw} / \gamma_{m0}$ (Cl. 8.4.3).

### ✅ Deflection Formulae — Memorize
- [ ] SS Beam + UDL → $\delta_{max} = 5wL^4 / 384EI$
- [ ] SS Beam + Point Load at center → $\delta_{max} = PL^3 / 48EI$
- [ ] Cantilever + UDL → $\delta_{max} = wL^4 / 8EI$
- [ ] Cantilever + Point Load at tip → $\delta_{max} = PL^3 / 3EI$

### ✅ Deflection Limits
- [ ] SS beam (general): **L/300** (imposed load only).
- [ ] SS beam (brittle finishes): **L/360**.
- [ ] Cantilever: **L/150**.
- [ ] Total deflection (DL+LL): **L/250** (typical).

### ✅ Numerical Problem Strategy
- [ ] **Step 1**: Compute factored loads (×1.5) and factored moment.
- [ ] **Step 2**: Compute $Z_{req}$ and select trial section.
- [ ] **Step 3**: ULS checks (bending, shear).
- [ ] **Step 4**: Compute service loads (×1.0) and service deflection.
- [ ] **Step 5**: Compare with permissible limit; iterate if needed.
- [ ] **Step 6**: Final conclusion — section accepted or rejected.

### ✅ KTU Code References to Quote
- [ ] Bending strength → **Cl. 8.2.1** (supported) and **Cl. 8.2.2** (un-supported).
- [ ] Shear strength → **Cl. 8.4**.
- [ ] Web buckling → **Cl. 8.4.2**.
- [ ] Web crippling → **Cl. 8.4.3**.
- [ ] Bearing stiffeners → **Cl. 8.7**.
- [ ] Deflection limits → **Cl. 8.6** (and **IS 875 Part 2** for load combinations).

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Don't confuse SLS (service load, no factor) with ULS (factored load, 1.5 factor).
- [ ] Don't forget to convert $I$ from cm⁴ to mm⁴ and $Z$ from cm³ to mm³.
- [ ] Don't write $E$ in GPa when $w$ is in N/mm — keep units consistent.
- [ ] Always state the section classification before computing $M_d$.
- [ ] Always end with a **clear conclusion** — "Section is safe" or "Section is inadequate, redesign required."

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Beams and Deflection of Beams.**
> **All the best for your KTU Board Examination!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Stresses and Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET864 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Combined Stresses and Connections # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What Are "Combined Stresses and Connections"? In real steel structures, members and connections rarely carry a single, "pure" type of internal force. A column in an industrial frame is usually compressed **and** bent at the same time by frame action. A bracket welded to a column carries a vertical load and a side thrust. A beam-column splice must transfer an axial force **and** a bending moment simultaneously. This simultaneous occurrence is what we call **Combined Stresses and Connections** under the KTU 2024 PECET864 syllabus. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (IS 800:2007 framework):** > *Combined stresses* refer to the state in a steel member (or its connecting elements) when two or more primary internal actions — axial force (tension or compression), shear force, and bending moment — act together, producing a compound stress state that must be checked against an **interaction equation** instead of a single-stress capacity. > *Combined connections* are joints (bolted or welded) that must safely transfer more than one type of force/moment simultaneously between connected members. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a person pushing a heavy suitcase while also trying to **lift** its handle upwards. - The **pushing** is like an **axial force** (compression or tension). - The **lifting** is like a **bending moment** trying to tilt the suitcase. - Your **wrist** is the **connection** that has to safely hand over BOTH actions to your arm. A designer cannot check only "can I push it?" or "can I lift it?" — the wrist must be strong enough for the **combined effort**. Similarly, the IS 800:2007 code provides **interaction formulas** that say: *"the combined effect of all actions, taken together as a fraction of each individual capacity, must not exceed 1.0."* ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Partial safety factor for material** (γ_m0) = **1.10** (yielding), γ_m1 = **1.25** (ultimate), γ_mb = **1.25** (bolt), γ_mw = **1.25** (weld). > - **Elastic modulus of steel**, E = **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200 GPa)**. > - **Poisson's ratio**, ν = **0.30** for structural steel. > - **Ultimate strength grades (f_ub)** for bolt classes: 4.6 → 400 MPa, 8.8 → 800 MPa, 10.9 → 1040 MPa. > - **Yield strength (f_y)** of standard steel: 250 MPa (Fe 410) and 350 MPa (Fe 490). ## 1.4 Why This Topic Is High-Yield in KTU 2024 The combined-stress module is one of the most frequently tested areas because: 1. Real KTU university problems almost always ask design/check questions on **beam-columns**. 2. Combined connections (shear + tension on a bolt group) form the standard 14-mark ESE question. 3. The interaction equations are the gateway to advanced topics like **portal frame design** and **plate girders** that appear in higher semesters. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Philosophy of IS 800:2007 — "Interaction Approach" Instead of inventing a brand-new strength formula for the combined case, IS 800:2007 uses a **linear/non-linear interaction (sum-of-ratios)** approach. Each individual action (N, M, V) is normalized by its standalone capacity. When the sum (or sum-of-squares) is ≤ 1.0, the section/member is **safe**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Idea:** A structure is safe under combined loading if the *fractional* demand on each capacity, added together, does not exceed unity. ## 2.2 Classification of Combined-Stress Problems (Syllabus Roadmap) | # | Sub-Topic | IS 800 Clause | Typical KTU Question | |---|-----------|---------------|----------------------| | 1 | Axial Tension + Bending | 9.3.2 | Gantry girder tie + bending | | 2 | Axial Compression + Bending (Beam-Column) | 9.3.1 | Building column with eccentric load | | 3 | Shear + Bending (High Shear Region) | 9.2.2 | Plate girder, coped beam | | 4 | Bearing + Bending in beams (web) | 8.4 | Beams over supports | | 5 | Bolt under Combined Shear + Tension | 10.4.5 | Bracket connection | | 6 | Weld under Combined Direct + Bending | 10.5.7 | Welded seat/bracket | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below must be memorised for the KTU ESE. Units = N, mm, MPa. ### (A) Member — Combined Axial + Bending (IS 800 Cl. 9.3) | Case | Design Check (Interaction Equation) | Cross-Reference | |------|-------------------------------------|-----------------| | Tension + Bending | $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{t,d}} + \dfrac{M_{d,x}}{M_{d,x,cap}} + \dfrac{M_{d,y}}{M_{d,y,cap}} \leq 1.0$$ | Cl. 9.3.2.1 | | Compression + Bending (Lateral-Torsional, both axes) | $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}} + K_x\,\dfrac{C_{m,x}\,M_{d,x}}{M_{d,x,cap}} + K_y\,\dfrac{C_{m,y}\,M_{d,y}}{M_{d,y,cap}} \leq 1.0$$ | Cl. 9.3.1.1 | | Compression + Bending (overall member) | $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}} + \dfrac{M_{d,x}}{M_{d,x,cap}} + \dfrac{M_{d,y}}{M_{d,y,cap}} \leq 1.0$$ | Cl. 9.3.1.2 | where K_x, K_y = 1.0 (in-plane frames) or 1.0 − (0.2 n') ... 1.0 (per Cl. 9.3.1.1), and n' = N_d / N_c,d. ### (B) Member — Combined Shear + Bending (IS 800 Cl. 9.2.2) | Condition | Provision | |-----------|-----------| | V_d ≤ 0.6 V_dy | Full moment capacity M_d (no reduction) | | V_d > 0.6 V_dy | Reduce moment capacity to **M_dv** (high-shear provision) | $$M_{dv} = M_d - \beta_b\,(M_{fd} - M_{fdv})$$ where β_b = 1.0 (for plastic/compact), M_fd = plastic moment, M_fdv = 0.5 × shear area × f_yw / γ_m0 (cl. 9.2.2.2). ### (C) Bolt — Combined Shear + Tension (IS 800 Cl. 10.4.5) | Step | Formula | |------|---------| | Reduced shear capacity | $$V_{dsb} = \dfrac{V_{nsb}}{\gamma_{mb}}\left(1 - 0.8\,\dfrac{f_{tb}}{f_{ub}}\right)$$ | | Combined check | $$\dfrac{V_{sb}}{V_{dsb}} + \dfrac{T_b}{T_{db}} \leq 1.0$$ | ### (D) Weld — Combined Direct + Bending (IS 800 Cl. 10.5.7) | Resultant normal + shear stress | Check | |--------------------------------|------| | σ_re = √(f_a² + 3 q²) ≤ f_e / (√3 γ_mw) | Combined normal + shear | | f_a = σ_b = M/Z_e, q = V/A_e (for fillet weld) | Use throat area A_e | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial buildings:** Crane columns carry vertical load + bending from crane surge. - **Transmission towers:** Members at joints carry axial + bending due to oblique wind. - **Portal frames:** Columns are designed as **beam-columns** in every single practical project. - **Welded brackets, gusseted bases, gantry girders** all use the combined-stress interaction checks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Beam-Column Interaction Equation (Cl. 9.3.1.1) ### 3.1.1 Physical Setting Consider a **W-shape (I-section)** column of length L, pin-ended, subjected to: - An axial compressive force **N_d**, - A uniaxial bending moment **M_dx** about the major axis. Under pure compression, the member buckles elastically at Euler load N_c. Under pure bending, it attains moment capacity M_d. When **both** act together, plastic-zone interaction begins at a smaller combination. IS 800 uses a **generalised Perry-Robertson** interaction. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation The KTU-style derivation proceeds as follows: **Step 1 —** Define the **non-dimensional load ratio**: $$n' = \dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}}$$ This expresses how close the column is to its pure compression capacity. **Step 2 —** Define the **equivalent moment** that accounts for in-plane moment magnification (Cl. 9.3.1.1, Eq. 14): $$M_{eff,x} = C_{m,x}\left[\dfrac{M_{d,x}}{1 - n'}\right] \cdot K_x \cdot (\text{reduction factor})$$ with $$K_x = 1.0 \quad \text{when } n' \leq 0.2$$ $$K_x = 1.0 - 0.6\,(n' - 0.2) \quad \text{when } n' > 0.2$$ **Step 3 —** Form the **additive linear interaction** as a lower-bound, conservative envelope of the actual non-linear failure surface: $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}} + \dfrac{M_{eff,x}}{M_{d,x,cap}} \leq 1.0$$ **Step 4 —** Substitute M_eff,x from Step 2: $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}} + \dfrac{K_x\,C_{m,x}\,M_{d,x}}{(1 - n')\,M_{d,x,cap}} \leq 1.0$$ **Step 5 —** Replace (1 − n') in the denominator with the in-plane reduction factor (since (1 − n') ≤ 1 amplifies the moment — a conservative simplification merges this into K_x): $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}} + \dfrac{K_x\,C_{m,x}\,M_{d,x}}{M_{d,x,cap}} \leq 1.0 \quad \text{...(Cl. 9.3.1.1, Eq. 14)}$$ **Step 6 —** Extend **bi-axially** for I-sections bent about both axes: $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}} + K_x\,\dfrac{C_{m,x}\,M_{d,x}}{M_{d,x,cap}} + K_y\,\dfrac{C_{m,y}\,M_{d,y}}{M_{d,y,cap}} \leq 1.0 \quad \text{...(Cl. 9.3.1.1)}$$ **Step 7 —** Note that for the **overall member stability check** (Cl. 9.3.1.2), the code uses the simpler form (no K-factors, no C_m) on the **member buckling** side: $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}} + \dfrac{M_{d,x}}{M_{d,x,cap}} + \dfrac{M_{d,y}}{M_{d,y,cap}} \leq 1.0$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic behind linearity:** Although the true failure surface is *curved* (parabolic-like in n′-m space), IS 800 uses a **straight line** because: > (a) it is conservative for steel (linear ⇒ slight over-design), > (b) it is *simple to apply* in design offices, > (c) it agrees with AISC 360 and Eurocode 3 in spirit, making it internationally accepted. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Bolt Under Combined Shear and Tension **Problem (typical KTU 3-mark style).** A 10.9-grade M20 bolt in a bracket connection carries a factored shear V_sb = 50 kN and a factored tension T_b = 40 kN. The plate is 12 mm thick; γ_mb = 1.25. Check the bolt for combined loading. **Given:** - f_ub = 1040 N/mm² (10.9 grade), f_yb = 940 N/mm², - Nominal shear area A_nb = 0.78 × (π/4) × 20² = 245 mm², - Nominal tensile stress area A_sb = 245 mm². **Step 1 — Nominal shear capacity:** $$V_{nsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot A_{nb} = \dfrac{1040}{\sqrt{3}} \times 245 = 1.47 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 147.1 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Nominal tensile capacity:** $$T_{nb} = 0.9\,f_{ub}\,A_{sb} = 0.9 \times 1040 \times 245 = 2.293 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 229.3 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Design capacities:** $$V_{dsb,0} = \dfrac{V_{nsb}}{\gamma_{mb}} = \dfrac{147.1}{1.25} = 117.7 \text{ kN}$$ $$T_{db} = \dfrac{T_{nb}}{\gamma_{mb}} = \dfrac{229.3}{1.25} = 183.4 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Average tensile stress in bolt due to direct tension:** $$f_{tb} = \dfrac{T_b}{A_{sb}} = \dfrac{40\,000}{245} = 163.3 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5 — Reduced shear capacity because the bolt is in tension:** $$V_{dsb} = V_{dsb,0}\left(1 - 0.8\,\dfrac{f_{tb}}{f_{ub}}\right) = 117.7 \left(1 - 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{163.3}{1040}\right)$$ $$= 117.7\,(1 - 0.1256) = 117.7 \times 0.8744 = 102.9 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 6 — Combined interaction check:** $$\dfrac{V_{sb}}{V_{dsb}} + \dfrac{T_b}{T_{db}} = \dfrac{50}{102.9} + \dfrac{40}{183.4} = 0.486 + 0.218 = 0.704 < 1.0$$ **Result:** Bolt is **SAFE** (utilisation = 70.4 %). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Member Cross-Checks for Beam-Columns | Check No. | Action | IS 800 Reference | Critical Case | |-----------|--------|------------------|---------------| | 1 | Overall column strength | Cl. 9.3.1.1 | Compression + Major-axis Bending | | 2 | Overall column strength | Cl. 9.3.1.1 | Compression + Minor-axis Bending | | 3 | Overall column strength | Cl. 9.3.1.1 | Compression + Biaxial Bending | | 4 | Local capacity check | Cl. 9.3.1.2 | Section strength (no buckling) | | 5 | Section shear check | Cl. 9.2.2 | When V_d > 0.6 V_dy | | 6 | LTB combined | Cl. 9.3.1.1 | When M_dx dominant | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Combined-Stress Design The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete design-check logic** a KTU student must follow when a member is subjected to combined stresses. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Identify Actions on Member
N_d, V_d, M_dx, M_dy"] --> nodeB{"Axial Force Present?"} nodeB -- "Yes" --> nodeC{"Tension or Compression?"} nodeB -- "No" --> nodeD{"Shear + Bending Only?"} nodeC -- "Tension" --> nodeE["Apply Cl. 9.3.2.1
Tension + Bending"] nodeC -- "Compression" --> nodeF["Apply Cl. 9.3.1.1
Beam-Column Check"] nodeD -- "Yes" --> nodeG["Check V_d vs 0.6 V_dy"] nodeG --> nodeH{"V_d less than 0.6 V_dy?"} nodeH -- "Yes" --> nodeI["Full M_d Allowable"] nodeH -- "No" --> nodeJ["Use M_dv Reduced
Moment Capacity"] nodeE --> nodeK["Compute N_t,d, M_dx,cap, M_dy,cap"] nodeF --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL["Evaluate Interaction Ratio Sum"] nodeL --> nodeM{"Sum less than or equal to 1.0?"} nodeM -- "Yes" --> nodeN["Member SAFE"] nodeM -- "No" --> nodeO["Revise Section"] nodeI --> nodeN nodeJ --> nodeN ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Bolt Under Combined Shear and Tension ```mermaid flowchart TD boltA["Compute V_sb and T_b
from analysis"] --> boltB["Determine f_ub, A_nb, A_sb"] boltB --> boltC["Compute V_nsb and T_nb"] boltC --> boltD["Compute V_dsb,0 and T_db
using partial safety factors"] boltD --> boltE["Compute f_tb = T_b / A_sb"] boltE --> boltF["Compute V_dsb reduced
V_dsb equals V_dsb,0 into 1 minus 0.8 f_tb by f_ub"] boltF --> boltG["Compute Utilisation Ratio
V_sb by V_dsb plus T_b by T_db"] boltG --> boltH{"Ratio less than or equal to 1.0?"} boltH -- "Yes" --> boltI["Bolt SAFE"] boltH -- "No" --> boltJ["Increase Bolt Grade or Section"] ``` ## 4.3 Free-Body Diagram of a Typical Beam-Column (Textual Schematic) ``` ↑ M_dx | _____ | | | | --| I |--- → N_d | |_____| | ↑ V_d ==================== (ground) ``` - **N_d** acts along the member axis (compression positive). - **V_d** is the transverse shear from frame action. - **M_dx** is the major-axis bending at the section. - The combined-stress zone is the **bottom of the column near the base**, where all three coexist. ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Weld Under Combined Direct + Bending Stresses ```mermaid flowchart TD weldA["Compute Direct Shear f_a
and Bending Stress sigma_b"] --> weldB["Compute Shear from V
q equals V divided by A_e"] weldB --> weldC["Compute Resultant
sigma_re equals sqrt of f_a squared plus 3 q squared"] weldC --> weldD["Compute Permissible
f_e divided by sqrt 3 times gamma_mw"] weldD --> weldE{"sigma_re less than f_e by sqrt 3 gamma_mw?"} weldE -- "Yes" --> weldF["Weld SAFE"] weldE -- "No" --> weldG["Increase Throat Size"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **State the IS 800:2007 interaction check for a member subjected to combined axial tension and bending. Mention the clause number.** **Model Answer:** For combined axial tension and bending (IS 800:2007 Cl. 9.3.2.1): $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{t,d}} + \dfrac{M_{d,x}}{M_{d,x,cap}} + \dfrac{M_{d,y}}{M_{d,y,cap}} \leq 1.0$$ where N_t,d = design tensile strength = A_g f_y / γ_m0, and M_dx,cap, M_dy,cap are the bending capacities about the respective axes reduced appropriately when high shear is present. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the IS 800:2007 interaction equation for a bolt under combined shear and tension?** **Model Answer:** For a bolt carrying combined shear V_sb and tension T_b (Cl. 10.4.5): $$\dfrac{V_{sb}}{V_{dsb}} + \dfrac{T_b}{T_{db}} \leq 1.0$$ where $$V_{dsb} = \dfrac{V_{nsb}}{\gamma_{mb}}\left(1 - 0.8\,\dfrac{f_{tb}}{f_{ub}}\right), \quad T_{db} = \dfrac{0.9\,f_{ub}\,A_{sb}}{\gamma_{mb}}, \quad f_{tb} = \dfrac{T_b}{A_{sb}}$$ ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > Both alternatives are full 14-mark ESE-style problems. Each is split into 7-mark sub-questions (a) and (b). The student must answer **either** (i) **OR** (ii) as the ESE question choice. --- ### ❖ Question Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, ESE Pattern]** A built-up column of section ISHB 450 @ 907 N/m carries an **axial compression of 1200 kN** and a **major-axis bending moment of 150 kNm** under factored loads. The column is 4.5 m long, pin-ended about both axes, with effective lengths L_ex = L_ey = 4.5 m. The grade of steel is Fe 410 (f_y = 250 N/mm²). Check the adequacy of the section as per IS 800:2007. Given for ISHB 450: A = 11534 mm², r_x = 174.6 mm, r_y = 60.1 mm, Z_p,x = 1533.4 × 10³ mm³, Z_p,y = 273.5 × 10³ mm³, Z_e,x = 1362.4 × 10³ mm³, Z_e,y = 227.7 × 10³ mm³, t_f = 17.4 mm, t_w = 9.8 mm, f_yw = 250 MPa. #### Sub-part (a) [7 Marks] **Compute the buckling class, slenderness ratios, design compressive strength N_c,d and design bending capacity M_dx,cap.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Slenderness ratios:** $$\lambda_x = \dfrac{L_{ex}}{r_x} = \dfrac{4500}{174.6} = 25.77$$ $$\lambda_y = \dfrac{L_{ey}}{r_y} = \dfrac{4500}{60.1} = 74.88$$ **Step 2 — Buckling class (IS 800 Table 10):** For ISHB, rolled section, axis y-y: buckling class **'b'**; axis x-x: class **'a'** (use Table 10 of IS 800:2007). For λ ≈ 75 → f_yw = 250 MPa, class 'b': design compressive stress f_c,d ≈ 165 N/mm² (from Table 9, interpolating for λ = 75 and f_y = 250). **Step 3 — Design compressive strength:** $$N_{c,d} = f_{c,d} \cdot A = 165 \times 11534 \times 10^{-3} = 1903.1 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Bending capacity (plastic, since the section is compact):** $$M_{d,x,cap} = \dfrac{f_y \cdot Z_{p,x}}{\gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{250 \times 1533.4 \times 10^3}{1.10} \times 10^{-3} = 348.5 \text{ kNm}$$ #### Sub-part (b) [7 Marks] **Apply the combined-stress interaction check and conclude.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute n' (load ratio):** $$n' = \dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}} = \dfrac{1200}{1903.1} = 0.631$$ Since n' > 0.2, K_x ≠ 1.0: $$K_x = 1.0 - 0.6\,(n' - 0.2) = 1.0 - 0.6 \times 0.431 = 0.741$$ **Step 2 — Assume C_m,x = 1.0 (no transverse loading on a beam-column pin-ended).** **Step 3 — Apply Cl. 9.3.1.1 interaction:** $$\dfrac{N_d}{N_{c,d}} + K_x \cdot \dfrac{C_{m,x}\,M_{d,x}}{M_{d,x,cap}} \leq 1.0$$ $$= 0.631 + 0.741 \times \dfrac{150}{348.5} = 0.631 + 0.319 = 0.950 < 1.0$$ **Conclusion:** The section is **SAFE** with ~5 % reserve capacity. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. Forgetting to **apply the K_x factor** when n' > 0.2 (loses 2 marks). > 2. Reading the wrong **buckling class** from Table 10 (loses 1 mark). > 3. Confusing the **overall member check (Cl. 9.3.1.1)** with the **section check (Cl. 9.3.1.2)** — KTU often requires BOTH. > 4. Not mentioning **γ_m0 = 1.10** explicitly in the answer. --- ### ❖ Question Choice B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, ESE Pattern]** A **bracket connection** in an industrial building is made using 6 bolts of grade 8.8 and diameter 20 mm, arranged in two vertical rows of three. The bracket carries a factored **vertical load V = 90 kN** (shear) and a **factored tensile force N = 60 kN** acting along the bolt axis. The connecting plate is 14 mm thick (Fe 410). Check the bolts for combined shear and tension as per IS 800:2007. Given: f_ub = 800 N/mm² (8.8 grade), A_nb = 245 mm², A_sb = 245 mm², γ_mb = 1.25. #### Sub-part (a) [7 Marks] **Determine the design shear capacity of the bolts (with no tension) and design tensile capacity.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Nominal shear capacity (threads in shear plane — assume worst case):** $$V_{nsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot A_{nb} = \dfrac{800}{\sqrt{3}} \times 245 = 1.132 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 113.2 \text{ kN per bolt}$$ **Step 2 — Design shear capacity per bolt:** $$V_{dsb,0} = \dfrac{113.2}{1.25} = 90.5 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Nominal tensile capacity:** $$T_{nb} = 0.9 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{sb} = 0.9 \times 800 \times 245 = 1.764 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 176.4 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Design tensile capacity:** $$T_{db} = \dfrac{176.4}{1.25} = 141.1 \text{ kN}$$ #### Sub-part (b) [7 Marks] **Apply the combined interaction check per IS 800:2007.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Per-bolt forces (assuming equal distribution):** $$V_{sb} = \dfrac{90}{6} = 15.0 \text{ kN per bolt}, \quad T_b = \dfrac{60}{6} = 10.0 \text{ kN per bolt}$$ **Step 2 — Tensile stress in bolt:** $$f_{tb} = \dfrac{T_b}{A_{sb}} = \dfrac{10\,000}{245} = 40.82 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Reduced shear capacity:** $$V_{dsb} = V_{dsb,0} \left(1 - 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{f_{tb}}{f_{ub}}\right) = 90.5 \left(1 - 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{40.82}{800}\right)$$ $$= 90.5 \times (1 - 0.0408) = 90.5 \times 0.9592 = 86.8 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Combined interaction check:** $$\dfrac{V_{sb}}{V_{dsb}} + \dfrac{T_b}{T_{db}} = \dfrac{15.0}{86.8} + \dfrac{10.0}{141.1} = 0.1728 + 0.0709 = 0.244 < 1.0$$ **Conclusion:** The bolts are **SAFE** (utilisation ≈ 24.4 %). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. Forgetting to use **A_nb = 0.78 × (π/4) × d²** for threads-in-shear-plane case — losing 2 marks. > 2. Not using γ_mb = **1.25** (some students use 1.10 by mistake). > 3. Failing to check that **T_b / T_db ≤ 1.0** alone (a bolt cannot take more tension than its design capacity regardless of shear). > 4. Not computing **prying action** if the bracket is a T-stub (advanced — loses bonus marks if missed). ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) - ✅ Combined stresses = multiple actions on a single member; the **interaction sum ≤ 1.0** is the universal IS 800 acceptance criterion. - ✅ **Beam-column (Cl. 9.3.1.1)** uses the amplification factor K = 1.0 − 0.6(n' − 0.2) when n' > 0.2. - ✅ **Tension + Bending (Cl. 9.3.2.1)** uses a *linear* sum with no K-factor. - ✅ **High shear (V_d > 0.6 V_dy)** → reduce moment capacity to M_dv per Cl. 9.2.2. - ✅ **Bolt combined check (Cl. 10.4.5):** V/V_dsb + T/T_db ≤ 1.0; reduced shear capacity uses 0.8 f_tb/f_ub factor. - ✅ **Weld combined check (Cl. 10.5.7):** √(f_a² + 3 q²) ≤ f_e/(√3 γ_mw). - ✅ Partial safety factors to remember: γ_m0 = 1.10, γ_m1 = 1.25, γ_mb = γ_mw = 1.25. - ✅ Always state **clause numbers** in KTU answers — they earn easy marks. - ✅ Distinguish **overall buckling (member)** from **section (cross-section) capacity** — KTU regularly tests this distinction. - ✅ For Fe 410 steel, f_y = 250 N/mm²; for Fe 490, f_y = 350 N/mm². - ✅ The **n' value** controls whether the K-factor kicks in: n' ≤ 0.2 → K = 1.0. - ✅ Always write the **load ratio** n' and the **moment ratio** separately before summing them. - ✅ In a **biaxial bending** beam-column, include **both** major and minor-axis moment terms with their respective K and C_m factors.
